bcvfd-crab-feed.title: The BCVFD Crab Feed
bcvfd-crab-feed.html:
The Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department's Crab Feed is the department's late-winter fundraiser: an evening of fresh crab, pasta, salad, and bread at the fire station, served by the volunteers, with tickets limited and the proceeds supporting the all-volunteer department. It has landed in March in recent years, and tickets tend to sell out because the room only holds so many. This page keeps the details in one place and points you to the department for the next confirmed date.

This year

The next crab feed date had not been announced as of July 2026. The department has held it in March in recent years, so it's worth checking in late winter. When the date is set, it will appear on the Boulder Creek Fire Department website. Tickets are limited, so if you want to go, watch for the announcement and buy early rather than counting on the door.

What to expect

The crab feed is an indoor, sit-down evening at the fire station, 13230 Central Avenue. The department serves fresh crab with pasta, salad, and bread, and past years have had wine, beer, and non-alcoholic drinks available for purchase. It's a cracking-crab-with-your-neighbors kind of night, run by the volunteers themselves.

Two things to plan around. First, tickets are capped. The department has listed 250 tickets in a recent year, and when they're gone they're gone, so this is not a walk-up event. Second, the price and exact timing move year to year. Recent tickets have run in the range of about $55 to $70 per person, and start times have varied, so go by whatever the department posts for the current year. If you have questions, the department has listed 831-338-7222 for the crab feed.

History

The crab feed is one of a handful of fundraisers the Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department runs through the year, alongside the July 4 pancake breakfast and the summer BBQ and dance. They all serve the same purpose: keeping an all-volunteer department equipped.

That department goes back to 1892. According to the department's own history, Boulder Creek's volunteer firefighters organized after two large fires nearly destroyed downtown and the nearby village of Lorenzo in 1891, and about twenty local residents trained as the town's first crew through 1891 into 1892. The fire station in the middle of downtown dates to 1939. More than a century on, the town still funds the crew's equipment the old-fashioned way, one dinner at a time, and the crab feed is the winter one.

Frequently asked questions

When is the Boulder Creek Fire Department crab feed?

It's typically held in March, but the next date is set and announced by the department each year. As of July 2026 the next date had not been announced. Check the Boulder Creek Fire Department crab feed page in late winter.

How much are tickets, and do they sell out?

Recent prices have run about $55 to $70 per person, and yes, tickets are capped (the department has listed 250 in a recent year), so buy early once the date is announced. Go by the current-year price the department posts.

What's served?

Fresh crab with pasta, salad, and bread, at the fire station. Wine, beer, and non-alcoholic drinks have been available for purchase in past years.

Where does the money go?

It supports the all-volunteer Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department, one of the fundraisers that keeps the crew equipped alongside the July 4 pancake breakfast and the summer BBQ and dance.

Who do I contact with questions?

The department has listed 831-338-7222 for the crab feed. For the current date and tickets, the department's crab feed page is the source to watch.

See what else is happening around town on the Boulder Creek Local events page. Part of the department, or spot a detail here that needs fixing? Send it to us. For the next confirmed date and tickets, check the Boulder Creek Fire Department.

bcvfd-summer-bbq-dance.title: The BCVFD Summer BBQ & Dance
bcvfd-summer-bbq-dance.html:
Once a year, on a Saturday evening in late August, the big bay doors at the Boulder Creek fire station roll up, the engines roll out, and the apparatus bay becomes a dining hall. The Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department's Summer BBQ & Dance is the department's biggest fundraiser of the year: a tri-tip and chicken dinner served by the firefighters, live bands and dancing out back, and activities for kids, with the money going toward rescue and medical equipment for the all-volunteer crew. In 2026 it's expected on a late-August Saturday, likely August 22, at the fire station on Central Avenue, though the department hadn't posted the confirmed date as of mid-July. Check with the department before you plan around it. This page keeps the details in one place and points you to the department for each year's confirmed times, prices, and tickets.

This year (2026)

The 2026 Summer BBQ & Dance hadn't been posted with a confirmed date as of mid-July 2026. If the department keeps to its usual pattern, the fourth Saturday of August, it would fall on Saturday, August 22, 2026, at the Boulder Creek Fire Station, 13230 Central Avenue. Treat that as the likely date, not a confirmed one, and check the department before you plan around it.

The rest of the evening's specifics are expected to follow the department's usual pattern, but as of this writing the current-year times, prices, band lineup, and ticket link had not all been confirmed. Recent years have opened doors in the early evening with dinner, bands, and dancing running into the night. For the confirmed 2026 schedule, prices, and tickets, watch the Boulder Creek Fire Department website and its Facebook page. If you can't make it on the 22nd, the department also takes donations year-round at bcfd.com.

What to expect

The BBQ has a shape the department has kept for years, and it's an easy night out for the whole family.

Firefighters cook and serve a tri-tip and chicken dinner in the apparatus bay, the same garage the engines live in the other 364 days of the year. Out back, live bands play and there's room to dance. For kids, past years have brought a bounce house and face painting, which makes it one of the rare valley evenings where nobody needs a babysitter.

The evening usually starts in the early-evening dinner hour and runs late, with the last dance toward 11 p.m. Ticket options in recent years have covered dinner and dance together, dinner only, or dance only, with young kids free. Because prices and tickets change year to year, and the department moved off its old ticket platform after 2025, buy through whatever link the department posts for the current year rather than an old one.

A few practical notes. The station lot is part of the party, so the department asks people to park on the street, carpool, or walk if they can, and to plan a safe ride home. Bring cash as a backup even if you buy online. And this is a working fire station, so if the crew gets a call during the event, they go, and that's the whole point of what you're supporting.

History

The department throwing you dinner has been Boulder Creek's since 1892. According to the department's own history, the volunteer fire department formed after two large fires nearly destroyed downtown Boulder Creek and the nearby village of Lorenzo in 1891. Within days of the last fire, townspeople gathered to organize, and through 1891 into the spring of 1892 about twenty local businessmen and laborers trained as the town's first firefighters. The Boulder Creek Fire Protection District followed in 1923, and the fire station at the town's main intersection went up in 1939. The department has stayed all-volunteer the whole way.

That history is why the BBQ matters as more than a party. It's the department's biggest fundraiser of the year, and the money doesn't go somewhere abstract: it buys rescue and medical equipment for the crew that answers wildfires, car wrecks, and late-night medical calls across the San Lorenzo Valley. The department is a registered nonprofit (the Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Dept, EIN 94-6106801), and its donation page says the large majority of donations go toward that equipment. One honest note on taxes: donations and sponsorships to the nonprofit are tax-deductible, but event tickets are not, since you're getting dinner and a show for them.

Frequently asked questions

When and where is the 2026 BCVFD Summer BBQ & Dance?

The department hadn't posted a confirmed 2026 date as of mid-July. Based on its usual late-August pattern it's likely Saturday, August 22, 2026, at the Boulder Creek Fire Station, 13230 Central Avenue, in downtown Boulder Creek. Watch bcfd.com and the department's Facebook page for the confirmed date and start time.

How much are tickets and where do I buy them?

Prices and the ticket link change year to year, and the department moved off its old ticket platform after 2025, so buy through whatever link the department posts for the current year. Recent years have offered dinner-and-dance, dinner-only, and dance-only options, with young kids free. Check bcfd.com for the current prices and link.

What's on the menu, and is it kid-friendly?

The firefighters serve a tri-tip and chicken dinner in the apparatus bay, with bands and dancing out back. Past years have included a bounce house and face painting for kids, and it's built as a family event.

Where does the money go?

It's the department's biggest fundraiser of the year, and proceeds buy rescue and medical equipment for the all-volunteer Boulder Creek Fire Department. The department's donation page says the large majority of donations go toward that equipment.

Can I still help if I can't attend?

Yes. The Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department takes donations year-round through bcfd.com, and unlike event tickets, donations to the nonprofit are tax-deductible.

See what else is happening around town on the Boulder Creek Local events page. Part of the department, or spot a detail here that needs fixing? Send it to us. For confirmed times, prices, and tickets, check the Boulder Creek Fire Department.

big-basin-day-trip-boulder-creek.title: A Responsible Big Basin Day Trip From Boulder Creek
big-basin-day-trip-boulder-creek.html:
The short version first. Big Basin Redwoods State Park is open for limited day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, the main entrance sits about 9 miles up Highway 236 from downtown Boulder Creek, parking reservations are strongly recommended, and all campgrounds in the main park remain closed. The park is still recovering from the 2020 CZU fire, so what's open changes regularly. Before you drive up, check the official Big Basin Redwoods State Park page. That page, not this one, is the source of truth for current conditions, and nothing below is a guarantee of access.

All that said, a Big Basin day trip from Boulder Creek is very doable, and doing it thoughtfully matters more now than it ever did. Here's how to plan it.

Know what you're visiting

Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, and Boulder Creek has been its gateway town ever since. Then, in August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex fire changed everything. Per CAL FIRE's incident record, the fire burned 86,509 acres across Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties. It reached Big Basin two days after igniting and, according to Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks, burned roughly 18,000 acres inside the park, over 97 percent of it, and destroyed nearly every structure, including the historic Park Headquarters.

The recovery is real, though. State Parks reports that most of the old-growth redwoods survived, new growth is coming in fast, and wildlife has returned. The park reopened for limited access in July 2022, and Sempervirens Fund notes that access has kept evolving since, shaped by both the fire and the severe storms of 2023. You won't find the shaded, built-out Big Basin of the old photos. You'll find something rarer: an old-growth forest rebuilding itself in the open sun, with new trail mileage opening in phases.

So set your expectations accordingly. Facilities are limited, shade is thinner than it used to be, and a trail that was open last month may be closed today.

Reserve your parking before you go

Day-use parking reservations are highly recommended. State Parks lists regular-sized autos at $6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 per vehicle without a reservation, and parking is limited enough that showing up without one is a gamble on busy days. Book through the Big Basin parking reservation page run by Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks. Passenger vehicles use the main form; disabled parking and bus or horse trailer parking have separate reservations.

Two more parking notes. Oversized vehicles and trailers can't be accommodated for day use right now, so leave the RV at camp. And fees or rules can change, so confirm current pricing on the official park page when you book.

Getting there from Boulder Creek

From downtown Boulder Creek, turn onto Highway 236 (the Big Basin Highway) and follow it about 9 miles to the main entrance. Every road into Big Basin is curvy, and lower 236 is narrow, so give yourself 25 to 30 unhurried minutes, watch for cyclists, and use the turnouts if you're holding up locals.

If you'd rather not drive in, two alternatives are worth checking. On summer weekends between Memorial Day weekend and Labor Day weekend, State Parks has run a free pilot shuttle from the Saddle Mountain temporary parking area along Highway 236 to the main day-use area. And Santa Cruz METRO's Route 35B offers weekend bus service to the park. Both are subject to schedules and seasons, so verify each is running before you count on it.

What's open in the park

Conditions change, so treat this as a snapshot from mid-July 2026, not a promise.

The classic first stop is the Redwood Loop Trail, an easy 0.6-mile walk through the old-growth grove near the historic park core. It's flat, family-friendly, and the best place to see fire scars and new growth side by side on trees that have been alive for well over a thousand years.

The trail network keeps growing. Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks reports that a recent round of work opened about 4 more miles of trail, restoring access to Sempervirens Falls, along with segments of the Skyline to the Sea Trail from the main day-use area to China Grade Road and beyond toward Castle Rock, plus the Hihn Hammond connector along Opal Creek. At the same time, closures still happen: State Parks currently lists Hollow Tree Trail as temporarily closed from winter storm damage. Check the current trail map on the park page before you commit to a route.

Camping is the big limitation. All campgrounds in the main park are closed, and no camping reservations are available there. The one exception on the property is Rancho del Oso, the coastal side of Big Basin near Waddell Beach, which is partially reopened and offers walk-in and bike-in camping. If an overnight matters to you, plan around that or look at other parks nearby.

Expect limited services inside the park: temporary facilities rather than the old lodge-and-store setup. Bring your own water, food, and layers.

How to visit responsibly

A recovering forest asks a little more of its visitors. Stay on open trails, both for your own safety and because the regrowth on either side of the tread is doing important work. Respect every closure sign; they usually mark hazard trees, unstable ground, or active restoration, even when the path beyond looks fine. Pack out everything you bring in. Skip drones and speakers. And don't count on cell coverage in the basin, so download maps ahead of time and tell someone your plan.

Weather deserves respect here too. Heat, wind after storms, and winter road damage all affect this park, and this site never interprets conditions for you. Check the forecast, check the park page the morning you go, and if the park says don't come, don't come.

One more way to be responsible: support the recovery. Sempervirens Fund, the land trust that helped create the park in the first place, publishes updates and supports the ongoing restoration work.

Make Boulder Creek part of the day

The most direct route into Big Basin runs straight through downtown Boulder Creek, and the town is the natural place to bookend the trip. Fuel up, grab coffee and sandwiches, and buy anything you forgot before you head up 236, because you won't find services along the way. On the way back down, dinner or a cold drink in town lands differently when you've spent the day in the basin.

There's more to it than a nicety. Gateway towns carry the quiet costs of a beloved park, and they felt the fire as directly as the park did. Spending your money in Boulder Creek is the most direct way a day visitor helps this corner of the Santa Cruz Mountains keep rebuilding. If you want the fuller story of the fire and what it meant here, read our companion piece on the events that shaped Boulder Creek once it's live.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a reservation to visit Big Basin?

For parking, a reservation is highly recommended rather than strictly required: $6 per regular vehicle plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without one, per State Parks. Spaces are limited, so booking ahead through the official reservation page is the safe move. Confirm current rules on the park page before you go.

Is camping open at Big Basin?

No. All campgrounds in the main park are closed and camping reservations aren't available there. Rancho del Oso, the park's coastal unit, is partially reopened with walk-in and bike-in camping options.

How far is Big Basin from Boulder Creek?

The main entrance is about 9 miles north of downtown Boulder Creek on Highway 236. The road is narrow and curvy, so plan on roughly half an hour rather than a quick hop.

Did the redwoods survive the CZU fire?

Mostly, yes. The 2020 fire affected 97 percent of the park and destroyed nearly all of its buildings, but State Parks reports that most of the old-growth redwoods survived and are regrowing. The forest looks different now, more open and sunlit, with green regrowth climbing blackened trunks.

What's the best short hike right now?

The Redwood Loop Trail, an easy 0.6-mile walk through the old-growth grove, is the reliable choice for a first visit. Longer options like the reopened stretch to Sempervirens Falls exist, but check the current trail map on the official park page, since individual trails open and close as recovery work continues.

big-basin-first-state-park-history.title: How Big Basin Became California's First State Park
big-basin-first-state-park-history.html:
Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, after a two-year campaign by the Sempervirens Club, a citizens' group formed in the basin itself in May 1900 to stop the last big stands of old-growth redwood from being logged. The purchase created California Redwood Park, which the Sempervirens Fund describes as "the first park established in California in the current state park system." The whole thing started nine miles up the road from Boulder Creek, and it began with a photographer who refused to hand over his negatives.

The basin before the park

People were part of this forest long before the preservation fight. California State Parks notes that humans lived in or near Big Basin for at least 10,000 years, and it identifies the Cotoni and Quiroste peoples, part of the broader Ohlone culture of the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas, as the tribes of this landscape. They harvested seeds and acorns, wove baskets, and hunted elk and deer in these canyons.

That history isn't past tense. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, made up of the surviving descendant families of Indigenous people taken into Mission Santa Cruz (Awaswas speakers) and Mission San Juan Bautista (Mutsun speakers), works today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous stewardship across ancestral lands that run from Ano Nuevo through these mountains to Monterey Bay. State Parks adds that descendants of the basin's tribes are working toward federal recognition.

By the late 1800s, the same forest was being cut fast. Sawmills worked the San Lorenzo Valley below, Boulder Creek was shipping lumber by rail, and the old growth in the basin was privately owned timberland with a short life expectancy.

A photographer refuses to hand over his negatives

The usual telling starts with Andrew P. Hill, a San Jose photographer. According to State Parks' cultural history, Hill photographed redwoods at Welch's Grove in the Santa Cruz Mountains, and the grove's owner accused him of trespassing and demanded his negatives. Hill refused, and vowed to himself to save the trees for future generations. The run-in came in 1899 or 1900, depending on the account; the Sempervirens Fund dates the start of his campaign to 1899.

Either way, Hill went home to San Jose and started organizing. A meeting at Stanford University in May 1900 pulled together journalists, politicians, artists, business owners, and scholars, and the group headed into the mountains to see the threatened forest for themselves.

Thirty-two dollars and a two-year campaign

What happened next is commemorated by a bronze plaque inside the park. California Historical Landmark 827 records that "a group of conservationists led by Andrew P. Hill camped at the base of Slippery Rock on May 15, 1900 and formed the Sempervirens Club to preserve the redwoods of Big Basin." The Sempervirens Fund's own history puts the founding gathering on May 18, along Sempervirens Creek, so accounts differ by a few days. On the substance they agree: a small group camped in the basin, decided the forest had to be saved, passed a hat, and collected 32 dollars to finance the effort.

Club member Carrie Stevens Walter put the goal plainly in 1901, writing of preserving "this magnificent domain of redwood forests," a line State Parks still quotes on the park's page. The club spent more than two years lobbying Sacramento. In 1901 the legislature passed the enabling legislation (unanimously, by State Parks' account), and in 1902 the State of California bought the heart of the basin: roughly 3,500 to 3,800 acres of ancient forest, depending on the account. The landmark plaque dates the deeding of the land to September 20, 1902, and calls it the beginning of the California State Park System.

From California Redwood Park to Big Basin Redwoods

The new park was called California Redwood Park, and it grew from there. State Parks records that the park was renamed Big Basin Redwoods in 1927, and additional purchases (including 3,901 acres acquired from the Big Basin Lumber Company in 1906) kept expanding it. Today the park preserves more than 18,000 acres, including the largest continuous stand of ancient coast redwoods south of San Francisco, with trees between 1,000 and 1,800 years old.

The 1930s gave the park the look longtime visitors remember. Civilian Conservation Corps crews built the redwood Nature Lodge and Park Headquarters, a campfire center, a footbridge, cabins, and much of the trail network.

The club that started it all kept going too. After helping establish Castle Rock State Park in the late 1960s, the organization renamed itself the Sempervirens Fund in 1968, and it's still buying and protecting redwood forest in these mountains today.

The fire that changed everything

On August 18, 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex fire swept through 97 percent of the park's property, according to State Parks. Every historic structure was lost, including the CCC-era headquarters and lodge. The Sempervirens Fund calls the CZU fire, which burned more than 86,000 acres across the Santa Cruz Mountains, the most devastating fire ever recorded in the region.

The trees told a different story than the buildings. State Parks reports that most of the old-growth redwoods survived, and new growth has been coming back across the burned landscape ever since. Coast redwoods have lived through fire for a very long time; the visitor infrastructure hadn't.

What Big Basin looks like now

The park partially reopened in July 2022 and has been recovering in stages since. As of mid-2026, the park's official page lists day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, strongly recommends parking reservations because spaces are limited, and notes that all campgrounds remain closed. Individual trails open and close as work continues, so check current conditions on parks.ca.gov before you drive up; this article can't promise what will be open on any given day. The Rancho del Oso side of the park, off Highway 1 north of Santa Cruz, is open with no entrance fee.

The long-term rebuild runs through Reimagining Big Basin, State Parks' multi-year planning effort. A public visioning process wrapped up in summer 2022, and the project has since produced a Facilities Management Plan, a General Plan Amendment, and an environmental impact report, with a State Park and Recreation Commission hearing on the plans held July 2, 2026. The stated goal is to reestablish facilities that support visitors while protecting the forest that made the park worth fighting for in the first place.

Boulder Creek, the gateway then and now

Boulder Creek's own history is tangled up in this story. The town shipped the lumber that came out of these mountains, and when the logging economy faded in the early twentieth century, it leaned into visitors headed for the park instead. Rail historian Derek Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains research describes Boulder Creek transitioning "from a lumber town to a tourist town" built around attractions like Big Basin.

The geography hasn't changed. The park's official directions start here: from Highway 9 in downtown Boulder Creek, turn north onto Highway 236, and the entrance kiosk is nine miles up the road. If you're headed to the park, town is the last stop for gas, food, and supplies, the same role it's played for well over a century.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Big Basin open right now?

Partially. As of mid-2026 the park is open for day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, parking reservations are strongly recommended, and all campgrounds are closed while rebuilding continues. Trail openings change as recovery work progresses, so check parks.ca.gov before you go.

Did the old-growth redwoods survive the 2020 fire?

Most did, according to State Parks. The CZU fire burned through 97 percent of the park and destroyed every historic building, but coast redwoods are adapted to fire, and the surviving giants have been putting out new growth since. The forest looks different than it did before 2020, and it's still recovering.

What does "California's first state park" actually mean?

Big Basin, established in 1902 as California Redwood Park, was the first park in what became the current California state park system. The state historical landmark at Slippery Rock marks the 1902 land transfer as the beginning of the California State Park System, and State Parks calls Big Basin its oldest park.

How do I get to Big Basin from Boulder Creek?

Take Highway 236 north from Highway 9 in downtown Boulder Creek; the park entrance kiosk is nine miles up. The road is narrow and winding, so allow more time than the mileage suggests, and confirm current access before you go.

Who lived in Big Basin before it was a park?

State Parks identifies the Cotoni and Quiroste peoples, part of the Ohlone culture, and notes that people lived in or near the basin for at least 10,000 years. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, descended from Indigenous people taken into the Santa Cruz and San Juan Bautista missions, stewards ancestral lands in these mountains today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust.

boulder-creek-4th-of-july-parade.title: The Boulder Creek 4th of July Parade
boulder-creek-4th-of-july-parade.html:
Downtown Boulder Creek runs the Fourth of July the same way every year, in the same order. The volunteer firefighters cook a pancake breakfast at the fire house through the morning. The parade rolls down Highway 9 around 10 a.m. Then the whole town shifts to Junction Park for a barbecue and live music into the afternoon. It's an all-day, all-ages tradition the Boulder Creek Business Association calls a parade the town has held "for generations." This page keeps the shape of the day in one place and points you to the organizers for each year's confirmed times and details.

This year (2026)

July 4 is a fixed date, so the parade always lands on the Fourth. In 2026 that fell on a Saturday, and the day ran to its usual shape. Good Times reported the 2026 parade aimed for about 70 entries and was grand marshaled by Dr. Kenneth Reed, a longtime community physician, with the Boulder Creek Business Association, led by president Tess Fitzgerald, organizing.

For the next Fourth of July, the specifics (grand marshal, entry count, exact breakfast pricing, and any road-closure timing) usually get confirmed closer to the date. Watch the Boulder Creek Business Association for parade sign-ups and schedule, and the Boulder Creek Volunteer Fire Department for the pancake breakfast.

What to expect

The day keeps a reliable rhythm, and you can catch as much or as little of it as you like.

Morning: the pancake breakfast. The Boulder Creek Volunteer Firefighters serve breakfast at the fire house at 13230 Central Avenue, starting around 7 a.m. and running toward noon or while supplies last. Recent menus have been pancakes, eggs, sausage, and fruit. It's a fundraiser, so there's a per-plate charge, and the department says all the money raised goes toward buying medical and rescue equipment. Prices shift year to year, so check bcfd.com for the current cost.

Mid-morning: the parade. The parade runs roughly 10 a.m. to noon through downtown Boulder Creek. Per Good Times, it typically steps off near Mountain Street and works its way to the Highway 9 and Highway 236 junction, with Highway 9 closed through downtown for the duration. Entries range from fire engines and local law enforcement to community groups, kids, and decorated everything. Want a spot on the curb? Come early. The sidewalks fill in.

Afternoon: the party at Junction Park. Once the parade clears, the Boulder Creek Recreation and Parks District hosts a barbecue and bands after-party at Junction Park, roughly noon to 4 p.m. Good Times describes gold panning for children, local vendors, food trucks, drinks, and two live bands. Junction Park sits a block east of the highway at Middleton Avenue, the river right there, so it's an easy place to land for the rest of the afternoon.

A few practical notes. Highway 9 closes through downtown while the parade runs, so plan your driving around it or park once and walk. Bring water, sun cover, and cash for the breakfast and vendors. The whole day is built for families, and you can walk it end to end.

History

Boulder Creek has marked the Fourth with a downtown parade for a long time. The Business Association describes it as a tradition the town has kept "for generations," and Good Times reports the parade tradition spans more than a hundred years. We haven't found a verified first-year date, so we won't invent one, but the through-line is clear: a small mountain town that closes its main street once a year to walk down it together.

The pancake breakfast ties the day back to the town's oldest institution. Boulder Creek's fire department has been all-volunteer since it formed in 1892, after two large fires nearly destroyed downtown, and the breakfast is one of the ways residents still fund the equipment those volunteers carry.

Frequently asked questions

When is the Boulder Creek 4th of July parade?

It's on July 4 every year, since it's tied to the holiday. The parade itself runs roughly 10 a.m. to noon, with the pancake breakfast before it and the Junction Park party after. For the coming year's confirmed schedule, check the Boulder Creek Business Association.

Where does the parade go, and does Highway 9 close?

The parade runs through downtown Boulder Creek. Good Times reports it starts near Mountain Street and ends at the Highway 9 and Highway 236 junction, and that Highway 9 closes through downtown while it runs. Plan to park once and walk, or route around downtown that morning.

How much is the pancake breakfast, and where does the money go?

There's a modest per-plate charge that changes year to year, so check bcfd.com for the current price. The Boulder Creek Volunteer Firefighters host it, and the department says all the money raised goes toward medical and rescue equipment.

Can I be in the parade?

Yes. The Boulder Creek Business Association handles parade registration through its July 4th parade page. Sign-ups usually open in the weeks before the Fourth.

Is there anything for kids?

Plenty. The parade itself is the main event, and the afternoon party at Junction Park has included gold panning for children, food trucks, and live music. The river at Junction Park is right there too, though it's unsupervised, so keep an eye on little ones.

See what else is happening around town on the Boulder Creek Local events page. Organizing something, or spot a detail here that needs fixing? Send it to us. For this event's official source, check the Boulder Creek Business Association.

boulder-creek-emergency-contacts.title: Boulder Creek Emergency Contacts: The One-Page List
boulder-creek-emergency-contacts.html:
When a winter storm puts a redwood across the road and the power's been out since dinner, the last thing you want is to be hunting for a phone number. For any emergency in Boulder Creek, call 911. That's the whole first rule, and it covers fire, medical, crime in progress, a downed power line, or any situation where someone could get hurt. Everything below is for the moments that aren't 911 calls: the tree across a county road, the water main bubbling up on your street, the outage that's stretched into hour six. Every number and link on this page is checked against the agency's own website, and we re-verify the list quarterly. Print it, stick it on the fridge, and you won't have to dig through a dozen sites during a storm.

When to Call 911

Call 911 for anything that threatens life or property right now: smoke or flames, a medical emergency, a crash with injuries, a crime happening in front of you, a gas smell, or a power line on the ground. Don't worry about whether it's "bad enough." Dispatchers would rather sort that out than have you wait. Everything else on this page is for situations that are a problem but not an emergency.

Fire: Boulder Creek Fire Protection District

The Boulder Creek Fire Protection District is our local fire agency, based at 13230 Central Ave in the middle of town.

The district office line is (831) 338-7222. Use it for district business: questions about the department, defensible space, permits, and community programs. It is not a dispatch line. If something is burning, call 911.

For wildfire information at the regional level, CAL FIRE covers our area through its San Mateo-Santa Cruz Unit (CZU), headquartered at 6059 Highway 9 in Felton. The unit office number is (831) 335-5353, again for administrative questions rather than reporting fires.

Law Enforcement: Santa Cruz County Sheriff

Boulder Creek is unincorporated, so law enforcement is the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office.

For a deputy when it's not an emergency (a cold burglary, ongoing noise, suspicious activity that's already over), call non-emergency dispatch at (831) 471-1121. The main Sheriff's Office line is (831) 454-7600 for records and general business. The full list of division numbers is on the Sheriff's phone directory.

There's also a Boulder Creek Sheriff's Service Center right in town at 13210 Central Avenue, reachable at (831) 454-7440. It's a satellite office, not a dispatch center, so hours vary. Call before you drive over. (The countywide phone directory doesn't carry a Boulder Creek line; it lists the San Lorenzo Valley service center separately at (831) 454-7400. Those are two different numbers, one digit apart, and 454-7440 is the Boulder Creek one.)

Roads: County Public Works and Caltrans

Road problems split two ways in Boulder Creek, and knowing which agency owns the road saves you a transfer.

County-maintained roads (most of the neighborhood roads off the highway) belong to Santa Cruz County Public Works, part of the county's Community Development and Infrastructure department. Their road dispatch line, (831) 477-3999, is answered 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and it's the number for a tree down across a county road, a slide, a flooded section, or a failing culvert. The department's main line is (831) 454-2160, weekdays 8 to noon and 1 to 5. Current closures are posted on the county's road closure dashboard. On the county's Transportation and Roads page, that dashboard is now labeled "Road Advisories," not "Road Closures," so scan for that wording.

Highway 9 and Highway 236 are state highways, so they're Caltrans territory. For current highway conditions, check the Caltrans road information page or call 1-800-427-7623 and enter the route number.

If a downed tree involves power lines, treat it as an emergency: call 911 and stay well away.

Power and Gas: PG&E

For outages, start at the PG&E outage center, where you can report an outage and check the outage map. PG&E's customer service line is 1-877-660-6789.

For a downed power line, PG&E's own guidance is blunt and worth repeating: call 911 first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000. Keep people and pets far away, and assume every line is live.

If you smell gas, get outside and away from the building first, then call 911. Once you are safe, call PG&E at 1-800-743-5000. Their outage and safety support page covers other reporting options.

PG&E also offers outage updates by text, email, or phone if you sign up through your PG&E online account. Worth doing before storm season, since those alerts are tied to your service address.

Water: San Lorenzo Valley Water District

Most Boulder Creek homes get water from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, headquartered at 13060 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek.

One number covers everything: (831) 338-2153. During office hours (weekdays 8:30 to noon and 1 to 5, phones until 4:30) you'll reach staff directly. For a water emergency after hours, a main break, or a big leak, call the same number; the district says calls forward to an answering service that reaches their on-call staff. Details are on their customer service page.

If you're on a different system (some streets are served by small mutual water companies), your bill has the right number. Add it to your copy of this list.

Alerts: Sign Up for CruzAware

CruzAware is the county's emergency and non-emergency alert system, operated by the County of Santa Cruz with Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1. It sends evacuation notices, severe weather warnings, and other public safety alerts by text, phone call, and email. Signing up takes a few minutes at cruzaware.org, and the county's alert information page explains the system. No alert system can promise delivery in every situation, so registering more than one contact method is smart. We have a full walkthrough in our CruzAware setup guide.

Back yourself up with channels that don't depend on CruzAware: Nixle is a separate service you can join by texting your ZIP code to 888777, and Wireless Emergency Alerts and the Emergency Alert System reach your phone and radio with no signup at all.

Keep This List Where You Can Find It

Phones die in outages and cell service up here gets thin in a storm. A paper copy in a kitchen drawer costs you nothing. We re-check every number on this page against the agencies' own sites each quarter; if you ever find one that's changed before we do, tell us and we'll fix it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who do I call about a tree down across my road?

If lines are involved or it's blocking an evacuation route during an active emergency, call 911. Otherwise: county road dispatch at (831) 477-3999 for county-maintained roads, any hour. If it's on Highway 9 or 236, it's Caltrans; check conditions or call 1-800-427-7623. On a private road, clearing it is generally the road association's job.

What's the non-emergency number for the Sheriff in Boulder Creek?

Call (831) 471-1121 for non-emergency dispatch when you need a deputy. The Boulder Creek Sheriff's Service Center on Central Avenue is (831) 454-7440 for local, non-urgent matters.

Who do I call when the power goes out?

Report it and track it through the PG&E outage center, or call 1-877-660-6789. If you see a downed line, that's a 911 call first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000.

Is there one number for water problems?

For San Lorenzo Valley Water District customers, yes: (831) 338-2153, day or night. After hours it forwards to an answering service that contacts on-call crews.

How current is this list?

Every contact is checked against the agency's own website, and we review the page quarterly. If an agency's information isn't listed here, go straight to that agency's site rather than guessing.

boulder-creek-golf-country-club.title: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club: What to Know Before You Play
boulder-creek-golf-country-club.html:
The redwoods stand close enough to the fairways here that a bad slice disappears into shade and pine duff, and you stop looking. That's the round at Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club, which is open to the public and playing as a 9-hole course at 16901 Big Basin Highway, with tee times you can book up to seven days out, an 18-hole disc golf course, and a restaurant and bar on site. The property lost its full 18-hole layout to the 2020 CZU fires and reopened after rebuilding the front nine, so if your last memory of the place is an 18-hole round, that part has changed. The Pro Shop answers at (831) 338-2111. Hours, rates, and course status change, so treat the links as the source of truth and call before a trip.

The course today: nine holes in the redwoods

The club has been here since 1961. It started as a nine-hole course, added a second nine in 1966 for a full 18, and was laid out by golf architect Jack Fleming. Then the 2020 CZU Lightning Complex fires burned through the property. What came back is a restored nine holes threaded among redwoods and small lakes. The club describes it as fresh air and quiet fairways, which is a fair description of a short mountain round rather than a championship marathon.

The club posts its scorecard on its about page as an image. Because the current 9-hole par and yardage aren't listed in plain text on the site, this guide doesn't print a number that might be stale. Check the posted scorecard, or ask the Pro Shop when you book, so you know exactly what you're walking.

What it costs and how to book

Green fees live on the club's rates page. As posted, nine holes runs $24 on a weekday and $28 on weekends and holidays, with a same-day replay at $14 and $16. Twilight after 4 p.m. drops to $19 and $22, and super twilight after 5:30 p.m. drops again to $17 and $19. Juniors play for $12, and the Youth on Course rate is $5 with ID. A power cart for nine holes is $16 per person, and a pull cart is $5. The rates page carries no dated stamp, so confirm the current price when you reserve.

Book online through the club's EZLinks tee sheet, where tee times open up to seven days in advance, or call the Pro Shop at (831) 338-2111. The Pro Shop is open 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily.

Disc golf on the back of the property

If you throw discs instead of swinging clubs, the club runs an 18-hole disc golf course called Redwood Run, with an option to play 27, open to the public at $12 per player from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily. Check in at the clubhouse before you play. A few house rules are worth knowing on the drive over: shirts and shoes are required, tank tops aren't allowed, and dogs aren't permitted on the course. Private homes border several holes, so if a disc sails onto someone's property, get the homeowner's okay before you go looking for it.

The restaurant and bar

There's a full bar and restaurant on site, and the hours shift by day, which makes them worth reading before you count on a meal. As the club posts them: the bar is open Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday from 1 to 9 p.m. with no kitchen those days; Tuesday is Taco Tuesday with the kitchen serving 11 a.m. to 8 p.m. and the bar open 11 to 9; Friday the bar runs 11 to 9 with the menu 11 to 7; Saturday the bar opens at 8 a.m. and the menu runs 11 to 7; and Sunday brings breakfast from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. plus the regular menu 11 to 3, with the bar open from 8 a.m. The club notes the bar reserves the right to close earlier or later, so a call to (831) 338-2111 saves a wasted drive on a slow day.

Where to stay if you're making a weekend of it

There's no lodging at the club itself. The club's own lodging page points visitors to the Brookdale Lodge, a historic redwood inn a few minutes down Highway 9 in Brookdale. If you'd rather be closer to downtown Boulder Creek, our guide to where to stay covers the local options.

Getting there

The club sits at 16901 Big Basin Highway, on the Big Basin Way side of town heading toward the state park. It's a short drive from downtown Boulder Creek, and the last stretch is classic mountain road: give yourself a few extra minutes and watch for cyclists and one-lane pinch points. If you're new to driving these roads, our mountain roads guide is worth a read.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club open to the public?

Yes. It's a public course, and anyone can book a tee time online through the club's EZLinks page up to seven days ahead or by calling the Pro Shop at (831) 338-2111. The disc golf course is public too.

How many holes is the golf course now?

Nine. The property was an 18-hole course before the 2020 CZU fires, and it reopened after rebuilding the front nine. The separate disc golf course is 18 holes with an option to play 27.

How much does it cost to play nine holes?

As posted on the club's rates page, $24 on a weekday and $28 on weekends and holidays, with cheaper twilight, junior, and replay rates. The page isn't dated, so confirm the current fee when you book.

Does the golf club have a restaurant?

Yes, there's a full bar and restaurant on site, but the kitchen isn't open every day. Dining runs Tuesday and Friday through Sunday, with the bar open more days than the kitchen. Call (831) 338-2111 to confirm before you rely on a meal.

boulder-creek-logging-history.title: The Logging Boom: When Boulder Creek Ran on Timber
boulder-creek-logging-history.html:
For about half a century, the town had one job: cut the redwood and get it down out of the mountains. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project is the best-documented account of this era, writes that Boulder Creek was one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915. That's not small-town boosterism about a small town. It's the reason the town is here at all, and the near-loss of the forest around it is the reason Big Basin exists. What follows is the story of the mills, the flume, and the trains, and what's left of them today.

The forest before the saws

Start with what the loggers found. The Santa Cruz Mountains held roughly 300 square miles of ancient redwood forest, and the Sempervirens Fund doesn't soften it: in the late 1800s, most of it was "logged down to bald hills." Trees a thousand years old and older came down in a few decades.

That forest wasn't wilderness in the empty sense. People have lived in this region for more than 10,000 years, according to the Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History, and the Awaswas-speaking peoples here tended the landscape rather than simply occupying it. Their descendants in the Amah Mutsun Tribal Band steward ancestral lands in these mountains today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust. The logging era wasn't the beginning of human history in this valley. It was a violent interruption of a much longer one.

First mills on the upper river

Commercial logging reached the upper San Lorenzo Valley in the early 1860s, when Joseph Wilbur Peery put up a small sawmill across from Bear Creek. A settlement called Boulder grew up around the milling work, got a post office named Boulder Creek in 1872, and kept the name. The early operations stayed small and local because they had to. A mill in the 1860s could cut lumber all day, but hauling it down a rough mountain road to Santa Cruz was slow, costly work, and that one bottleneck kept the upper valley quiet through its first decade.

The flume years, 1875 to 1885

The first real attempt to break the bottleneck was a wooden trough. A flume company incorporated in August 1874 (Whaley's pages render the name as both the San Lorenzo Valley Flume & Lumber Company and the Flume & Transportation Company) began construction in early 1875, and by that October a V-shaped flume was carrying rough-cut lumber roughly twelve miles from the upper San Lorenzo down to Felton, where it met the railroad to Santa Cruz. The company built a large mill near the confluence of Kings Creek and the San Lorenzo River, upstream of town, and Whaley's company history records that the flume turned the little settlement of Boulder into a much more substantial Boulder Creek.

It also records the flume's problems, and they showed up almost at once. The 40-inch trough couldn't keep up with the valley's lumber output, it leaked, and its water supply ran dangerously low every summer. A severe winter storm in early 1876 damaged it badly. In 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad took over the flume company, and in June 1883 it incorporated a subsidiary, the Felton & Pescadero Railroad, to replace the trough with rails. When the narrow-gauge line reached Boulder Creek in April 1885, crews tore out the flume along the route. The trough era lasted ten years.

The boom, 1885 to 1915

The railroad changed the scale of everything. With the first train in April 1885, Boulder Creek became the end of the line and the shipping point for every mill working the upper valley, and the town's year-round population climbed past 800. Lumber went out by the trainload for thirty years, the stretch when Whaley ranks Boulder Creek among the largest lumber exporters in the country.

The clearest picture of how industrial it got is the railroad the loggers built for themselves. After the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company's mill burned in 1886, brothers James and William P. Dougherty didn't wait for Southern Pacific to extend the branch. In the late winter of 1887 they started building their own narrow-gauge line, the Dougherty Extension Railroad, running north from the Boulder Creek freight yard along the San Lorenzo River. By 1899 it reached roughly eight and a half miles upriver, into the shadow of Castle Rock Ridge, longer than the Southern Pacific branch it fed into. Spurs served a string of operations: Ephraim Bradbury Morrell's mill at Two Bar Creek, Cunningham & Company, and the S. H. Chase Lumber Company among them.

Demand rarely let up. The San Lorenzo Valley Chamber of Commerce notes that the whole valley ran as a logging center in these years, and that San Francisco's rebuilding after the 1906 earthquake pushed demand for local lumber even higher. The quake that leveled the city barely touched Boulder Creek, and the town spent the next several years shipping the redwood that rebuilt it.

Running out of trees

The boom ended the way every cut-out-and-get-out boom ends. By the early 1900s the big timber within reach of the rails was going fast, and mills began closing as the accessible stands ran out. The Dougherty line's regular service wound down around 1915, and on April 4, 1917, its rails were sold for scrap, feeding the steel demand of the First World War. Downriver, the Southern Pacific branch hung on with resort passengers and lighter freight, but the direction was set. Passenger trains gave way to buses in 1931, and in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek.

Whaley's one-line summary of what followed is the fairest one: the town "transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town." The mills and the freight yard gave way to summer cabins and resort traffic, and the town settled into the quieter shape it still has. Our short history of Boulder Creek follows that longer arc.

The trees that were saved

The logging story has a second ending, nine miles up the road. By 1900, most of Big Basin's old growth was privately owned, and Whaley's Big Basin history notes that businessman Henry L. Middleton had bought up the majority of the basin intending to harvest it. That May, a group led by San Jose photographer Andrew P. Hill camped in the basin, formed the Sempervirens Club, and passed a hat that collected 32 dollars to start the campaign. Two years of lobbying produced a state bill in 1901, and in 1902 California bought 3,800 acres of ancient forest, which the Sempervirens Fund calls the first park established in the current state park system.

Big Basin Redwoods State Park now preserves more than 18,000 acres, including the largest continuous stand of ancient coast redwoods south of San Francisco. The 2020 CZU fire burned through 97 percent of the park, but most of the old growth survived, which says something about what those trees have already lived through. The full preservation story is in our article on how Big Basin became California's first state park.

What you can still see today

The logging era hides in plain sight around town. The San Lorenzo Valley Museum at 12547 Highway 9 keeps the photographs, tools, and records of the mill years, and it's the right first stop if this article leaves you wanting more. Along the river between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek, concrete piers and sawed-off pilings from the railroad still stand in and beside the water, though most of the old right-of-way now runs through private property, so look from public road crossings rather than wandering the grade. And the second-growth redwoods on every ridge around town are the boom's most visible legacy. Nearly all of them sprouted from the stumps the loggers left, which means the forest you drive through on Highway 9 is itself an artifact of the timber years, still quietly repairing the damage.

Frequently Asked Questions

When was Boulder Creek's logging era?

Commercial milling started in the early 1860s with Joseph Wilbur Peery's small sawmill, but the industrial boom ran from the railroad's arrival in April 1885 until about 1915, when the accessible big timber was largely gone. The last freight train left town in January 1934.

How did lumber get out of the valley before the railroad?

From 1875 to 1885, a twelve-mile V-shaped flume floated rough-cut lumber from the upper San Lorenzo Valley down to Felton. It leaked, ran short of water in summer, and couldn't handle the valley's output, so the South Pacific Coast Railroad replaced it with the rail line that reached Boulder Creek in 1885.

Was all the old-growth forest around Boulder Creek cut down?

Most of it. The Sempervirens Fund estimates roughly 300 square miles of ancient redwood in the Santa Cruz Mountains were logged in the late 1800s. The major surviving old growth near Boulder Creek is in Big Basin, which the state began protecting with a 3,800-acre purchase in 1902.

What was the Dougherty Extension Railroad?

A private narrow-gauge logging railroad the Dougherty brothers built north from Boulder Creek starting in 1887 for the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company. It grew to roughly eight and a half miles and served several mills upriver. Service ended around 1915 and the rails were sold for scrap in 1917.

Where can I see remnants of the logging era?

The San Lorenzo Valley Museum in Boulder Creek covers the period in depth, and concrete piers and cut-off pilings from the rail line are still visible along the San Lorenzo River, mostly viewable from public crossings since much of the old route is private property. Big Basin's old growth shows what the whole range once looked like.

boulder-creek-notable-disasters-history.title: Fires, Floods, and Storms: The Events That Shaped Boulder Creek
boulder-creek-notable-disasters-history.html:
Around here, people talk about go-bags, generator plans, and river levels the way other towns talk about traffic. There's a reason, and it's on the record. The San Lorenzo Valley has come through a documented run of hard events: damaging floods in 1940, 1955, and 1958, the deadly storm and landslides of January 1982, the CZU Lightning Complex fire in 2020, and the atmospheric river storms of January 2023. Here's what happened in each, drawn from flood reports, USGS studies, and CAL FIRE records, along with the official alert systems that exist because of this history.

Why this valley sees big water and big fire

Boulder Creek sits near the top of the San Lorenzo River watershed, in a steep, forested canyon that catches enormous amounts of rain. The numbers say so. During the December 1955 storm, the Boulder Creek Locatelli Ranch station recorded 9.11 inches of rain in a single 24-hour period, and 20.11 inches over about two weeks, according to a US Army Corps of Engineers flood report digitized by Santa Cruz Public Libraries. In January 1982, the US Geological Survey documented a storm that dropped as much as half the region's average annual precipitation in about 32 hours.

Steep slopes. Saturated soil. A fast river, and a forest that evolved with fire. That combination has shaped the valley's biggest days, and it's why the events below still come up in conversation here.

The Christmas flood of December 1955

The flood old-timers still measure everything against arrived on the evening of December 22, 1955. A Santa Cruz Public Libraries factsheet calls it the highest flooding in Santa Cruz history. On the upper river near Boulder Creek, the water ran roughly 3 to 3.5 feet higher than the previous flood of record from 1940, according to the Corps report.

The damage ran the length of the valley. Heavy rain scoured out large trees, which floated downstream and jammed at narrow points in the channel, backing up water and shoving the river out of its normal alignment. Bridges, road fills, and small channel dams were undercut and washed out. A Santa Cruz Sentinel-News account from December 25, 1955, quoted in the Corps report, put it plainly: nearly 100 homes along the river were destroyed, and Highway 9 was blocked by slides and downed trees. The road to Big Basin closed too.

Seven people lost their lives in the San Lorenzo River Basin during the flood, five in the city of Santa Cruz and two near Mount Hermon, and an estimated 2,830 people were displaced. Boulder Creek itself came through lighter than downstream: mostly scoured channel banks, damaged cabin camps, and undercut bridge approaches. The lesson the Corps drew is worth keeping. Many people had heard the warnings on local radio and didn't believe the water could rise that far.

A smaller flood followed in April 1958. Minor by comparison, but it still forced about 250 people in the northern part of the county from their homes and caused an estimated $170,000 in damage.

January 1982: the storm and the Love Creek slide

The storm of January 3 through 5, 1982, was a different kind of event. Rain fell so hard and so fast that hillsides across the region gave way. The USGS documented more than 18,000 slides that turned into debris flows across the San Francisco Bay region, and landslides accounted for 25 of the storm's 33 deaths.

The worst of it happened just down the valley from Boulder Creek. Above the community of Love Creek, near Ben Lomond, more than 459,000 cubic meters of earth and rock slid off a mountainside and buried ten people in their homes. Countywide, the Santa Cruz Public Libraries factsheet records 22 dead, 50 injured, 135 homes destroyed, 300 damaged, and more than $106 million in combined public and private losses. Unlike 1955, when most of the damage hit the city of Santa Cruz, about 90 percent of the 1982 damage fell outside city limits, in the mountains and valley.

That storm is why debris flow risk gets treated as seriously as river flooding here, especially on slopes that have burned.

August 2020: the CZU Lightning Complex fire

The CZU Lightning Complex began with lightning strikes on August 16, 2020, and became the event that most directly shaped the Boulder Creek of today. According to CAL FIRE's incident record, the fire burned 86,509 acres across Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties and was declared contained on September 22, 2020, after 37 days. It destroyed 1,490 structures, damaged 140 more, and took one civilian life.

Within Santa Cruz County, the losses centered on the San Lorenzo Valley and the ridges around it. Lookout Santa Cruz has reported the county's tally of 911 homes destroyed, a number this community knows without looking it up. Boulder Creek's own fire department served in the unified command that fought the fire, alongside CAL FIRE and neighboring districts.

The fire also burned 97 percent of Big Basin Redwoods State Park, the park this town has been the gateway to since 1902. Most of the old-growth redwoods survived, and the park has been reopening in stages since July 2022.

Years later, the rebuilding is still under way, and many neighbors are still working through permits, insurance, and construction. That's part of daily life here, not a closed chapter, and it's the main reason this site treats preparedness as a permanent topic rather than a seasonal one.

January 2023: atmospheric rivers on a burn scar

The winter of 2023 tested the valley again. A series of atmospheric river storms delivered unusually large amounts of rain in January 2023, enough that the USGS deployed temporary rain gauges in the county, including one in Boulder Creek along Hilton Drive, specifically to support emergency response and track how the landscape was holding up after the fire. Roads and trails in and around Big Basin that had been reopening after the fire took new damage, and Sempervirens Fund notes that park access has continued to evolve because of both the fire and those storms.

The 2023 storms made the modern pattern clear. In this valley, fire and flood aren't separate stories. A burned slope sheds water differently for years, so a wet winter after a fire year deserves extra respect.

What this history means for living here now

The practical takeaway isn't fear. It's the habits this town already keeps: know your evacuation routes, keep the truck fueled in storm season, and sign up for official alerts before you need them.

Santa Cruz County's alert system is CruzAware, and you can register through the county's alert notification page to get warnings by text, phone call, email, or the CruzAware app. For weather itself, the National Weather Service San Francisco Bay Area/Monterey office issues the forecasts, watches, and warnings that cover Santa Cruz County. In any immediate emergency, call 911. This site shares history and pointers, but it never interprets live emergency conditions, and neither should any website. Official alerts and dispatchers do that job.

If the history itself pulls you in, the San Lorenzo Valley Museum keeps a gallery right in Boulder Creek at 12547 Highway 9, and it's the natural next stop for photographs and records of the valley's floods, fires, and the people who rebuilt after each one.

Frequently Asked Questions

What was the worst flood in Boulder Creek history?

The December 1955 flood is the flood of record for the San Lorenzo River. Per the Army Corps of Engineers report, it exceeded the previous record flood of 1940 by roughly 3 to 3.5 feet near Boulder Creek, killed seven people in the river basin, and displaced an estimated 2,830 residents.

How big was the CZU fire, and how did it affect Boulder Creek?

Per CAL FIRE, the CZU Lightning Complex started August 16, 2020, burned 86,509 acres in Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties, destroyed 1,490 structures, and caused one civilian death. The losses in Santa Cruz County were concentrated in and around the San Lorenzo Valley, including hundreds of homes in the Boulder Creek area.

How do I sign up for local emergency alerts?

Register for CruzAware through the county's alert notification page. You can receive alerts by text, call, email, or the mobile app. Pair that with forecasts from the National Weather Service Monterey office, and call 911 in any immediate emergency.

Why does a fire make future floods worse?

Burned slopes absorb less water and shed soil and debris more easily, which raises the risk of flooding and debris flows for years afterward. That's why the USGS put rain gauges in Boulder Creek during the January 2023 storms, and why post-fire winters get extra attention here.

Did Big Basin survive the fire?

The fire burned 97 percent of the park and nearly all of its buildings, but most of the old-growth redwoods survived and the park has been reopening in stages since July 2022. Check the official state parks page before visiting, since access is still limited and changes.

boulder-creek-power-outage-plan.title: Power Outage Plan for Mountain Households
boulder-creek-power-outage-plan.html:
The first big storm of the season usually announces itself the same way up here: the wind funnels up the canyon, a fir comes down across a line somewhere along Highway 9, and the lights blink once and quit. Anyone who's spent a winter in Boulder Creek knows outages come with the territory, and a little planning turns a three-day one from a crisis into an inconvenience.

A solid outage plan for a Boulder Creek household comes down to five things you set up before the wind picks up: alerts from PG&E and the county, a safe way to run backup power, stored water if you're on a well, a plan for food and medicine, and a habit of checking on the neighbors. Most of it takes one weekend afternoon. If you only do one thing today, sign up for outage alerts and save PG&E's 24-hour outage line, 1-800-743-5002, in your phone. When the lights go out, PG&E's outage map and alerts are the source of truth for what's happening and when power might come back, and even their estimates can change as crews find the actual damage.

If You See a Downed Line

Assume every downed line is live.

  1. Stay back and keep everyone away from it, including pets. Don't touch it, and don't touch anything it's touching, like a fence, a puddle, or a branch.
  2. Don't drive over it.
  3. Call 911. Then call PG&E at 1-800-743-5000, the 24-hour emergency line.

That is a different number from the outage-reporting line further down this page. For a line on the ground, use 911 and 1-800-743-5000.

Know the Three Kinds of Outages

Most of our outages are storm outages: wind, falling trees and limbs, or equipment trouble. They arrive with little warning, and restoration in steep, wooded terrain can take longer than it does in town because crews have to find the damage before they can fix it.

The second kind is a Public Safety Power Shutoff, or PSPS. That's a planned shutoff PG&E calls during dry, windy, high fire-risk weather so its lines can't spark a wildfire. PG&E says it does its best to alert customers ahead of time by email, phone call, and text, but the company also notes that in some cases the first alert may not come until the same day the power goes off. The California Public Utilities Commission oversees these events and describes them as a measure of last resort. Either way, the practical lesson is the same: don't count on long notice. Have your plan ready before fire season, not during it.

The third kind is the one most people here have felt without knowing its name: Enhanced Powerline Safety Settings, or EPSS. In high fire-risk areas, PG&E sets its lines to trip instantly on any contact. A branch, an animal, anything. The power then stays off until crews patrol the entire line, which in this terrain is not quick. PG&E turns the settings on based on daily wildfire risk, most likely May through November. That's why fire-season outages up here are sudden, frequent, and long, and why they can happen on a clear day with no PSPS announced.

Set Up Alerts Before You Need Them

Start with PG&E. If the account is in your name, you automatically get outage notifications for your service address, and you can fine-tune how you're contacted through your online account. If you rent, or you want updates on a parent's place or a second property, use PG&E's address-level alerts: search any address on the outage map, select "Get outage updates," and enter your phone or email. You don't need an online account for that, and PSPS address alerts are available by call and text too.

Then add the county layer. CruzAware is Santa Cruz County's alert and warning system, and it's how you'll hear about evacuations, road closures, and other emergencies that often travel with big outages. Sign up at cruzaware.org and pick how you want to be reached. The county also lists a quick option to text your ZIP code to 888777 for basic text alerts. That 888777 number is Nixle, a separate service the county also uses. Signing up for one does not sign you up for the other, so if you want both, do both.

Finally, report what you see. If your power's been out a while and nothing shows on the map, report it through the PG&E Outage Center or call 1-800-743-5002. Outages in the far reaches of the district sometimes need a phone call before anyone knows a tap line is dead.

Generator Safety Comes First

A generator is the difference between a rough week and a manageable one, but it's also the most dangerous thing in this plan, so be direct with yourself about it.

  1. Never run a generator inside your home or garage, even with the doors and windows open.
  2. Run it outside only, more than 20 feet from any windows, doors, and vents.
  3. Put a battery-powered or battery-backup carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area, and check it when you check your smoke alarms.

Those rules come from the CDC. Carbon monoxide is odorless and colorless, it kills more than 400 Americans a year, and people who are asleep can die before they ever feel sick. Ready.gov gives the same 20-foot rule and adds a few worth keeping: keep the generator dry and out of the rain, let it cool before refueling, and use heavy-duty extension cords rated for the load.

The generator isn't the only source of carbon monoxide in a cold house. Never heat your home with a gas stove or oven. Never bring a charcoal grill or a camp stove indoors or into a garage, not even to cook. Anything that burns fuel belongs outside, away from windows, doors, and vents.

The second rule is about your wiring. Never plug a generator into a wall outlet or connect it directly to your panel without proper equipment. PG&E's backup power safety page is blunt about this: power that backfeeds from your generator into the lines can injure the crews working to restore your neighborhood. If you want the generator to run household circuits rather than extension cords, have an electrician install a transfer switch or equivalent equipment that isolates your home from the grid. And store fuel outside the house, never indoors.

If you're considering a battery system instead, the same isolation rules apply, and an electrician or installer should handle the interconnection. Batteries avoid the carbon monoxide risk, which is a real advantage in a house full of people waiting out a storm.

PG&E Programs That Help Pay for Backup Power

Two PG&E programs are worth checking before you buy anything.

The Portable Battery Program offers no-cost pre-qualification to Medical Baseline or Self-Identified Vulnerable customers who rely on a medical device, assistive technology, or durable medical equipment, and who have had at least one PSPS since 2024 or three or more EPSS outages since 2024. If you pre-qualify, PG&E's program partners reach out to assess your household and your equipment.

The Generator and Battery Rebate Program pays a $300 base rebate, with up to $200 more for customers on CARE or FERA. It covers addresses in high fire-threat districts or on an EPSS circuit. Eligible equipment is a portable battery between 290Wh and 1,000Wh, or a CARB-compliant portable generator. One rebate per household. You have to apply within 12 months of purchase or by December 31, 2026, whichever comes first. If you bought equipment a while ago, that end-of-2026 date is the one to watch.

Water When the Well Pump Is Down

If you're on a private well, no electricity means no pump, which means no water. Plan for stored water as a matter of course: a common rule of thumb is one gallon per person per day, and more is better when outages can run multiple days. Cases of bottled water work, and so do food-grade containers you fill and rotate a couple of times a year. Keep separate buckets or jugs for flushing toilets, since that's where household water use quietly adds up. A pressure tank buys you a little water after the power drops, but don't count on much.

Running the well off a generator is not a plug-in job. A submersible well pump is typically 240 volts and hardwired, which means a properly installed transfer switch and a generator sized by an electrician, not an extension cord run out from the porch. Sort that out before storm season if you want it, because nobody is coming to wire it during one.

Septic Systems

If your septic system has a pump or an alarm panel, it stops working when the power does. Treat wastewater as limited for the duration: minimize water use, skip the laundry, and go easy on flushing. Gravity-fed systems generally keep draining without power, so the useful thing is to know which kind you have before storm season rather than during one. If you're not sure, a septic contractor can tell you.

If you're on San Lorenzo Valley Water District water, your situation is different from a well household, but storing some water is still smart. Treatment and pumping infrastructure need power too, and storms that cause outages can also cause line breaks. If you have questions about how the district handles outages in your neighborhood, call them at 831-338-2153 and ask. No system can promise uninterrupted service in a storm, so a few stored gallons is cheap insurance either way.

Food, Refrigeration, and Medicine

The rules here are simpler than most people think, and they come straight from foodsafety.gov. An unopened refrigerator keeps food safe for about 4 hours. A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about 48 hours, and a half-full one for about 24. So the strategy is: don't open the doors unless you have to, and eat from the pantry first. Perishables like meat, poultry, fish, eggs, and leftovers that spend more than 2 hours above 40 degrees should be tossed. Frozen food that still has ice crystals, or that reads 40 degrees or below, can be safely refrozen. Never taste something to decide whether it's safe. When in doubt, throw it out.

An appliance thermometer in the fridge and one in the freezer take the guesswork out of all of this, and a cooler with ice extends your window if the outage stretches on.

Refrigerated medication has its own clock. Ready.gov advises discarding refrigerated medicine after a day without power unless the label says otherwise, and calling your doctor or pharmacist right away for a replacement. If your household depends on any medication that's temperature-sensitive, ask your pharmacist ahead of time exactly how long it lasts warm, and write the answer on the box.

One more habit that pays off: when an outage starts, unplug electronics and appliances. Power sometimes comes back with surges that can damage what's plugged in. Leave one lamp on so you know when service returns.

If Someone Depends on Powered Medical Equipment

If anyone in your home relies on electricity for medical needs, get them enrolled in PG&E's Medical Baseline Program now. Eligibility is based on medical need, not income. Enrollees get extra electricity at the lowest rate tier, and, more importantly for us, extra advance notifications before a PSPS. PG&E sends Medical Baseline customers additional alerts at 48 hours, at 24 hours, and again just before the power goes off.

A medical practitioner certifies the application, and you can start it online. Two details trip people up. The application has to be submitted within 90 days of the practitioner's signature, so don't let a signed form sit in a drawer. And enrollment has to be renewed: permanent conditions self-recertify every four years, while non-permanent conditions recertify every two years with a practitioner. PG&E's Medical Baseline line is 1-877-660-6789 if you have questions about either.

Enrollment is not a backup power plan by itself, though. Talk with the medical provider about how long each device runs on battery, what the fallback is, and at what point you'd relocate to somewhere with power. Write that threshold down before you need it.

Check on the Neighbors

This is the part of the plan Boulder Creek already does well. Know which of your neighbors are older, live alone, are on wells, or depend on medical equipment, and trade phone numbers before storm season. A text thread for your road costs nothing and works as long as someone has signal. When an outage hits, a quick knock on the right door can matter more than everything else on this page.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find out when my power will be back on?

Check the PG&E Outage Center map or call the 24-hour outage line at 1-800-743-5002. PG&E posts estimated restoration times once crews assess the damage, and those estimates can change. Nobody else, including this site, can promise a restoration time, so treat PG&E's latest update as the answer.

Will PG&E warn me before a Public Safety Power Shutoff?

PG&E says it tries to notify customers ahead of a PSPS by email, phone, and text, but it also says the first alert may sometimes come the same day power is turned off. Keep your contact info current on your PG&E account, sign up for address alerts if you're not the account holder, and have your supplies ready before fire weather arrives.

How far from the house does a generator need to be?

More than 20 feet from any windows, doors, and vents, per the CDC, and never inside a home or garage, even with everything open. Pair it with a battery-powered carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area. If you want it wired to the house, have an electrician install a transfer switch so it can't backfeed into the lines.

How long is food safe when the power's out?

About 4 hours in an unopened refrigerator, about 48 hours in a full freezer, and about 24 in a half-full one, according to foodsafety.gov. Toss perishables that spend more than 2 hours above 40 degrees, and never taste food to test it.

What if someone in my house uses an oxygen machine or other powered medical device?

Enroll them in PG&E's Medical Baseline Program for extra notifications before planned shutoffs, and work out a written backup plan with their medical provider: battery runtimes, a charging plan, and the point at which you'd move them somewhere with power.

boulder-creek-public-transportation.title: Getting Around Without a Car: Transit Options From Boulder Creek
boulder-creek-public-transportation.html:
Yes, you can get from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz on a bus, seven days a week. Santa Cruz METRO's Route 35 runs the length of Highway 9 through Felton and Scotts Valley to downtown Santa Cruz. A weekday express called Route 35X makes the same trip faster during commute hours, and a local ride costs $2 in cash, with K-12 students riding free.
