For neighbors who can't use a regular bus, METRO ParaCruz and Lift Line both provide door-to-door rides. Bus schedules change with each service update, so treat the linked timetables as the source of truth and this page as the map.

Route 35 is the backbone

Route 35, the Highway 9/Scotts Valley line, is how Boulder Creek connects to the rest of the county without a car. It runs between the RiverFront transit stops on Front Street in downtown Santa Cruz and the Country Club end of Big Basin Way, passing through Felton and Scotts Valley on the way.

In and around town, the schedule's timepoints include Highway 9 at Middleton Avenue in the middle of downtown, Pool Drive at Highway 9 by the Mountain Store, and Big Basin Way out toward the Country Club neighborhood. Heading down the hill, buses stop at San Lorenzo Valley High School and Highlands Park before continuing through Felton.

Service runs seven days a week, from early morning until late evening, with buses roughly every 30 to 60 minutes through the middle of the day. Don't build a tight connection around those generalities, though. Pull up the live Route 35 timetable for the exact times at your stop, because first and last trips differ by direction and day of week.

If you're new in town and still setting up the rest of your life here, our new resident quick-start guide covers the non-bus logistics.

Route 35X, the commuter's shortcut

On weekdays only, METRO runs Route 35X, the Santa Cruz/Boulder Creek Express. It serves the same Boulder Creek timepoints, including Highway 9 and Middleton and Pool Drive at the Mountain Store, but takes a quicker routing on the trip to and from Santa Cruz. The current schedule shows roughly a dozen trips in each direction spread across the workday, starting around 6:30 in the morning.

If you commute to Santa Cruz on a regular clock, the 35X is the one to learn. Check the Route 35X schedule for the exact departures, since express trips are the first thing to shift when METRO adjusts service.

What it costs

Fares are the easy part, and they're posted on METRO's Fares & Passes page:

A single local ride is $2.00 cash for adults, or $1.00 for riders 65 and older and riders with disabilities. A day pass runs $6, and a 31-day pass is $65, with discount versions at $3 and $32.

The best deal in the system goes to students. K-12 riders travel free through the Youth Cruz Free program, which makes Route 35 a real option for SLV High and middle school students heading up and down the valley. College students with a valid student ID also ride free, and so do riders who are legally blind.

Getting to San Jose and over the hill

There's no direct bus from Boulder Creek over Highway 17, but the connection is straightforward. Ride Route 35 to its downtown Santa Cruz end at the RiverFront stops on Front Street. The Highway 17 Express boards a short walk away in the same RiverFront area, at Front Street near Trader Joe's, and runs weekdays and weekends to Diridon Station in San Jose, where Caltrain, Amtrak, and light rail all connect.

The Highway 17 Express uses its own fare: $7.00 for a single ride, $3.50 discount, or a $14 day pass. If you're commuting to the South Bay a few days a week, do the math on passes before you settle into paying cash each way.

Weekend bus to Big Basin

On weekends, Route 35B runs from the Cavallaro Transit Center in Scotts Valley up Highway 9 and out to Big Basin Redwoods State Park, with five trips in each direction. Check the route page for its full stop list before planning around a specific boarding point. If you're headed that way, our Big Basin day trip guide covers what to expect once you're there, including the park's parking-reservation advice, which makes the bus a smarter play than it might sound.

Door-to-door rides: ParaCruz and Lift Line

Two services cover trips a fixed-route bus can't.

METRO ParaCruz is METRO's ADA paratransit: accessible, shared door-to-door rides for people who can't use regular buses because of a disability. It serves destinations within three-quarters of a mile of an operating bus route, which in our corner of the county means the Highway 9 corridor. You apply through ADAride, online at adaride.com or by phone at 877-232-7433, and a decision comes within 21 days. Once you're eligible, rides are booked one to three days ahead at 831-425-4664, and the base fare is $4 per trip.

Lift Line is run by Community Bridges, a local nonprofit, and provides door-to-door rides for seniors 60 and older and people with disabilities across Santa Cruz County, with a focus on medical trips, meal-site rides, and essential errands. Some of its programs carry income qualifications, and rides need to be scheduled in advance. Start with the application on its site or call 831-688-9663 on a weekday to sort out which program fits.

If a ride is about a medical appointment, ask both services what applies to you. The eligibility rules differ, and plenty of valley residents qualify for one and not the other.

School buses for SLVUSD students

San Lorenzo Valley Unified runs its own home-to-school buses, separate from METRO, and posts route sheets by bus name on its Transportation Department bus information page. Ridership is fee-based: as posted for the current cycle, an annual pass is $220 round trip or $116 one way, a semester pass is $116 or $58, and ticket books run $20 for 20 tickets. Students with qualifying IEPs are exempt from fees, and there's a hardship application for families receiving county assistance. The transportation office in Ben Lomond answers questions at 831-336-2223. Confirm prices and routes with the district before the school year starts, since both get updated over the summer.

Remember the free alternative for older kids: K-12 students ride METRO at no cost, and Route 35 stops at SLV High.

A few caveats to plan around

The bus works well here, with two limits worth knowing before you give up a car entirely. Frequency is the first: miss a midday bus and the next one can be 30 to 60 minutes out. Storm season is the second: when Highway 9 closes for trees or slides, buses reroute or hold just like everyone else, so sign up for alerts through CruzAware and check METRO's rider alerts on the route pages before heading out in bad weather.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a bus from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz?

Yes. METRO Route 35 runs seven days a week along Highway 9 from Boulder Creek through Felton and Scotts Valley to downtown Santa Cruz, and the weekday Route 35X express makes the same trip with a faster routing during commute hours.

How much does the bus cost from Boulder Creek?

A local single ride is $2.00 cash, with a $1.00 fare for riders 65 and older and riders with disabilities. Day passes are $6 and 31-day passes are $65. K-12 students, college students with ID, and riders who are legally blind ride free.

Can I get from Boulder Creek to San Jose by bus?

Yes, with one transfer. Take Route 35 to the RiverFront stops in downtown Santa Cruz, then board the Highway 17 Express to Diridon Station in San Jose. The express runs weekdays and weekends and costs $7.00 for a single ride.

What if I can't ride a regular bus?

Two door-to-door options serve Boulder Creek. METRO ParaCruz covers riders whose disability prevents using fixed-route buses, with an application through ADAride and $4 base fares. Lift Line, run by Community Bridges, serves seniors 60 and older and people with disabilities, with some income-qualified programs.

Do school kids ride METRO free?

Yes. The Youth Cruz Free program lets K-12 students ride any METRO route at no cost, no pass purchase required. SLVUSD's own yellow-bus service is separate and fee-based, with passes sold through the district's transportation office.

boulder-creek-roads-parking-notes.title: Mountain Roads, Parking, and When Highway 9 Gets Complicated
boulder-creek-roads-parking-notes.html:
If you're driving to Boulder Creek, here's the short version: Highway 9 is a genuine mountain road, narrow and winding under the redwoods, and it asks for more patience than speed. Check conditions before you leave on Caltrans QuickMap or our Mountain Status page, expect cyclists on every curve, plan for small-town parking rather than big-lot parking, and don't count on cell signal the whole way up. None of that should scare you off. It's a beautiful drive. It just goes better for everyone, visitors and locals alike, when you know what kind of road you're on before you're on it.

This page covers the durable patterns, the things that are true most days. For what's happening right now, always go to the live sources linked throughout, because we can't promise you an open road or an empty parking spot, and neither can anyone else.

Know Whose Road You're On

Two highways define driving here, and both are state routes maintained by Caltrans. Highway 9 runs the length of the San Lorenzo Valley, connecting Santa Cruz to Felton, Ben Lomond, Brookdale, and Boulder Creek before climbing over the ridge toward Saratoga. Highway 236 splits off from downtown Boulder Creek and loops through Big Basin Redwoods State Park. For either route, you can check current conditions by entering the route number at Caltrans' road information page, calling 1-800-427-7623, or opening QuickMap, which shows closures and incidents in real time.

Nearly everything else, the smaller roads that branch off the highway, is maintained by Santa Cruz County, and those closures show up on the county's Road Closure Dashboard instead. If you come across a tree down or a flooded stretch on a county road, the county's 24-hour road dispatch is (831) 477-3999. Knowing which agency owns the road you're on saves you a phone transfer, and our emergency contacts page keeps all of these numbers in one place. For anything dangerous happening right now, call 911.

What Driving Highway 9 Is Actually Like

Highway 9 between Santa Cruz and Boulder Creek is about thirty minutes of continuous curves through redwood shade. The trees make it darker than you expect at midday and slick with needles and moisture long after rain has stopped. Sight lines are short, shoulders are thin or missing, and passing opportunities are rare, so the pace of the whole road is set by its slowest vehicle. Build that into your schedule and the drive is a pleasure instead of a frustration.

If that slowest vehicle is you, that's fine, but use the turnouts. California law, Vehicle Code 21656, requires a slow-moving vehicle on a two-lane road to pull into the next turnout or safe area whenever five or more vehicles stack up behind it. Locals use the turnouts reflexively and notice, kindly or otherwise, who doesn't. Pulling over for ten seconds is the single most neighborly thing a visitor can do on this road.

A few other habits that fit the road: don't stop in the travel lane for photos, even briefly, since the next driver comes around the curve blind. Use your headlights in the redwood shade. And leave the shortcuts to the map apps alone in bad weather, because the side roads they suggest are narrower, steeper, and slower than the highway they're avoiding.

Sharing the Curves with Cyclists

Highway 9 is one of the classic road cycling climbs in the Bay Area, and you will meet riders on it, especially on weekend mornings. The law here is specific. California's Three Feet for Safety Act, Vehicle Code 21760, requires drivers to give at least three feet of clearance when passing a bicycle, and when three feet isn't possible, to slow down and pass only when it won't endanger the rider.

On a road with this many blind curves, that mostly means waiting. Hang back at a comfortable distance, wait for a straight stretch or a turnout, then pass with room to spare. The wait is rarely more than a minute, and it beats meeting an oncoming car mid-pass with a cyclist on your right. Riders, for their part, tend to use the turnouts too when a line forms; the road works because most people extend each other that courtesy.

Parking in Boulder Creek

Boulder Creek's downtown is a few blocks of Highway 9, and parking is small-town scale: street parking along the highway and side streets, plus modest lots serving individual businesses. On an ordinary weekday you'll likely find a spot within a short walk. On sunny summer weekends, holiday events, and festival days, the easy spots go early, so arrive on the early side or be ready to park a few blocks out and walk.

A few requests from the people who live here. Watch for posted signs and don't block driveways, fire hydrants, or the fire station's approach on Central Avenue. Don't park in business lots you're not patronizing. Leave room at trailheads and turnouts for residents whose driveways share them, and never park where your car narrows the road itself, because fire engines and delivery trucks need every foot of width these roads have. If a spot feels questionable, it probably is.

Highway 236 and the Big Basin Run

The drive from downtown Boulder Creek to Big Basin Redwoods State Park is about 9 miles up Highway 236, and the park's own guidance says it plainly: all roads into Big Basin are curvy. Sections of 236 are effectively single-lane threading between old-growth trunks, so take it slow and expect to yield.

Two things to know before you commit to the drive. First, the park currently can't accommodate oversized vehicles or trailers for day-use parking, so leave the RV at camp. Second, day-use parking is limited and the park highly recommends reservations. Check the park page for current reservation details before you go, since post-fire recovery means conditions there keep evolving. When 236 has a problem, it shows up in the same Caltrans tools as Highway 9: QuickMap, the route lookup, or 1-800-427-7623.

Don't Bet on Your Phone

Cell coverage in the San Lorenzo Valley is real but patchy, and it drops out in stretches of the canyon along both highways, with dead pockets that vary by carrier. The practical response is simple: download offline maps before you leave, screenshot your destination and any reservation, and tell someone where you're headed if you're hiking. If your plans depend on calling ahead, make the call from town rather than the road. Coverage can also degrade in storms and outages, which is one more reason the paper-map habit never really died up here.

When Storms Change Everything

In winter, atmospheric river storms can close sections of Highway 9, Highway 236, and the county roads with little warning, through flooding, slides, or downed trees and lines. The durable advice is to check before you drive: the NWS Bay Area forecast for what's coming, QuickMap and the county Road Closure Dashboard for what's closed, and our Mountain Status page, which gathers the official links. If you reach water across the road, turn around. The National Weather Service warns that twelve inches of moving water can carry away most cars and that the road under floodwater may already be gone. Never drive around closure barriers; they're there because someone confirmed the road isn't safe.

Storm days are not the days to visit. But if you're already here when weather rolls in, businesses in town stay friendly, and waiting out a squall with coffee beats testing a flooded dip in the road every time.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Highway 9 open right now?

We never answer that from a static page, and you shouldn't trust any page that does. Check Caltrans QuickMap, enter route 9 at the Caltrans road information page, or call 1-800-427-7623. Our Mountain Status page links all of the live sources in one place.

Can I drive an RV or trailer to Big Basin?

Not for day use right now. The park's official page says no oversized vehicles or trailers can be accommodated for day-use parking, and stretches of Highway 236 are narrow enough that big rigs struggle regardless. Park the RV elsewhere and drive in with a passenger vehicle, ideally with a parking reservation.

Where should I park for a day in downtown Boulder Creek?

Street parking on and just off Highway 9 is the main supply, with small business lots for customers. Come early on weekends and event days, obey posted signs, and don't block driveways, hydrants, or road width. There's no big central lot, so expect a short walk and treat it as part of the visit.

Will I have cell service on the drive up?

Sometimes, and you shouldn't plan around it. Coverage drops in stretches of the canyon on both highways and varies by carrier, so download offline maps, save what you need before leaving Santa Cruz or Saratoga, and let someone know your plans if you're heading into the parks.

What do I do if a tree is down across the road?

If wires are involved or it's blocking traffic dangerously, call 911 and keep your distance. Otherwise report it: (831) 477-3999 reaches Santa Cruz County's 24-hour road dispatch for county roads, and Caltrans handles Highways 9 and 236, reachable via roads.dot.ca.gov or 1-800-427-7623. Our emergency contacts list has the full set of numbers.

boulder-creek-tree-work-questions.title: Tree Work in Boulder Creek: Questions to Ask Before Anyone Touches a Redwood
boulder-creek-tree-work-questions.html:
Before anyone puts a saw to a tree on your Boulder Creek property, get three things straight: whether the job needs a permit, whether the outfit doing it is licensed and insured for tree work specifically, and whether the plan actually serves your fire safety rather than just someone's invoice. Tree work is the most consequential home service in this town. Done right, it protects your house. Done wrong, it can cost you a code enforcement case, a lawsuit, or the redwood that made you buy the place. None of this is legal advice; it's a list of the questions that keep neighbors out of trouble.

First Question: Does This Tree Need a Permit?

Don't let anyone, including a tree company, tell you flatly that "you don't need a permit up here." Sometimes you do, and it depends entirely on where the tree stands.

Santa Cruz County's tree protection and removal rules require a county permit when a tree sits in the county's Coastal Zone and meets the "significant tree" definition, and also when a tree stands in designated sensitive habitat. The Significant Trees Protection chapter of the county code spells out the definitions; inside urban and rural service lines the trigger starts at 20 inches diameter at breast height, and in the Coastal Zone the rules cover not just removal but trimming more than one third of a tree's green foliage. Whether any of that applies to your parcel is a map question, and the county points people to its GIS tool to check.

Closer to home for most of us: creeks. The county's riparian corridor ordinance protects a band along every stream, 50 feet out from each side of a perennial stream and 30 feet from an intermittent one, and it defines tree and shrub removal, meaning topping or felling anything taller than 8 feet, as a regulated development activity inside that corridor. In a town threaded with creeks that feed the San Lorenzo River, a surprising number of backyards include riparian corridor. Cutting there without county sign-off is how well-meaning people end up in enforcement.

Two more wrinkles. Commercial timber operations are a separate world regulated through CAL FIRE under the state Forest Practice Act, and the county's tree page notes that some removals need a CAL FIRE permit rather than a county one. And for genuine emergencies, the code does allow immediate action when a hazardous tree threatens life or property, with a report to the county afterward. That's for real emergencies, not for trees that have looked sketchy for three years.

The short version: permits can be required in Boulder Creek depending on your parcel's overlays, and the only reliable answers come from the county's Environmental Planning pages or a call to the Unified Permit Center. A good local tree company will know this and say so. Treat "no permit needed, trust me" as a red flag, and get the answer in writing either way.

Defensible Space Is Not a Blank Check

Fire safety is the reason for most tree work here, and the state's actual guidance is more surgical than a clear-cut. CAL FIRE's defensible space program requires 100 feet of defensible space around structures under Public Resources Code 4291, organized in zones. Zone 0 is the first 5 feet from the house, kept ember-resistant. Zone 1 runs to 30 feet, kept "lean, clean and green." Zone 2 continues to 100 feet or your property line, where the goal is reducing fuel, not eliminating trees.

Notice what the guidance actually asks for: spacing between trees that widens with the slope, limbs removed within 10 feet of chimneys and stovepipes, lower branches limbed up at least 6 feet from the ground, dead material out. The spacing numbers matter up here, because they're written for terrain like ours. CAL FIRE asks for 10 feet between trees on flat or mild ground under a 20 percent slope, 20 feet on a 20 to 40 percent slope, and 30 feet on anything steeper. Healthy, well-spaced, limbed-up trees are compatible with defensible space. So when a bidder's fire-safety plan is "take them all down," ask them to walk you through it zone by zone against CAL FIRE's own standards. Defensible space work also doesn't override the permit rules above; fire safety and habitat protection can apply to the same tree, which is exactly why the county wants eyes on removals in protected areas. Your local fire agency can weigh in too, and CAL FIRE notes local standards are sometimes stricter than the state minimum.

Licenses, Insurance, and Why Tree Work Is Special

Tree work above $1,000 is licensed contracting in California, and the classification to look for is C-49 Tree and Palm, which replaced the old D-49 designation. Check any company in the CSLB license lookup before they climb anything.

Here's the part too few homeowners know. Because the work is so dangerous, CSLB requires tree service contractors to carry workers' compensation insurance whether or not they have employees. Most trades can claim an exemption if they work alone; tree contractors can't. The license lookup shows the workers' comp status, so verify it there rather than taking a business card's word. Ask separately for a certificate of general liability insurance sent directly from the insurer. If a climber gets hurt or your neighbor's roof gets a new skylight, the difference between an insured and uninsured outfit is the difference between a claim and a catastrophe.

For the broader hiring mechanics, including the down payment cap and the three-bid habit, see our guide to hiring a contractor in the Santa Cruz Mountains. All of it applies doubly to tree work.

What an ISA Certified Arborist Adds

A contractor license says a business can legally do the work. It says nothing about whether the tree needs the work. That's where an International Society of Arboriculture credential comes in; you can find and verify credentialed professionals through the ISA's Find an Arborist tool. One caveat: listing is voluntary, so a legitimate arborist might not appear, but a credential claim you can't verify there is worth questioning.

For anything involving a permit, expect an arborist's report to be part of the application; the county's tree pages spell out what its reviewers want to see. And for a healthy tree you merely worry about, an arborist consult is cheap insurance against removing something that just needed pruning.

After the Storm: Slow Down

Every big winter blow shakes loose a wave of door-knockers with chainsaws, and the patterns reported after storms in many communities are consistent enough to name. Be careful with anyone who shows up uninvited and says your tree is about to fall, pushes you to decide on the spot, wants cash or a big deposit up front, has no license number on the truck or the paperwork, or can't produce insurance certificates. Urgency is the tool; the pitch works because you're rattled and the driveway is blocked.

The counter-moves are boring and effective. If a tree is genuinely dangerous right now, on a structure or blocking your only way out, that can justify emergency action; document everything with photos first. If it's into power lines, that's PG&E's problem to make safe, not a job for anyone's chainsaw, yours included. For everything else, take a day. Check the license, verify workers' comp, call a second company for a competing bid. A tree that has stood for eighty years will almost always stand through your due diligence.

Questions to Ask Before You Sign

Ask every tree bidder these, and note who answers cleanly. What's your C-49 license number? Does this job need a county permit or CAL FIRE involvement, and who confirms that? Can your insurer send me certificates for liability and workers' comp? Is removal necessary, or would pruning meet the fire-safety goal? What happens to the wood and slash, and is hauling included in the bid? Who's responsible if the neighbor's fence takes a hit? Local companies that work Boulder Creek every week answer these without flinching. Our directory lists local tree services as a starting point; being listed isn't an endorsement, so run the checks regardless.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a permit to remove a tree on my own property in Boulder Creek?

Sometimes. It depends on whether the tree stands in the Coastal Zone, a riparian corridor along a creek, sensitive habitat, or another protected overlay, and that's parcel-specific. Check your parcel in the county GIS tool and confirm with county planning before cutting. Never rely on a contractor's verbal assurance that no permit is needed.

Does trimming count, or just removal?

In protected situations it can count. In the Coastal Zone, the county's significant tree rules cover trimming more than one third of a tree's green foliage, and the riparian ordinance regulates topping as well as felling. Light pruning for health and clearance is a different animal from major canopy reduction, so describe the actual scope when you ask the county.

What if a tree is about to come down on my house right now?

True emergencies are treated differently; the county code allows immediate action when a hazardous tree requires it for safety of life or property, with a report to the Planning Director afterward, within 10 working days under the significant tree chapter. Photograph the situation before and after, and if power lines are involved, stay clear and call PG&E first.

How do I verify a tree company is legitimate?

Three lookups: their license and workers' comp status at CSLB, any arborist credential at Trees Are Good, and insurance certificates sent directly from their insurer. Remember that tree contractors must carry workers' comp even as one-person operations.

Will clearing trees satisfy my defensible space requirements?

Not necessarily, and it may be more than the standard asks. CAL FIRE's guidance is built on spacing, limbing, and fuel reduction across three zones out to 100 feet, not blanket removal. Get a zone-by-zone plan, and check whether your local fire agency applies stricter standards before committing to removals you can't undo.

boulder-creek-utilities-guide.title: Utilities, Water, and Waste in Boulder Creek: Who Serves What
boulder-creek-utilities-guide.html:
Here's the short version for anyone new to town or just tired of guessing. Electricity in Boulder Creek comes from PG&E. Water for most homes comes from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, though plenty of properties are on private wells. Most homes are on septic systems overseen by Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, while a portion of town near the core is on a county-run sewer. Trash, recycling, and organics pickup is GreenWaste Recovery, the county's franchised hauler, and it's not optional anymore. Heating fuel for many households is delivered propane from private suppliers you choose yourself. Internet depends on your exact address, so look it up rather than trusting a list. Because we're unincorporated county, there's no city hall handling any of this; each service has its own provider, and the details below tell you who to call for what.

One standing caveat for the whole page: service territories and providers change, and boundaries up here are parcel-specific. When a decision matters, confirm with the provider directly.

Electricity: PG&E

PG&E is the electric utility for Boulder Creek and the rest of the San Lorenzo Valley. For outages, the PG&E Outage Center has the live outage map, reporting tools, and alert signups, and the 24-hour outage line is 1-800-743-5002. General customer service, including starting or stopping service when you move, is 1-877-660-6789, around the clock.

Two things are worth knowing as a mountain customer. First, PG&E is regulated by the California Public Utilities Commission, which also oversees Public Safety Power Shutoffs, the planned outages PG&E can call during high fire-risk weather. Second, outages are enough of a fact of life here that they deserve their own plan; we cover alerts, generators, and food safety in our power outage plan for mountain households.

On the gas side, don't assume. Some addresses have PG&E natural gas service and many mountain properties don't, relying on delivered propane instead. Check with PG&E about what's actually connected at your address before you buy appliances that expect one or the other.

Water: SLV Water District or Your Own Well

The San Lorenzo Valley Water District has been supplying water in the valley since 1941 and serves Boulder Creek, Brookdale, Ben Lomond, Lompico, Zayante, Scotts Valley, Manana Woods, and Felton through more than 7,900 connections. The district office is right in town at 13060 Highway 9, and the phone number is 831-338-2153. Starting or stopping service, billing questions, and leak reports all go through the district; the website has a start and stop service section, and for anything urgent, call.

Not every Boulder Creek property is a district customer, though. Outlying parcels and some older neighborhoods run on private wells or small shared systems, and the only reliable way to know a specific property's status is to ask the district or look at what the seller discloses in escrow. If you're on a well, you're your own water utility: testing, treatment, and the pump are on you, and Santa Cruz County Environmental Health is the local agency that regulates individual water systems. It's also worth remembering that a well pump needs electricity, which is exactly why well households should read our outage plan article.

Sewer or Septic: Probably Septic

Most Boulder Creek homes treat wastewater on-site with a septic system. The oversight agency is Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, which permits new systems, repairs, and upgrades, and handles maintenance requirements. If you're buying, building, or your drainfield is acting up, they're the call: 831-454-2022, or EnvironmentalHealth@santacruzcountyca.gov. Septic in the San Lorenzo Valley is its own subject, with small lots, steep slopes, and creeks nearby, so involve the county early rather than after a contractor has started digging.

There are two exceptions to the septic rule. A portion of the Boulder Creek community is connected to a sanitary sewer run by the county as County Service Area 7, Boulder Creek Sanitation, which operates its own treatment facility; connected properties pay an annual sewer charge on the property tax bill, and the county's number for the district is 831-454-2160. Separately, the water district owns and operates a small wastewater system serving roughly 56 homes in Bear Creek Estates. If you're not sure which category your property falls into, check your property tax bill for a sewer service charge, or call the county.

Trash, Recycling, and Organics: GreenWaste Recovery

Curbside collection in unincorporated Santa Cruz County, which includes all of Boulder Creek, is franchised to GreenWaste Recovery. Single-family homes get weekly pickup of garbage, recycling, and organics, with garbage carts from 20 to 96 gallons and recycling and organics carts included at no extra charge. To sign up or ask about service on a private road, call 831-426-2711 or email SantaCruzCS@GreenWaste.com.

Here's the part that catches people: service is now mandatory. Under the county's universal service requirement, every developed property in the unincorporated county must either subscribe with GreenWaste or register with the county as a self-hauler, and the county has set a compliance deadline of November 1, 2026, with fines that start at $100 and escalate from there. If you've been quietly trunk-hauling your own bags for years, this is the year to get legal, one way or the other.

For everything the truck won't take, the nearest facility is the Ben Lomond Transfer Station at 9835 Newell Creek Road in Ben Lomond. It also hosts a household hazardous waste drop-off on Thursdays from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm, which is where old paint, batteries, pesticides, and motor oil should go; bring proof of residency, and call 831-454-2606 with questions. Check the county Public Works site for the station's current general hours before you load the truck.

Propane and Heating Fuel

If your home heats or cooks with propane, you're dealing with a private, competitive market rather than a utility with an assigned territory. That has a real upside: you can shop. Suppliers differ on per-gallon price, tank rental versus ownership, delivery schedules, and minimums, so it's worth calling two or three, asking neighbors who they use, and requesting current pricing in writing before you commit. If you're new to a propane house, find the tank, learn to read the percentage gauge, and don't let it run low heading into a stretch of winter storms, when delivery schedules get tight for everyone. We're deliberately not naming suppliers here because companies and coverage change; your neighbors' current recommendations will be fresher than any list we could publish.

Internet and Phone

Anyone who promises you a tidy list of Boulder Creek internet providers is guessing, because availability genuinely varies street by street up here. The reliable method takes five minutes: put your exact address into the FCC National Broadband Map, which shows every provider that reports service at your location and what speeds they claim. Treat those listings as claims to verify, not guarantees, then confirm with each provider's own address checker before you schedule anything, and ask the closest neighbors what they actually use and how it holds up in winter. That last step is the one that saves you, because a provider that's fine in town can be a different experience at the end of a long canyon road.

Whatever you choose, remember that home internet and cordless phones die with the power. If you work from home, think through your outage fallback, whether that's a cell plan with hotspot data or simply knowing which nearby spots have power and signal.

Who to Call: The Short List

Keep this somewhere findable. Power outage or downed line area: PG&E, 1-800-743-5002, and stay far away from any downed line. Water service, leaks, or billing on district water: San Lorenzo Valley Water District, 831-338-2153. Septic permits and problems: County Environmental Health, 831-454-2022. Sewer questions in the CSA 7 area: 831-454-2160. Trash, recycling, and organics: GreenWaste Recovery, 831-426-2711. Household hazardous waste: 831-454-2606. And for county emergency alerts, sign up for CruzAware at cruzaware.org so road closures and evacuation notices reach you directly.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if my house is on SLVWD water or a well?

Check your mail for a water bill from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, or call the district at 831-338-2153 with your address and ask. If you're buying a home, the disclosures should say, but verify with the district anyway, because boundaries are parcel-specific and small shared systems exist too.

Is garbage service really required in Boulder Creek?

Yes. The county's universal service requirement says every developed property in the unincorporated county must subscribe with GreenWaste Recovery or register as a self-hauler, with a compliance deadline of November 1, 2026 and escalating fines after that. Call GreenWaste at 831-426-2711 to get set up.

Is Boulder Creek on sewer or septic?

Mostly septic, overseen by County Environmental Health. The exceptions are properties connected to the CSA 7 Boulder Creek Sanitation sewer in part of town, which show a sewer charge on the property tax bill, and about 56 homes in Bear Creek Estates on a small district-run system.

What internet providers serve Boulder Creek?

It depends on your exact address, so look yours up on the FCC National Broadband Map and then confirm with each provider directly. Coverage up here changes street by street, and neighbors on your own road are the best source for how a connection really performs.

Where do I take paint, batteries, and other hazardous waste?

To the household hazardous waste drop-off at the Ben Lomond Transfer Station, 9835 Newell Creek Road, on Thursdays from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm, per the county's HHW page. Bring proof of residency, and call 831-454-2606 if you're not sure an item is accepted.

boulder-creek-weekend-itinerary.title: A Boulder Creek Weekend: The Locally Supportive Itinerary
boulder-creek-weekend-itinerary.html:
Sleep under the redwoods at the campground on Grove Street, provision at the town's two markets, spend Saturday in Big Basin, and give Sunday to a sandstone ridge or a river beach. That's the shape of a two-day Boulder Creek weekend that keeps your money in town, with the museum, the shops, and a mountain-grown wine tasting filling the edges. Two things to settle before anything else: reserve your Big Basin parking, since same-day reservations aren't available, and book your lodging, because the town keeps only a few options and is upfront about it. This plan assumes the spring-through-fall window. In winter, the parks and the river play by different rules.

First, the real lodging picture

Boulder Creek isn't a hotel town, and there's no use pretending otherwise. Our verified directory currently lists two lodging operations. Redwood Resort at 150 Grove Street is the one built for a weekend like this: a seasonal campground with tent and RV sites among the redwoods, a pool, dogs welcome, and online booking, all within an easy walk of downtown. Stay there and your lodging dollars stay in town while your car mostly stays parked. The other listing, Redwood Christian Park, is a retreat center that hosts groups rather than individual weekenders, so it's the answer for a reunion or retreat, not a couple's getaway.

If canvas isn't your style, short-term rentals turn up around town on the usual platforms, though we don't list or vouch for individual rentals. And if you end up basing down the valley instead, the rest of this itinerary still works as two day trips.

Before the weekend: two reservations

Book your Big Basin day-use parking through the Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks form. Per California State Parks, reservations are highly recommended because spaces are limited: $6 for a regular vehicle plus a $2 reservation fee, versus $10 without one. The form doesn't take same-day bookings, so what's left on the morning of is first come, first served. Oversized vehicles and trailers can't be accommodated right now.

Planning Sunday wine tasting? Reserve at Big Basin Vineyards too. The estate tasting room on Memory Lane pours Saturdays and Sundays from noon to 5, by reservation through its site.

Saturday morning: provision in town

Start downtown. Grab coffee and breakfast at one of the town's cafes, but lean on our current food guide and its phone numbers rather than a search result, since hours shift seasonally at small operations.

Then build a picnic. Johnnie's Super Market, the town grocery since 1938, opens at 8 every morning and makes deli sandwiches from family recipes. Wild Roots Market, locally owned since 1991, opens at 9 with a deli counter and shelves stocked heavily from nearby farms. Either one outfits a day in the park better than anything you'd have packed from home, and there are no services on the road into Big Basin, so buy your water and snacks here.

Saturday: Big Basin, thoughtfully

Big Basin Redwoods State Park, California's oldest state park, is about 9 miles up Highway 236 from downtown, a curvy, unhurried 25 to 30 minutes. The park is open 8 a.m. to sunset for limited day use while it recovers from the 2020 CZU fire, and what's open changes, so check the official park page the morning you go.

Walk the Redwood Loop through the old-growth grove first, then pick a longer route from the current trail map if you want more miles. Expect limited facilities, thinner shade than the old photos suggest, and a forest doing something you won't see many other places: rebuilding itself in the open sun. We've written a full companion piece on doing this day well, shuttle and bus options included, in our Big Basin day trip guide. One planning note shapes the whole weekend: all campgrounds in the main park remain closed, which is why staying in Boulder Creek and day-tripping in is the practical move.

Come down the mountain by late afternoon and give the town's shops an hour before they close. Air & Fire at 13150 Highway 9 stays open until 6:30 most days, with crystals, incense, jewelry, and work from more than 50 regional artists. SWAG at 13026 Highway 9 deals in restored mid-century stereos, vinyl, and vintage and local apparel, in an 1893 building the shop notes was the first in the country owned and operated by women; call 831-295-9191 for the day's hours.

For dinner, Scopazzi's at 13300 Big Basin Way is the town's historic landmark dining room, serving dinner from 4 p.m. Wednesday through Sunday. If you'd rather end the day outdoors, The Sawmill south of downtown does American fare and craft beer with a big outdoor area and a music stage; its hours aren't posted online, so call 831-610-8196. The food guide has the full spread if you want other speeds.

Sunday morning: ridge or river

Two good options, depending on your legs.

The hike: Castle Rock State Park sits at the top of Highway 9 on Skyline Boulevard, a slower mountain drive from town. Per State Parks, it's open for day use from 6 a.m. to sunset, the Kirkwood entrance station runs 8 to 5, and vehicle day use is $10. You get sculpted sandstone, big views toward the ocean, and 34 miles of trails, plus open rock climbing if that's your thing. Know before you go: no dogs on trails, and no cell reception in the park, so download your map first.

The slow morning: sleep in and walk to Junction Park at Middleton and Railroad Avenues, where a small beach meets the San Lorenzo River beside a lawn and picnic tables. In summer the river here is the town's swimming hole. Bring the coffee you bought downtown and call it recovery.

Either way, lunch is easy: River Dogs, the outdoor stand at 123 Forest Street behind Joe's Bar, serves Chicago-style Vienna Beef dogs with a fire pit for cool days and an acoustic stage when there's music. Its posted schedule runs weekend hours from 11:30 to 5, and it closes for bad weather, which is fair for a place with no roof.

Sunday afternoon: history, then a glass

The San Lorenzo Valley Museum's Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 opens Wednesday through Sunday from 1 to 4, admission free, donations welcome. The museum is marking 50 years in 2026, and the current Retrospect exhibit looks back across all of them. An hour here reframes everything you drove past this weekend, from the logging years to the fire.

Then, if you reserved, finish at Big Basin Vineyards' estate tasting room at 830 Memory Lane, pouring until 5 on Sundays. It's a short drive up a narrow lane from town, so sort out a designated driver before the first pour, not after. If wine's not your thing, a last unhurried lap of downtown with an early dinner does the same job of ending the weekend in town rather than in traffic.

If your weekend stretches to Monday

Monday is Boulder Creek's quiet day. Several restaurants, Scopazzi's among them, close Monday and Tuesday, and the museum galleries stay dark until Wednesday. The markets carry the day: Johnnie's and Wild Roots are open daily. Check the food guide before assuming anything else is open.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need reservations for this weekend?

Two are worth making ahead: Big Basin day-use parking, since the reservation form doesn't take same-day bookings and walk-up spaces are first come, first served, and the Big Basin Vineyards tasting room if you want Sunday wine. Lodging at Redwood Resort books online, and summer weekends are its season, so don't sleep on that one either.

Can I camp inside Big Basin instead of staying in town?

No. All campgrounds in the main park are closed while recovery work continues, per State Parks. The campground at Redwood Resort in town is the closest way to sleep under the redwoods and still walk to dinner, which is not a bad consolation.

Is this itinerary doable without hiking?

Yes. Swap Castle Rock for the river morning, keep the Redwood Loop at Big Basin, which is a flat 0.6-mile walk rather than a hike, and the rest of the weekend is markets, shops, museum, meals, and wine.

What about winter?

This plan is built for spring through fall. In winter the river runs high, River Dogs closes in bad weather, park trails close after storms, and Highway 9 itself sometimes has opinions. The bones still work on a clear winter weekend, but check the park pages and call ahead everywhere.

Why aren't there any lodging recommendations beyond the campground?

Because we only name what we can verify, and town lodging is genuinely limited: the directory lists the campground and a group retreat center, period. Short-term rentals exist on the usual platforms, but we don't list private rentals or pretend to have vetted them. When that changes, this page will change.

boulder-creek-winter-storm-checklist.title: Winter Storm Checklist for Boulder Creek Homes
boulder-creek-winter-storm-checklist.html:
The first big atmospheric river of the season tends to find whatever you didn't get to: the clogged culvert, the dead limb over the driveway, the flashlight with dead batteries. Getting a Boulder Creek home ready comes down to five jobs you can knock out in one good October afternoon: sign up for alerts, clear your drainage, deal with your trees, stock a week of supplies, and learn where to check road conditions before you need them. Do those five and you've handled most of what an atmospheric river can throw at a house up here. This checklist walks through each one, in the order that tends to matter most, with links to the official sources so you can go deeper where your property needs it. And one rule stands over the whole page: in any emergency, call 911 first.

Why Storms Hit Harder Up Here

The big winter storms that soak the Santa Cruz Mountains are usually atmospheric rivers, long corridors of moisture that NOAA describes as rivers in the sky. Most are harmless rain-makers, but the strong ones can stall over a watershed and, in NOAA's words, create extreme rainfall and floods, disrupt travel, and set off mudslides. Our steep canyons, tall trees, and one main highway mean the same storm that's a gray Tuesday in Santa Cruz can be downed lines, a closed road, and a three-day outage in Boulder Creek.

That's not a reason to dread winter. It's a reason to spend one afternoon getting ahead of it.

First: Set Up Your Alerts

Everything else on this list works better when you hear about weather early.

Sign up for CruzAware, Santa Cruz County's alert and warning system, at cruzaware.org. It's how the county sends evacuation notices, severe weather warnings, and other public safety alerts by text, call, and email. Register more than one contact method, since no alert system can promise delivery every time.

Bookmark the National Weather Service Bay Area office, which writes the forecasts and warnings for our mountains. When a storm is coming, their watches and warnings are the source of truth, not a weather app's blue icon.

Then set up outage alerts with PG&E. Account holders get them automatically for their service address, and anyone can sign up for updates on any address through the outage map. Our full walkthrough of outage prep, including generators, food safety, and the Medical Baseline program, is in the power outage plan.

Clear the Water's Path

Most storm damage to homes up here starts with water going where it shouldn't. Before the first big rain, walk your property in work gloves and look at where water flows and pools.

Clean the gutters and downspouts, and make sure downspouts actually carry water away from the foundation instead of dumping it against the house. Clear leaves and redwood duff out of any ditches, culverts, and drains on your property, and check them again mid-season, because one storm can refill them. If your driveway has a culvert, look through it; a plugged culvert is how driveways wash out. The county's storm preparedness page has a downloadable checklist and property-specific guidance worth reading before the season.

If water on your parcel is a recurring problem, the Resource Conservation District of Santa Cruz County works with rural homeowners on erosion and drainage, and you can request services through their site.

For flood-prone spots, sandbags help more than people expect. In Boulder Creek, the fire district keeps two self-serve sand bunkers through the winter season, with sand and bags at both: the Boulder Creek Golf Course at Highway 236 and E Hilton, and Redwood Elementary School at 16900 Highway 9, north of town. Bring your own shovel. Locations can change season to season, so call Boulder Creek Fire at (831) 338-7222 before you drive out. The county also posts a countywide list on its storm preparedness page, with a limit of 15 bags and no contractors. A county tip worth repeating: only fill bags halfway, because half-full bags stacked up hold water back better than bags filled to the top. Our sandbags and road closures guide has the wider valley list.

Look Up at Your Trees

Do this on a clear day, not when the wind's already blowing. Walk around the house and look for dead limbs over the roof, driveway, and parking spots, and for trees that lean harder than they used to. You don't need to be an arborist to spot an obviously dead top or a big broken limb hanging in the canopy, and it's worth having a professional look at anything that worries you before the ground gets saturated. Winter tree work gets booked out fast once the first storm hits.

Two hard rules. If a tree or limb is tangled in power lines, don't touch it, don't cut near it, and call 911, then PG&E. And if a tree comes down across a county road, that's road dispatch at (831) 477-3999, any hour; our emergency contacts list has every number on one page.

Storm-season tree work overlaps with fire-season work. CAL FIRE's defensible space guidance calls for removing dead and dying vegetation, keeping the first five feet around the house clear of combustible material, and maintaining 100 feet of defensible space, which state law requires. Limbs you remove in October are limbs that can't land on your roof in January or carry fire in August.

Stock Supplies for a Week of Weird

Boulder Creek's version of storm supplies assumes the power might be out and the road might be closed for a few days. Ready.gov's kit guidance is the baseline: one gallon of water per person per day for several days, several days of food that doesn't need cooking, a flashlight, a battery-powered or hand-crank radio, phone chargers with a backup battery, and your prescription medications, plus basics like pain relievers.

Up here, add the mountain extras: extra water if you're on a well, since no power means no pump, a full tank of gas or charge in at least one vehicle, cash in small bills, pet food, and anything a baby or an elder in your house can't go without. If you heat with propane, check the tank gauge going into winter rather than during the first cold snap; our utilities guide covers who to call about propane, water, and everything else.

The Generator Rule That Saves Lives

If your storm plan includes a generator, this is the part of the checklist you memorize. Never run a generator inside your home or garage, even with the doors and windows open. The CDC says to operate generators only outdoors, more than 20 feet from windows, doors, and vents. Carbon monoxide is odorless and colorless, it kills more than 400 Americans a year, and sleeping people can die without ever waking up. Put a battery-powered carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area and test it when you test your smoke alarms.

Never plug a generator into a wall outlet or your panel without a transfer switch installed by an electrician; backfed power can hurt the line crews working to get your neighborhood back on. The full setup details are in the power outage plan.

Know Your Roads Before the Storm

The middle of a storm is a bad time to learn where road information lives. Save these now.

For county-maintained roads, the county's Road Closure Dashboard shows current closures, and road dispatch at (831) 477-3999 answers around the clock. For Highway 9 and Highway 236, which are state routes, check Caltrans road conditions or call 1-800-427-7623. Our Mountain Status page pulls the official links together in one place.

And one rule from the National Weather Service that's absolute: never drive into water on a road. Six inches of fast-moving water can knock an adult down, twelve inches can float most cars, and the road under that water may not be there anymore. Turn around. No shortcut in the valley is worth it.

The One-Afternoon Version

Here's the whole checklist, compressed. Sign up for CruzAware and PG&E alerts. Clean gutters, downspouts, ditches, and culverts. Walk the property and flag dead limbs and leaning trees, and book tree work early if you need it. Build the ready.gov kit plus mountain extras, especially stored water if you're on a well. Confirm the generator lives outside, 20 feet from openings, with a CO detector inside. Save the road dispatch number and bookmark the closure dashboard, Caltrans, and Mountain Status. Then check the drainage again after the first storm, because the first storm always finds something.

None of this guarantees an easy winter, and nobody can promise you one. But a prepared house turns most storms into stories instead of emergencies.

Frequently Asked Questions

When should I start storm prep in Boulder Creek?

Aim to finish by mid-October. The big atmospheric river storms typically arrive between late fall and early spring, and tree crews, roofers, and gutter cleaners book up once the first system is in the forecast. The NWS Bay Area office is the place to watch for what's actually coming.

Where do I get sandbags near Boulder Creek?

Boulder Creek Fire keeps two self-serve sand bunkers through the winter season: the Boulder Creek Golf Course at Highway 236 and E Hilton, and Redwood Elementary School at 16900 Highway 9. Sand and bags are at both; bring your own shovel. Call (831) 338-7222 to confirm before you drive out, since locations can change. Santa Cruz County also posts a countywide list on its storm preparedness page, with a limit of 15 bags and no contractors.

Who do I call when a tree falls across the road?

If power lines are involved or anyone's in danger, call 911. Otherwise, for county-maintained roads, call road dispatch at (831) 477-3999, answered 24 hours a day. If it's on Highway 9 or 236, that's Caltrans territory, 1-800-427-7623. On a private road, clearing it is generally up to the road association or the neighbors.

How much water should we store?

Start with ready.gov's rule of one gallon per person per day for several days, then add more if you're on a well, since your pump stops when the power does. Separate jugs for flushing toilets stretch your drinking water further. A multi-day outage after a storm is common enough here that erring on the high side is cheap insurance.

Is it ever safe to drive through a flooded stretch of Highway 9?

No. The National Weather Service is blunt: it's never safe to drive into floodwater, twelve inches can carry away most cars, and the pavement underneath may have collapsed. Turn around, check the Road Closure Dashboard or Mountain Status, and wait it out.

buying-local-boulder-creek.title: Why Buying Local Hits Different in a Mountain Town
buying-local-boulder-creek.html:
Boulder Creek has one main street. That's the whole argument, really. On a street that short, every storefront is load-bearing, and whether a business stays or goes often comes down to one thing: whether the people who live here spend here first. No lecture coming, no scorecard, and nobody's asking you to cancel your Prime account.

But if you've ever wondered whether your grocery run or hardware trip actually registers with the town, the answer is yes. It's worth understanding why it counts for more here than it would in a city.

A town this size doesn't have spare storefronts

In a city, when a shop closes, three more like it are a short drive away. Boulder Creek doesn't work that way. Here, one business often is the category. One pharmacy. One lumber yard. A couple of markets. When a storefront goes dark on Highway 9, the thing it sold doesn't get slightly less convenient. It usually leaves town entirely, and the replacement is a drive down the valley.

That drive is the real price of a closure. It's not only your time, though winter road conditions and summer weekend traffic stretch the round trip past what the map promises. It's that every errand leaving the valley makes the next closure a little more likely, because the businesses that remain are splitting a smaller pie. Small towns lose services in a slow spiral, not all at once. The spiral stays quiet until suddenly it isn't.

The flip side is just as real. Every business that holds on keeps the town more complete: more reasons to walk downtown, more jobs that don't require a commute over the hill, more lit windows on a rainy January evening. To see what's actually here right now, the directory is the current, verified inventory, and our guide to eating in town covers the food side in detail.

The fire made the stakes plain

Boulder Creek doesn't need a hypothetical to understand fragility. In August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex burned through the mountains around us. According to Cal OES, the fire leveled 1,490 structures, damaged 140 more, and burned 86,509 acres across Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties, tearing through Big Basin Redwoods State Park on its way.

The town itself held. The years since haven't been simple: rebuilding, insurance battles, storm damage on top of fire damage, and a gateway economy waiting for its state park to fully reopen. Through all of it, the businesses that stayed open were more than commerce. They were where people got supplies, information, and the reassuring sight of something normal still running.

That's the part of local buying that never shows up in an economics chart. A town's businesses are part of its disaster resilience. The market that knows its regulars. The hardware store with generator parts in October. The restaurant that becomes a de facto meeting hall when the power's out down the road. You can't spin those up on demand. You keep them by using them in the ordinary years.

What the numbers support, and what they don't

You've probably seen big claims about local spending, like "a dollar spent locally circulates three times longer." We're not going to hang the argument on those numbers, because the most-quoted versions come from studies commissioned by local-business advocacy groups, and the figures vary a lot depending on who ran the study and how. If we can't verify a number to a source we trust, we don't print it.

Here's what does hold up. The SBA Office of Advocacy counts 36.2 million small businesses in the United States, which is 99.9 percent of all businesses. They employ 62.3 million people, 45.9 percent of the private-sector workforce, and their activity makes up 43.5 percent of GDP. Small business isn't a niche. It's roughly half the working economy, and in a town like this one, it's nearly all of it.

The circulation argument works fine without an exact multiplier. When you buy from a Boulder Creek business, the money you spend has a decent chance of paying a local wage, covering rent on a local building, hiring a local tradesperson, or backing the kind of small community sponsorship that never makes headlines. Spend that same money with a distant retailer and essentially none of it comes back up Highway 9. You don't need a precise ratio to see which direction helps the town you live in.

What local-first looks like in practice

The habit that matters most: make town your first stop, not your fallback. Before defaulting to a drive or a browser tab, take thirty seconds to check whether someone in Boulder Creek sells the thing or does the thing. The directory exists for exactly that question, and every listing in it has been verified by a person.

Some of it is easy, because the local options are genuinely good. The town has two full grocery stores: Johnnie's Super Market, which has been feeding Boulder Creek since 1938 and makes its deli sandwiches from family recipes, and Wild Roots Market, locally owned since 1991 and stocked heavily from local farms. Between them, most weekly shopping never needs to leave town.

A few other low-effort shifts, take them or leave them:

Eat in town once more per month than you do now. The current food guide lists everything verified open, from sit-down dinners to a hot dog stand with a fire pit.

Think local for gifts. Downtown's shops and studios cover more ground than people expect, and a gift bought in town is two gifts, one for the recipient and one for the street.

Hire local for services when the option exists. Trades, repairs, lessons, care. Service dollars are some of the stickiest local dollars there are.

Tell people. A recommendation to a neighbor costs nothing and is worth more to a small business than most advertising.

The no-guilt clause

Everyone in this town buys things online, and everyone drives over the hill sometimes for selection or price. That's fine. A local-first habit isn't a purity test, and a household budget is a real constraint that no one gets to lecture you about.

The goal is only this: when the local option exists and roughly works, give it the first look. If the town's businesses get the first look from the people who live here, most of them will still be here the next time the road closes, the power drops, or the smoke rolls in. That's the whole trade, and it's a good one.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is buying local always more expensive?

Sometimes yes, sometimes no, and it depends on what you're counting. Sticker prices on identical goods can run higher in a small store, but once you count the drive, the time, and shipping, plenty of local purchases come out even or ahead. We won't pretend the difference never exists; we'd just suggest comparing the whole cost, not only the tag.

What if the local shop doesn't carry what I need?

Then buy it wherever you can get it, without guilt. It's also worth asking, though. Small shops can often order items, and they stock based on what people request. Telling a local business what you were looking for is quietly one of the most useful things a customer can do.

Does one household's spending really matter?

In a town this size, yes, more than intuition suggests. Boulder Creek businesses run on a small base of regulars, so a single household shifting a few purchases a month is a visible fraction of somebody's week. You won't save a business alone, but nobody's asking you to. This works as a shared habit, not a heroic act.

How do I find out what's actually in town?

Start with the directory. Every listing is checked by a person against first-party sources and carries a verified date, and nothing in it is paid placement. If you spot something missing or wrong, tell us and we'll fix it. The details of that process are in how directory verification works.

cruzaware-setup-guide.title: How to Set Up CruzAware Alerts (and What They Cover)
cruzaware-setup-guide.html:
Up here, a fire or a flood can move faster than word of mouth, and the phone tree that used to warn a canyon town doesn't outrun either one. So if you do one preparedness task this month, make it this: sign up for CruzAware, Santa Cruz County's alert system, at cruzaware.org. The county publishes no fee for registration, standard carrier rates apply, and it takes about ten minutes. It's how the county sends evacuation notices and other urgent alerts by text, phone call, email, and a mobile app. That's the single highest-value ten minutes of preparedness on offer for a Boulder Creek address. First things first, though: CruzAware is for receiving information. In an actual emergency, call 911. This guide walks through what the system is, exactly what the signup form asks for, and how CruzAware differs from the weather and power alerts you may already get.

What CruzAware Is

CruzAware is the official emergency and non-emergency alert system for Santa Cruz County, operated by the County of Santa Cruz together with Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1, the agency that dispatches for communities across Santa Cruz and San Benito counties. The county's alert information page describes what it carries: critical information during natural disasters, severe weather events, and public safety incidents, plus non-emergency notices like roadwork and community-level impacts. The county lists four ways those messages reach you: text message, phone call, email, and a mobile app.

The non-emergency messages are for residents of unincorporated areas of the county. Boulder Creek is unincorporated, so that part of the system applies here: road work, utility work, and other community-level impacts on our side of the county line.

The signup site lives at cruzaware.org, which takes you to a registration portal hosted by Genasys, the county's alert platform vendor. The portal describes itself as "the emergency and non-emergency alert and warning system for the residents of Santa Cruz County." It's available in English, Spanish, and Portuguese, and per the county's CruzAware FAQ, your information is used only for alerts and isn't sold or shared outside the system.

Anyone with a stake in the county can register: people who live here, work here, own property here, or just want to keep an eye on conditions for family who do.

What the Alerts Cover

The county's FAQ lists the range: earthquakes, floods, gas leaks, power outages, fires, and other situations that affect safety. Alerts can be geo-targeted, meaning they go to people who registered an address in the affected area. That's the detail that matters most for San Lorenzo Valley residents. An evacuation warning for a specific slice of Boulder Creek reaches the accounts that put an address in that slice, which is why the address step below isn't optional busywork.

Topic-based alerts, including weather, are a separate opt-in. Registering does not turn them on. You select them in your account's Notifications area after you sign up, and step 4 below walks through it.

There's a limit worth knowing, straight from the county's own FAQ: no system can guarantee delivery. Phones get turned off, texts fail, emails land in spam. The county's advice is to register more than one contact method, and it's good advice.

How to Sign Up, Step by Step

Here's what the registration form asks for. You'll need an email address, your phone number, and your street address.

  1. Go to cruzaware.org. You'll land on the registration portal. Under the Login box, find "Don't have an account?" and select "Click here to register."
  2. Enter your email address. The form marks this required, and it's used for alerts and account recovery.
  3. Enter your cell phone number and leave the "Receive text messages on this phone" box checked if the phone can get texts. The form lets you register with phone, email, or both, and recommends both so alerts reach you in more situations. If your phone can't receive texts, an email is required.
  4. Enter your full street address and pick the match from the dropdown list as you type. This is required, and it's what ties you to location-specific alerts. The form's own tip: if your address doesn't appear, add your zip code, or click your location on the little map. Rural addresses up here sometimes need that nudge.
  5. Create a password, confirm it, and select Register. The password must be at least 12 characters and include at least one number, one capital letter, and one lowercase letter, with no spaces. Special characters are not required. The form runs a reCAPTCHA check as part of submission.

That's the core signup. If anything on the form looks different when you get there, follow what the portal says; the form is the source of truth and it does get updated.

After You Register: Five Minutes of Fine-Tuning

Once you're in, the account menu lets you strengthen your setup. The county FAQ walks through each item.

  1. Add more locations. You can register multiple addresses: home, work, a kid's school, an aging parent's place in the valley. Each one extends your geo-targeted coverage.
  2. Add more contact methods. Additional emails and phone numbers, including a landline or a family member's cell, can all receive your alerts.
  3. Save the senders in your phone. The portal lists exactly how alerts arrive: phone calls from +1 (831) 453-0048, texts from short code 65513, and email from CruzAware. The portal gives the email sender as noreply@genasys.com and the county FAQ gives it as cruzaware@genasys.com, so safelist both addresses, or safelist the genasys.com domain. Create a contact called "Santa Cruz County Alerts" with that phone number so a 2 a.m. call gets answered instead of ignored.
  4. Turn on weather alerts. This is a separate opt-in. Registering does not give you CruzAware weather alerts. The portal's own warning: to receive weather alerts you must select the weather categories in the Notifications area of your account and have an address on file. Do both. In a canyon that floods, this is the step to not skip.
  5. Check the prescribed-burn box. In the same Notifications area, the county directs residents to check "Prescribed burn" to get prescribed-fire notices from the County, CAL FIRE, and State Parks. Useful here, where smoke in the air is a question worth answering fast.

If you have questions the portal doesn't answer, the county's contact for the system is CruzAware@santacruzcountyca.gov, listed right on the portal.

If It Says You're Already Registered

Common situation: you try to sign up and get a pop-up saying your email or number is already registered. That's usually not an error. When the county moved from its old CodeRED system to CruzAware, existing contact information was transferred over. The portal's instruction is to go back to the login screen, choose "Forgot your Password?", and enter your email or cell number to reset access. Once you're in, check that your addresses and contact methods are current, because a migrated account may be carrying old information.

How CruzAware Differs from NWS and PG&E Alerts

These three systems overlap less than people assume, and the safest setup is all three.

CruzAware is county-operated and opt-in. It's the channel for local emergency instructions, including evacuation warnings and orders, and it only knows the contact methods and addresses you give it.

National Weather Service products come from the federal forecast office for the San Francisco Bay Area (based in Monterey), which covers Santa Cruz County. The NWS issues the underlying weather watches and warnings. The most severe ones can also reach your phone automatically through Wireless Emergency Alerts, the federal push system that needs no signup, which the county lists as a separate channel alongside NOAA Weather Radio and the Emergency Alert System. NWS tells you a dangerous storm is coming; CruzAware is where local agencies tell you what to do about it here.

PG&E notifications are separate again. PG&E offers outage updates by text, email, or phone when you sign up through your PG&E online account, and those alerts are about electric service tied to your account. They won't carry evacuation information, and CruzAware won't give you outage restoration estimates. And PG&E's own rule for a downed line applies no matter what alerts you have: call 911 first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does CruzAware cost anything?

The county publishes no fee for registration, and standard text and call rates from your carrier apply. Registration asks only for contact details, an address, and a password.

Do I need to give my address?

The live form requires an address at signup, and it's central to how the system works: geo-targeted alerts, including evacuation notices for your specific area, depend on it. An address alone isn't enough for weather alerts, though. Those also require selecting the weather categories in your account's Notifications area. You can add more addresses after registering.

Will I get alerts if I never sign up?

Some, maybe. The most severe warnings can arrive through Wireless Emergency Alerts, which are automatic. But WEA is a blunt instrument, and county alerts targeted to your neighborhood go only to registered CruzAware contacts. Don't count on being covered without signing up.

Is signing up a guarantee I'll be warned in time?

No, and the county says as much. Delivery can fail if your phone is off, your contact info is stale, or a message lands in spam. Register multiple contact methods, keep them current, and treat alerts as one layer alongside your own awareness of conditions.

How do I update my info or unsubscribe?

Log in at cruzaware.org and use the account menu to edit contacts, addresses, and notification settings. There's an Unsubscribe option that the county FAQ says deletes your contact data from the system.

day-with-kids-boulder-creek.title: A Day in Boulder Creek with Kids
day-with-kids-boulder-creek.html:
You can build a full day with young kids in Boulder Creek without leaving the valley, and most of the good parts are free. The short version: start at the library or a playgroup in the morning, grab a treat and an easy lunch downtown, spend the afternoon at the river or a park, and keep an indoor backup in your pocket for hot afternoons or rainy days. Hours and program schedules shift by season, so treat each link as the source of truth and call ahead when a plan hinges on one stop.

Morning: library programs and free playgroups

The Boulder Creek Branch Library at 13390 West Park Avenue runs the most reliable free kids' programming in town. Toddler and Preschool Storytime lands every Wednesday at 11 a.m. with stories and songs for little ones and their grownups. Discovery Zone Playgroup, a drop-in free-play hour for young kids and a caregiver, runs Friday mornings at 11. For the six-and-up crowd, Make and Explore is a STEAM building program on Wednesday afternoons at 1:30, with a new project most weeks. Branch hours are Monday through Thursday 10 to 6, Friday 11 to 5, and Saturday 10 to 5, and the events calendar is where seasonal one-off performances show up too.

In summer, the rec district adds Water Park Wednesdays at Junction Park, a free outdoor playgroup with water play, sand building, and river exploration, Wednesdays from noon to 3 p.m., June through August. It's run by the Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District, and it's genuinely one of the best weekday mornings in town for the under-eight set.

A treat break downtown

Boulder Creek does bakeries well. Dessert First Bakery at 13090 Highway 9, Suite 2, is open daily 6:30 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. and keeps a case of cupcakes, cookies, and pastries alongside its custom cakes, so it's an easy mid-morning stop with kids. Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed cottage bakery working out of Boulder Creek with small-batch goods and custom orders by arrangement rather than a walk-in counter. And Wildstone Bakery sells its naturally fermented breads and pastries at regional farmers markets, including the Ben Lomond Village Market on first Saturdays through October. If you're downtown on a Thursday afternoon, the Boulder Creek Farmers Market at Junction Park (1:30 to 5:30 p.m.) is its own treat, with local vendors and music.

Lunch that works with a stroller

Downtown lunch is short, casual, and close to the river. Taqueria Los Gallos and El Rey Leon both do quick Mexican counter service on the main drag. The Sawmill Restaurant and Alehouse has burgers, pub fare, and outdoor seating up Highway 9. For grab-and-go, Johnnie's Super Market and Liquor makes fresh deli sandwiches daily and Wild Roots Market has a deli and prepared foods you can carry to a picnic table. When it's open, River Dogs of Boulder Creek, the outdoor hot dog stand on Forest Street, is about as kid-friendly as lunch gets, with vegetarian options and occasional live music. Full hours and phone numbers are on our food and drink page; call ahead if you're set on a particular spot, since small kitchens keep limited days.

Afternoon: river and parks

The classic Boulder Creek afternoon is the river. Junction Park, a block off the main street at Middleton and Railroad Avenues, has a small beach, San Lorenzo River access, a lawn, and picnic tables. The river is a popular summer swim spot, and it is swim at your own risk: there's no lifeguard, the current and depth change with the season, and you keep your own eyes on your kids near moving water. Bring water shoes for the rocks.

If you want play structures and room to run, Garrahan Park at 15555 Highway 9 has a walking track, a soccer field, play structures, a gazebo, benches, and a barbecue area, open sunrise to sunset. It's the better pick for a picnic-and-playground afternoon or a birthday gathering. Around town you'll also catch pop-up messy-play and water-play events run by Messy Play Kits, a Boulder Creek maker of handcrafted sensory and STEAM kits for ages 3 to 8 that partners with the rec district on park events like Water Park Wednesdays and the September Splashfest at Garrahan Park.

Indoor and rainy-day backup

Boulder Creek doesn't have an indoor play space of its own, so the honest answer for a hot or rainy afternoon is that you either head to the library, which is a legitimate indoor plan with books and programs, or you drive. The nearest dedicated indoor option is Haven Play Lounge and Cafe at 108 Whispering Pines Drive, Suite 125, in Scotts Valley, an indoor play lounge and cafe with open-play sessions for ages 0 to 10. It's roughly a 25 to 35 minute drive over the hill, so it's a plan-ahead outing rather than a spur-of-the-moment stop, and its posted hours are limited (open Monday through Wednesday and Friday 9 to 5, Saturday 10 to 4, closed Thursday and Sunday). Check before you drive.

For the toddler-and-under crowd

The youngest kids have their own small scene here. Miss Brooke's Mini and Me Music Classes, led by Boulder Creek early-childhood educator Brooke Powell, are mommy-and-me music and movement sessions for infants, toddlers, and preschoolers, with reach-out and scheduling through her Instagram.
