The rec district's Circle program serves ages 3 to 5 with a play-based morning at the Bear Creek Community Center. And the library's Friday Discovery Zone Playgroup is built exactly for this age. Once a year the town also throws The Great Summer Playdate, a free playdate for kids up to age 5 that Miss Brooke's and Messy Play Kits co-host; the next one is Saturday, July 25 at 10:30 a.m., with the location shared through the signup.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there an indoor play place in Boulder Creek?

Not in town. The library is the best indoor option locally, especially on program days. The nearest dedicated indoor play lounge is Haven Play Lounge and Cafe in Scotts Valley, about a 25 to 35 minute drive, with open play for ages 0 to 10 and limited posted hours, so confirm before heading over.

Can kids swim in the river at Junction Park?

The river beach at Junction Park is a popular summer swim spot, but it's swim at your own risk. There's no lifeguard, water conditions change through the season, and young kids need close, constant supervision near moving water. Water shoes help on the rocks.

What free kids' programs run each week?

As of mid-July 2026, the library hosts Toddler and Preschool Storytime on Wednesdays at 11 a.m., Discovery Zone Playgroup on Fridays at 11 a.m., and Make and Explore STEAM on Wednesday afternoons. The rec district runs free Water Park Wednesdays at Junction Park, noon to 3 p.m., June through August. Check the library and BCRPD calendars for the current schedule.

Where can I take a toddler on a rainy day?

Start with the Boulder Creek library and its storytime and playgroup, then keep Haven Play Lounge in Scotts Valley as a drive-worthy backup. Miss Brooke's Mini and Me music classes are another indoor option for the youngest kids; reach out through her Instagram for the current schedule.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a starting point. See the full directory and events calendar for hours and details, and if a program or listing has changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit so the next family gets it right.

does-it-snow-boulder-creek.title: Does It Snow in Boulder Creek? Mountain Weather Through the Year
does-it-snow-boulder-creek.html:
Short answer: not really, and rarely enough that when it does, it makes the local news. Boulder Creek sits at about 500 feet, low enough that snow at town level is a once-in-a-few-years event that usually melts by lunch, though the higher ridges above the valley catch a dusting more often. What Boulder Creek actually gets is a lot of rain in winter, dry warm summers, and a microclimate that runs warmer and sunnier than the foggy coast just over the hill. Here's what the weather really asks of you through the year.

The one-line climate

Boulder Creek has what climatologists call a warm-summer Mediterranean climate: cool, wet winters and warm, dry summers, with almost all the year's precipitation falling between roughly November and April. Summers are close to rainless. Town-center temperatures run to an August high around 80 degrees and a December low in the high 30s, so hard freezes and heat waves both happen, but neither is the norm.

The thing to understand about weather up here is that elevation does most of the work. The town center sits near 500 feet, but the surrounding hills climb toward 2,700 feet on the ridges, and every thousand feet changes the rain, the temperature, and the odds of snow. Your road can be clear while a friend a few hundred feet up the mountain is socked in.

Rain is the real story

Boulder Creek is one of the wetter spots in the greater Bay Area, and that's the number that actually matters here. Estimates of average annual rainfall vary a lot depending on the source and exactly where it's measured, generally landing somewhere in the 40-to-55-inch range for the town, well above the roughly 30 inches that falls on the Santa Cruz coast. The ridges above town collect even more. And the year-to-year swing is enormous: local records have run from under 20 inches in a dry year to over 100 inches in a wet one.

That rain is why winter, not snow, is the season that tests a mountain house. The big storms are usually atmospheric rivers, long plumes of moisture that can stall over the watershed and drop rain for days. The result up here tends to be downed trees, saturated ground, and road closures rather than snowbanks. Our winter storm checklist walks through getting a Boulder Creek home ready for exactly that.

So when does it actually snow?

Rarely, and mostly up high. As one longtime local record puts it, snow falls two or three times a year on the higher elevations of the Santa Cruz Mountains, but very rarely as low as Boulder Creek at around 500 feet, and even more rarely does it stick to the ground. So a dusting on the ridges or up toward the Highway 236 and Highway 9 high points a few times each winter is normal. Snow that reaches downtown and sticks is the rare event people remember and photograph.

It does happen. In February 2023, a cold storm dropped snow across the Santa Cruz Mountains, including the Boulder Creek area, with up to about 8 inches piling up at the highest peaks. It followed the region's first winter storm watch in more than a decade, which tells you how unusual a real snow event is here. Go back to February 2001 and there's a documented morning when heavy, wet snow fell on downtown Boulder Creek before dawn and built up until late morning, then melted off. Those are the kinds of events that punctuate the record. We're not going to put a frequency on town-level snow, because the honest answer is that it's rare and irregular, not something you can set a calendar by.

What it means for the roads

When snow and ice do show up, they mainly complicate the higher mountain highways, not downtown. In that February 2023 storm, both directions of Highway 17 over the summit shut down for a mix of downed trees, a jackknifed truck, and snow at the top. On Highways 9 and 236 the more common cold-weather hazard is ice on the shaded high stretches rather than accumulation, and in a genuinely cold storm Caltrans can post chain controls on the mountain routes. Chains are a rare need here, but not an impossible one, so if a cold system is in the forecast, check Caltrans road conditions or the Caltrans QuickMap before you drive, and carry chains if you're heading over a summit. Our Mountain Status page pulls the official road and weather sources together, and driving mountain roads covers the habits that keep you out of trouble in fog, rain, and the occasional ice.

Fog, heat, and the coastal difference

The quiet surprise for newcomers is how different Boulder Creek feels from Santa Cruz, 15 miles away. The town sits inland of the coastal fog belt, so summer days here often burn off sunny and warm while the coast stays gray and cool under the marine layer. That's the tradeoff people move up here for: more sun in summer, at the cost of colder winter nights, since the valley can pool cold air and hold morning fog once the rains come. Under the redwoods it's shadier and cooler than open ground any time of year, which is its own kind of air conditioning in August.

The practical takeaways are simple. Dress in layers you can shed as the day warms. Expect it to be cooler and wetter than the forecast for Santa Cruz, since most apps pull from the coast or from San Jose over the hill, neither of which is your weather. And for what's actually coming, watch the National Weather Service Bay Area office, which writes the forecasts and warnings for these mountains, rather than a generic weather icon.

Frequently asked questions

Does it snow in Boulder Creek?

Very rarely at town level. At about 500 feet, Boulder Creek sees snow that reaches the ground only once in a few years, and it usually melts fast. The higher ridges above the valley catch a light snow a few times most winters. Real town-level snow, like February 2023, is uncommon enough to make the local news.

How much rain does Boulder Creek get?

A lot by Bay Area standards. Estimates vary by source and location, generally in the 40-to-55-inch range a year for the town, versus about 30 inches on the Santa Cruz coast, with even more on the ridges. Wet and dry years swing hugely, from under 20 inches to over 100.

Do I need snow chains for Highway 9?

Almost never, but occasionally yes. Snow and ice are rare down in town, but the shaded high stretches of Highways 9 and 236 can ice up in a cold storm, and Caltrans can post chain controls on the mountain routes. Check Caltrans before a cold-weather drive and carry chains if you're crossing a summit.

Why is Boulder Creek warmer than Santa Cruz in summer?

Because it sits inland of the coastal fog. The marine layer that keeps the coast gray and cool often burns off, or never reaches, up the valley, so summer afternoons here run warmer and sunnier. The flip side is colder winter nights and morning fog in the valley.

What months are the rainy season?

Roughly November through April, with the wettest stretch in the heart of winter around January. Summers are nearly rainless. If you're planning outdoor projects or a visit, the dry window runs late spring through early fall.

Getting a mountain home ready for winter? Start with the winter storm checklist and the power outage plan. For live conditions any day of the year, check Mountain Status. Spot something here that needs updating? Send it to us.

downtown-boulder-creek-guide.title: Downtown Boulder Creek in an Afternoon
downtown-boulder-creek-guide.html:
An afternoon is about the right amount of time for downtown Boulder Creek. Browse the shops along Central Avenue, catch the free San Lorenzo Valley Museum between 1 and 4, put your feet in the river at Junction Park, and eat before you head home. What follows is what's actually open, laid out in the order an afternoon tends to take it.

One thing to know before you come up the hill. Boulder Creek is a working mountain town of homes, hardware, and groceries, not a resort built for visitors. That's a good part of why it's worth the trip, and it's also why hours shift with the seasons and staffing. If one particular stop matters to you, check its site or call before you drive.

Getting oriented

Downtown runs along Central Avenue, which is also State Route 9, so some addresses in this guide read "Highway 9" and others "Central Avenue." Same street. Everything sits between roughly 12550 and 13300, so once you've parked you can walk the whole strip. Plan to park once and stay on foot. Visit Santa Cruz County describes the row as "boomtown architecture that gives you the feeling that you've stepped back in time," which is a fair account of the view from the sidewalk. If the old storefronts hook you, our walking tour of historic downtown turns the same blocks into a self-guided history route.

The shops

Air & Fire, A Mystical Bazaar at 13150 Highway 9 is the shop people remember after they've gone home: handcrafted soaps and candles, incense, crystals, tarot decks, books, and jewelry, with work from more than 50 local artists. It also keeps the most generous hours on the strip, Tuesday through Sunday 11 to 6:30 and Monday 11 to 4, with seasonal variation.

SWAG at 13026 Highway 9 calls itself "the eclectic collective," and the inventory backs that up: restored midcentury stereos, vinyl, vintage and local apparel, jewelry, art, consignment pieces, and the occasional music event. It doesn't post hours online, so treat it as a happy discovery rather than a scheduled stop, or check before you go.

Viscosity at 13158 Central Avenue is a working blown-glass studio with a gallery of locally made pieces, plus classes if you want to try the torch yourself. Public hours are limited, with appointments available outside them, so look at the site if the glass is your main event.

lille aeske arthouse at 13160 Highway 9 is a small gallery, performance venue, and gathering space with a real 2026 calendar of shows and workshops. Its regular hours are short, Saturday and Sunday 11 to 3, but if your visit lines up with an evening performance, that's the most memorable way to spend time in town after dark.

Beck's Shoes at 12550 Highway 9, near the south end of the strip, is the outpost of a family shoe company that's been fitting feet since 1919. It runs a short week, currently Thursday through Sunday, so check the store page if you're coming early in the week.

Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 is the Ace Hardware affiliate that keeps the town running, open daily. A hardware store might seem like an odd visitor stop, but it's the fastest way to read what kind of town this is, and it's where you'll find the forgotten flashlight, rain shell, or barbecue tool.

Two smaller stops round out a browse. Ginia's Gems sells memory-wire jewelry with semiprecious stones alongside herbal remedies and local honey; its web presence is thin, so check the site for current details. And Hennig Studios & Gallery at 211 Grove Street, a ceramics studio and gallery, opens to the public the first Sunday of every month from noon to 5, by appointment otherwise. If your afternoon happens to fall on a first Sunday, take it.

The museum hour

Time your walk so you pass 12547 Highway 9, at the south end of town, between 1 and 4 on a Wednesday through Sunday. The San Lorenzo Valley Museum keeps its main gallery in a 1906 former church listed on the National Register of Historic Places, admission is free, and 2026 is its 50th anniversary year, with a retrospective exhibit up through late 2026. It's the best hour of context you can get on everything else you'll see. Our full museum guide covers the exhibits, the archives, and the second gallery in Felton.

The river

Boulder Creek's park district keeps Junction Park at the intersection of Middleton and Railroad Avenues, a block east of the highway, with a beach, river access, grass, and picnic tables. The district's own page calls the river here "a favorite swimming hole" in summer, and on a hot July day you'll see why. Use judgment, though: it's an unsupervised mountain river, conditions change, and there's no lifeguard. Dogs are welcome on leash. A couple hundred feet downstream, Barbara Day Park (locals say Dam Park) is getting improvements, and north of town Garrahan Park adds a walking track and play structures, sunrise to sunset.

The park sits on ground worth a second look while you're standing on it. This was Boulder Creek's railroad yard until 1934, which is why the streets around you are named Railroad, Junction, and Middleton. The walking tour tells that story properly.

Eating without a plan

You won't go hungry, and you don't need reservations anywhere downtown. The full picture is in our where to eat in Boulder Creek guide, but for an afternoon's purposes: Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9, the town grocery since 1938, makes deli sandwiches from family recipes and is open daily from 8 a.m. Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural grocery, open daily 9 to 9, with a deli kitchen for prepared food, and either one outfits a picnic for Junction Park. For something cooked outdoors, River Dogs does Chicago-style hot dogs from its stand on Forest Street, weather permitting. Sit-down options run from sushi to Italian; again, the eating guide has hours and the fine print.

If you have more time: Big Basin

The road to Big Basin Redwoods State Park leaves Highway 9 right downtown; turn onto Highway 236 and the entrance kiosk is nine miles up. Know before you go: the park is open 8 a.m. to sunset for day use only, all campgrounds are currently closed, and parking is limited, so State Parks recommends reserving a spot ($6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without a reservation, when space exists). The CZU Fire changed how the park looks, but most of the old-growth redwoods survived and the forest is visibly coming back. Check parks.ca.gov for current conditions before making the drive, and note that dogs are limited to the parking area and one paved road.

Closer to town, Big Basin Vineyards pours its mountain-grown wines at the estate on Memory Lane on Saturday and Sunday afternoons, noon to 5, with reservations through its site.

Visiting a small town well

A few habits make you the kind of visitor Boulder Creek is glad to see. Buy something where you browse, since these are single-location businesses, not chains. Don't block driveways or the highway shoulder when you park. Pack out what you bring to the river, keep dogs leashed in the parks, and take the posted hours in this guide as snapshots from July 2026 rather than promises; the town's businesses are small enough that one sick employee changes the day. When in doubt, call.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which day is most of downtown open?

Saturday gives you the widest overlap: the museum (1 to 4), Air & Fire, Beck's, Scarborough, both markets, plus the weekend-only spots like lille aeske, Viscosity's posted gallery hours, and the Big Basin Vineyards tasting room. Monday and Tuesday are the quiet days, with the museum closed and several shops dark.

Do I need a reservation for Big Basin?

For day-use parking, State Parks recommends one because spaces are limited, and camping isn't an option at all right now since the campgrounds are closed. Check parks.ca.gov for current conditions and reservation links before you drive up Highway 236.

Can you swim in the river downtown?

Junction Park has river and beach access, and the park district itself calls it a favorite summer swimming hole. There's no lifeguard and conditions vary with the season, so treat it like the wild river it is, watch kids closely, and keep dogs on leash.

How much time should I plan?

Three to four hours covers a browse of the shops, the museum hour, and food, without rushing. Add a half day if you're continuing to Big Basin, and check the park's reservation situation first.

Is downtown walkable?

Yes. The businesses in this guide sit between roughly 12550 and 13300 on Highway 9, which is Central Avenue through downtown (the same road), so it's a park-once town. The compact center is the reason an afternoon works.

driving-mountain-roads.title: How to Drive Mountain Roads: Deer, Fog, Downed Lines, and the Turnout Habit
driving-mountain-roads.html:
Driving well up here comes down to four habits: expect deer at dawn and dusk and brake instead of swerving, use the turnouts when you're the slow one, treat every downed line as live and stay in the car, and slow way down for fog with your low beams on. That's most of it. This article covers the hazards and techniques specific to mountain driving; for the lay of the roads themselves, who maintains what, parking, cyclists, and the Big Basin run, see our roads and parking notes, and for storm-season prep, the winter storm checklist. Live conditions always come from the official sources gathered on our Mountain Status page, never from a static article, and anything dangerous happening right now is a 911 call.

Deer: The Everyday Hazard

Forget the dramatic stuff; the animal most likely to dent your week is a deer at 6 a.m. on Highway 9. Caltrans' deer safety guidance leads with timing: "deer are most active at dusk and dawn: periods when your vision is most compromised." Around here that's the commute, both directions. Build the expectation into those drives, especially the stretches where forest runs right to the pavement, which is most of them.

The technique matters more than the worry. Caltrans' advice, condensed: slow down when you see a deer, because it's unpredictable. Expect company, deer rarely travel alone, so one crossing means more are likely behind it; wait a beat before rolling on. And if one steps out in front of you, "brake firmly and calmly, and stay in your lane." Swerving is how a deer strike becomes a tree strike or a head-on; Caltrans warns it can make you lose control and turn a bad situation much worse. A long blast of the horn, they note, can scare deer off the road. Your headlights, your speed, and your willingness to just brake are the whole toolkit.

The Turnout Habit

If you drive these roads slower than the traffic behind you, and everyone does sometimes, towing, sightseeing, new to the curves, the turnouts are yours to use, and not just as a courtesy. California Vehicle Code 21656 requires a slow-moving vehicle on a two-lane road where passing is unsafe, with five or more vehicles lined up behind, to pull into the nearest designated turnout or any safe turnout area and let them by. Five is when the law kicks in; good neighbors up here do it at two or three. Ten seconds in a turnout resets everyone's day, defuses the tailgater problem, and marks you as someone who gets it. Our roads and parking notes cover the turnout etiquette in more detail, along with the three-foot law for passing the cyclists you'll share every climb with.

Downed Lines: Stay in the Car

Storms drop trees, trees drop lines, and this is the piece of storm driving that isn't intuitive, so it gets its own section. PG&E's rule is absolute: always assume a fallen power line is live. Never touch one, never drive over one, and keep people and pets away.

If a line comes down on or near your car, the counterintuitive part begins: stay inside. The ground around the car may be energized, and stepping out can put your body between two voltages. PG&E's instructions: sound the horn, roll down your window, call 911, and warn anyone approaching to stay back, then wait until a firefighter, police officer, or PG&E worker tells you it's safe to exit. After 911, PG&E's line is 1-800-743-5000. The single exception is fire. If the car catches fire, remove loose clothing, jump clear so you're never touching the car and the ground at the same time, land with your feet together, and shuffle away keeping both feet close together on the ground.

That's worth rehearsing mentally precisely because every instinct says get out. On a stormy night up here, the car is the safest room you have.

Fog: Low Beams and Humility

Fog rolls up the valley and pools in the canyon dips, sometimes in bands a few hundred yards long, so you can drive from clear to blind in seconds. The National Weather Service's fog guidance starts simple: slow down, allow extra time, and put your low beams on, which also lights your taillights for the driver behind you. High beams are the classic mistake; the NWS notes they cause glare that makes it harder to see ahead, not easier. Leave generous following distance, since the car ahead may stop for something you can't see yet.

If it gets bad enough that you can't safely continue, don't creep along blind. The NWS procedure: hazard lights on, pull into a safe spot or well off the road, and once stopped, turn off all lights except the hazard flashers, set the emergency brake, and take your foot off the brake pedal so your brake lights don't lure a following driver into your bumper. That last detail is the one nobody knows and the one that prevents the chain collision.

Chains, Traction, and the Occasional White Morning

Snow is a rare visitor at Boulder Creek's elevation, more common up on Skyline and the Highway 9 summit, but when Caltrans posts chain controls anywhere you're driving, they're law, not advice. The definitions from Caltrans' chain control page: R-1 requires chains on all vehicles except passenger vehicles and light trucks under 6,000 pounds with snow tires on at least two drive wheels, and R-2 requires chains or traction devices on everything except four-wheel or all-wheel drives with snow-tread tires on all four wheels. Even exempt vehicles must carry chains or traction devices in the control area, and the speed limit when chains are required is 25 or 30 mph.

Two practical notes. Install chains only where you can pull completely off the roadway to the right; Caltrans is explicit that stopping in a traffic lane endangers you and blocks everyone. And check conditions before a cold-snap drive over the hill via Caltrans road information or 1-800-427-7623. Ice is the sneakier local threat: shaded curves and bridge decks stay frozen long after sunny stretches thaw, so a clear blue morning after a cold night deserves gentle inputs in the shade.

Storms, Water, and the Shortcut Trap

The storm-driving fundamentals live in two companion pieces, so here they are by reference rather than repetition. Never drive into water across the road, period; the reasoning and the numbers are in the winter storm checklist, along with how to check closures before you leave. And when Highway 9 does close, resist the map app's creative alternative. The side roads it offers are narrower and steeper than the highway, often single-lane, sometimes private or dead-end, and they shed the same trees and rocks with less room to escape and worse cell coverage when it goes wrong, as covered in our roads and parking notes. If the main road is closed in a storm, the move is usually coffee in town, not adventure routing. When conditions are changing, check Mountain Status for the live official sources before you commit to the drive.

No page can promise you a safe trip, and we won't. But these mountains reward drivers who are patient, lit, and predictable, and the habits above are what that looks like at the wheel.

Frequently Asked Questions

When am I legally required to pull over and let cars pass?

On a two-lane road where passing is unsafe, CVC 21656 requires you to use the nearest turnout or safe pull-off once five or more vehicles are lined up behind you. Local practice is to do it sooner. It takes seconds and it's the single most appreciated habit on Highway 9.

What do I actually do if a deer jumps out?

Brake firmly, stay in your lane, and don't swerve, per Caltrans; swerving trades a survivable impact for an unpredictable one. Then remember deer travel in groups, so proceed slowly. If people are hurt or the car is disabled in the roadway, call 911.

A power line is down across the road. Can I drive over it if it looks dead?

No. PG&E says to always assume a fallen line is live and never drive over one. Stop well back, call 911, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000. If a line contacts your car, stay inside, honk, and wait for responders; exit only for fire, jumping clear with feet together.

Do I really need chains in Boulder Creek?

Rarely in town, but carry them for cold-snap drives over Highway 9's summit or Skyline, because when chain controls are posted they apply to you, and even exempt 4WD vehicles must carry traction devices in the control area. Check Caltrans or 1-800-427-7623 before you go; icy shaded curves are the more common local reality.

What's the right way to drive fog on Highway 9?

Slow down, low beams on (never high beams), and leave extra following distance, per the National Weather Service. If visibility collapses, pull well off, hazards on, all other lights off, parking brake set, foot off the brake pedal. Fog pools in the canyon dips, so expect it to come and go along the same drive.

earthquakes-santa-cruz-mountains.title: Earthquakes in the Santa Cruz Mountains: A Boulder Creek Preparedness Guide
earthquakes-santa-cruz-mountains.html:
Ask anyone who was here for Loma Prieta and they'll tell you where they were standing. One of the most famous faults on Earth runs through these mountains, and the 1989 quake started about 15 miles east of here, across the ridge. No one, including the U.S. Geological Survey, can tell you when the next big one will hit, so the useful work isn't worrying about timing. It's the handful of things that make your household safer whenever it comes: secure the heavy stuff, store water and supplies, learn Drop, Cover, and Hold On, and sign up for alerts. Most of it takes a weekend, and none of it requires you to become a seismologist.

We live in earthquake country. That's not a reason to be scared. It's a reason to be ready, the same way we get ready for winter storms and fire season.

The San Andreas Fault Runs Through These Mountains

The San Andreas is not a distant California abstraction up here. The USGS describes the San Andreas Fault Zone as "the main part of the boundary between the Pacific tectonic plate on the west side and the North American plate on the east side." Those two plates grind past each other at a rate the USGS puts at "50 mm/yr (about 2 inches/yr)." That steady motion loads the fault with the strain that earthquakes eventually release.

The stretch nearest us has a name. The USGS calls it the Santa Cruz Mountains section, and says it "stretches 62 km (39 mi) from Los Gatos (near San Jose) to San Juan Bautista, CA and was last ruptured in the famous 1906 San Francisco earthquake." Boulder Creek sits in the mountains just west of that stretch of the fault. Other mapped faults are closer to home than the well-known Bay Area names: the San Gregorio fault runs offshore, following the coast, and the Zayante-Vergeles fault zone crosses the Santa Cruz Mountains. The Hayward and Calaveras faults run through the wider Bay Area too, which is part of why this region is studied as closely as it is.

One point is worth holding onto: proximity to a fault tells you an earthquake is possible, not that one is imminent. Nobody can predict earthquakes. For the specific hazard at your parcel, the authority is the USGS, not a blog and not a neighbor's hunch. Their earthquake hazards program publishes the maps and forecasts that professionals rely on, and it's the right place to send any question that starts with "how likely is."

What the 1989 Loma Prieta Earthquake Tells Us

Loma Prieta is local history, not ancient history, and it's the clearest picture we have of what a major quake does to this terrain. According to the USGS, "On October 17, 1989, at 5:04 PM (PDT), a magnitude 6.9 earthquake" struck the region. The USGS places the epicenter "near Loma Prieta peak in the Santa Cruz Mountains, approximately 9 miles northeast of Santa Cruz and 60 miles southeast of San Francisco." That peak is in our mountains, roughly 15 miles east of Boulder Creek, across the ridge.

The toll was serious. The USGS records "63 deaths, 3757 injuries, and an estimated $6 billion in property damage," and calls it "the strongest earthquake to hit the San Francisco Bay region since the M 7.9 Great San Francisco earthquake of 1906." Longtime residents remember the collapsed section of the Bay Bridge and the Cypress Street Viaduct in Oakland, but the shaking was felt hardest right here in the mountains, where roads cracked, chimneys came down, and homes shifted on their foundations.

The damage patterns were predictable, and most of them are the kind you can plan around: things falling, water and power cut off, roads blocked, and older houses moving because they weren't bolted down. Every item below addresses one of those.

Secure Your Space and Store Supplies

The Earthquake Country Alliance, a public-private partnership that includes the USGS and California's emergency management agencies, organizes what to do before, during, and after a quake into Seven Steps to Earthquake Safety. The first is "Secure your space." Walk your house and picture it shaking hard for fifteen seconds. What falls? A water heater that isn't strapped can topple, break a gas line, and cut off a big reservoir of drinkable water all at once. Tall bookcases and dressers can tip onto a bed or a doorway. Heavy mirrors and framed art over the couch become projectiles. Straps, flexible fasteners, and museum putty are cheap, and a Saturday spent anchoring the worst offenders is the highest-value hour in this whole guide.

The third step is to organize disaster supplies, and up here that starts with water. Ready.gov's standard is one gallon per person per day, and because mountain roads and utilities can stay down for days after a big quake, more is better. If you're on a well, the pump won't run without power, so stored water isn't optional. Keep a few days of non-perishable food, a manual can opener, flashlights, a battery or hand-crank radio, spare batteries, a first-aid kit, any critical medications, and sturdy shoes and work gloves next to each bed for the walk across broken glass in the dark. Our power outage plan for mountain households covers the water, food, and backup-power side in more depth, and most of it applies directly here.

Retrofit and Bracing: Route This to a Pro

Loma Prieta was hard on older houses that weren't fastened to their foundations, and a lot of Boulder Creek's housing stock predates modern seismic standards. A well-established fix exists. The California Residential Mitigation Program, a joint effort of the California Earthquake Authority and state emergency management, runs the Earthquake Brace + Bolt (EBB) program. It describes the retrofit plainly: "the foundation is 'bolted' to the frame of the house, and when there are walls called 'cripple walls' in the crawl space under the house, they are 'braced' with plywood," which helps "prevent the house from sliding or toppling off of its foundation during an earthquake."

The program is limited to "wood-framed, pre-1980 homes with a raised foundation," and when funds are available it offers "up to $3,000 toward a code-compliant seismic retrofit." Income-eligible homeowners may qualify for more.

Two conditions the program's summary pages tend to bury. First, the house also has to sit in a designated eligible ZIP Code. Good news for us: Boulder Creek's 95006 appears on CRMP's current eligible-ZIP list, which is posted with a December 2024 date, and Ben Lomond's 95005 is on it too. Second, registration is not open year-round. It opens for a limited period each year, and we could not verify a 2026 window, so treat the dates as unknown until you check. Check at earthquakebracebolt.com or call CRMP at (877) 232-4300 to find out whether registration is open and when the next window is.

Whether your specific house is a candidate, and how the work should be done, is a question for a licensed contractor or a structural engineer, not a DIY weekend and not this article. Check your eligibility with the program first, then get a professional's eyes on your crawl space. This is one of the few home projects that can change whether your house is livable after a major quake.

Learn Drop, Cover, and Hold On

When the ground moves, the goal is to avoid being knocked down and avoid being hit by what's falling. The Great ShakeOut, run by the same earthquake-safety partnership, gives the three steps. "DROP where you are, onto your hands and knees. This position protects you from being knocked down and reduces your chances of being hit by falling or flying objects." Then "COVER your head and neck with one arm and hand," crawling under a sturdy table if one is close or next to an interior wall if not. Finally, "HOLD ON until the shaking stops," gripping your shelter if you're under one and protecting your head and neck if you're not.

If you are in bed when it starts, stay there. Turn face down and cover your head and neck with a pillow.

Skip the old advice about doorways and running outside. Most injuries come from moving during the shaking. If you use a wheelchair, the guidance is to "LOCK your wheels (if applicable)," then cover your head and neck with your arms, a book, or a pillow, and hold on. Practice it once with the kids so the motion is automatic, because a real quake is not the moment to think it through.

After the Shaking Stops

The minutes after a quake are when a lot of the injuries happen. Work through these in order.

Expect aftershocks. They can come within seconds and can be strong enough to bring down what the first shock loosened. When one starts, Drop, Cover, and Hold On again.

Check yourself for injuries first, then the people with you. Put on the shoes and gloves you staged by the bed before you walk anywhere, because broken glass is the most common post-quake injury.

If you smell gas or propane, get everyone out of the building, then call 911 from outside. Do not flip light switches, use a phone, or start a vehicle inside or next to the building. Most of the San Lorenzo Valley runs on propane rather than PG&E natural gas, so the standard "shut off the gas at the meter" advice does not fit many homes here. If you are on propane, the shutoff is the valve on the tank itself, not a meter at the house. Find that valve now, while nothing is wrong, and know which system your house is on. If you do shut off gas or propane, leave it off and have the utility or your propane supplier turn it back on. On PG&E natural gas, call 911 first and then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000.

Do not call 911 for information, to ask what happened, or to report a power outage. Keep the line open for people who are hurt or trapped. Report an outage to PG&E at 1-800-743-5002 instead.

Check on neighbors once you are safe, especially older residents and anyone living alone. On these roads, a neighbor is going to reach you before a crew does.

Watch for CruzAware messages. Evacuation orders after a quake can follow from landslides, damaged roads, or fire, and the county sends them through that system. If the county tells you to leave, leave.

Sign Up for Earthquake and Emergency Alerts

Two different systems are worth setting up, and they do different jobs.

The first is earthquake early warning. MyShake is a free app built by UC Berkeley's Seismology Lab in partnership with "USGS ShakeAlert and the California Governor's Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES)." It delivers ShakeAlert-powered notifications and is available in the Apple App Store and on Google Play. The point of ShakeAlert is to give you a few seconds of warning before strong shaking arrives, enough time to Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Be clear about what it is and isn't: the USGS states plainly that ShakeAlert "is not earthquake prediction." It detects a quake that has already started and races the alert ahead of the shaking. Alerts can also arrive through your phone's Wireless Emergency Alerts and, on many Android phones, through the built-in Android Earthquake Alerts system.

The second is your county's emergency notifications. CruzAware is Santa Cruz County's alert and warning system, and it's how you'll hear about evacuations, road closures, and other emergencies that often follow a major quake. Register at CruzAware.org and choose how you want to be reached: text, phone, email, or app. While you're on the county's Plan and Prepare pages, learn your evacuation zone and grab the household safety checklist, since the same plan serves earthquakes, fires, and storms.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which faults are near Boulder Creek?

The San Andreas is the big one. The USGS identifies a "Santa Cruz Mountains section" running "62 km (39 mi) from Los Gatos (near San Jose) to San Juan Bautista," and Boulder Creek sits in the mountains just west of that stretch. The fault marks the boundary between the Pacific and North American plates. Other mapped faults nearby include the San Gregorio, offshore along the coast, and the Zayante-Vergeles fault zone. We are not going to quote you a distance from your driveway to a fault trace, because that depends on which mapped strand you measure to. For parcel-level answers, use the USGS Quaternary Fault and Fold Database and the California Geological Survey's EQ Zapp mapping tool, which show mapped faults and state-designated fault zones by address.

Was the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake near here?

Yes. The USGS places the magnitude 6.9 quake's epicenter "near Loma Prieta peak in the Santa Cruz Mountains, approximately 9 miles northeast of Santa Cruz," on October 17, 1989. It caused 63 deaths, thousands of injuries, and about $6 billion in damage, and the shaking hit the mountains hard.

Can anyone predict when the next big earthquake will happen?

No. The USGS is direct that its ShakeAlert system "is not earthquake prediction." No credible source can tell you the date of a future quake. Warning systems like ShakeAlert detect a quake that has already begun and send an alert seconds ahead of the shaking. That's why preparation, not prediction, is where your effort belongs.

What's the single most important thing to do first?

Two things, and both take minutes. Install MyShake and sign up for CruzAware so you get warnings and evacuation notices, then strap your water heater and anchor the tallest furniture in your house. After that, work on water storage and a family Drop, Cover, and Hold On practice.

Is my older Boulder Creek house safe in an earthquake?

That depends on your specific house, and it's a question for a professional. Many older homes on raised foundations can be strengthened by bolting the house to the foundation and bracing the cripple walls, work supported by the Earthquake Brace + Bolt program for qualifying pre-1980 homes. Eligibility is also tied to ZIP Code, and 95006 is on the program's current eligible list, dated December 2024. Registration opens only for a limited period each year, so check earthquakebracebolt.com or call CRMP at (877) 232-4300 to see whether it's open. Then have a licensed contractor or structural engineer assess your crawl space.

essential-shops-boulder-creek.title: The Essential Shops: What You Can Actually Buy in Town
essential-shops-boulder-creek.html:
Run a full Saturday of errands in Boulder Creek and you'll get further than most towns this size let you: hardware and lumber, two full grocery stores, an independent pharmacy that delivers across the valley for free, a full-service vet, and a deeper bench of gift and specialty shops than a drive-through suggests, all inside about a mile of Highway 9. What follows is a needs-based tour. Hours shift with the seasons and with who's staffing up here, so check before you make a special trip. For everything in town beyond the essentials, the full directory is the master list.

Hardware, lumber, and the endless mountain-house fix

Two related stores split the work. Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 takes the home-and-garden side: pet food, gardening supplies, canning gear, cleaning supplies, and the everyday household stuff. It's open seven days, Monday through Saturday 8 to 5 and Sunday 9 to 5.

For building projects, Scarborough Building Supply runs the yard at 12990 Highway 9 and stocks the building standards: lumber, paint, tools, and hardware. It's open weekdays 7:30 to 6 with shorter weekend hours. Between the two, most repair-and-project runs never need to leave the valley.

Groceries: two real markets, different personalities

Boulder Creek keeps two full grocery stores a couple of blocks apart, which is its own kind of small-town luxury.

Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9 has anchored the north end of downtown since 1938, and it's a complete market: produce sourced locally where possible, a meat and seafood counter, a deli making sandwiches daily, a bakery case, and a deep wine, spirits, and beer selection. It opens at 8 a.m. every day, running to 9 p.m. most nights and 10 on Friday and Saturday.

Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural-foods counterpart, locally owned since 1991, with organic produce, a deli and kitchen, and baked goods from its Sunflour Bakehouse. It's open daily 9 to 9. There's a second Wild Roots down the valley in Felton, but the Boulder Creek store is its own full market.

Between them, the grocery question in town isn't "can I get it here" so much as "which store carries my version of it."

Pharmacy: independent, and it delivers

Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue has served the town for over 40 years as a locally owned, family-operated pharmacy. Beyond prescriptions and over-the-counter basics, it handles medication synchronization and multi-dose packaging, and it offers free delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, which counts for a lot on this side of the hill when a car is in the shop or a storm has the roads a mess. Hours are Monday through Friday 9 to 6 and Saturday 10 to 4, closed Sunday. The phone is 831-338-2144.

Pets and gardens

There's no standalone pet store in town, but Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic at 12870 Highway 9 covers the animal side thoroughly: a full-service clinic with surgery, dental, boarding, exotic pet care, and an online pharmacy, open six days a week including two evenings. The phone is 831-338-7205.

For pet food and garden needs, Scarborough Home & Garden carries pet food, gardening, and outdoor supplies, and Good Vibes, covered below, leans into houseplants.

Gifts, style, and the specialty row

Downtown's specialty shops cluster along a few blocks of Highway 9, and they run more varied than a first drive-through suggests.

Air & Fire, the "mystical bazaar" at 13150 Highway 9, stocks handmade soaps, candles, incense, crystals, jewelry, tarot decks, books, and local art, and hosts classes and readings; it's open daily with seasonal hours posted on its site. SWAG at 13026 Highway 9 mixes restored mid-century stereos, vinyl, vintage apparel, and local art and jewelry inside an 1893 building the shop notes was the first US commercial building owned and operated by women; the business association lists it open Tuesday through Saturday afternoons. SWAG also carries select items from Stumptown Supply, a Boulder Creek-born online brand making apparel and gifts around San Lorenzo Valley history, so you can see the goods in person even though the brand itself has no storefront.

Ginia's Gems at 13133 Highway 9 sells handmade memory-wire jewelry with semi-precious stones plus herbal remedies and local honey; call ahead for current hours. Boulder Creek Antiques at Mac's Place, at 13164 Highway 9, deals in furniture, collectibles, and vintage finds, and the business association's current listing shows it open daily in the afternoon range. The same listing covers Wild Lilith, an independent boutique at 13120 Highway 9 with limited weekday hours, and Good Vibes at 13124 Highway 9, a newer shop carrying plants, clothing, and locally made goods.

And in a genuine small-town plot twist, there's a shoe store: Beck's Shoes runs a discount showroom at 12550 Highway 9 at the south end of town, open Thursday through Sunday, 10 to 6, selling premium work, outdoor, and casual footwear at reduced prices, in-store only.

Cannabis, for adults 21 and over

Boulder Creek also has a licensed adult-use dispensary. Creekside Wellness at 12603 Highway 9 has operated since 2018 under state license C10-0000008-LIC, open Monday through Saturday 10 to 7 and Sunday noon to 6. It's a 21-and-over business, ID required.

What this list is, and isn't

This is the errand-running core: the places that keep a household fed, medicated, repaired, and now and then gift-wrapped without a trip over the hill. It's a working list, not the whole town. Boulder Creek's cafes, restaurants, and bars get their own guide in where to eat in Boulder Creek, services from plumbers to salons live in the directory, and if you've just moved here, the new resident quick-start guide puts these stops in week-one order. Missed a shop, or spotted a detail that's changed? The directory page is the place to tell us.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a hardware store in Boulder Creek?

Yes, two related ones. Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 covers home, garden, pet, and household goods, open seven days a week, and Scarborough Building Supply at 12990 Highway 9 handles lumber, building supplies, paint, and tools.

Does Boulder Creek have a real grocery store?

It has two. Johnnie's Super Market, a full-line independent grocery in business since 1938, and Wild Roots Market, a natural-foods grocery with a deli and bakery, sit within a few blocks of each other downtown, both open daily.

Is there a pharmacy in town?

Yes. Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue is independent and family-operated, open weekdays and Saturdays, with free prescription delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley.

Where can I buy gifts locally?

The downtown blocks of Highway 9 hold most of it: Air & Fire for candles, crystals, and artisan goods, SWAG for vinyl, vintage, and local art, Ginia's Gems for handmade jewelry, Boulder Creek Antiques for vintage finds, and Wild Lilith and Good Vibes for boutique goods and plants. Hours vary by shop, so check before a special trip.

What will I still need to drive down the hill for?

Big-box selection, mostly. The town covers hardware, groceries, pharmacy, shoes, gifts, and pet care through the vet, but department-store shopping and specialty chains mean a run to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz. The full local list lives in our directory.

every-home-service-boulder-creek.title: Every Home Service You Need in the Mountains
every-home-service-boulder-creek.html:
When the water heater quits on a Sunday morning, the mountains ask you a question the flatlands don't: who do you actually call? The hardware store, the plumber, and the tree crew aren't around every corner the way they are down the hill. So owning a home up here means keeping a short list of people you can reach when something goes wrong, and this is that list, organized by the problem you're trying to solve rather than by trade name. Inclusion here isn't an endorsement. Where we could confirm a state contractor license, we've noted the number. That points to the one habit worth building first.

Check the license before you hire

For anything structural, electrical, plumbing, roofing, or $1,000 or more in combined labor and materials, California wants the contractor licensed. You can look up any license in about two minutes at the CSLB license lookup, which shows whether it's active, what classification it covers, and whether the bond and workers' comp are current. We verify licenses when we add a contractor, but statuses change, so confirm it yourself before money changes hands. For the bigger picture on vetting a contractor up here, see our guide on hiring a contractor in the Santa Cruz Mountains.

Water: plumbing, wells, and septic

Everyday plumbing has depth right in town. Apollo Plumbing (Boulder Creek) handles drains, pipes, fixtures, and water heaters. Travis Martin Plumbing (Boulder Creek, CSLB #1048423, C-36), which also does business as San Lorenzo Valley Water Heaters, covers drains, fixtures, water heaters, and filtration. Hwy 9 Plumbing (Boulder Creek, CSLB #1045811, C-36) does residential repairs, water heaters, and leak diagnosis; find its number in our directory. Down valley, Collins Coastal Plumbing (Ben Lomond, CSLB #1004212, C-36), Wade's, Inc. (Ben Lomond, CSLB #1087260), and J Fisher Plumbing (Felton, CSLB #985600) round out the options.

Wells and pumps are their own specialty. Tavares Plumbing and Pumps serves Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, and Felton with pump installation, tanks, and 24-hour emergency service. Maggiora Bros. Drilling (Watsonville) has drilled and serviced county wells since 1962; confirm mountain coverage for your specific job.

For septic, Affordable Septic & Excavating Services (Boulder Creek) advertises pumping, inspections, repairs, installations, and 24-hour service. New mountain homeowners should also read our septic basics primer before their first pump-out.

Power: electricians

Boulder Creek has a solid bench of licensed electricians. SE Electric (Boulder Creek) does residential and commercial installation, repair, and lighting. Big Basin Electric (Boulder Creek), Wilson Electric (Boulder Creek, CSLB #1058620, C-10), and PCIS (Boulder Creek, electrical and low-voltage, with 24/7 emergency service) are all in the directory. Down valley you'll find Jessi Phillips Electric (Ben Lomond, CSLB #980978, C-10) and SLV Electric Inc (Felton, CSLB #881810, C-10). Adding solar? Farinsky Energy Solutions (Ben Lomond, CSLB #1118587, C-46 and C-10) installs photovoltaic systems.

Build and remodel: general contractors

For a build, an addition, or a remodel, the valley's roster of licensed general contractors runs deep. In Boulder Creek: Geist Builders Group, Meadows Custom Builders, LK Construction and Management, Markey Construction (home builds, remodels, decks, fences, retaining walls, and concrete), New Solutions Construction, Kevin Foster Construction, JRG Construction, GNN Construction, Aaron Chase Construction, and Platinum Contracting Services. In Ben Lomond: Eric Martin Construction and Lotz Construction. In Felton: Dixon Construction and Oliveira & Sons. For cabinets and finish carpentry, Gary Fox Cabinets (Boulder Creek, CSLB #697984, B) is a licensed option. Phone numbers for the ones without a linked site are in the directory, and we have a dedicated roundup of valley-based general contractors coming as the hub series fills out.

One gap worth flagging: we have not verified a dedicated handyman service in Boulder Creek. For small odd jobs, many of the licensed builders above will take on minor work, or you can ask at the counter at Scarborough Building Supply for a referral.

Surfaces: paint, concrete, roofing, and floors

Painters are well covered. In Boulder Creek: Jeff Vedder Painting, Machado Bros. Painting (CSLB #1012390, C-33), and California Custom Painting and Design (CSLB #1019283, C-33), which also handles cabinetry and tile. In Felton, Fine Edge Painting (CSLB #1110102, C-33).

For concrete, Boulder Creek Concrete (Boulder Creek, CSLB #1142895, C-8) does flatwork, foundations, and retaining walls, and Terry Miller Concrete (Ben Lomond, CSLB #755895, C-8) is a long-established option.

Roofing is thinner. The one verified roofer serving the valley is Christopher A New Roofing (Ben Lomond, CSLB #668255, C-39); its number is in the directory. And flooring is a genuine gap: we have not verified a flooring contractor based in or serving Boulder Creek. If you install floors up here, please tell us so we can fill it.

Outside: landscaping, tree work, and gutters

For landscape design and installation, Baxter Landscaping, Inc. (Ben Lomond, CSLB #829811, C-27), Angus Hill Landscapes (Boulder Creek, CSLB #1127823, C-27), and Good Earth Landscape & Design (Ben Lomond, CSLB #874175, C-27) are licensed C-27 contractors. One thing to know going in: these are design-and-install firms. We have not yet verified a regular maintenance gardener, the mow-weed-and-haul kind of crew, based in the valley, and the demand for one is real. If you run that kind of route, let us know.

Tree work is the one trade Boulder Creek has in real depth, which makes sense in a redwood forest. Christianson Tree Experts, Stellar Tree Care, Sunderland Tree Service, and Travis Tree Professionals are all Boulder Creek based and cover pruning, removal, and defensible-space work. Before anyone climbs, read our questions to ask in tree work in Boulder Creek, including the county permit thresholds for large trees.

For gutters, Daniel's Cleaning Services (Seaside) cleans rain gutters, chimneys, and dryer vents across the county, including the San Lorenzo Valley; its number is in the directory.

Heat: HVAC, chimney, firewood, and propane

Two licensed HVAC contractors serve the area: Herranen Heating & Air (Ben Lomond, CSLB #814852, C-20) and Zephyr Climate Solutions (Felton, CSLB #1072237, C-20), which specializes in ducted and ductless mini-splits.

If you burn wood, get the chimney swept yearly. We have not verified a chimney sweep based inside the valley; the two verified options both come from outside it: Safeguard Chimney Sweep Company (Watsonville, operating since 1979) and Daniel's Cleaning Services (Seaside). For the wood itself, Cozy Firewood (Felton) delivers and stacks seasoned oak, almond, and eucalyptus. On propane, Mountain Propane Service (Felton) is the locally owned choice, and AmeriGas covers the 95006 area as a national provider.

Clean: house cleaners

Three cleaning services are based right in Boulder Creek: San Lorenzo Valley Housekeeping (operating since 2012), SCM Custom Cleaning, and Clean Mountain House Cleaning. Further out you'll find Clean Impressions LLC (Felton), Christi's Green Cleaning (Scotts Valley), and Santa Cruz Maids. Our full house cleaners guide has the details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which home services don't exist locally?

We don't yet have a dedicated handyman, a flooring contractor, a routine maintenance gardener, or a chimney sweep based inside the San Lorenzo Valley on this list. Chimney sweeps that serve the area come from Watsonville and Seaside, and licensed builders often take on the small jobs a handyman would. If you know of one we've missed, tell us.

How do I check that a contractor is licensed?

Use the CSLB license lookup at cslb.ca.gov. Enter the license number or business name and confirm the license is active, matches the classification for your work, and carries a current bond. We list the numbers we verified, but always confirm before you hire.

Do these companies actually come up the hill?

The ones listed as Boulder Creek based do, and each entry notes its home town and service area. For businesses based down valley or over the hill, especially wells and specialty trades, call and confirm they cover your exact address before scheduling. Access and road conditions are part of every mountain job.

Why isn't my go-to person on here?

Probably because we haven't verified them yet, not because anything is wrong. We only list businesses we can confirm directly, and the directory grows every week. Send us a name through the submit page and we'll check it out.

Inclusion isn't an endorsement, just verified facts to plan around. Full contact details for every trade above live in the business directory, current happenings are on the events calendar, and if a listing has changed or we're missing someone you'd call, tell us through the submit page.

family-day-trips-boulder-creek.title: Family Day Trips From Boulder Creek
family-day-trips-boulder-creek.html:
Boulder Creek is a good home base for a family day trip because most of the big draws sit within a short mountain drive: redwood trains in Felton, state parks in every direction, a zipline course, and the beach at the bottom of Highway 9. The short version is that the closest, most kid-proof options are down the valley toward Felton, the state parks all reward a check-first phone call or website visit before you commit, and every drive time below is an approximation because mountain roads, roadwork, and weather change the math constantly. Check Caltrans QuickMap and our Mountain Status page before you load the car, and treat each destination's official site as the source of truth for hours, fees, and closures.

Roaring Camp Railroads (Felton)

If you want the single most reliable family outing near Boulder Creek, Roaring Camp Railroads in Felton is it. It runs heritage steam and diesel trains through the redwoods and down to the Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk, and it's built around families, with a kids' club, birthday packages, and seasonal event trains. Plan for roughly a 15 to 20 minute drive south on Highway 9, keeping in mind that's an estimate. Ticketing, ride times, and event schedules change through the year and sell out on busy weekends, so book and confirm on Roaring Camp's own site before you go. We don't publish its prices or times here because they move; the official page is where to check.

Big Basin Redwoods State Park: check first

Big Basin Redwoods State Park is California's oldest state park and Boulder Creek's backyard, but it's still recovering from the 2020 CZU fire, and much of it remains closed. It's open for limited day use only, parking is first come, first served, and reservations are encouraged rather than required. As of July 16, 2026, State Parks listed day-use fees of $6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without a reservation, and no fee to arrive on foot, by bike, or by bus. There's no drinking water on site except at the kiosk, so pack your own. This is a real destination again, just a different one than the old photos show, with thinner shade and trails that open in phases. Confirm current access before you drive up. For the full planning picture, see our responsible Big Basin day trip guide.

Henry Cowell and Fall Creek (Felton)

Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park in Felton is the easier redwood walk for families, with a flat, stroller-friendly loop through old-growth trees near the main entrance, plus a quieter Fall Creek day-use unit a little further on with about twenty miles of trails and historic lime kilns. The Fall Creek trailhead and free parking are on Felton Empire Road, and that unit has no facilities and no dogs, bikes, or fires. State Parks runs seasonal junior ranger and interpretive programs at Henry Cowell, but the times aren't something we can pin down here because they change by season, so check the park's site or call ahead for the current schedule. Figure on about a 15 to 20 minute drive down Highway 9, roads permitting.

A beach day down Highway 9

The Santa Cruz beaches sit at the bottom of the valley, and the drive down Highway 9 through Felton and Ben Lomond is the reality to plan around: it's a winding, mostly two-lane mountain road, so give yourself a comfortable buffer rather than a tight door-to-door estimate. Plan for something like 35 to 50 minutes to the Santa Cruz waterfront depending on traffic and where you're headed, and expect summer weekend congestion both on the road and at beach parking. Save your directions before you drive, since cell service drops out along stretches of Highway 9. The Beach Boardwalk is also reachable as the end of the line on a Roaring Camp beach train, which turns the drive-and-park problem into part of the fun.

Mount Hermon Adventures zipline (Felton)

For older kids and teens with a taste for heights, Mount Hermon Adventures in Felton runs a redwood zipline and aerial adventure course. Courses have age, height, and weight requirements, reservations are typically needed, and availability is seasonal and weather-dependent, so this is one to book and confirm directly on their site rather than show up for. It's a short drive down the valley, in the same Felton cluster as Roaring Camp and Henry Cowell, which makes it easy to pair with another stop.

Castle Rock, for a bigger hike

If your family hikes and wants ridge-top views, Castle Rock State Park sits up on Highway 35 about two and a half miles southeast of the Highway 9 junction, with sandstone formations along Skyline. Parking is $10 a day at a self-serve kiosk, and the gate locks at sunset, so it's a daytime trip. Weekdays are quieter for parking. It's more of an outing for kids who can handle real trail miles than a toddler stop.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the best family day trip that isn't weather-dependent?

Roaring Camp Railroads in Felton is the most weather-proof and family-oriented option nearby, since the train rides run rain or shine on a set schedule and the site is built around kids. Confirm ride times and book on Roaring Camp's own site, because schedules and availability change seasonally.

Is Big Basin open for a family visit right now?

Big Basin is open for limited day use as it recovers from the 2020 fire, with much of the park still closed, first-come parking, and encouraged reservations. Facilities and water are limited. Always check the official State Parks access page before you drive up, because what's open changes.

How long is the drive to the beach from Boulder Creek?

Plan for roughly 35 to 50 minutes down Highway 9 to the Santa Cruz waterfront, but treat that as an approximation. Highway 9 is a winding two-lane mountain road, summer weekends get congested, and cell service is patchy, so save directions ahead and check QuickMap and Mountain Status before you go.

Are there redwood walks easy enough for little kids?

Yes. Henry Cowell in Felton has a flat, stroller-friendly loop through old-growth redwoods near the main entrance, which is gentler than most of Big Basin's current trails. Check the park site for seasonal junior ranger programs, and note the Fall Creek unit has no facilities and no dogs.

These are starting points, not guarantees; conditions and hours change, so each destination's official site rules. See more local ideas on our Visit and Around Town pages, and if something here is out of date, let us know at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

fire-insurance-santa-cruz-mountains.title: Fire Insurance in the Santa Cruz Mountains: How It Works and Where to Get Help
fire-insurance-santa-cruz-mountains.html:
The envelope from the insurance company is the one nobody up here wants to open, and plenty of neighbors have opened it. If you were non-renewed, or your premium jumped to a number that made you sit down, that experience is common in high-fire country and it isn't a verdict on how you keep your property. This guide covers why insurers pull back, what the California FAIR Plan is, why a FAIR Plan policy usually needs a companion policy, and how home hardening fits in. Read this as general information, not insurance advice. It cannot tell you what to buy or what your policy should cost. For that, talk to a licensed insurance agent or broker and to the California Department of Insurance, whose consumer hotline is 800-927-4357.

Why Insurers Are Pulling Back in High-Fire Areas

Santa Cruz County is officially in the high-fire conversation. In its January 2025 fact sheet Summary on Residential Insurance Policies and the FAIR Plan, the California Department of Insurance lists Santa Cruz among the counties "at or above the 50th percentile of structures at high fire risk." That designation sits behind everything a mountain homeowner runs into at renewal time.

The department's data shows the strain. In that same January 2025 fact sheet, working from 2023 data, it found that the FAIR Plan, the state's fallback fire insurer, grew "from 1.6% of the residential market to 3.7% of the residential market" through 2023, noting the increase is "mostly occurring in counties that have more structures at high wildfire risk." More homeowners in places like ours were being pushed out of the regular market and onto the fallback. The FAIR Plan has kept growing since. Its own reporting puts it at 684,388 policies in force and roughly $750 billion in exposure as of March 2026.

The cause and effect is messier than it looks, though, and that matters when you're trying to read your own situation. In the January 2025 fact sheet, again using 2023 data, the department reports that most non-renewals are routine: "75-80% of non-renewals are initiated by policyholders," for instance when they buy or sell a home or switch companies, while the remaining "20-25%" are initiated by insurers for reasons that include wildfire risk. The department also notes that non-renewals fluctuate year to year, dropping after 2019 "before climbing again in 2023 as some larger insurers began to reduce their presence in California." Coverage is tighter in high-fire zones. But no one can tell you why a particular insurer made a particular decision. If you were non-renewed, the reason is specific to your insurer and your policy, and you're entitled to ask them for it in writing.

Those percentages are a snapshot of 2023, published in January 2025. They describe the shape of the problem, not the state of the market this month. Your own address is the only number that decides your renewal.

The Market Is Moving in Both Directions Now

The pullback is real, and it isn't the whole story anymore. Under the Department of Insurance's Sustainable Insurance Strategy, insurers may use catastrophe models and the net cost of reinsurance when they file for rates, which they could not do before, but only if they commit to writing at least 85% of their statewide market share in wildfire-distressed areas. The trade is explicit: better rate math in exchange for writing business in places like the San Lorenzo Valley.

The department reported in July 2026 that rate filings are surging and that pending filings are at their lowest level since 2014. What that means for you is practical, and it's the single most useful thing on this page. Re-shop the admitted market before you settle for the FAIR Plan, because a carrier that had nothing for your address a year ago may have something now. And if you're already on the FAIR Plan, re-shop every year anyway. Nobody will call to tell you the market reopened. None of this guarantees a carrier will write your house. It means the door is worth pushing on more than once.

If You Just Got a Non-Renewal Notice

First, don't let your coverage lapse while you sort it out. A lapse can make you look riskier to the next insurer and can violate your mortgage terms.

Second, check whether you're protected by California's wildfire non-renewal moratorium. The law behind it is California Insurance Code section 675.1, and it's worth knowing by name, because you can cite it to an insurer. After a governor declares a wildfire emergency, that statute can freeze non-renewals in the affected area. In a 2025 example, the Department of Insurance explained that homeowners in and around the ZIP codes covered by a declared wildfire emergency get at least one year of protection from the date of the Governor's emergency declaration, whether or not they suffered a loss, and that homeowners who suffered a total loss get up to 24 months. The protection is tied to specific ZIP codes and a specific declaration. Whether it covers you is a fact to confirm on the department's mandatory one-year moratorium page, which lists the active moratoriums and the ZIP codes each one covers, not an assumption to make.

Third, start shopping early with a licensed agent or broker who knows the mountains. Some brokers specialize in high-fire properties and can reach carriers you won't find on your own. If the regular market has nothing for you, that's where the FAIR Plan comes in.

The California FAIR Plan: The Insurer of Last Resort

The California FAIR Plan is not a state agency and it's not a charity. It's an insurance pool that exists as a backstop. In its own words, it "was established so that all California property owners have access to basic fire insurance when access to coverage in the traditional market is not available." It's blunt about its role: "The California FAIR Plan is an insurer of last resort. If you cannot obtain coverage in the traditional insurance market, the California FAIR Plan may be a temporary solution."

Two things to understand. It's meant to be temporary, a place to hold coverage while you keep looking for a regular policy, not a permanent home. And the FAIR Plan's basic policy covers fire and a short list of related perils. It is not a full homeowners policy. That gap is the reason the next section exists.

There's also a ceiling. The FAIR Plan's dwelling policy currently caps coverage at $3 million. If what it would actually cost to rebuild your house exceeds that number, the FAIR Plan on its own cannot cover the rebuild, and that's a conversation to have with a broker before you need it, not after. Mountain construction costs are not what they were, and a house that seemed comfortably under the cap a few years ago may not be.

One more thing on the horizon. News outlets have reported that the Department of Insurance approved an average dwelling rate increase of about 29.1% for the FAIR Plan, taking effect October 15, 2026, on new and renewal business, with wildfire-exposed properties potentially seeing more than the average and some properties seeing decreases. We have not found that figure in a department press release or on the FAIR Plan's own site, so treat it as reporting rather than an official announcement, and treat the "average" as an average. What it should change is your timing: ask your broker what the FAIR Plan would actually cost for your address today and what they expect after October, and get it in writing. Confirm current pricing with your broker before you budget around any number in a news story, including this one.

Why a FAIR Plan Policy Usually Isn't Enough on Its Own

A standard homeowners policy bundles a lot together: fire, yes, but also water damage, theft, and personal liability if someone is hurt on your property. The FAIR Plan's basic policy leaves those out. To rebuild something close to full coverage, homeowners pair a FAIR Plan policy with a second policy called a Difference in Conditions, or DIC, policy.

The FAIR Plan explains the split clearly: "DIC policies provide coverages that are not available through the California FAIR Plan, such as water damage, theft and liability coverage," and they're "designed to combine with a California FAIR Plan policy to provide coverage similar to that in a comprehensive homeowner's policy." One catch that surprises people: "The California FAIR Plan does not offer DIC policies." You buy the wrap separately, through a licensed agent or broker, and it's a separate contract with its own carrier and its own deductible.

This is where a lot of mountain homeowners get exposed. In its January 2025 fact sheet, using 2023 data, the Department of Insurance counted the policies rather than the households: for every two FAIR Plan policies, the state counts roughly one DIC policy, meaning "many homeowners with FAIR Plan policies do not have an accompanying DIC policy." If you go the FAIR Plan route, treat the DIC wrap as part of the plan, not an optional extra, and ask your agent to quote both together so you can see the full picture.

You may hear that this two-policy structure is on its way out. Be careful with that. A California Court of Appeal ruled in December 2025 that the Insurance Commissioner lacked the authority to order the FAIR Plan to write full HO-3 homeowners policies. In February 2026, legislators announced AB 1680, the "Make It FAIR Act," which would require the FAIR Plan to offer comprehensive homeowners coverage and would remove the need for a separate DIC policy. It has not passed, and until it does the rules are the rules as they stand today. Plan around today's rules, and buy the wrap.

Home Hardening and Defensible Space Can Help

You can't move your house out of the mountains, but you can make it measurably more resistant to fire, and California rules now tie that work to insurance. Under the Department of Insurance's Safer from Wildfires framework, "Every action under Safer from Wildfires will qualify you for an insurance discount. By doing more, you can save more."

The qualifying actions run from small to significant: a five-foot ember-resistant zone right around the house (the department describes "removing greenery and replacing wood chips with stone or decomposed granite 5 feet around your home"), a Class-A fire-rated roof, ember- and fire-resistant vents, and defensible space that follows state and local law by "trimming trees and removal of brush and debris from yard." Community-wide efforts count too, including recognized Firewise USA and Fire Risk Reduction Communities.

The tree and brush side of this is a big job in a redwood town, and it's worth doing right. Our guide to tree work in Boulder Creek walks through permits, licensing, and how defensible space actually works so you clear the right things without over-cutting, and our tree services directory lists local companies to start from. One limit to be clear about: discounts vary by insurer and by the specifics of your property, and no one can promise a particular saving or that hardening alone will get you renewed. It improves your odds and can lower your bill. That's the accurate claim.

Questions to Ask a Licensed Agent

Specific questions get you further than "why is this so expensive." A few worth writing down:

  • Am I currently protected by any wildfire non-renewal moratorium, and when does it end?
  • Have you checked every admitted carrier and the surplus-lines market for my address, or just a couple?
  • Which carriers have started writing again in my area since the state's new rate rules took effect, and can we re-check next renewal?
  • What would it cost to rebuild my house today, and does that number sit above or below the FAIR Plan's $3 million dwelling cap?
  • If the FAIR Plan is my option, can you quote the FAIR Plan policy and a DIC wrap together, with both deductibles spelled out?
  • Which Safer from Wildfires actions would lower my premium, and by roughly how much for my property?
  • What exactly is and isn't covered, especially for water damage, theft, and liability?
  • Is my dwelling coverage limit enough to actually rebuild at today's mountain construction costs?

Where to Get Help

Two places to start. A licensed insurance agent or broker can shop the market and build the FAIR Plan and DIC combination if it comes to that, though a broker may have nothing to sell you on a given day, and that isn't the end of the search. And the California Department of Insurance helps consumers directly: its hotline is 800-927-4357, and its staff can explain your rights, tell you whether a moratorium covers your ZIP code, and take a complaint if an insurer isn't following the law.

Once more, because it matters: nothing here is insurance, legal, or financial advice, and nothing here promises you a price or a policy. It's a map of how the system works so you can walk into those conversations knowing the terms. The decisions belong to you and the licensed professionals you hire.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the California FAIR Plan?

It's the state's insurer of last resort for basic fire coverage. The FAIR Plan describes itself as a pool that gives property owners "access to basic fire insurance when access to coverage in the traditional market is not available," and calls itself "a temporary solution" for those who can't get a regular policy. It is not a full homeowners policy on its own.

Why do I need a second policy with the FAIR Plan?

Because the FAIR Plan covers fire and related perils but leaves out things a standard policy includes. A Difference in Conditions (DIC) "wrap" policy adds them back. The FAIR Plan says DIC policies cover "water damage, theft and liability coverage" and are "designed to combine with a California FAIR Plan policy." You buy the DIC separately through an agent, since the FAIR Plan doesn't sell it.

My insurer non-renewed me after a nearby fire. Is that allowed?

It depends. California Insurance Code section 675.1 creates a non-renewal moratorium that can protect homeowners "living within or adjacent to a declared wildfire emergency," for at least one year from the Governor's emergency declaration. The department's moratorium page lists the active declarations and the ZIP codes each covers. Whether it applies to your address and dates is specific. Confirm with the California Department of Insurance at 800-927-4357 before assuming either way.

Will clearing brush and hardening my home lower my premium?

It can, though results vary. Under the state's Safer from Wildfires framework, "Every action under Safer from Wildfires will qualify you for an insurance discount." The size of the discount depends on your insurer and your property, so no one can promise a specific number. Ask your agent which actions apply to you and keep records of the work.

Who do I call if I can't find any coverage?

Start with a licensed insurance agent or broker who works high-fire properties, and ask them to re-check the admitted market before defaulting to the FAIR Plan, since some carriers have been filing to write again in wildfire-distressed areas. If the regular market has nothing, ask them about a FAIR Plan policy plus a DIC wrap. For your rights and for help understanding your options, call the California Department of Insurance consumer hotline at 800-927-4357.

Are FAIR Plan rates going up?
