News outlets have reported an approved average dwelling rate increase of about 29.1% effective October 15, 2026, on new and renewal business, with wildfire-exposed properties potentially seeing more than the average and some seeing decreases. That figure comes from reporting, not from a department press release or the FAIR Plan's own site, and an average is not a quote. Ask your broker what the FAIR Plan costs for your address now and what they expect after October.

free-resources-boulder-creek.title: Free Resources in Boulder Creek
free-resources-boulder-creek.html:
Boulder Creek isn't a big town, but there's a real network of free help here if you know where to look. Whether you're new, between paychecks, or just don't want to drive down the hill for something you can get for free up here, this is the running list. Everything below is free, and we note the source and the last time we checked it so you can trust it.

The Boulder Creek Branch Library

A free library card is one of the best deals in town. The Boulder Creek Branch Library is at 13390 West Park Avenue (831-427-7703), open Monday through Thursday 10am to 6pm, Friday 11am to 5pm, and Saturday 10am to 5pm.

A card gets you a lot more than books:

  • Books, movies, audiobooks, and music, plus free wifi, public computers, and wireless printing.
  • Discover & Go: free and discounted passes to museums and parks around the region.
  • The Library of Things: borrowable objects and equipment, not just media.
  • The Felton Seed Library: free garden seeds you can take and grow, which is handy in a valley full of gardeners.
  • Story time, book groups, and craft and STEAM programs across all the branches.

Free Food Fridays

Every Friday, free food is given out to anyone who wants it, no questions asked. It runs from 3pm until the food is gone, rain or shine, in front of the open lot next to Jenna Sue's Cafe downtown. If it's raining, the giveaway moves to Junction Park.

It's genuinely for everyone. There's no proof of income needed and no sign-up. Come and take what you need. Donations are welcome but never required. The food is leftover produce and groceries from the Grey Bears Healthy Food program, distributed here through the Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District, a local nonprofit, thanks to a crew of volunteers who set it up every week.

Valley Churches United Missions food pantry (in Ben Lomond)

A few miles down Highway 9, Valley Churches United Missions (VCUM) runs a food pantry for San Lorenzo Valley residents at 9400 Highway 9 in Ben Lomond (831-336-8258). Enrolled households can pick up groceries every other week: staples like pasta, cereal, canned goods, and meat, plus fresh milk, eggs, and produce. Pantry hours are Tuesday through Thursday 9am to noon, with a seniors-only window on the first and third Fridays from 9 to 11am. VCUM also helps with clothing and seasonal programs, so it's worth a call if you need more than food.

The BCRPD Community Tool Shed

Born after the CZU fire, the BCRPD Community Tool Shed lends out tools for free, everything from shovels and rakes to log splitters and cement mixers, so you don't have to buy a tool you'll use once. It's run out of the Bear Creek Recreation Center.

Heads up: as of mid-2026, the Rec District's own page says the shed's weekly rentals are closing in August 2026. If you're reading this after then, check the BCRPD site or email bctoolshed@gmail.com for the current status before you head over.

Little Free Libraries around town

Boulder Creek has a good scattering of Little Free Libraries, the little take-a-book, leave-a-book boxes you'll find around town. Here's where to look:

  • Garrahan Park
  • The Boulder Creek Rec Center, near the swing set
  • Along Highway 9 northbound, just south of Bear Creek Road
  • A bookshelf inside the Treehouse Cafe
  • At Joe's Bar (note: Joe's is a 21-and-over bar)
  • Sunshine Lane, in the alley between Redwood and Hazel avenues
  • In front of Camp Joy

More of these are dotted through the rest of the valley in Ben Lomond and Felton, too.

A few more free things

  • Buy Nothing Boulder Creek: a local Facebook group where neighbors give away and ask for free stuff, from furniture to garden starts. Part of the Buy Nothing Project.
  • The BCRPD Foundation community potluck: a free, everyone-welcome community meal on the second Sunday of the month, 4 to 7pm. It's outdoors at Garrahan Park (15501 Highway 9) in the warm months and moves indoors to the Bear Creek Recreation Center (15685 Forest Hill Drive) from October through March. Bring a dish to share, and canned goods for the food drive if you can.
  • Junction Park and the town's other parks are open to everyone at no cost, and the park is also the rainy-day home for Free Food Fridays.
  • Free wifi is available at the library during open hours.

If you need a little more help

Most county services are down the hill in Santa Cruz, but a few are worth knowing about up here, and some come to the valley or work over the phone:

  • 211 Santa Cruz County: dial 211 or search online for help with food, housing, benefits, and just about anything else. It's the single best starting point.
  • Second Harvest Food Bank: runs free grocery distributions across the county. Find the nearest one at thefoodbank.org or call 831-662-0991.
  • Meals on Wheels: hot weekday lunches for adults 60 and older at the Highlands Park Senior Center on Highway 9 in Ben Lomond, plus delivery for homebound seniors. Call 831-464-3180.
  • Grey Bears: county residents 55 and older can get a free weekly grocery bag. Call 831-479-1055.
  • CalFresh (SNAP): monthly grocery money if you qualify. Apply at BenefitsCal.com or call 888-421-8080.

Ways to give back

If you're on the other side of it and want to help, the Buy Nothing group is the easiest place to pass things on, Free Food Fridays and VCUM both take donations, and Grey Bears will pick up extra food or glean fruit from your trees (danielle@greybears.org).

Know a free resource we missed? Tell us at hello@bouldercreeklocal.com and we'll add it.

fun-things-boulder-creek.title: Fun Things to Do in and Around Boulder Creek
fun-things-boulder-creek.html:
There's more to do in and around Boulder Creek than a town this size has any right to offer, as long as you know where to look and check a few things before you go. This is a roundup organized by mood, from a lazy afternoon by the river to a night of live music, pulled from our verified directory and the local pages we keep current. Access up here changes with the weather and the seasons, so confirm hours and conditions with each place the day you head out. None of this is a ranking, and inclusion isn't an endorsement.

On the water

When it's warm, the San Lorenzo River is the center of gravity. Junction Park, a block off the main street at Middleton Avenue and Railroad Avenue, has a small beach, river access, a lawn, and picnic tables, kept up by the Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District. It's a popular summer swim spot, and it's swim at your own risk, so watch your footing on the rocks and keep an eye on kids near moving water. Just downstream sits Barbara Day Park, a small park at the old dam, and up Highway 9 you'll find Garrahan Park, with a walking track, fields, shade, and barbecue areas.

For a pool instead of a river, the rec district runs Bear Creek Pool on Forest Hill Drive seasonally, and community swim nights turn up on the events calendar through the summer. Check the rec district for the current pool schedule, since it changes with the season.

In the trees

The redwoods are the reason a lot of people come, and three parks anchor the options. Access at all of them keeps shifting after the 2020 CZU fire, so confirm each one on its official page before you drive up.

Big Basin Redwoods State Park, California's oldest state park, is open for limited day-use as it recovers, and much of it is still closed. Parking is limited and first come, first served, with reservations encouraged but not required. As of July 16, 2026, State Parks listed day-use fees of $6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without a reservation, and no fee to arrive on foot, by bike, or by bus. There's no drinking water on site except at the kiosk. Confirm the current status at State Parks before you count on it. We also have a full guide to a responsible Big Basin day trip.

Castle Rock State Park, up on Skyline about two and a half miles southeast of the Highway 9 junction, has sandstone formations and ridge-top views. Parking runs $10 a day at a self-serve kiosk, and the gate locks at sunset; weekdays are quieter. Fall Creek, a day-use unit of Henry Cowell, offers about twenty miles of quiet trails through redwoods and old lime kilns, with a free trailhead on Felton Empire Road off Highway 9. There are no facilities there, and dogs, bikes, and fires are not allowed. Details for both are at State Parks.

Around town

Downtown is walkable and easy to loop on foot. The main stretch of Highway 9 rewards a slow lap: Air & Fire: A Mystical Bazaar for crystals, jewelry, and gifts, SWAG for vintage apparel, vinyl, and local art, Viscosity Glass for locally made glass art, and Beck's Shoes for footwear on its weekends-only schedule. Scarborough Home & Garden covers hardware, plants, and a little of everything. Many keep limited days, so check posted hours or the directory before a special trip.

For art and history, the San Lorenzo Valley Museum presents valley history at its Grace Episcopal Gallery on Highway 9, with the office open Wednesday through Saturday afternoons; call or email for an appointment. Galleries and studios like Hennig Studios (ceramics) and lille aeske arthouse add to the scene, most by appointment or on limited days. And on any given week there's more happening than a list can hold: the Boulder Creek Farmers Market runs every Thursday from 1:30 to 5:30 p.m. at Junction Park with local vendors, farms, and music, and the events calendar tracks live music, library programs, First Fridays downtown, and seasonal festivals.

For the active

If you'd rather be doing than watching, Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club is a public course tucked into the redwoods off Big Basin Highway, with a clubhouse and event facilities; call ahead for tee times, since we don't publish course hours. For a class instead, Mountain Sangha offers community yoga and movement right on Highway 9 in town. Looking for a full gym or a martial arts studio? Most of those are down valley in Felton and Scotts Valley, and we've compared the closest gyms to Boulder Creek so you can pick the drive that fits.

In the evening

Boulder Creek keeps a real nightlife for its size. Joe's Bar downtown hosts live bands most Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday nights, with karaoke on Tuesdays and a Thursday pool tournament; it's a 21-and-over room. Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge pairs a historic Italian dining room with a lounge, and The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse up Highway 9 has craft beer, pub fare, outdoor seating, and a music stage. For wine, Big Basin Vineyards pours estate tastings at its Boulder Creek vineyard on weekends from noon to 5 p.m. For the full rundown, including live-music venues down valley, see our guide to a night out in and around Boulder Creek.

Make a day of it

You don't have to pick just one. A classic Boulder Creek day stacks a few of these: coffee downtown, a morning on the trails or the golf course, lunch on the main drag, a slow lap through the shops, and dinner or live music to close it out. Our Visit page lays out a choose-your-own perfect day with several options at each stop, and it's built for exactly this kind of mixing and matching.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Big Basin open right now?

Partly. Big Basin is open for limited day-use as it recovers from the 2020 CZU fire, and large portions remain closed. Parking is first come, first served and reservations are encouraged. Always confirm the current status at State Parks before you drive up, because access changes.

Can you swim in the river at Junction Park?

People do swim there in summer, and it's swim at your own risk. There are no lifeguards, currents and depths change with the season, and you should watch footing on the rocks and keep close watch on children near the water.

What's the one thing happening every week?

The Boulder Creek Farmers Market at Junction Park, every Thursday from 1:30 to 5:30 p.m., with local vendors, farms, music, and activities. Only certified service dogs are allowed at the park during the market. Everything else, from live music to library programs, is on our events calendar.

Where can I hear live music?

Joe's Bar downtown has bands most Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday nights (21 and over), the Sawmill has a music stage, and SWAG runs a summer concert series. The events calendar lists specific acts and dates, and our night out guide covers the venues.

None of this is a ranking, and inclusion isn't an endorsement, just verified places to plan around. Full details and hours for every spot are in the directory, what's happening this week is on the events calendar, and if we've missed a place or something has changed, tell us through the submit page.

gardeners-yard-help-boulder-creek.title: Finding a Gardener or Yard Help in Boulder Creek
gardeners-yard-help-boulder-creek.html:
Before you call anyone about your yard, figure out what you're actually asking for, because "gardener" and "landscaper" are two different trades and you hire them two different ways. For ongoing upkeep, the mowing, weeding, pruning, and hauling, you want a maintenance gardener, and several serve the valley. For a bigger project, redesigning a yard, building a patio, installing irrigation from scratch, or grading, you want a licensed landscape contractor. Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a starting point, and because most of these are home-based or mobile operations, we list the community and phone number, not a home address. A quick call is how you'd sort out fit and scheduling anyway.

Maintenance gardeners versus landscape contractors

Here's the distinction that saves confusion. A maintenance gardener keeps an existing yard healthy and tidy: mowing, weeding, pruning shrubs and small trees, seasonal cleanups, and hauling away green waste. In California, routine gardening and maintenance can be done without a contractor's license as long as the jobs stay under the state's threshold and don't involve the kind of structural or installation work that requires one. A landscape contractor, licensed by the state under the C-27 classification, does the bigger stuff: design and installation, hardscaping, retaining walls, drainage and grading, and irrigation systems. If your project involves building, installing, or moving earth at any real scale, that's licensed-contractor territory, and you can verify any license at the Contractors State License Board. Below, we've split the local options along exactly that line.

Maintenance gardeners and yard help

These are the crews and independent gardeners for ongoing care and cleanups.

Simple Wonders Landscape and Design (831-333-6709) is a Boulder Creek yard-maintenance and design service run by Ariana and Shawn, covering garden maintenance and design work from Boulder Creek down to Santa Cruz, with free consultations. Greenman Gardening (831-566-6045) is a San Lorenzo Valley gardening service with more than 20 years of local experience, offering ornamental tree pruning and irrigation installation and maintenance; it comes owner-recommended, and details are best confirmed by phone. Silverfern Gardens (831-239-8221), run by Aileen Addleman, is a local garden-care business that came recommended through community referrals; call or text to talk through your garden. Dragonfly Landscape Services (831-227-3848) is a permaculture-certified garden and landscape service, also owner-recommended from community referrals, with details to confirm by phone. And Hannah Soriano Gardens (303-882-8161) is a landscape gardener with an agroecology degree and an edible-landscaping background; she's reachable by text or on Instagram.

A note on the "owner-recommended" framing: where a listing says that, it means the business surfaced through neighbor referrals in the community rather than a formal vetting of work quality. It's a starting point for your own conversation, not a guarantee. Get a clear scope and price before any work begins.

Landscape design and build (licensed contractors)

For projects rather than upkeep, these are licensed C-27 landscape contractors serving the valley. Baxter Landscaping, Inc. (831-246-0537, CSLB #829811) is a Ben Lomond contractor handling residential and commercial landscape installation and grounds work. Good Earth Landscape and Design (831-359-6211, CSLB #874175) is a Ben Lomond landscape design and installation contractor. Angus Hill Landscapes (650-799-0477, CSLB #1127823) is a Boulder Creek landscaping contractor. All three hold active C-27 licenses, which you can confirm any time at the state license board. For a bigger build, a written contract, a license check, and a couple of references are the basics worth doing before you commit.

Yard help and defensible space

A lot of yard work up here is really fire-safety work. Clearing brush, cutting back weeds and grass, and creating defensible space around your home is exactly the kind of ongoing job a maintenance gardener can take on. Where it crosses into cutting down trees, removing large limbs, or working near power lines, that's a job for a tree service, not a gardener. We cover that separately in Tree Services in Boulder Creek, and the wider fire-readiness picture, including what defensible space means for a mountain parcel, lives on our Residents page. Matching the right trade to the job keeps you safer and usually cheaper.

The lay of the land

Boulder Creek is decently covered for both kinds of help. The maintenance-gardener bench is mostly small, independent, home-based operations, which means you'll do your scheduling by phone or text and should confirm availability rather than expect a storefront. The licensed landscape-contractor options for design-and-build projects sit mostly just down the valley in Ben Lomond. If you can't reach anyone or you have a specialized job, the directory has the full current list, and the county and state license lookups are always there to verify anyone you're considering.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a licensed contractor to mow and weed my yard?

No. Routine gardening and maintenance, like mowing, weeding, pruning, and cleanups, can generally be done by a maintenance gardener without a contractor's license, as long as the work stays under California's threshold and doesn't involve structural or installation work. Bigger installation, hardscaping, grading, or irrigation-system jobs call for a licensed C-27 landscape contractor.

What's the difference between a gardener and a landscaper here?

A maintenance gardener keeps an existing yard healthy: mowing, weeding, pruning, and hauling. A landscape contractor designs and builds: patios, walls, drainage, grading, and full irrigation installs, and holds a state C-27 license. Match the person to the job, and verify any license at the Contractors State License Board.

Can a gardener help with fire defensible space?

Yes, for the brush-and-weed side of it. Clearing grass, cutting back brush, and maintaining defensible space around a home is standard maintenance-gardener work. Cutting down trees or removing large limbs, especially near power lines, is a job for a licensed tree service instead. See our tree services and Residents guides for the fire-readiness details.

How do I check that a contractor is licensed?

Use the free license lookup at the Contractors State License Board (cslb.ca.gov). Each licensed contractor above lists its CSLB number, and you can confirm the license is active and in the right classification before you sign anything.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a starting point. See the full directory for contact details, and if a listing has changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit so the next neighbor gets it right. For the full rundown of trades, see Every Home Service You Need in the Mountains.

general-contractors-boulder-creek.title: General Contractors Based in the Valley
general-contractors-boulder-creek.html:
A builder who's already patched a washed-out driveway in February knows things a resume can't teach. If you're planning a build, remodel, or addition and want a general contractor who lives with these slopes, driveways, and winters, the San Lorenzo Valley has a deep local roster. There are fifteen general building contractors based in Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, or Felton, and most carry an active California "B" General Building license we've noted below. This page is the local roster. For how to vet whichever one you call, read its companion, our guide on hiring a contractor in the Santa Cruz Mountains, which walks through licenses, bids, deposits, and permits in detail. These are grouped by town, nothing more, and a listing here isn't an endorsement. Run the checks regardless of who you contact.

A quick word on the license numbers. Where our data has a contractor's CSLB number, we list it so you can confirm it yourself. A license number in a directory is a convenience, not a substitute for the free, real-time CSLB license lookup, which shows current status, classifications, bond, workers' compensation, and any complaint disclosure. Always look it up before you sign or hand over a deposit.

Boulder Creek

Geist Builders Group at 13119 Highway 9 (831-218-5730) is a licensed general contractor doing residential construction, remodeling, and project services, open Monday to Friday, 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. It's one of the few on this list with a listed street address in town.

LK Construction and Management, Inc. (831-901-1030, CSLB #1010899, active B) handles ground-up builds, remodels, and tenant improvements, and adds construction-management services.

Meadows Custom Builders (408-489-8145, CSLB #1077781, active B) focuses on custom home construction, renovations, and additions.

Markey Construction (831-359-1634) is a licensed general contractor for home builds, remodels, decks, fences, retaining walls, and concrete work. On valley lots, retaining walls and concrete carry a lot of the load.

New Solutions Construction (831-291-0560, CSLB #700075, active B), Kevin Foster Construction (831-588-2055, CSLB #936857, active B), JRG Construction (831-345-2917, CSLB #967855, active B), GNN Construction (831-247-9771, CSLB #997768, active B), Aaron Chase Construction (831-566-1336, CSLB #1063144, active B), and Platinum Contracting Services (650-732-2168, CSLB #1037868, active B) are all Boulder Creek general building contractors offering residential construction and remodeling. VSG Builder (831-818-5733, CSLB #1141184, active B), run by Victor Soto, adds framing and concrete work including pumping. These work from the field rather than a storefront, so contact runs by phone, and each is in our directory.

Ben Lomond

Eric Martin Construction, Inc. (408-768-3905, CSLB #981846, active B) is a Ben Lomond general building contractor for residential construction and remodeling. Lotz Construction (831-252-3767, CSLB #865919, active B) is a second Ben Lomond general building contractor covering the same residential work.

Felton

Dixon Construction (831-335-3162, CSLB #635141, active B) is a Felton general building contractor handling remodels, additions, decks, and new construction across the valley. Oliveira & Sons (831-239-9655, CSLB #940607, active B) is a second Felton general building contractor for residential construction and remodeling.

What This Roster Covers, and What It Doesn't

Every contractor above holds a general building classification, the right license for whole-house work: builds, additions, remodels, and the structural pieces around them. That's a deep bench for a small mountain community, and it's an advantage. A builder who already knows valley soils, steep access, septic setbacks, and storm-season scheduling starts ahead of one who doesn't.

Two gaps to plan around. Dedicated local roofing contractors are thin in our verified data right now, and reroofs on mountain homes are their own specialty, so you may end up sourcing a roofer from over the hill or asking one of these general builders who they subcontract to. And we don't currently have a verified valley-based flooring contractor, so plan to bring one in from the wider county for flooring-specific jobs. Several of the general contractors above take on remodels that include roofing and flooring scopes, so ask directly whether the job you have fits under their license and their crew, or whether they'll subcontract it.

Also worth remembering: the general building license is not the license for every job. Septic work is a C-42 classification, wells are C-57, and tree work is C-49, as we cover in the hiring-a-contractor guide and the tree-work questions guide. If a bid's scope doesn't match the license's classification, that's your cue to keep asking.

Before You Hire Any of Them

Being valley-based is a real plus, but it doesn't replace the checks. Look up each contractor's license in the CSLB license lookup and confirm status, classification, and workers' compensation. Get at least three written bids on identical plans. Never put down more than $1,000 or 10 percent of the contract price, whichever is less. And ask the questions that reveal mountain experience, about your septic system, slope drainage, well or district water, and winter scheduling. The full checklist, including how Santa Cruz County permits work on unincorporated parcels, is in our hiring-a-contractor guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many general contractors are based in the San Lorenzo Valley?

There are fifteen general building contractors based in Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, or Felton, most with an active California "B" license. That's the roster on this page. New businesses appear and licenses change, so confirm any contractor's current status in the CSLB lookup before hiring.

What license should a general contractor have for my remodel or addition?

For whole-house work like remodels, additions, and new builds, the right classification is a "B" General Building license, which every contractor on this page holds. Specialty scopes carry their own classifications, such as C-42 for septic, C-57 for wells, C-39 for roofing, and C-49 for tree work. Check that the license classification matches the job in the CSLB lookup.

Are there local roofers and flooring contractors in the valley?

Dedicated valley-based roofing contractors are thin in our verified data, and we don't currently have a verified local flooring specialist, so those jobs may mean bringing someone in from the wider county or asking one of the general contractors here who they use. Several of these general builders take on remodels that include roofing and flooring scopes; ask directly whether your job fits their license and crew.

How do I check a contractor's license and deposit rules?

Use the free CSLB license lookup to confirm status, classifications, bond, and workers' compensation. On a California home improvement contract, the down payment is capped at $1,000 or 10 percent of the price, whichever is less, and later payments can't run ahead of the work done. A request for a large sum up front is a serious red flag. Our hiring-a-contractor guide covers the money and permit rules in full.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a verified place to start. Browse the full directory for local trades, read the hiring-a-contractor guide before you sign anything, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so the next neighbor gets accurate information.

gyms-near-boulder-creek.title: The Closest Gyms to Boulder Creek, Compared
gyms-near-boulder-creek.html:
You can walk to a yoga class in Boulder Creek. You cannot walk to a squat rack. There's no full gym in town, so a weight room, machines, group classes, or a pool means a drive down Highway 9, and the closest of those is about fifteen minutes out in Felton, with a bigger cluster around twenty-five minutes on in Scotts Valley. What follows is what exists and roughly how far it is, grouped by drive time from downtown Boulder Creek. Those drive times bend with traffic, weather, and whatever mood Highway 9 is in, so treat them as ballpark, not a schedule.

In town: Mountain Sangha (Boulder Creek)

Address: 12788 Highway 9, Suite 1, Boulder Creek. Phone: 831-217-3018. Hours: Monday through Friday 6am to 7:30pm, Saturday and Sunday 8am to 1pm; the class schedule controls access.

Mountain Sangha is the one fitness business in Boulder Creek itself, a community yoga and movement studio with regular classes, workshops, and wellness programs. It isn't a gym in the weights-and-machines sense, so set expectations accordingly. For yoga, mobility, and a practice you can keep up without starting the car, it's the only walk-to option in the valley. Access follows the posted class schedule, so check what's on before you go.

About 15 minutes: Felton

SCM Community Fitness (Felton)

Address: 6500 Highway 9, Felton. Phone: 831-334-0264. Hours: Monday through Friday 6am to 6:30pm, Saturday 9am to 11am, closed Sunday.

SCM Community Fitness is the closest thing to a true gym to Boulder Creek, a functional-fitness gym running CrossFit-style group classes that combine strength training, conditioning, and mobility work, plus yoga sessions. It's coached group training rather than an open floor you wander into alone, which suits people who want structure and someone counting on them to show up. At roughly fifteen minutes down Highway 9, it's the nearest strength-and-conditioning option.

San Lorenzo Valley Martial Arts (Felton)

Address: 6116 Highway 9, Suite 2B, Felton. Phone: 831-704-7924. Hours: Monday through Friday 2pm to 7:30pm (Wednesday opens 1pm), Saturday 9:30am to noon, closed Sunday.

If your idea of a workout involves a heavy bag, San Lorenzo Valley Martial Arts offers Tae Kwon Do for all ages, plus cardio kickboxing, Tabata bootcamp, and after-school programs. That mix works for families as well as adults who want a conditioning class that isn't a standard gym. The schedule leans afternoon and evening, so check the class times against your own.

Oakstrong Martial Arts (Felton)

Address: 6191 Highway 9, at the Felton Community Hall. Website: oakstrong.net. Hours: Tuesday and Thursday 5:30pm to 7pm, Saturday 10am to 11:30am.

Oakstrong Martial Arts is a Tang Soo Do karate school teaching self-defense and weapons classes for teens and adults out of the Felton Community Hall. It runs a limited weekly class schedule rather than open hours, so think of it as a set of standing classes to join, not a drop-in facility. For adults who want a disciplined martial-arts track close to home, it's the nearest of its kind.

About 25 minutes: Scotts Valley

Scotts Valley Gym (Scotts Valley)

Address: 105 Whispering Pines Drive, Scotts Valley. Phone: 831-332-5555. Hours: 24/7 member access, with staffed hours posted on site.

Scotts Valley Gym is a strength and powerlifting gym with Rogue equipment, personal training, and small-group coaching, open around the clock to members. If you want a serious weight room and the freedom to train at 5am or 10pm, this is the closest 24/7 access to Boulder Creek. The tradeoff is the drive, roughly twenty-five minutes each way.

Toadal Fitness Scotts Valley (Scotts Valley)

Address: 269 Mount Hermon Road, Scotts Valley. Phone: 831-430-9200. Hours: Monday through Friday 6am to 8pm, Saturday and Sunday 8am to 4pm.

Toadal Fitness is a family-oriented full-service health club with group classes and a supervised kids area for ages 3 months to 12 years. For parents, that childcare is the whole argument, since it's rare this close to the valley. Otherwise it's a broad general-membership club, covering most everyday fitness under one roof.

MADabolic Scotts Valley (Scotts Valley)

Address: 262 Mount Hermon Road, Suite 101, Scotts Valley. Phone: 831-202-5234. Website: madabolic.com.

MADabolic is a strength-driven interval training studio built around structured work-to-rest ratios, and it's noted as the franchise's first California location. Like SCM Community Fitness, it's coached class training rather than an open gym floor, aimed at people who want the session programmed for them. We don't have its current class hours confirmed, so check the website or call before planning a visit.

Enterprise Sports Club (Scotts Valley)

Address: 100 Enterprise Way, Suite G100, Scotts Valley. Phone: 831-920-0912. Hours: Monday through Friday 5:30am to 9pm, Saturday and Sunday 7am to 8pm.

Enterprise Sports Club is the largest facility on this list, a 26,000-square-foot athletic club with exercise equipment, group fitness classes, personal training, a heated outdoor pool, a spa and sauna, and tennis, pickleball, and basketball courts. It was a private employee club before opening to the public in 2020. If you want the most under one roof and don't mind the drive, this is the full-service end of the spectrum.

How to think about the drive

A real gym means leaving Boulder Creek. There's no arranging around that. For the shortest trip and a strength-and-conditioning focus, Felton's SCM Community Fitness is the closest bet at about fifteen minutes. For a traditional weight room, 24/7 access, childcare, or a pool, the Scotts Valley cluster at roughly twenty-five minutes covers every style. And if you'd rather not drive at all, Mountain Sangha keeps a yoga practice available in town. The right pick is whichever one you'll actually use often enough to justify the miles.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a gym in Boulder Creek?

Not a full gym. Mountain Sangha, a yoga and movement studio, is the only fitness business in Boulder Creek proper. For weights, machines, group classes, or a pool, the nearest options are in Felton, about fifteen minutes away, and Scotts Valley, roughly twenty-five minutes.

What's the closest gym to Boulder Creek?

SCM Community Fitness in Felton, at about a fifteen-minute drive down Highway 9. It's a functional-fitness gym with coached CrossFit-style classes rather than an open-floor gym, so it suits people who want structured group training.

Which nearby gym has childcare or a pool?

For childcare, Toadal Fitness in Scotts Valley has a supervised kids area for ages 3 months to 12 years. For a pool, Enterprise Sports Club in Scotts Valley has a heated outdoor pool along with a spa, sauna, and courts. Both are about a twenty-five-minute drive from downtown Boulder Creek.

Are the drive times exact?

No, they're approximate. Fifteen minutes to Felton and twenty-five to Scotts Valley are typical estimates from downtown Boulder Creek under normal conditions, but Highway 9 traffic, weather, and road work all change that. Give yourself margin, especially in winter.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a verified place to start. See the full directory for contact details, and send corrections or new listings through the submit page so this stays accurate for the next neighbor.

halloween-downtown-boulder-creek.title: Downtown Boulder Creek Trick-or-Treat
halloween-downtown-boulder-creek.html:
On Halloween evening the sidewalk along Highway 9 fills up with costumed kids, costumed parents, and a good number of costumed dogs. Downtown Boulder Creek turns its main street into the trick-or-treat route: merchants hand out candy in the early evening, usually from about 5 to 7 p.m., and volunteers help families cross the highway. The Boulder Creek Business Association and the town's merchants run it, and the reason it exists is a mountain one. Homes up here sit too far apart for door-to-door, so the town brings the candy to one walkable strip instead. This page holds the shape of the evening and points you to the organizers for each year's confirmed details.

This year (2026)

Halloween 2026 falls on Saturday, October 31. In past years the downtown trick-or-treat has run from about 5 to 7 p.m. along Highway 9, with the Boulder Creek Business Association and downtown merchants hosting.

The exact hours, participating businesses, and any after-event activities are usually confirmed closer to the date. Watch the Boulder Creek Business Association for this year's confirmed times and details.

What to expect

The evening keeps a simple shape. Downtown businesses along Highway 9 prop their doors open and hand out candy, and families work the strip storefront by storefront. Over 20 downtown merchants have taken part in recent years, and the Press Banner has named stops like The Adventure Gift Store, Air and Fire, Boulder Creek Outfitters, Treasures from the Attic, Viscosity, lille aeske, and the Springy Girl among them. That roster shifts year to year, so treat those as examples rather than a guaranteed list.

The crowd is wider than the kids. Adults dress up, dogs get dressed up, and some years there are activities afterward at the Boulder Creek Recreation Hall behind the firehouse. The whole thing is compact and walkable, which is the point.

Crossing Highway 9

The trick-or-treat happens right along Highway 9, in the early evening as the light goes. Traffic keeps moving through downtown. Cross at the crossing points, keep younger kids by the hand, and give yourself extra time as it gets dark. For years the Valley Women's Club of San Lorenzo Valley has stationed volunteer crossing guards to help families across Highway 9. They help, but they're not a substitute for watching your own group, and drivers should slow down and expect kids on the street. If there's ever a real emergency, call 911.

History

The downtown trick-or-treat has a clear reason to exist. As one merchant told the Press Banner, "in the mountains, homes are spaced too far apart, and kids are not safe to go trick-or-treating," so the merchants gather the candy into one downtown strip instead. It's become an annual event run by the Business Association and the town's shops.

The safety side of it has a history of its own. The Valley Women's Club says it has been "conjuring safety and smiles in downtown Boulder Creek since 1985," standing at the Highway 9 crossings so generations of kids could get across. We haven't found a verified first year for the trick-or-treat itself, and we're not going to guess one, but the crossing-guard tradition alone goes back four decades.

Frequently asked questions

When is the Boulder Creek downtown trick-or-treat?

It's on Halloween, October 31, every year. In past years it has run from about 5 to 7 p.m. For the coming year's confirmed hours, check the Boulder Creek Business Association.

Where is it?

Along Highway 9 in downtown Boulder Creek. Downtown merchants hand out candy from their storefronts, and families walk the strip.

Is it safe to cross Highway 9 with kids?

The event runs on a working highway in the evening, so treat it with care: cross at the crossing points, hold little ones' hands, and go slow as it gets dark. The Valley Women's Club has provided volunteer crossing guards since 1985 to help families across Highway 9, and drivers should expect kids on the street. Watch your own group, and call 911 for any emergency.

Which businesses hand out candy?

Over 20 downtown businesses have taken part in recent years, and the list changes annually. Past coverage has named The Adventure Gift Store, Air and Fire, Boulder Creek Outfitters, Treasures from the Attic, Viscosity, lille aeske, and the Springy Girl, among others.

Why not trick-or-treat around the neighborhood?

Mountain homes are spread far apart, which makes door-to-door trick-or-treating hard and, as local merchants put it, less safe. The downtown event gathers the candy into one walkable place so families can do it together.

See what else is happening around town on the Boulder Creek Local events page. Hosting something, or spot a detail here that needs fixing? Send it to us. For this event's official source, check the Boulder Creek Business Association.

healthcare-near-boulder-creek.title: Healthcare Near Boulder Creek: Pharmacy, Urgent Care, and the Nearest ER
healthcare-near-boulder-creek.html:
The plain reality first: there is no hospital, emergency room, or urgent care inside the San Lorenzo Valley. Boulder Creek has a pharmacy in town and a community primary-care clinic one town south in Ben Lomond, but for a walk-in urgent care you're driving to Scotts Valley, and for a true emergency room you're driving to Santa Cruz. If someone's having a real emergency, call 911 and let them come to you. Don't drive yourself over Highway 9 and Highway 17. This guide doesn't diagnose anything or promise that any provider can see you; it just tells you plainly where care is and how far. Hours and details change, so treat each link as the source of truth.

Call 911 first for a real emergency

Some things don't wait for a drive to Santa Cruz. Call 911 right away for chest pain or pressure, signs of a stroke (face drooping, arm weakness, or slurred and difficult speech), severe or uncontrolled bleeding, or trouble breathing. Up here, where the nearest ER is a good half hour away on a clear day, that call matters more than it did wherever you lived before. Paramedics can start care at your door and get you to the right hospital faster than you can, and they can radio ahead. Don't talk yourself out of it, and don't get behind the wheel with a sick passenger if it's serious.

For a possible poisoning, an accidental overdose, or a "what did the toddler just swallow" moment, the California Poison Control System is free, staffed by pharmacists and physicians, and open 24 hours a day at 1-800-222-1222. It's worth putting in your phone right now.

The pharmacy in town

You don't have to leave Boulder Creek to fill a prescription. Boulder Creek Pharmacy is an independent pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue, open weekdays 9 a.m. to 6 p.m. and Saturday 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., closed Sunday. The number is 831-338-2144.

The part that actually matters when you live up a long driveway: they deliver free throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, and they've extended complimentary delivery to Felton and Scotts Valley for former Rite Aid customers. For anyone who'd rather not drive down the hill sick, or who's caring for someone who can't, a local pharmacy that brings the medicine to you is a genuine asset.

Primary care in the valley

For the ordinary stuff, checkups, a nagging cough, managing an ongoing condition, there's a community clinic without leaving the valley. Santa Cruz Mountain Health Center, run by the nonprofit Santa Cruz Community Health, sits at 231 Main Street, Suite A, in Ben Lomond, a few minutes south on Highway 9. It's open weekdays 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (closed 11 to noon for lunch), with Saturday morning hours offered at the group's Live Oak location. The number is 831-427-3500.

They handle primary care for adults and kids, prenatal care, and behavioral health, and they accept Medi-Cal, Medicare, and most commercial plans, with a sliding scale for people without insurance. It runs by appointment, so call to book rather than dropping in. One important thing to be clear about: this is a primary-care clinic, not an urgent care and not an emergency room. It's the right call for a scheduled visit or an ongoing health need, not for a middle-of-the-night crisis.

The nearest urgent care

When it's more than a phone call to the doctor but less than a 911 emergency, a sprain, a deep cut that might need stitches, a fever that won't quit, you're driving down the hill for a walk-in urgent care. There isn't one in the valley, so plan on a longer drive than you might expect. Sutter Health closed its Scotts Valley walk-in urgent care in 2021, but two others in Santa Cruz are open to the general public.

Doctors on Duty is at 615 Ocean Street, open weekdays 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. and weekends 8 a.m. to 2 p.m., at 831-425-7991. Sutter's Westside Center urgent care is at 1301 Mission Street, open weekdays 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. and weekends 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., at 831-458-6310, which makes it the later option if something flares up in the evening. Either is roughly a thirty-minute drive down Highway 9 and Highway 17. Call before you go, especially late in the day, since hours shift.

If you're a Kaiser Permanente member, Kaiser runs urgent care closer, at its Scotts Valley Medical Offices, 5615 Scotts Valley Drive (2nd floor), about twenty minutes down Highway 9, at 831-430-2700, with a 24-hour advice line for members at 831-430-2740. Kaiser members can walk in without an appointment, and it's open weekdays 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. and weekends and holidays 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. Hours can change, so confirm through Kaiser before heading over.

The nearest emergency room

For anything that needs a real emergency room, broken bones, deep wounds, severe pain, a bad reaction, chest or breathing trouble that a paramedic tells you to bring in, the closest ER is in Santa Cruz. Dignity Health's Dominican Hospital has an emergency room at 1555 Soquel Drive that's open 24 hours a day, every day, with specially trained teams for stroke and heart care. The hospital's number is 831-462-7700. From Boulder Creek, plan on thirty to forty minutes down Highway 9 and Highway 17, longer in traffic or bad weather.

There's a second 24-hour ER farther south at Watsonville Community Hospital, 75 Nielson Street, at 831-724-4741. It's a longer drive, closer to an hour, so for most Boulder Creek residents Dominican is the practical choice. Either way, if it's a genuine emergency, the fastest route to an ER is often the ambulance, not your own car. Call 911.

Telehealth and the nurse-line question

There's no telehealth service that belongs to the valley, but you may already have one and not know it. Most health plans include a nurse advice line and video visits, which are the fastest way to get a real answer at 10 p.m. without a drive. Kaiser members have that 24-hour advice line at 831-430-2740. Sutter and PAMF patients can do video visits through My Health Online. Check your insurance card and your plan's app; the option is usually there, and it can save you a trip down the hill or tell you plainly when it's time to make one.

The bottom line on distance

None of this is meant to scare anyone off mountain living. It's just the trade you make for the redwoods: care is real, but it's down the hill. Keep the pharmacy's number and the poison line in your phone, know that walk-in urgent care and the ER are both down in Santa Cruz (Kaiser members have urgent care closer, in Scotts Valley), and keep a small first-aid kit and any critical medications stocked at home so a minor thing doesn't become a midnight drive. For the serious stuff, 911 is always the right first move.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there an urgent care in Boulder Creek?

No. There's no urgent care, emergency room, or hospital anywhere in the San Lorenzo Valley. The nearest walk-in urgent cares open to everyone are Doctors on Duty (615 Ocean Street) and Sutter's Westside Center (1301 Mission Street), both in Santa Cruz, about thirty minutes down the hill; Kaiser members have one closer, in Scotts Valley. The nearest 24-hour ER is Dominican Hospital in Santa Cruz.

Where's the closest emergency room to Boulder Creek?

Dignity Health's Dominican Hospital at 1555 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz has a 24-hour ER, roughly thirty to forty minutes away. Watsonville Community Hospital also has a 24-hour ER, but it's a longer drive. For a true emergency, call 911 rather than driving yourself.

Does the Boulder Creek pharmacy deliver?

Yes. Boulder Creek Pharmacy, at 13081 Central Avenue, delivers free throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, and it has extended free delivery to Felton and Scotts Valley for former Rite Aid customers. Call 831-338-2144.

Is there a doctor's office in the valley?

Yes. Santa Cruz Mountain Health Center in Ben Lomond offers primary care for adults and children, takes Medi-Cal and most insurance, and has a sliding scale for the uninsured. It's appointment-based at 831-427-3500. It's a primary-care clinic, not an urgent care or ER.

What should I do for a poisoning or if my kid swallows something?

Call the California Poison Control System at 1-800-222-1222. It's free, open 24 hours, and staffed by pharmacists and physicians. If the person is unconscious, not breathing, or having a seizure, call 911 first.

hiring-contractor-santa-cruz-mountains.title: Hiring a Contractor in the Santa Cruz Mountains: What's Different Up Here
hiring-contractor-santa-cruz-mountains.html:
Here's the short version. Before you hire anyone for work on a mountain property, check their license for free at the Contractors State License Board license lookup, get at least three written bids, never put down more than $1,000 or 10 percent of the contract price (whichever is less), and ask the questions that reveal whether they've actually worked in the Santa Cruz Mountains before. A contractor who's brilliant in a Willow Glen subdivision can get in real trouble on a Boulder Creek parcel with a septic system, a steep driveway, and a creek at the bottom of the yard. This isn't legal advice, just a neighbor's checklist for asking better questions before you sign anything.

Start With the License, Every Time

California requires a contractor license for any job where labor and materials add up to $1,000 or more. That threshold went up from $500 on January 1, 2025, under AB 2622, and even the under-$1,000 exemption only holds if the work needs no permit and the person doing it hires no helpers. In practice, almost anything beyond a small handyman job requires a licensed contractor.

Checking a license takes about two minutes. The CSLB license lookup lets you search by license number, business name, or a person's name, and it shows license status, classifications, bond information, workers' compensation coverage, and complaint disclosure. Every contractor ad in California is supposed to carry the license number, so ask for it up front and look it up before anyone sets foot on your property. When they show up to bid, CSLB recommends asking to see their pocket license along with a photo ID, so you know the person in your driveway is the person on the license.

While you're in the lookup, check two insurance items. Workers' compensation matters because if an uninsured worker gets hurt on your property, you can end up holding the problem. And ask for a certificate of commercial general liability insurance, ideally sent directly from the insurer, for everything else that can go wrong.

The License Classes That Matter Up Here

CSLB issues dozens of license classifications, and mountain projects touch more of them than flatland ones do. The ones you'll run into most around Boulder Creek:

A "B" General Building contractor handles whole-house projects like remodels and additions. An "A" General Engineering contractor takes on the heavy site work that mountain lots often need. C-12 Earthwork and Paving covers grading and driveways, which on our slopes is its own specialty. C-42 Sanitation System is the classification for septic installation and repair. C-57 Well Drilling covers wells, C-39 covers roofing, and C-49 Tree and Palm covers tree work, which is regulated enough here that we wrote a separate guide to tree work questions.

The point isn't to memorize codes. It's to notice when the classification doesn't match the job. If someone bidding your drainfield repair holds only a landscaping license, that's your cue to keep looking. The lookup shows every classification a license carries.

Money Rules That Protect You

California's home improvement contract rules are unusually protective, and they exist because people get burned. Per CSLB's contract guidance, the down payment on a home improvement contract can't be more than $1,000 or 10 percent of the contract price, whichever is less. After that, payments can't run ahead of the work actually performed. A written contract is required for home improvement projects over $500, and any changes need a written change order signed by both sides before the new work starts.

So when someone asks for half up front "to buy materials," that's not a negotiating position, it's a violation, and it's the single most common way people lose money. Get at least three written bids on identical plans and specifications, and be suspicious of a bid that comes in far below the others. Up here that often means the bidder didn't price the slope, the access, or the septic setbacks, and you'll meet the difference later as change orders.

Permits Work Differently in the Mountains

Boulder Creek is unincorporated Santa Cruz County, so there's no city building department. Permits go through the county's Unified Permit Center, which handles building permits, appointments, plan review, and same-day EZ permits for simple jobs like water heaters, electrical work, and reroofs.

The county's rule of thumb is that a building permit is required unless the work is specifically exempted. The exemption list is short and literal: things like a detached shed of 120 square feet or less, a detached deck under 200 square feet that sits less than 30 inches off the ground and isn't attached to the house, or a wall retaining no more than 3 feet of material. And the mountain catch: even exempt work can be overridden by other rules, and the county names them, including required setbacks from property lines, grading permit requirements, zoning designations, Coastal Zone Review, riparian corridors, habitat for endangered plants or animals, and fault zones. That list reads like a description of the average Boulder Creek parcel. When in doubt, email the Zoning Counter at planning.zoninginfo@santacruzcountyca.gov before work starts.

Ask every bidder two permit questions. First, does this job need a permit? Then, who pulls it? A contractor who suggests skipping a permit "to save you money" is offering to make their problem your problem, because unpermitted work follows the property, not the contractor. It surfaces in inspections, insurance claims, and escrow disclosures years later.

Questions That Reveal Mountain Experience

Licenses prove legality. These questions probe for the judgment that only comes from working up here.

Where does my septic system sit, and how will you protect it? Anyone digging, grading, or parking heavy equipment on your lot should ask about the tank and drainfield before the first trench. If they look at you blankly, that's a bad sign. Our septic basics guide explains why a crushed drainfield is a five-figure mistake.

How do you handle slopes and drainage? Ask what they'd do about runoff from the new patio, or where the water goes when the driveway is regraded. Mountain water always goes somewhere, and February will find the answer even if your contractor doesn't.

Have you worked on homes with wells or off district water? Plenty of properties here run on wells or small shared systems, as we cover in the utilities guide, and that changes everything from irrigation tie-ins to concrete washout.

What's your winter plan? Ask how they schedule around storm season, what happens to an open roof or trench when a system rolls in, and whether their crew can reliably get up Highway 9 when it's a mess. An experienced local answers these without hesitating, usually with a story attached.

Finding Local People

We keep a directory of local businesses and tradespeople as a starting point. Being listed isn't an endorsement, and nothing replaces your own license check, bids, and references. CSLB suggests asking for references from recent customers with similar projects; up here, your best reference is often the neighbor whose retaining wall survived last winter.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I check whether a contractor is licensed in California?

Use the free CSLB license lookup. You can search by license number, business name, or personal name, and the record shows status, classifications, bond, workers' compensation, and complaint disclosure. Ask to see the contractor's pocket license and a photo ID when they come out to bid.

How much of a deposit is a contractor allowed to ask for?

For home improvement contracts in California, the down payment is capped at $1,000 or 10 percent of the contract price, whichever is less, per CSLB's contract rules. After that, payments can't exceed the value of work actually completed. A request for a large upfront payment is a serious red flag.

Do small jobs really require a licensed contractor?

Any job of $1,000 or more in combined labor and materials requires a license. Under $1,000, an unlicensed person can only do the work if no permit is required and they don't hire anyone to help. Many small mountain jobs trip the permit condition, so don't assume a little job is exempt.

Do I need a county permit for my project in Boulder Creek?

Assume yes until the county says otherwise. Santa Cruz County requires building permits unless the work is on its short exemption list, and even exempt projects can trigger setback, grading, zoning, riparian, or fault-zone rules on mountain parcels. Check with the Unified Permit Center or email planning.zoninginfo@santacruzcountyca.gov before work begins.

What can I do if a job goes wrong?

Document everything and file a complaint with CSLB through its complaint process. CSLB addresses violations of California contractors license law whether the contractor is licensed or unlicensed, and the contractor's license bond is filed partly for the benefit of consumers damaged by defective construction or other license law violations. Complaints also become part of the public disclosure that protects the next homeowner.

history-of-boulder-creek.title: A Short History of Boulder Creek, California
history-of-boulder-creek.html:
The redwoods explain Boulder Creek. The trees brought the mills, the mills brought a flume and then a railroad, and once the logging wound down, the forest that survived (much of it protected in Big Basin) became the reason people kept driving up Highway 9. The story runs in three chapters: a scatter of sawmill settlements in the 1860s and 1870s, a rail-served lumber boomtown from 1885 to about 1915, and the quieter mountain community and Big Basin gateway it's been ever since. Here's how each one unfolded, with sources you can check yourself.

The valley's first people

People lived here long before anyone milled a board. The Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History puts human presence in the Santa Cruz region at more than 10,000 years, and the Indigenous people of this area spoke Awaswas, one of at least eight languages in the Ohlone language family.

Their descendants are still here. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band is made up of the surviving descendant families of the Indigenous people taken into Mission Santa Cruz (Awaswas speakers) and Mission San Juan Bautista (Mutsun speakers). Today the tribe works through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous knowledge and stewardship across ancestral lands that run from Ano Nuevo along the Santa Cruz Mountains to Monterey Bay. That work is happening now, in these mountains, not just in the past tense.

Sawmills, a flume, and a town called Lorenzo

There's no single founding moment. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project documents the county's railroad era in detail, traces the settlement to a small sawmill Joseph Wilbur Peery put up in the early 1860s. By 1872 a post office named Boulder Creek was running out of the general store, and the name stuck.

The first big change came in 1875, when the San Lorenzo Valley Flume & Lumber Company completed a V-shaped flume that carried rough lumber down the valley to Felton. The flume turned the little settlement into a company town, and in 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad bought the flume company outright.

Boulder Creek wasn't the only town on this stretch of the river, either. A separate village called Lorenzo grew up along the county road between today's Boulder Creek and Brookdale. The San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society notes that its museum building, the original Grace Episcopal Church, stood "in what was originally the town of Lorenzo." Lorenzo didn't last. Its church did, and you can visit it today.

The railroad years, 1885 to 1915

Flumes leak, jam, and lose lumber, so the railroad came next. Crews extended a line up the valley from Felton, and the first train rolled into Boulder Creek in April 1885. The town became the end of the line, the shipping point for the mills working the upper San Lorenzo Valley and the ridges toward Big Basin.

The boom that followed was real. Whaley writes that Boulder Creek was "one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915," and the year-round population passed 800 at the height of it. The San Lorenzo Valley Chamber of Commerce describes the whole valley as a logging center in those years, especially while San Francisco rebuilt after the 1906 earthquake. Local business owners organized a San Lorenzo Valley Board of Trade around the turn of the century; the chamber that succeeded it formed in 1926 and is still going.

The end of the line

It couldn't last, and it didn't. By the early twentieth century the big timber close to the rail line was mostly gone, and logging began to decline. The Dougherty Extension line above town was abandoned around 1917. In March 1931 Southern Pacific replaced the passenger trains with Pacific Greyhound bus service, and in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek.

Whaley's summary of what came next is hard to improve on: the town "transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town." The sawmill economy gave way to summer cabins, resorts, and day visitors headed for the big trees.

Big Basin changes the story

The reason those big trees were still standing is its own piece of Boulder Creek history. In May 1900, while the mills were still running full tilt, a group of conservationists led by San Jose photographer Andrew P. Hill camped in Big Basin and formed the Sempervirens Club to save the old growth from logging. The state historical landmark at the site, registered as Landmark 827, dates the founding camp at the base of Slippery Rock to May 15, 1900. Two years of lobbying later, the state bought thousands of acres of ancient forest, and the Sempervirens Fund (the club's modern successor) notes that the 1902 purchase created "the first park established in California in the current state park system."

That decision reshaped the town's future. Once the logs stopped moving, the park kept Boulder Creek on the map. It still does: Highway 236 to Big Basin leaves Highway 9 right in downtown Boulder Creek, and the park entrance kiosk sits nine miles up the road.

Fire and recovery

The hardest recent chapter came in August 2020, when the CZU Lightning Complex fire burned through the mountains. The Sempervirens Fund calls it "the most devastating fire ever recorded in the Santa Cruz mountains region," with more than 86,000 acres burned. California State Parks reports the fire swept through 97 percent of Big Basin and destroyed the park's historic buildings, though most of the old-growth redwoods survived.

The fire also destroyed homes throughout the valley's communities, and rebuilding has taken years. Santa Cruz County's own 2024 Grand Jury report on the aftermath is titled "Four Years Later The Flame Still Burns," which tells you how long recovery runs in mountain country. Anyone who lived here through it doesn't need the reminder.

Boulder Creek today

Boulder Creek is an unincorporated community in Santa Cruz County today, but it wasn't always. The town incorporated as a city during the boom years, and in 1915 residents voted to disincorporate, handing the area back to county rule as the timber economy collapsed. Boulder Creek appears with that 1915 date in the statewide record of California municipal disincorporations, alongside neighboring Felton in 1917. Boulder Creek has stayed unincorporated ever since, and the chamber counts more than 35,000 people across the San Lorenzo Valley as a whole. The lumber wharves and rail yards are long gone, but the town still works the way the railroad laid it out, with the commercial strip along Highway 9 and the road to Big Basin splitting off at the center of it.

To go deeper than this article, start with the San Lorenzo Valley Museum. The historical society behind it was founded in Boulder Creek in March 1976, opened the museum in the old Grace Episcopal Church in December 1999, and now runs two galleries: the Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek and the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery in Felton. Both buildings are former churches listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Gallery hours and exhibits change, so check the museum's site before you make the trip.

Frequently Asked Questions

When was Boulder Creek founded?

There's no single founding date. The settlement began with a sawmill in the early 1860s, got its post office (and its name) in 1872, and took its lasting shape when the railroad arrived in April 1885. It did incorporate as a city in the early 1900s, but residents voted to disincorporate in 1915, so there's no incorporation date in force today.

Why is it called Boulder Creek?

The town took its name from the Boulder Creek post office, established in 1872 and named for the creek that joins the San Lorenzo River near downtown. Neighboring settlements called Boulder and Lorenzo faded, and the post office name is the one that survived.

How big was Boulder Creek during the lumber boom?

The year-round population passed 800 in the boom years, according to rail historian Derek Whaley, who also ranks the town among the largest lumber exporters in the country between 1885 and 1915. The seasonal workforce in the surrounding mill camps came and went with the cutting.

Where can I see Boulder Creek history in person?

The San Lorenzo Valley Museum's Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek is the closest stop, with a second gallery in Felton. Hours change seasonally, so confirm on slvmuseum.org before visiting. The Slippery Rock landmark commemorating the Sempervirens Club is inside Big Basin, where access depends on current park conditions.

house-cleaners-boulder-creek.title: House Cleaners Who Serve Boulder Creek
house-cleaners-boulder-creek.html:
Most house cleaners around here work out of their own homes, so there's no storefront to walk into and no rate posted on a website. It comes down to a phone call. Three cleaners are based right in town, and they give you the strongest coverage for a mountain address. Beyond those, a Felton service and two larger county cleaners work parts of the valley too, though it's worth confirming they'll come up the mountain to your address. What follows is who serves the area and what they say they do. House cleaning is almost never priced online, so call for an estimate rather than expecting a rate on a website. These are small operations, so booking ahead is the norm.

San Lorenzo Valley Housekeeping (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-566-8979. Website: lochdog.com/SLVHousekeeping. Service area: Boulder Creek and the San Lorenzo Valley. Home-based, no public address.

San Lorenzo Valley Housekeeping is a locally owned cleaning business that has operated since 2012, one of the longer-running cleaners in the valley. It offers basic house cleaning, deep cleaning, laundry, window cleaning, and move-in or move-out service. It's based in Boulder Creek and works the whole San Lorenzo Valley, so coverage for a mountain address is a safe assumption.

SCM Custom Cleaning (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-222-0402. Website: scmcustomcleaning.wordpress.com. Service area: San Lorenzo Valley and surrounding Santa Cruz Mountains. Home-based, no public address.

SCM Custom Cleaning is a locally owned Boulder Creek service. It starts with an initial deep clean, then keeps a weekly, bi-weekly, or monthly maintenance schedule for homes, rentals, and offices. That recurring model fits households that want a standing appointment rather than a one-off. Its stated service area covers the San Lorenzo Valley and the surrounding mountains, so mountain addresses fall within its normal range.

Clean Mountain House Cleaning (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-585-5841. Website: facebook.com/cleanmountainhc. Service area: Boulder Creek and the Santa Cruz Mountains. Home-based, no public address.

Clean Mountain House Cleaning is a Boulder Creek business providing regular, deep, and move-in or move-out cleaning for homes throughout the Santa Cruz Mountains. Like the other two local cleaners, it works out of a home rather than a storefront, so contact runs through its Facebook page or by phone. The mountain focus in its own description bodes well for hard-to-reach or long-driveway properties.

Clean Impressions LLC (Felton)

Phone: 209-559-7199. Service area: Felton and Ben Lomond. Home-based, no public address.

Clean Impressions is a Felton-based service handling residential cleaning and short-term-rental turnovers, and it advertises free estimates. Worth noting: its published service area names Felton and Ben Lomond, not Boulder Creek specifically. It's close enough to be worth a call if the in-town cleaners are booked, but confirm it will drive up to a Boulder Creek address before you count on it.

Christi's Green Cleaning (Scotts Valley)

Phone: 831-676-1271. Website: christisgreencleaning.com. Service area: Scotts Valley and Santa Cruz County. Home-based, no public address.

Christi's Green Cleaning is an eco-friendly residential cleaning company that has operated since 2007, offering weekly, biweekly, and monthly service with background-checked staff and non-toxic products. It's based in Scotts Valley and lists Santa Cruz County as its service area, so Boulder Creek falls within that broad range on paper. If green products and vetted staff matter to you, call to confirm it services your specific address and ask about the drive.

Santa Cruz Maids (Santa Cruz)

Phone: 831-480-6420. Website: scruzmaids.com. Hours: Monday through Sunday 8am to 8pm. Service area: Santa Cruz County, including Scotts Valley and Felton.

Santa Cruz Maids, which also operates as Dynamic House Cleaning, is a county-wide service running seven days a week with long daily hours, useful if scheduling flexibility is your priority. Its published service area calls out Scotts Valley and Felton by name rather than Boulder Creek, so treat mountain coverage as a question to ask rather than a given. For a farther-out address up Highway 9, confirm it will make the trip before booking.

How to read this list

For most Boulder Creek households, the answer is one of the three town-based cleaners: San Lorenzo Valley Housekeeping, SCM Custom Cleaning, and Clean Mountain House Cleaning. All three are local, all three name the mountains in their own service areas, and all three do the deep-clean-then-maintain pattern most homes want. The Felton and county options can widen your choices, especially for short-notice or seven-day scheduling, but their published service areas don't specifically list Boulder Creek, so a quick coverage call saves a wasted booking.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which house cleaners are actually based in Boulder Creek?

Three: San Lorenzo Valley Housekeeping, SCM Custom Cleaning, and Clean Mountain House Cleaning. All three are locally owned, home-based operations that name Boulder Creek or the Santa Cruz Mountains in their own service areas, so they're the most reliable bet for a mountain address.

Will cleaners from Felton, Scotts Valley, or Santa Cruz drive up to Boulder Creek?

Maybe, and it's worth asking directly. Clean Impressions lists Felton and Ben Lomond, and Santa Cruz Maids names Scotts Valley and Felton, none of them Boulder Creek by name. Christi's Green Cleaning lists Santa Cruz County broadly. Confirm your specific address is covered, and ask whether the drive affects scheduling or pricing.

How much does house cleaning cost here?

None of these cleaners publishes flat pricing, which is normal for the trade because cost depends on home size, condition, and frequency. Call for an estimate. Several, including Clean Impressions, advertise free estimates, and the recurring-service cleaners often price a first deep clean differently from ongoing visits.

A cleaner I use isn't listed. Why?

This list only includes cleaners we could verify as current directory listings. Plenty of good local help works by word of mouth without a public web presence, and we can only list what we can confirm. If you know a cleaner who serves Boulder Creek and wants to be listed, or you spot an error, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, only a verified place to start. Browse the full directory for contact details, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so this stays accurate for the next neighbor.

how-boulder-creek-got-its-name.title: How Boulder Creek Got Its Name and Who Was Here First
how-boulder-creek-got-its-name.html:
Look at the streambed and the name explains itself. The town takes it from the water. Boulder Creek, the waterway, comes down off the east side of Ben Lomond mountain and meets the San Lorenzo River at the north end of town, and the settlement that grew up near that junction took the creek's name when it got a post office in 1872. The creek's own name is about as literal as names get. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project documents this valley in detail, credits it to early trappers and lumbermen who wanted a plain, descriptive label for a rocky feeder creek of the San Lorenzo. No legend, no founder's ego, just boulders.

That's the short answer. The longer one runs through a sawmill man named Joseph Wilbur Peery, a rival town called Lorenzo that burned, and a railroad that made the name permanent. And before any of it, this was a lived-in landscape for thousands of years, which is where the story properly starts.

The people who were here first

People have lived in the Santa Cruz region for more than 10,000 years, according to the Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History, and the Indigenous people of this area spoke Awaswas, one of at least eight languages in what anthropologists later grouped together as the Ohlone language family. These weren't passing visitors. The museum describes communities that actively tended the landscape, managed resources through the seasons, and knew these mountains in a way no one since has matched.

Their descendants are still here, and they speak for themselves. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band is made up of the surviving descendant families of the tribal groups taken into Mission Santa Cruz, where Awaswas was spoken, and Mission San Juan Bautista, where Mutsun was spoken. The tribe counts nearly 600 enrolled members today. Through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust, they steward ancestral lands they know as Popeloutchom, running from Ano Nuevo along the ridgelines of the Santa Cruz Mountains to Monterey Bay. That work is happening right now, with programs like the Native Stewardship Corps working in these mountains.

One caveat, stated plainly: written records for the upper San Lorenzo Valley specifically are thin, and this article won't pretend to name the exact village communities that lived along this stretch of river. What's well documented is that Awaswas-speaking people lived throughout the region for millennia before a single tree was milled, and that their descendants never left the story.

A creek named for what's in it

Whaley's dictionary of local place names keeps the entry simple: the town "refers to Boulder Creek, a year-round tributary of the San Lorenzo River" and was named after the creek. The creek itself got its name from the trappers and lumbermen who worked the valley early on, and anyone who's looked at the streambed understands why. It's a boulder-choked mountain creek, and the men naming things around here weren't poets.

The geography matters more than it looks. Boulder Creek and Bear Creek both meet the San Lorenzo River close together at the north end of the valley floor. When railroad planners later mapped the area, they called that three-way junction of waterways "the Turkey Foot," a detail preserved in Whaley's history of the Felton & Pescadero Railroad. That confluence made this spot the natural gathering point for the upper valley's timber, which is why a town formed here at all.

Boulder before Boulder Creek

The first Anglo settlement here didn't start with a grand plan. Joseph Wilbur Peery put up a small sawmill across from Bear Creek in the early 1860s, and a modest settlement called Boulder grew around the milling activity. Peery eventually moved on, and the Crediford family kept the little settlement going.

The name change came through the mail. In 1872 a post office opened inside the general store, registered as Boulder Creek. Post office names had a way of becoming official names in rural California, since the postmark was often the only piece of paper that regularly said where you were. From 1872 on, the place was Boulder Creek in the eyes of the federal government, whatever the locals called it day to day.

Lorenzo, the town that almost won

Here's the twist most newcomers haven't heard: Boulder Creek had a rival a mile down the road, founded by the same man who built the first mill. In January 1875, Peery established a town he named Lorenzo, after the river, on the county road south of Boulder. Whaley's history of the site records that Lorenzo grew into a real village with hotels, saloons, and a town hall, sitting between today's Boulder Creek and Brookdale.

For a moment, Lorenzo even looked like the winner. When the railroad built up the valley in 1885, a station at Lorenzo briefly served as the end of the line while crews finished the last stretch north. But the permanent terminus went in at Boulder Creek, closer to the timber and the Turkey Foot, and the railroad's choice decided the contest. Southern Pacific renamed the Lorenzo station Filbert in 1887, probably for the California hazel tree, mostly to avoid confusion with other stations carrying the Lorenzo name.

The end came quickly after that. In 1897 a kitchen fire spread through Lorenzo and destroyed the two major hotels, the town hall, and other buildings along the county road. Peery made a brief attempt at rebuilding, then gave it up, and Lorenzo was absorbed into Boulder Creek. One building from the Lorenzo era outlived the town: the original Grace Episcopal Church, which the San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society notes stood "in what was originally the town of Lorenzo." That church now houses the historical society's museum gallery at 12547 Highway 9, so the best surviving piece of Lorenzo sits in downtown Boulder Creek.

The railroad makes it permanent

The name question was settled for good in April 1885, when the first train rolled into the new Boulder Creek terminus and, as Whaley puts it, the prosperity of Boulder Creek truly began. Railroads printed timetables, painted station signs, and shipped freight under one name, and that kind of repetition ends arguments. Boulder faded into Boulder Creek, Lorenzo burned and folded in, and the post office name from 1872 became the only name left standing.
