Everything that followed, the lumber boom, the resort era, the quiet decades after the trains stopped, happened under that name. If you want the full sweep of what came next, our short history of Boulder Creek picks up the story from here.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Boulder Creek named after the creek or the boulders?

Both, in sequence. The creek was named first, by early trappers and lumbermen who gave a rocky stream an obvious, descriptive name. The town then took its name from the creek by way of the post office that opened in 1872. So the town is named after the creek, and the creek is named after what's sitting in it.

What was the town of Lorenzo?

Lorenzo was a village Joseph Wilbur Peery founded in January 1875 about a mile south of Boulder Creek, named for the San Lorenzo River. It had hotels, a town hall, and its own railroad station for a time. A kitchen fire destroyed most of it in 1897, and the site was absorbed into Boulder Creek. Its old Grace Episcopal Church survives as the San Lorenzo Valley Museum's gallery on Highway 9.

Who lived in the San Lorenzo Valley before settlers arrived?

Awaswas-speaking peoples, part of the broader group anthropologists call Ohlone, lived throughout the Santa Cruz region for more than 10,000 years. Their descendants belong to the Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, which works today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous stewardship across ancestral lands that include the Santa Cruz Mountains.

Was Boulder Creek ever called something else?

The settlement was originally just Boulder, named for the creek beside it. The post office established in 1872 carried the name Boulder Creek, and once the railroad arrived in 1885 with a station of the same name, Boulder Creek was the name that stuck.

how-directory-verification-works.title: How Directory Verification Works (and How to Fix a Listing)
how-directory-verification-works.html:
Every entry in the Boulder Creek Local directory had a person look at it before it went live. That's the short version. Each listing carries a verification date and a confidence level, we recheck them on a schedule, and being listed is informational, not an endorsement, and never something a business pays for. If a listing's wrong, tell us through the form at /submit, and a person will check it against a first-party source before anything changes.

The rest of this page explains what each of those sentences actually means, because a directory is only useful if you know how much to trust it.

Where listing information comes from

We prefer official and first-party sources, and we reach for them in a set order: official agency, district, school, park, and utility pages; a business or organization's own website; an official business social profile when no maintained website exists; and current licensing or registration records for regulated services.

Association directories, maps, review sites, and older local directories help us find candidates, and that's all we use them for. They aren't treated as proof that a business is open or that its details are current. Anyone who's searched for a Boulder Creek restaurant and landed on a place that closed years ago knows why that rule exists.

What a person does before a listing goes live

Every listing is reviewed by a human before it's published. Automation helps us collect and organize, but nothing appears in the directory because a script decided it should.

For each entry we record two things. A verification date, meaning the date a person last confirmed the details against a source. And a confidence level, our own note on how solid the sourcing is. Then we recheck on a schedule. Things that change fast, like restaurant hours and emergency resources, get rechecked more often than stable resident resources.

When we can't confirm a detail, we mark it as unavailable. Missing information is never an all clear. If a listing doesn't show hours, that means we couldn't verify hours, not that the business is always open.

What the verified date means, and what it doesn't

The verified date on a listing tells you when a person last confirmed the entry against a first-party source. It's a snapshot, not a promise. Hours, availability, prices, and services change, especially in a mountain town where weather, power, and staffing all take their turns. For anything time sensitive, confirm directly with the business or agency before you drive.

We never guarantee safety, availability, quality, admission, access, or outcome. That's not fine print for its own sake. It's the limit of what any directory can know.

What we deliberately leave out

Some absences are on purpose.

We don't rank or score businesses. There's no "best of Boulder Creek" here, no stars, and no ordering that rewards anyone.

We don't publish private home addresses. Home-based businesses are listed without one.

We don't list individual informal caregivers, and we don't list unverified emergency contractors. Those are categories where a wrong or stale listing can do real harm.

Money never changes a listing

A factual listing in this directory is free, and it stays free. We don't charge businesses to be listed, and we don't accept payment to change facts, placement, or verification.

The site may earn revenue to keep running, and the rules for that are strict. Sponsored content, sponsored placements, and advertisements are clearly labeled wherever they appear, and editorial listings and guides are kept separate from paid placements. We won't sell higher rankings, better reviews, or preferential facts. We won't present a paid message as independent editorial content. And we won't remove or downgrade a competitor's accurate listing because someone paid us. Every business is held to the same sourcing standard whether or not it ever pays us anything.

One more time, because it's the line everything else hangs on: inclusion in the directory is informational. It is not an endorsement.

How to fix a listing

If something is wrong, out of date, or shouldn't be here, we want to know. Business owners and their authorized representatives can ask, and so can any resident or visitor who spots an error. You don't need to be connected to the business to flag a problem.

The fastest route is the submit form. A useful request includes five things: the listing or page (a link is easiest), what's wrong or what you'd like changed, a first-party source we can use to confirm the correct information (an official website, an agency page, or a licensing record), your name and how you're connected to the business or content, and a way to reach you.

Here's what happens next. We acknowledge your request. We check the correct information against a first-party or official source, not against the request alone. Then we update, remove, or explain our decision, and a person reviews the change before it's published. Safety-related corrections are handled first.

Two special cases. A business may ask to be removed from the directory, and we'll remove the listing; where official or public-record information matters to residents, like an agency, utility, or public-safety contact, we may keep that information and note its official source. And if you believe content or an image infringes your copyright, trademark, or other rights, send the item, the rights you hold, and enough detail for us to locate it, and we'll remove or correct anything that shouldn't have been published.

One thing this process is not: an emergency channel. In an emergency, call 911.

The full policies behind this page, including sourcing, corrections, and the sponsorship disclosure, live at /legal.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is being listed an endorsement?

No. Inclusion is informational only. A listing means a person verified, on the date shown, that the business exists and the recorded details matched a first-party source. It says nothing about quality, and we don't rank or score anyone.

Can a business pay for a better listing?

No. Factual listings are free, and payment never changes facts, placement, description, or verification. If we ever run sponsored placements or ads, they're labeled as such and kept separate from editorial listings.

Why does a listing say a detail is unavailable?

Because we couldn't verify it against a source we trust. We'd rather show a gap than guess. Unavailable never means "probably fine," so for time-sensitive plans, contact the business directly.

How do I correct my business's listing?

Use the form at /submit. Include the listing, what needs to change, and a first-party source we can verify against, plus your name, your connection to the business, and a way to reach you. A person checks the source and reviews the change before it goes live, and safety-related fixes jump the line.

Can I have my business removed?

Yes. Ask through /submit and we'll remove the listing. The one exception is official or public-record information residents rely on, such as agency or utility contacts, which we may keep with its official source noted.

huckleberries-santa-cruz-mountains.title: Huckleberries in the Santa Cruz Mountains: What They Are and Where Picking Is Actually Legal
huckleberries-santa-cruz-mountains.html:
The short version first. The huckleberries that grow wild all over the Santa Cruz Mountains are evergreen huckleberry, a native shrub whose small dark berries ripen late in the year, typically around October or November. And the question most people are really asking, can I pick them, has an answer most people don't expect: not in the state parks. California State Parks regulations prohibit picking berries or any plant material, and Big Basin, Castle Rock, and Henry Cowell are all state parks. Legal picking around here mostly means private land where you have the owner's permission. This article covers what the plant is, how to identify it with confidence, and where the rules actually land, because a berry walk is one of the genuine pleasures of living up here and it's better done right.

One rule sits above everything else on this page: never eat any wild plant you haven't positively identified. If a child or anyone else eats an unknown plant or berry, call the California Poison Control hotline at 1-800-222-1222, free, 24 hours a day. When in doubt, don't.

The Plant: Evergreen Huckleberry

Evergreen huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum), also called California huckleberry, is a native shrub in the same genus as blueberries. The USDA's plant guide describes an erect, stout evergreen shrub roughly two to ten feet tall, with glossy green leaves that are small, leathery, finely toothed, and oval, held densely along reddish stems. In spring it hangs out little urn-shaped pink flowers, and Calscape, the California Native Plant Society's plant database, notes those bell-shaped blooms feed bees and hummingbirds. The berries that follow are small, about a quarter inch, and purplish-black.

You've walked past it a thousand times. It grows in the edges and clearings of coniferous forest, and its range runs down the coast from British Columbia into what the USDA calls the redwood area of California, which is exactly where we live. Under the redwoods it often forms the dense, glossy understory along trail edges, and because the leaves stay green all year, it's recognizable in any month even when there's not a berry in sight.

When the Berries Come

Patience is the whole game with this species. The USDA plant guide puts ripening late in the year, around October or November, and notes these are the last fruits of the gathering season. Tribes in the Pacific Northwest prized them, traveled long distances for them, ate them fresh and dried them for winter, and held that the berries taste even better after a frost. The exact timing here swings with the year and the site, so treat fall as the season and let color be your guide: ripe fruit is deep purple-black, not red.

Wildlife runs on the same calendar. The USDA guide lists grouse, chipmunks, black bears, mice, and a long roster of songbirds among the animals that depend on huckleberries, and deer browse the foliage. That matters for the ethics of picking: in a forest, those berries aren't surplus. They're somebody's winter.

Identifying It with Confidence

Positive identification means the whole plant agrees with the description, not just the berry. Check all of it: an evergreen shrub, woody stems with reddish new growth, small leathery leaves with finely serrated edges arranged alternately along the stem, glossy dark green on top. Flowers, if present, are small pink urns. Berries are small, round, and purple-black, sitting where the leaves meet the stem. If any part of the plant doesn't match, the leaves are floppy or large, the berries are red or clustered differently, the plant dies back in winter, then it isn't evergreen huckleberry and you walk on.

And the critical rule, worth repeating because it's the one that keeps this hobby safe: if you haven't positively identified it, you don't eat it. Not one berry, not "probably fine." California Poison Control notes that plants are a major cause of poisoning in children under six, and its advice extends to adults: know your plants, teach kids not to graze, and call 1-800-222-1222 with any question, even if you're not sure it's a problem. A good regional plant guidebook, a local native plant walk, or a knowledgeable neighbor is how you build real confidence. An app alone isn't identification.

Where Picking Is Not Allowed: The State Parks

Here's the part that surprises people. The land where most of us walk among huckleberries is state park land, and California State Parks regulations flatly prohibit collecting. Title 14, section 4306 of the California Code of Regulations, posted in the State Parks General Provisions, reads: "No person shall willfully or negligently pick, dig up, cut, mutilate, destroy, injure, disturb, move, molest, burn, or carry away any tree or plant or portion thereof, including but not limited to leaf mold, flowers, foliage, berries, fruit, grass, turf, humus, shrubs, cones, and dead wood."

Berries and fruit, named explicitly. That covers Big Basin Redwoods State Park, Castle Rock State Park, and Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park, all of them. The regulation does allow a narrow exception: a park district can post an authorization at a specific unit's headquarters permitting limited berry or mushroom gathering there. We're not aware of any such posting at the parks near Boulder Creek, and unless you've seen one posted at that park, the rule is the rule. Eating a berry off the bush is still picking it.

The same logic covers other protected lands. San Vicente Redwoods, the big preserve west of town with public trails managed by the Land Trust of Santa Cruz County, states its rule plainly: all plants, animals, and fungi are protected, and no collecting is allowed. Other public and conserved lands set their own rules, so before you pick anywhere, find out who manages the land and what their posted rules say. "Nobody stopped me" isn't the same as legal.

Where Picking Can Be Legal

Private land, with permission. If huckleberry grows on your own parcel, those berries are yours, and plenty of Boulder Creek properties back up into exactly the kind of forest edge this shrub loves. If it grows on a neighbor's land, ask. An offer to share a jar of jam has opened many gates up here.

That's genuinely most of the legal picture. It's a shorter list than forage-culture social media suggests, and we'd rather tell you the honest version: in this valley, huckleberry picking is a private-land activity or it's just huckleberry admiring. The good news is that admiring is free everywhere, the shrub is handsome twelve months a year, and watching a flock of songbirds strip a bush in November is its own kind of harvest.

One more option worth knowing: evergreen huckleberry is sold by native plant nurseries as a landscape shrub. Calscape lists it as a garden-worthy native that takes shade, which describes most Boulder Creek yards. Planting your own bush is the one way to get a guaranteed-legal patch, though you'll wait a few years and negotiate with the birds.

Enjoying Them Without Picking Them

A fall huckleberry walk asks nothing but attention. The glossy understory along the trail edges in Henry Cowell and Big Basin is often loaded by late October, and berries on the bush are part of what keeps those forests full of birdsong. State Parks rules protect the animals too, section 4305 prohibits feeding or disturbing wildlife, and leaving the crop in place is the same principle pointed at the plants. Take photos, learn the plant well enough to teach your kids, and if the picking itch is real, put a bush in the yard or make friends with someone who has one.

If you're heading up to the parks for that walk, our Big Basin day trip guide covers current access, and the roads and parking notes cover getting there gracefully.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I pick huckleberries in Big Basin, Castle Rock, or Henry Cowell?

No. All three are California State Parks, and Title 14, section 4306 prohibits picking berries, fruit, or any plant material in the State Park System. The only exception is a gathering authorization posted at a specific park's headquarters, and unless you've seen one posted there, assume it doesn't exist.

So where can I legally pick huckleberries near Boulder Creek?

On private land where you have the owner's permission, including your own property. Protected lands set their own rules, and the ones we've verified, like San Vicente Redwoods, prohibit collecting. When in doubt about any parcel, identify the land manager and ask before you pick.

When are huckleberries ripe here?

Late in the year. The USDA plant guide puts ripening around October or November, the last fruits of the season, and notes they're said to taste better after a cold snap. Ripe berries are deep purple-black. Timing shifts with the year, so watch the bushes, not the calendar.

How do I know a berry is really a huckleberry?

Identify the whole plant, not the berry: an evergreen shrub with small, glossy, leathery, finely toothed leaves on reddish stems, pink urn-shaped flowers in season, and small purple-black berries. If anything about the plant doesn't match, don't eat it. Never eat any wild plant you haven't positively identified, and if someone does eat an unknown plant, call Poison Control at 1-800-222-1222. For anyone with severe symptoms, call 911.

Are wild mushrooms okay to gather while I'm out there?

Treat mushrooms as a hard no. California Poison Control's guidance is that the only safe mushrooms are ones from established food markets, and deadly species grow in these mountains. Our poison oak and plants to avoid article covers that warning in full.

is-big-basin-open.title: Is Big Basin Open? What's Accessible in 2026
is-big-basin-open.html:
Short answer: yes, Big Basin Redwoods State Park is open, but only for limited day use while it keeps recovering from the 2020 CZU fire, and a lot of it is still closed. As of 2026-07-18, State Parks lists day-use hours of 8 a.m. to sunset, parking reservations that are highly recommended but not required, all campgrounds in the main park closed, and no drinking water on site except for purchase at the entrance kiosk. Because access here changes month to month, the official Big Basin Redwoods State Park page is the source of truth, and nothing on this page is a guarantee that a trail, road, or parking lot will be open the day you go.

This year (2026)

For 2026, the park is running its now-familiar recovery pattern: open for limited day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, with a paid parking reservation strongly encouraged and the main campgrounds still closed. On summer weekends, from Memorial Day weekend through Labor Day weekend, State Parks has run a free shuttle from a temporary parking area at Saddle Mountain, and Santa Cruz METRO has offered weekend summer bus service to the park. Both are seasonal and can change, so confirm each is running before you count on it.

What's open beyond that keeps shifting as crews clear more trails and roads. Rather than trust a snapshot, check the current park page and its map the morning you head up.

What "limited day use" actually means

This is the part that trips people up. Big Basin reopened, but it did not reopen as the park in the old postcards. Here's the honest shape of it.

Hours. Day use runs 8 a.m. to sunset. There's no overnight access in the main park.

Parking and fees. State Parks lists regular-sized autos at $6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 per vehicle if you show up without a reservation. Spaces are limited, so a reservation is highly recommended, though not strictly required. Reservations are handled through the Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks system at thatsmypark.org, and the passenger-vehicle form is separate from disabled parking and bus or trailer parking. Oversized vehicles and trailers can't be accommodated for day-use parking right now, so leave the RV behind.

Arriving car-free is free. If you come by foot, bike, or bus, State Parks says no fee or reservation is required. That's genuinely the cheapest and least stressful way in on a busy weekend.

Camping. All campgrounds in the main park are closed, and no camping reservations are available there. The one exception on the property is Rancho del Oso, the park's coastal side near Waddell Beach, which is partially reopened with walk-in and bike-in camping, and day use there needs no fee or reservation.

Water and services. Bring your own water. State Parks says there's no potable water on site, only bottled water for purchase at the parking kiosk. Facilities are temporary, not the old lodge-and-store setup, so pack food, layers, and anything else you'll need for the day.

What's open to hike, and what isn't

Treat any trail list as a dated snapshot, not a promise. As of mid-July 2026, the reliable short walk is the Redwood Loop Trail, an easy 0.6-mile loop through old-growth redwoods near the historic park core, where you can see fire scars and bright new growth on the same thousand-year-old trees. The trail network has been growing in phases as crews clear and reopen more mileage, and State Parks adds trails and backcountry roads "as they are cleared and made safe."

At the same time, closures come and go. As of this writing, State Parks lists the Hollow Tree Trail as temporarily closed after winter storm damage, which is a good example of why a trail that was open last month can be roped off today. Before you commit to a route, pull up the current trail map on the park page and respect every closure sign you pass. They usually mark hazard trees, unstable ground, or active restoration, even when the path beyond looks fine.

Getting there from Boulder Creek

Downtown Boulder Creek is the gateway. From the middle of town, turn onto Highway 236, the Big Basin Highway, and follow it about 9 miles to the main entrance. Every road into the park is narrow and curvy, and lower 236 is slow going, so give yourself close to half an hour, watch for cyclists, and use the turnouts to let faster traffic by. There are no services along 236, so fuel up, grab water and food, and buy anything you forgot in Boulder Creek before you climb.

Cell coverage in the basin is unreliable, so download your map ahead of time and tell someone your plan. For live road conditions on the way up, our Mountain Status page pulls the official sources together in one place.

Why it's still like this

Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, and for well over a century Boulder Creek has been its front door. In August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex fire tore through the park and burned the great majority of it, destroying nearly every building, including the historic headquarters. Most of the old-growth redwoods survived, and State Parks reports vigorous new growth and returning wildlife, but rebuilding roads, water systems, and safe trails across a fire-scarred park is slow, years-long work. That's why access opens in careful phases and why the answer to "is it open" is a real but qualified yes.

If you want the fuller story of how the park was created and what the fire meant here, read our history piece on how Big Basin became California's first state park. When you're ready to actually plan the outing, our responsible Big Basin day trip guide walks through the whole day from Boulder Creek.

Frequently asked questions

Is Big Basin open right now?

It's open for limited day use, 8 a.m. to sunset, as it recovers from the 2020 fire, with much of the park still closed. Conditions change often, so confirm on the official Big Basin Redwoods State Park page before you drive up. Nothing here guarantees a given trail or lot will be open.

Do I need a parking reservation?

A reservation is highly recommended but not strictly required. State Parks lists $6 per regular vehicle plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without one, and spaces are limited enough that a busy weekend without a reservation is a gamble. Book through the Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks reservation system, and confirm current pricing on the park page.

Can I get in without paying for parking?

Yes. State Parks says there's no fee or reservation for visitors arriving on foot, by bike, or by bus. On summer weekends there's also been a free shuttle from Saddle Mountain and weekend METRO bus service, both seasonal, so check that they're running first.

Is camping open at Big Basin?

Not in the main park. All campgrounds there are closed and no camping reservations are available. Rancho del Oso, the coastal side of the park, is partially reopened with walk-in and bike-in camping.

How far is Big Basin from Boulder Creek?

The main entrance is about 9 miles up Highway 236 from downtown Boulder Creek. The road is narrow and winding, so plan on roughly half an hour, not a quick hop, and fuel up in town first.

Is there water and food in the park?

Bring your own. State Parks says there's no potable water on site, only bottled water for sale at the parking kiosk, and services are limited and temporary. Pack water, food, and layers.

Planning the day around town too? See what's open on the Boulder Creek Local directory and check road conditions on Mountain Status before you go. Spot a detail here that's out of date? Send it to us. For current park conditions, the official source is always the Big Basin Redwoods State Park page.

is-boulder-creek-a-good-place-to-live.title: Is Boulder Creek a Good Place to Live? The Real Trade-offs
is-boulder-creek-a-good-place-to-live.html:
Here's a straight answer instead of a sales pitch: Boulder Creek is a wonderful place to live if you want redwoods, quiet, and a real community, and you go in clear-eyed about the trade-offs. The big ones are the cost and difficulty of fire insurance, one main road in and out, a real commute to most jobs, and a long drive for a big grocery run or a late-night pharmacy. None of that is a dealbreaker for the people who love it here. All of it matters more than a listing photo will tell you. This piece walks through the full ledger, both sides. If you're already leaning yes, our new resident quick-start guide covers the week-one logistics.

What homes actually cost

Boulder Creek is expensive, like most of the Santa Cruz Mountains, but not Silicon Valley expensive. The exact number depends on who's counting and what kind of home. As of mid-2026, Redfin put the median sale price around $678,000 as of May 2026, up a few percent year over year, while Zillow's typical home value sat higher, near $785,000. Single-family-only data from the local MLS ran lower, closer to $571,000 in the same window. Call it roughly $600,000 to $800,000 depending on the source, the month, and whether you're looking at a cabin or a full house on usable land. Prices move, so treat those as a snapshot, not a quote, and check the live figures before you make any decision.

What the median hides is the range. A small off-grid-ish cabin on a steep lot is a very different purchase from a rebuilt home on a sunny flat parcel, and the gap between them is wide up here. Condition, sun exposure, water source, and road access swing prices more than square footage does.

The insurance problem is real, and you have to plan for it

This is the part realtor blogs skip. Boulder Creek sits in a high fire-risk area, and standard home insurers have pulled back hard across the Santa Cruz Mountains. Many homeowners here have ended up on the California FAIR Plan, which the plan itself describes as "an insurer of last resort, established by statute to provide basic property insurance to Californians statewide when no other option is reasonably available." It's a backstop, not a bargain, and it covers less than a standard policy.

It's also getting pricier. As Lookout Santa Cruz reported in October 2025, the FAIR Plan proposed rate increases that would hit Boulder Creek's 95006 ZIP code with a roughly 47.7% jump for policy renewals after April 1, 2026, among the steepest in a county where nearly 11,000 residents rely on the plan. Budget for insurance as a serious line item, not a rounding error, and get real quotes for a specific address before you commit, because two homes a mile apart can price very differently.

One protection worth knowing: under state law, after the Governor declares a wildfire emergency, insurers can't cancel or non-renew residential policies for wildfire risk in the affected ZIP codes for one year from the declaration. That buys time, but it isn't a permanent fix, and the underlying market is the thing to plan around.

The commute is a real commute

If your job is over the hill, know what you're signing up for. Typical drive times from Boulder Creek run about 30 minutes to Santa Cruz, 40 to 50 minutes to San Jose by way of Highway 9 and Highway 17, and 50 to 60 minutes up to the Redwood City area. Those are good-day estimates. Highway 17 backs up, and winter weather or a downed tree on Highway 9 can turn any of them into something longer. People do these commutes daily and make peace with them, but it's worth driving your actual route at your actual hour before you decide, not on a quiet Sunday.

Remote and hybrid work makes Boulder Creek far more livable than it was a decade ago, which is a big reason people can choose the redwoods and still keep a valley job.

One way in, one way out

Boulder Creek is a mountain town with limited road access, and that shapes daily life and emergencies alike. During the 2020 CZU Lightning Complex fire, the whole town was evacuated along with Ben Lomond, Felton, and Lompico, and Highway 9 was a critical line. When thousands of people need to leave at once on a narrow mountain highway, it's slow, and that's the reality you accept living here.

The practical answer isn't fear, it's preparation. Sign up for CruzAware emergency alerts the day you move in, keep a go-bag ready in fire season, and know your routes. Our guides to a power outage plan and a winter storm checklist cover the rest. Living somewhere with one main road means the habits that feel like overkill in the suburbs are just normal here.

The "30 minutes for a carton of eggs" question

You'll hear this one a lot, and it's half true. There's no big-box store, no 24-hour anything, and a Costco run or a specific hardware part can mean a drive down to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz. If you're used to a supermarket on every corner, that adjustment is real.

The half that isn't true: you can cover most everyday needs without leaving town. Johnnie's Super Market and Wild Roots Market handle real grocery shopping, there's a local pharmacy and a hardware store, and downtown has coffee, restaurants, and the basics. Our guide to essential shops in Boulder Creek maps out what's actually here. You learn to keep a fuller pantry and to batch your valley errands, and after a while the extra planning just becomes how you live.

The upside nobody has to sell you

Here's why people stay. You live in the redwoods, with cool green air and quiet that city transplants describe as almost startling at first. The pace is slower, the night sky is dark, and the river and Big Basin are minutes away. The community is genuinely tight, the kind of place where neighbors check on each other during outages and the whole town turns out for the Fourth of July parade. It's small, it's a little rough around the edges, and the people who love Boulder Creek love exactly that.

So is it a good place to live? If you want redwoods and community more than convenience, and you plan realistically for insurance, the commute, and the road, yes. If you need a short commute, cheap coverage, and a store around every corner, it'll wear on you. Only you can weigh that, and the point of this guide is to let you weigh it with real numbers instead of a brochure.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does a house cost in Boulder Creek?

As of mid-2026, roughly $600,000 to $800,000 depending on the source and the type of home, with single-family figures on the lower end and Zillow's typical-value estimate on the higher end. Cabins and steep lots run cheaper, and full homes on good land run more. Prices move, so check current figures before you rely on a number.

Is it hard to get home insurance in Boulder Creek?

Yes. It's a high fire-risk area, and many homeowners rely on the California FAIR Plan, the state's insurer of last resort, which costs more and covers less than a standard policy. FAIR Plan rates for the 95006 ZIP were proposed to rise sharply for renewals after April 2026. Get a real quote for the specific address before you buy.

How long is the commute from Boulder Creek?

Typically about 30 minutes to Santa Cruz, 40 to 50 minutes to San Jose, and 50 to 60 minutes to the Redwood City area on a good day. Highway 17 traffic and winter weather can make it longer, so test your real route at your real commute time.

Is Boulder Creek safe from wildfires?

It's a fire-prone area, and the 2020 CZU fire evacuated the whole town, so the reality is that you live with real risk and prepare for it. Sign up for CruzAware alerts, keep a go-bag in fire season, and know your evacuation routes. Preparation, not luck, is what makes it manageable.

Do you have to drive far for groceries?

Not for everyday shopping. Johnnie's Super Market and Wild Roots Market cover real groceries in town, and there's a local pharmacy and hardware store. You'll drive down to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz for a big-box store or a Costco run, so most locals keep a fuller pantry and batch their valley errands.

kids-birthday-party-boulder-creek.title: Planning a Kids Birthday Party in Boulder Creek
kids-birthday-party-boulder-creek.html:
Throwing a kids party up here used to mean a drive over the hill. It doesn't anymore. In town, you can reserve a park or the community center; in Felton, Roaring Camp runs package parties; and if you want a full-service play venue, there's one a short drive out in Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz. The rest travels to you now: face painters, entertainers, balloon stylists, and bounce-house companies come up the hill. One thing to know before you build a plan around a date. Most in-town spots book by application or by phone, not by clicking a button, and several don't post flat prices, so you'll be asking for a quote. What follows is where to reserve, how each place books, and then cake, entertainment, balloons, and rentals. Being listed here isn't an endorsement, only a place to start.

Where to Reserve in Town

The Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District (BCRPD) handles reservations for in-town party spots. It rents Junction Park, Garrahan Park, the Bear Creek Community Center, and the Downtown Rec Center, plus the Bear Creek pool. There's a sequence to it: read the posted rental policy first (the pool has its own separate rules), then submit the rental application. BCRPD doesn't publish flat fees online, so expect a quote. The downtown office answers at 831-338-4144 (Tuesday to Friday, 10 a.m. to 2 p.m.); the Bear Creek office is at 831-703-4135. Current forms are at bcrpd.org/rentals.

Junction Park is the default outdoor pick, a downtown riverside park with a small beach, river access, lawn, and picnic facilities. Garrahan Park on Highway 9 has a walking track, play structures, a gazebo, and a barbecue area. Both open sunrise to sunset, and both run through the same BCRPD application. Pool parties come with one gate: read the pool rental policy before you apply, so start there if water's the plan.

Roaring Camp Party Packages (Felton)

Roaring Camp Railroads at 5401 Graham Hill Road in Felton (831-335-4484) runs two birthday packages. The outdoor picnic package covers party favors (paper hats and train whistles) for up to 28 kids, an engineer's cap for the birthday child, and up to six checkered picnic tables. The train package adds a Roaring Camp engineer hat and scarf for the honoree plus 28 train tickets for a scheduled train. Add-ons run to face painting, puppet shows, gold panning, candle making, and a bounce house. Book through group sales at 831-335-4484, extension 135; pricing is quote-on-request. It's a fixture up here, and it books ahead in peak season.

Indoor and Rain-Proof Options

When you need a roof, the valley's community halls rent out for parties. Felton Community Hall at 6191 Highway 9 (831-335-5621) has been a Felton institution since 1926, and it rents its auditorium and meeting room. Email reservations@feltonhall.org or call, check the online calendar, then complete the rental agreement, policies, and required event insurance. The kitchen comes only with a room rental, and nonprofits get a discount. A little farther down the road, the Highlands Park Senior & Community Center at 8500 Highway 9 in Ben Lomond (831-336-8900) rents reasonably priced rooms and welcomes all ages, not seniors only. Rates are by inquiry at seniorcenterslv8500@gmail.com.

For a full-service indoor party with almost nothing to set up, Haven Play Lounge and Cafe in Scotts Valley (108 Whispering Pines Drive, Suite 125; 831-331-7349) runs birthday and event packages of one to four hours for ages 0 to 10, with decor, cleaning, and food handled by the venue. It's the closest turnkey indoor-play option, about 25 minutes over the hill, and hours vary by day, so confirm party availability directly. And Camp Harmon on Highway 9 in Boulder Creek (831-338-3383), the Easterseals accessible camp, arranges group and facility use; for an accessible outdoor setting, ask them directly whether party rentals fit their calendar.

Full-Service Party Venues (a short drive)

If you'd rather book a package and let the venue do the work, a handful of spots are worth the drive over the hill. Closest is Vargas Academy in Scotts Valley (831-438-8242), a gymnastics and parkour gym whose parties include an in-gym bounce house, gym apparatus, decorated tables, and staff coaches. In Santa Cruz, JuneBugs Gym (831-464-2847) and the Santa Cruz Gymnastics Center both run coach-led gymnastics parties with foam pits and obstacle courses, the Santa Cruz Roller Palladium does roller-skating parties, and Kianti's Pizza & Pasta Bar (831-469-4400) runs a pizza-making party with a kitchen tour and a dough-spinning lesson. For a bigger group, the Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk has party packages for bowling, Neptune's Kingdom mini-golf and arcade, and laser tag. Confirm ages, minimums, and dates when you book.

Cake and Treats

The cake itself has real options in town. Dessert First Bakery at 13090 Highway 9, Suite 2 (831-338-7008) makes custom celebration cakes, cupcakes, and cookies, and it's open daily. Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed Boulder Creek cottage bakery that takes custom orders (510-390-5548), and Wildstone Bakery turns out naturally fermented breads and pastries you can catch at regional farmers markets.

Entertainment

Entertainment used to be the hard part up here, and it isn't anymore. One company lists the San Lorenzo Valley right in its service area, which is the line you're hoping to find. Special Memories Children's Entertainment names Boulder Creek, Felton, Ben Lomond, and Scotts Valley, and it brings costumed characters, magicians, puppet shows, exotic-animal and reptile shows, pony rides, and balloon twisting, enough to cover most of a party in one booking.

Plenty of solid Santa Cruz companies serve the wider county without naming the valley specifically, so book them the way you'd book any of them: call first, and confirm they'll drive to your address. Those include Santa Cruz Party Company (clowns, princesses, magic, face paint, balloons), Happily Ever Laughter (characters, magic, puppets, sing-alongs), the Surfing Magician (a Santa Cruz magic show with a live rabbit), Fiesta Face Paint, SmileyOrca Facepaint & Henna, Brenda's Face Painting, and Primal Henna Arts.

For an animal theme, Pet Shop Santa Cruz (831-429-1242) brings reptiles and animals to parties and classrooms, and Tails of Redwood Trails in Felton runs horse and farm-animal experiences and travels through the valley. And Roaring Camp, again, bundles its own entertainers into its party packages if you want it all in one place.

Balloons and Decor

For balloons and styling, Luzovich Celebrations handles custom balloon work and party decor (831-818-5407, luzovichcelebrations@gmail.com). Bash Works (831-234-0530) does balloon garlands, arches, and marquee letters, and it also rents bounce houses, so it can pull double duty. And Aesthetic Events & Co. does balloon garlands plus party and sleepover rentals.

Party Rentals and Bounce Houses

Bounce houses, tents, tables, and chairs are the other piece to line up early. A few Santa Cruz County companies deliver: Astro Jump of Santa Cruz (831-475-5055), Santa Cruz Bounce House Rentals (831-515-6869), and Bash Works (831-234-0530) all rent inflatables and handle setup and takedown. For tables, chairs, and tents, A to Z Party Rental is a longtime Central Coast option. One thing to keep in mind: these are county-wide operators, and delivery up a winding mountain road sometimes carries a distance fee or a minimum order, so confirm delivery to your exact address and ZIP before you count on it.

Gifts From Local Shops

The gift can stay local too. Boulder Creek's downtown shops carry toys, books, and kid-friendly finds, and our directory lists the current retailers in town. Buying the present a few doors from the party keeps the money in the valley and saves you the over-the-hill trip.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I reserve a Boulder Creek park for a birthday party?

Go through the Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District. Review the posted rental policy for the park or facility you want (the pool has its own rules), then submit the rental application. There's no online flat-fee list, so you'll get a quote. Call the downtown office at 831-338-4144 or the Bear Creek office at 831-703-4135, or start at bcrpd.org.

Is there a kids party entertainer who comes to Boulder Creek?

Yes. Special Memories Children's Entertainment lists the San Lorenzo Valley in its service area. Several other Santa Cruz companies, including SmileyOrca Facepaint & Henna and Brenda's Face Painting, serve the wider county and will usually travel up here, so call to confirm they'll come to your address. Roaring Camp also bundles its own entertainers into its party packages.

Where can I rent a bounce house in Boulder Creek?

There's no bounce-house company based in the valley, but Astro Jump of Santa Cruz, Santa Cruz Bounce House Rentals, and Bash Works all deliver in Santa Cruz County. Because Boulder Creek is a longer drive, confirm they deliver to your address and ask about any distance fee or minimum before you book.

What's the best rain-proof party option in the valley?

For an indoor room you set up yourself, rent Felton Community Hall or the Highlands Park Senior & Community Center in Ben Lomond, both of which have kitchens available with a room rental. For a fully handled indoor party, Haven Play Lounge in Scotts Valley runs packages with decor, cleaning, and food included. Confirm dates and policies directly with each.

Where can I order a custom birthday cake in town?

Dessert First Bakery in Boulder Creek (831-338-7008) does custom celebration cakes and is open daily, and Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed local cottage bakery taking custom orders. Order ahead, especially for a specific date or design.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, only a verified place to start. Browse the full directory for local shops and services, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so the next family gets accurate information.

mountain-home-maintenance-calendar.title: The Mountain Home Maintenance Calendar
mountain-home-maintenance-calendar.html:
Owning a home in Boulder Creek runs on two seasons, not four: the wet one and the dry one. Get your drainage, roof, and trees ready before the rains, and your defensible space and generator ready before fire season, and most of the year takes care of itself. This calendar lays out that rhythm month by month. One honest caveat before we start: every parcel up here is different. A house on a sunny ridge and a cabin in a creek-bottom redwood grove have different problems, so treat this as the rhythm, not a rulebook, and lean on the pros and the official sources linked below for anything specific to your property.

January and February: Storm Season, Eyes Open

Deep winter is less about projects and more about watching your systems work. During and after each big storm, walk the property when it's safe to do so. Check that gutters are overflowing nowhere, that downspouts are moving water away from the foundation, and that the ditches and culverts that drain your driveway are running clear. A culvert that plugs mid-January is how driveways disappear, and clearing leaves now is far cheaper than repairing washout later.

Keep an eye on the trees you flagged in fall. Saturated ground plus wind is when limbs and whole trees come down. If anything falls into power lines, stay away and call 911; our emergency contacts list has the numbers for everything less urgent, including county road dispatch at (831) 477-3999.

Indoors, make this the season you're strict about alarms. The U.S. Fire Administration says to test smoke alarms monthly, whatever type you have, and if anything in your house burns fuel or you run a generator, a carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area is non-negotiable. The CDC counts more than 400 American deaths a year from CO, a gas you can't see or smell.

March and April: Assess the Damage, Start the Fire Work

When the storm train slows down, take stock. Look at the roof from the ground with binoculars for lifted shingles, debris dams, and moss that holds moisture against the roofing. Check decks, railings, and stairs for winter rot. Note where water pooled or cut channels this winter and fix the grading or drainage while the memory is fresh; the county's storm preparedness page links a good home drainage guide if you want a starting point.

This is also when defensible space season begins in earnest, while the vegetation is still green and soft. CAL FIRE's defensible space guidance organizes the work in zones: the first 5 feet from the house should be ember-resistant, with hardscape instead of combustible mulch and nothing dead or dry against the structure; within 30 feet, keep things lean, clean, and green; and out to 100 feet or your property line, reduce fuel, with grass mowed to four inches or less. State law, Public Resources Code 4291, requires 100 feet of defensible space, and it's a year-round obligation, not a one-day deadline. Spring is simply when the work is most pleasant and the growth is easiest to cut.

May and June: Finish Defensible Space Before the Heat

By late spring, the goal is to have the heavy defensible space work done: dead vegetation removed, tree branches limbed up at least 6 feet from the ground per CAL FIRE, wood piles relocated out of the first 30 feet, and the roof and gutters cleared of needles again, because they're ember beds in summer and dams in winter. CAL FIRE and its partners conduct defensible space inspections in the State Responsibility Area that covers much of our valley, so if an inspector visits, this is the work they're looking at. Don't count on a warning before an inspection, and don't need one.

June is also generator month, because Public Safety Power Shutoff season arrives with the dry winds. Start the generator, run it under load, check your fuel supply, and reread the two safety rules that matter most: outdoors only, more than 20 feet from windows, doors, and vents per the CDC, and never plugged into house wiring without a proper transfer switch. Your owner's manual sets the maintenance schedule; the habit of testing before you need it is the part we'll insist on. The whole setup is covered in our power outage plan.

July and August: Vigilance, Water, and the Small Stuff

High summer is maintenance-light and awareness-heavy. Keep the first five feet around the house bare of dry debris, since redwood duff keeps falling all summer. Confirm your CruzAware alert registration is current, especially if you've changed phones or carriers, and make sure your PG&E outage alerts are set.

Use the slow months for the unglamorous items. Refresh the emergency kit against ready.gov's list, rotating stored water, one gallon per person per day for several days, and swapping out expired food and medications. If you're on a private well, summer is a sensible time for any pump or tank service, when a day without water is an annoyance instead of a crisis. If you're on district water, the San Lorenzo Valley Water District at (831) 338-2153 can answer system questions; our utilities guide sorts out who serves what.

September and October: The Big Push Before the Rains

This is the most important stretch of the calendar, and the one where procrastination costs real money. Before the first atmospheric river arrives, clean gutters and downspouts, clear every ditch, drain, and culvert you own, and get roof repairs done while roofers can still get to you. Walk the property and deal with dead limbs over the house, cars, and power drops; tree crews book out quickly once storms are in the forecast, so call early. If your place heats with a wood stove, have the stove and chimney serviced before burning season starts.

Two more fall jobs. First, if you heat with propane, get the tank filled before winter, when delivery schedules tighten for everyone. Second, restock the storm side of your kit: flashlights, batteries, a radio, and sandbags if your parcel needs them, which the county distributes at posted locations each storm season via the storm preparedness page. Our winter storm checklist turns this whole section into a single afternoon's work.

November and December: Switch to Storm Mode

By Thanksgiving, you want to be done preparing and simply operating. Keep vehicles fueled ahead of big systems, check drainage after each of the first storms, and keep the generator's fuel fresh and its extension cords where you can find them in the dark. Test the smoke and CO alarms with the monthly routine, and if you have a 9-volt-battery alarm, the USFA says replace that battery at least once a year, so pick a date you'll remember and make it annual.

December is also a good month for the paperwork nobody enjoys: photograph the house and outbuildings for insurance, back up the photos somewhere off the mountain, and confirm your policy reflects what's actually on the parcel.

The Recurring Beat, All Year

A few items ignore the seasons. Test smoke alarms monthly and replace any alarm that's 10 years old, per the USFA. If you're on septic, like most of Boulder Creek, the EPA says to have the system inspected at least every three years and expect to pump the tank every three to five, with yearly checks for systems that have pumps or floats. Put both on a calendar you'll actually see, because neither one reminds you until it fails, and a failed drainfield is one of the most expensive surprises a mountain house can serve up.

Frequently Asked Questions

When is the deadline for defensible space work?

There isn't a single statewide date. CAL FIRE frames the 100 feet of defensible space required by Public Resources Code 4291 as an ongoing obligation, maintained year-round, and its crews inspect properties in the State Responsibility Area on their own schedule. Practically, do the heavy cutting in spring and keep it maintained through fire season.

How often should a septic system be pumped in Boulder Creek?

The EPA's guidance is an inspection at least every three years and pumping typically every three to five years, more often for systems with mechanical parts. Your actual interval depends on tank size and household size, so ask your septic professional to put a recommendation in writing after the next inspection.

What's the most important maintenance month of the year?

October, and it's not close. Gutters, culverts, roof, trees, and storm supplies all need to be squared away before the first big rain, and every trade you might need is busiest right then. If you only ever plan one maintenance weekend a year, make it early October and work from our winter storm checklist.

Do I really need to test smoke alarms every month?

That's the U.S. Fire Administration's standing guidance for every alarm type: test monthly, replace 9-volt and backup batteries at least yearly, and replace the whole unit every 10 years. In a wooden house surrounded by forest, with winter fires burning and generators running, it's cheap discipline.

My property doesn't match this calendar. What then?

That's normal up here. Slope, sun, trees, water source, and road access change the priorities parcel by parcel, and this calendar can't see your land. Use it as the skeleton, then let a good local arborist, septic professional, and the official sources linked above fill in the specifics for your place.

new-resident-quick-start-boulder-creek.title: New to Boulder Creek? The Quick-Start Guide
new-resident-quick-start-boulder-creek.html:
Nobody hands you a manual when you move to Boulder Creek, which is a shame, because a mountain town runs on a different set of rules than wherever you came from. There's no city hall. Your water may come from a district office on the main drag, your trash day probably won't match your neighbor's across the valley, and the most useful thing you can do in your first week is sign up for an emergency alert before you've unpacked a single box. So consider this the manual. In rough order: get on CruzAware for alerts, put PG&E and water in your name, set up GreenWaste for trash, call your school's registrar if you've got kids, and grab a library card. Boulder Creek is unincorporated Santa Cruz County, so your services come from a patchwork of county departments and special districts rather than one city office. Hours and details change, so treat each link below as the last word.

Sign up for emergency alerts before you unpack

This is mountain country, and fire season, winter storms, and power outages come with the address. The county's wildfire preparedness page tells residents to register for CruzAware, the official Santa Cruz County alert system, and that's the first account to make at your new place. It takes a few minutes online.

While you're at it, set up outage alerts from PG&E. Account holders get notified about outages at their service address automatically once their contact info is current, and you can follow any address on the outage map without an account. If PG&E calls a Public Safety Power Shutoff during high fire risk, alerts go to account holders without a separate signup.

One more number for your phone: the county's 24-hour road dispatch line, 831-477-3999, listed on the Public Works contact page. That's who you call when a tree or slide blocks a county road. If a line is down or anyone's in danger, call 911 first.

Put the utilities in your name

Electricity and gas come from PG&E. Start there if the previous owner or tenant hasn't already closed the account.

Water for most homes in town comes from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, whose office sits right on the main drag at 13060 Highway 9. To start service, fill out the form on the district's Start/Stop Service page at least 24 business hours before you need water in your name, and expect a $20 establishment fee on your first monthly bill. One quirk for renters: the district keeps accounts in the property owner's name, so if you're leasing, your landlord handles the water account. The office is open weekdays 8:30 a.m. to noon and 1 to 5 p.m., and the main number, 831-338-2153, routes to an answering service for water emergencies after hours.

Not every property is on district water, and plenty of homes up here run on septic rather than sewer. If you're buying, ask directly whether the house is on district water or a well, and where the septic tank and leach field sit. Get that in writing before escrow closes, not after.

Trash, recycling, and organics

Garbage service in unincorporated Santa Cruz County, Boulder Creek included, comes from GreenWaste. Call 831-426-2711 or email SantaCruzCS@GreenWaste.com to set up carts, confirm your collection day, and ask about backyard service if your driveway is the kind of driveway this town is famous for. Collection days vary street by street, so don't assume yours matches a neighbor's across the valley.

For big cleanouts, the county runs its own disposal facilities; the Public Works page lists 831-454-2430 for landfill and recycling questions.

Enrolling kids in school

Boulder Creek sits in the San Lorenzo Valley Unified School District, which covers TK through 12th grade for the whole valley. Enrollment starts with a person, not a portal: the district's enrollment page says to contact your school's registrar first to set up pre-enrollment, after which you'll get online registration instructions to finish within a week.

Have these ready: proof of your child's age, immunization records, an oral health assessment for TK, kindergarten, and first grade, and proof of address, which can be a utility bill, lease, property tax receipt, or similar. For the 2026-27 year, kindergartners need to be five on or before September 1, and TK students need to turn four by that date.

The neighborhood elementary school is Boulder Creek Elementary at 400 Lomond Street, a short walk from downtown, and its site carries current 2026-27 registration information. If you have enrollment questions the registrar can't answer, the district's Student Services office is at 831-336-9678.

Get a library card

The Boulder Creek branch of Santa Cruz Public Libraries sits at 13390 West Park Avenue, a block off the highway, and it punches well above its weight after a full remodel. Current hours are Monday through Thursday 10 a.m. to 6 p.m., Friday 11 a.m. to 5 p.m., and Saturday 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., closed Sunday. Free WiFi, computers, kids' programs, and a local history collection make it one of the best rainy-day rooms in the valley.

Getting to Santa Cruz without a car

Santa Cruz METRO runs Route 35, the Highway 9/Scotts Valley line, which connects Boulder Creek to Felton, Scotts Valley, and downtown Santa Cruz. Its Boulder Creek timepoints include Highway 9 at Middleton Avenue in the middle of town and stops out to the Country Club end of Big Basin Way. On weekdays there's also Route 35X, the Santa Cruz/Boulder Creek Express, which skips part of the Scotts Valley routing during commute hours. Schedules shift with service changes, so check the live pages before you build a commute around them.

Fire, sheriff, and who to actually call

Fire protection comes from the Boulder Creek Fire Protection District, with its station at 13230 Central Avenue downtown. The office line, 831-338-7222, is for non-emergency business on weekdays 8 to 5; emergencies always go through 911.

There's no Boulder Creek police department. Law enforcement is the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office, which staffs a Boulder Creek Service Center at 13210 Central Avenue with a community deputy; the center's number is 831-454-7440. For things that need a deputy but not lights and sirens, the non-emergency dispatch line is 831-471-1121, per Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1.

Where to get the everyday basics

You can cover most errands without leaving town. Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9 has anchored Boulder Creek since 1938 and runs a full grocery with produce, meat, a deli, and a serious wine and spirits section, open daily from 8 a.m. Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural-foods counterpart, open daily 9 to 9 with a deli kitchen and local produce. Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue is an independent pharmacy with free delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, open weekdays 9 to 6 and Saturday 10 to 4. And for the endless small fixes an older mountain house demands, Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 stocks Ace Hardware lines seven days a week.

Your first weekend

Walk the town. The Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District runs Junction Park at Middleton and Railroad Avenues, with a small beach and river access a block from downtown, and Garrahan Park at 15555 Highway 9, with a walking track, soccer field, and play structures, open sunrise to sunset. Dogs are welcome on leash at both. The district's downtown office at 831-338-4144 can fill you in on classes, camps, and community events, which are the fastest way to stop feeling new here.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who provides trash service in Boulder Creek?

GreenWaste handles garbage, recycling, and organics for unincorporated Santa Cruz County, including Boulder Creek. Call 831-426-2711 to set up service and confirm your street's collection day, since days vary by route.

What school district serves Boulder Creek?

San Lorenzo Valley Unified School District, headquartered in Ben Lomond. Start enrollment by contacting the registrar at your child's school; Boulder Creek Elementary is the in-town elementary campus.

Is there a bus from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz?

Yes. METRO Route 35 runs along Highway 9 through Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz, and the weekday Route 35X express makes the trip faster during commute hours. Check the live schedules on METRO's route pages before relying on a specific trip.

Who do I call when a tree comes down across the road?

For county-maintained roads, the 24-hour road dispatch line is 831-477-3999. If the tree took wires down with it, stay well away and call 911 first. Anything threatening life or property is a 911 call, full stop.

How do I sign up for emergency alerts?

Register at CruzAware, the county's official alert system, and add PG&E outage alerts through your account. Both take a few minutes, and they matter more here than almost anywhere else you've lived.

night-out-boulder-creek.title: A Night Out in and Around Boulder Creek
night-out-boulder-creek.html:
Downtown Boulder Creek doesn't have much of a marquee glow after dark, but a few doors stay lit and worth walking toward. Inside town, that's Joe's Bar for drinks and live music, the lounge at Scopazzi's, and The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse for craft beer and a patio stage, with Big Basin Vineyards' tasting room open weekend afternoons. Drive a few minutes down the valley and two full live-music rooms open up: Henflings Tavern in Ben Lomond and Felton Music Hall in Felton. What follows is what's actually open when you want to go out, with the facts we can verify.

Joe's Bar

Address: 13118 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 338-9417. This is a 21-and-over venue.

Joe's Bar is the downtown bar and live-music room, the anchor of a Boulder Creek night out. Live music turns up regularly, and there's a pool tournament on Thursdays, so there's usually a reason to stop in. Check Joe's current calendar for the week's lineup. It's a 21-and-over place, so bring ID. Our listing doesn't carry confirmed opening hours, so call (831) 338-9417 if you want to line up a specific night.

Scopazzi's lounge

Address: 13300 Big Basin Way, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 338-4444. Dinner and lounge: Wednesday through Sunday from 4:00 p.m. Closed Monday and Tuesday.

Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge is the historic Italian restaurant in town, and the lounge is the quieter, sit-down side of an evening out. A drink and a table rather than a stage. It runs Wednesday through Sunday from 4 p.m., and it's closed Monday and Tuesday. Think of it as the spot for a calmer evening or a nightcap after dinner rather than a late night.

The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse

Address: 15520 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 610-8196.

The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse sits just south of downtown, an American restaurant and alehouse that pairs craft beer with outdoor seating and a music stage under the redwoods. On a warm evening it's the closest thing in town to dinner, drinks, and live music in one place. We don't have verified current hours for it, so check by phone at (831) 610-8196 before you plan around a specific night or a show.

Big Basin Vineyards tasting room

Address: 830 Memory Lane, Boulder Creek. Hours: Saturday and Sunday, 12:00 to 5:00 p.m.

Big Basin Vineyards Estate Vineyard & Tasting Room pours its mountain-grown wines at the estate vineyard on weekend afternoons. This one's a daytime stop, so it fits an early Saturday or Sunday plan more than a night out, but it belongs on any list of where to go for a drink around here. Our listing doesn't carry a public phone number, so use the winery's visit page to check current details and any reservation requirements.

Up the valley: Henflings Tavern, Ben Lomond

Address: 9450 Highway 9, Ben Lomond. Phone: (831) 289-3019. Hours: daily, 12:00 p.m. to 1:30 a.m.

Henflings Tavern has been a Santa Cruz Mountains tavern in downtown Ben Lomond since 1946, about ten minutes down Highway 9. Live music every weekend, a recurring Sunday Pro Jam, a full bar, a food menu, and a covered patio, and it's open daily until 1:30 a.m., which makes it the late-night option the valley doesn't have north of here. If Boulder Creek is winding down and you're not ready to, this is where the night keeps going.

Up the valley: Felton Music Hall, Felton

Address: 6275 Highway 9, Felton. Phone: (831) 704-7113.

Felton Music Hall is the dedicated live-music venue in Felton, presenting local and touring acts across genres, with craft food and cocktails from a full bar served in-house. It's the room to check when you want a real show with a lineup rather than a bar with a band in the corner. Shows and hours vary by the calendar, so use the venue's site to see who's playing and when doors open before you drive down.

What a Boulder Creek night out doesn't include

A few things worth naming. Boulder Creek doesn't have a nightclub, and the only place in town proper built for a late night is Joe's Bar. Big Basin Vineyards is weekend afternoons only, and Scopazzi's lounge keeps restaurant hours, so once those wind down, the genuinely late options are down the valley at Henflings. Plan a designated driver or a ride for the Highway 9 stretch, and check ages: Joe's Bar is 21 and over. For where to eat before you go out, see our dinner guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where can I hear live music near Boulder Creek?

In town, Joe's Bar regularly hosts live music, and The Sawmill has a stage on its patio. Down the valley, Henflings Tavern in Ben Lomond has live music every weekend plus a Sunday Pro Jam, and Felton Music Hall in Felton books local and touring acts. Check each venue's site for the current lineup.

What's open latest for a night out?

Henflings Tavern in Ben Lomond is open daily until 1:30 a.m., the latest confirmed closing time in this guide. In Boulder Creek proper, Joe's Bar is the late option, though we don't have its confirmed hours, so call ahead.

Is there anywhere to go out on a weeknight?

Yes. Joe's Bar has a pool tournament on Thursdays and regularly hosts live music, so check its current calendar for the week's lineup. Scopazzi's lounge is open Wednesday and Thursday evenings. Henflings is open daily if you're up for the short drive.

Can I go wine tasting near Boulder Creek?

Yes. Big Basin Vineyards runs its estate tasting room at 830 Memory Lane on Saturday and Sunday from noon to 5:00 p.m. It's an afternoon visit rather than an evening one, so plan accordingly.

A venue I know is missing. Why?

We list places we can point to through their own page or a current official listing. If a bar, tasting room, or music venue is missing or a detail has changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

parks-playgrounds-boulder-creek.title: Every Park a Kid Can Play At in the Valley
parks-playgrounds-boulder-creek.html:
Ask around town for a park with a real playground and you'll get a few different answers, because it depends on what your kid wants that afternoon: a swing, a river to splash in, a field to run out, or a ramp to skate. Here's what each spot near Boulder Creek actually has, versus what people assume it has. The rec district runs three parks close to town, the county runs a bigger playground-and-skate-park stop a few minutes down the valley, and the state parks are for hiking and nature days, not playgrounds, with their own rules. We only list amenities we could confirm; if a park isn't described as having something, assume it doesn't until you check.

Junction Park (Boulder Creek)

Junction Park sits a block off the main street at Middleton Avenue and Railroad Avenue, and it's the heart of family life in town. There's a small beach, San Lorenzo River access, a lawn, and picnic facilities. The river draws a crowd on summer afternoons, and it is swim at your own risk, with no lifeguard and conditions that change through the season, so young kids need close supervision near the water. Junction Park also hosts the Thursday Boulder Creek Farmers Market and, in summer, the free Water Park Wednesdays playgroup from the rec district. One thing worth knowing: during the Thursday market, only certified service dogs are allowed at Junction Park, per the county Health Department.

Barbara Day Park (Boulder Creek)

Barbara Day Park, also called Dam Park, is a small public park at the Boulder Creek dam, a short way downstream from Junction Park. It's a quieter riverside spot rather than a playground destination, worth knowing when Junction is busy but not the place to go looking for play structures.

Garrahan Park (Boulder Creek)

Garrahan Park at 15555 Highway 9 is where you go when you want playgrounds and space. It has a walking track, a soccer field, play structures, a gazebo, benches, and a barbecue area, and it's open sunrise to sunset. That mix makes it the spot for a picnic-and-playground afternoon, a kids' soccer kickabout, or a birthday gathering with a grill. It's up Highway 9 from downtown, so it's a quick drive rather than a walk from the town center.

Bear Creek Community Center grounds (Boulder Creek)

The Bear Creek Community Center at 15685 Forest Hill Drive is the rec district's larger facility, and its grounds include the Bear Creek pool, open seasonally in summer with recreational swim most afternoons. It's also where the rec district runs programs like the Circle preschool-age class and community events such as summer pool-and-movie nights. Center and pool hours vary by season, so check the BCRPD site for the current schedule and pool passes before you go.

Highlands County Park (Ben Lomond)

A few minutes down Highway 9 in Ben Lomond, Highlands County Park at 8500 Highway 9 brings together a playground, a skate park, ball fields, tennis courts, a nature trail, and picnic areas, open sunrise to sunset. The same property holds a couple of the valley's community buildings, including the Highlands Park Senior and Community Center and the Highlands House event venue, plus Wilder Hall, where the county runs its Kreative Kids preschool program. For a lot of valley families it's the default birthday-party and skate-session park, worth the short drive even if you rarely leave Boulder Creek.

State parks: nature days, not playgrounds

The redwood state parks around Boulder Creek are for hiking and nature, and they play by different rules than a neighborhood park. At Henry Cowell Redwoods in Felton, the main entrance has a flat, family-friendly redwood loop, but the Fall Creek unit has no facilities and allows no dogs, bikes, or fires. Big Basin Redwoods is open for limited day use only as it recovers from the 2020 fire, with first-come parking, day-use fees, and no water except at the kiosk. Castle Rock up on Highway 35 charges $10 for parking and locks its gate at sunset. The pattern holds across all of them: state parks generally keep dogs off trails, run on day-use hours, and charge parking fees, so they're a plan-ahead nature outing rather than a swing-by playground. Confirm access and rules on each park's official page the day you go.

The gaps worth naming

A couple of things are worth naming. Boulder Creek doesn't have an indoor play space, so rainy-day play means the library or a drive over the hill. And while the valley has other county open spaces, the parks above are the ones we can describe from verified amenity data. If you're eyeing a spot we haven't detailed here, check the county parks site for what it actually offers before you count on a playground being there.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which park near Boulder Creek has the best playground?

For play structures and space in town, Garrahan Park has a walking track, soccer field, play structures, a gazebo, and barbecues. For the larger option, Highlands County Park in Ben Lomond adds a skate park, ball fields, tennis courts, and a nature trail. Both are open sunrise to sunset. It comes down to whether you want in-town convenience or the larger county stop.

Where can kids swim near Boulder Creek?

The river beach at Junction Park is the classic summer swim spot, and it's swim at your own risk with no lifeguard. The rec district's Bear Creek pool at 15685 Forest Hill Drive is open seasonally with afternoon recreational swim; check BCRPD for current hours and passes.

Are dogs allowed at the parks?

Rules vary by park, so check each one. During the Thursday farmers market, only certified service dogs are allowed at Junction Park, per the county Health Department. State parks generally do not allow dogs on trails; the Fall Creek unit at Henry Cowell prohibits dogs, bikes, and fires.

Is there an indoor place to play on a rainy day?

Not in Boulder Creek itself. The library is the best local indoor option, especially on program days. The nearest dedicated indoor play lounge is in Scotts Valley, about a 25 to 35 minute drive.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, only a starting point. See the full directory for details, and if a park's amenities or hours have changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit so the next family gets it right.

poison-oak-and-plants-to-avoid.title: Poison Oak and the Plants to Avoid on Santa Cruz Mountains Trails
poison-oak-and-plants-to-avoid.html:
Three things to know before your next trail day. First, poison oak is everywhere in these mountains and it changes costume with the seasons, including a winter disguise as bare sticks that can still give you a rash. Second, if you touch it, washing within the first minutes matters enormously; after half an hour, the oil is mostly absorbed. Third, the phone number for any plant question or exposure is California Poison Control at 1-800-222-1222, free, 24 hours a day, and anything severe, especially trouble breathing, is a 911 call. The rest of this article is the detail: how to recognize poison oak year-round, what to do after contact, when to see a doctor, and the two other locals worth knowing, stinging nettle and wild mushrooms.

None of this is a reason to stay inside. Longtime residents hike here their whole lives with nothing worse than an occasional itchy forearm. They just know the plant.

Learning to See Poison Oak

"Leaves of three, let it be" is the right starting point. UC's statewide integrated pest management program describes Pacific poison oak's signature: leaflets in threes (occasionally more), with the middle leaflet on a longer stalk than the two side ones. The leaves are lobed a bit like a true oak's, and they can be glossy or dull, which is why shine alone isn't a reliable tell. The plant itself is a shapeshifter, growing as a shrub in the open or a climbing vine winding up trees and fences, and it likes exactly the places we like: trail edges, creek banks, oak woodland, sunny clearings. Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park puts it plainly: poison oak flourishes and is native in the Santa Cruz Mountains.

The seasonal costume changes are where newcomers get caught:

Spring: new leaves emerge green or light red, soft and innocent-looking, often right at the edge of the trail at leg height.

Summer: full green leaves, glossy or dull, blending into every other shrub. This is the season to be strict about the leaves-of-three check.

Fall: the leaves turn what UC IPM calls attractive shades of orange and red, and the plant is at its most photogenic and most picked-by-visitors. Those pretty red sprigs are not for bouquets.

Winter: the leaves drop, and this is the trap. What's left looks like a harmless thicket of bare gray-brown sticks. UC IPM warns that "bare stems in the winter can result in unintentional contact," because the oil is present in stems, roots, leaves, and even the skin of the berries, not just the foliage. A winter trail-clearing session with bare hands, or firewood gathered from the wrong thicket, can produce a rash as convincing as any summer hike. If you can't identify a bare-stemmed shrub along a trail edge, treat it as poison oak and keep your hands off.

Two mechanics worth knowing. UC IPM notes the plant usually has to be damaged, bruised, brushed hard, or broken, to release the oil, so walking past intact leaves isn't automatically exposure. And the oil, urushiol, is patient: it "can persist on objects and retain its ability to cause an allergic response for months or even years." That's why the dog, the gloves, the bootlaces, and the loppers keep re-gifting the rash long after the hike.

If You Touch It: The First Half Hour

Washing is a race against absorption. UC IPM's numbers are sobering: wash within about 10 minutes and you may remove around half the oil; by 15 minutes, a quarter; by 30 minutes, a tenth; after that, it's essentially all absorbed. So the moment you realize you've contacted poison oak, stop and wash then, not back at the car.

On the method, the two authorities we trust say it slightly differently, so here's both. California Poison Control says to wash the affected skin with lukewarm water right away, and that rubbing alcohol or an over-the-counter skin cleanser can help wash away the oil. UC IPM describes forceful, one-direction washing with a damp washcloth and liquid dishwashing soap under running water. The shared core: soon, thoroughly, with water and something that cuts oil. Carrying a small bottle of water and a travel soap in the trail bag turns this from advice into an option.

Then decontaminate everything else. Poison Control is specific: wash your clothes, tools, strollers, wheelchairs, and pets after outdoor time, because they carry the oil too. A dog that romped through a patch is a fur-covered urushiol applicator, and petting it counts as contact.

The Rash, and When to Get Help
