If you're sensitized, the reaction arrives as itching and redness followed by water blisters, typically developing over one to several days after exposure, per UC IPM. One myth worth killing: the fluid in those blisters doesn't spread the rash. Both UC IPM and Poison Control state the blister serum contains no urushiol. The rash "spreading" over days is usually areas that got less oil reacting later, or fresh contact from contaminated gear.

Most cases are miserable but manageable at home. California Poison Control says to contact a healthcare provider if the rash covers much of your body, if you have many blisters or swelling, especially on the eyelids, face, or genitals, or if you have trouble breathing or swallowing. Trouble breathing or swallowing means 911, not a waiting room. And for everything in between, the Poison Hotline at 1-800-222-1222 exists precisely for the "is this bad?" call. It's free, expert, and staffed around the clock, and calling when you're not sure is exactly what it's for.

Stinging Nettle: The Other Trailside Surprise

Along creeks and damp shady spots, which describes half of Boulder Creek, you'll meet stinging nettle. UC IPM notes it favors moist areas around creeks and rivers, and mature plants commonly stand taller than a person. The mechanism is genuinely a needle: hollow hairs on the leaves and stems break at a touch and inject irritants under the skin. The result is fast reddish patches with itching and burning, and UC IPM notes a tingling that can persist more than 12 hours even after the visible reaction fades.

The good news is nettle is honest. The sting is immediate, so unlike poison oak you know the moment it happens, and it resolves on its own in most cases. Long pants on creekside trails prevent nearly all of it. If a reaction seems outsized or a child gets a large exposure, the same hotline applies: 1-800-222-1222.

Wild Mushrooms: The One Absolute Rule

This one gets its own section because the stakes are different. Do not eat wild mushrooms here. Not ones that look like store mushrooms, not ones a confident friend picked, none. California Poison Control's guidance is unambiguous: "the only safe mushrooms are those purchased at established food markets." These mountains grow the death cap, Amanita phalloides, which Poison Control links to liver damage and the most serious illnesses and deaths, and the state health department warned in late 2025 about an outbreak of deadly amatoxin poisoning tied to wild, foraged mushrooms in California.

The cruel detail is the delay: Poison Control notes symptoms like abdominal pain, cramping, vomiting, and diarrhea typically develop 6 to 12 hours after eating, long after dinner seemed fine. If someone has eaten a wild mushroom and feels sick, call 911 immediately, then the Poison Hotline at 1-800-222-1222. Admiring mushrooms is a great mountain hobby; eating them from the forest floor is not one we'll ever encourage, and this pairs with the picking rules in our huckleberry article, since collecting anything is prohibited in the state parks anyway.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I recognize poison oak in winter when there are no leaves?

You often can't with certainty, and that's the point: the bare gray-brown stems still carry the oil, and UC IPM warns winter stems cause unintentional exposures. Treat unidentified bare-stemmed thickets along trails and creeks as poison oak, wear gloves and long sleeves for winter yard and trail work, and wash up afterward regardless.

I touched poison oak. What exactly do I do?

Wash the skin as soon as possible, ideally within minutes, with water and soap or an oil-cutting cleanser; Poison Control suggests lukewarm water right away, with rubbing alcohol or a skin cleanser to help lift the oil. Then wash the clothes, tools, and the dog. If a rash develops and is severe, widespread, or on the face or eyes, contact a doctor; for trouble breathing, call 911. Questions at any point: 1-800-222-1222.

Does scratching or blister fluid spread the rash?

No. Both UC IPM and Poison Control confirm blister fluid contains no urushiol. New patches usually mean delayed reactions or re-exposure from contaminated clothing, gear, or pets, which is why the laundry step matters as much as the shower.

Are any wild mushrooms around Boulder Creek safe to eat?

Our answer is no, full stop. California Poison Control says the only safe mushrooms are from established food markets, and death caps grow in California's live oak country. Deadly lookalikes fool experienced foragers, and symptoms can arrive many hours late. If someone eats one and feels sick: 911 first, then 1-800-222-1222.

What should go in the trail bag for plant problems?

A small bottle of water, travel-size soap or alcohol wipes, and gloves if you might clear brush. That covers the poison oak first aid window on the spot. Save the two numbers now: Poison Control 1-800-222-1222, and 911 for emergencies. Our wildlife article covers the animal side of the same trails.

san-lorenzo-valley-museum-guide.title: The San Lorenzo Valley Museum: What to See and How to Support It
san-lorenzo-valley-museum-guide.html:
Here's what you need to know before anything else: the San Lorenzo Valley Museum is free, and it's open more than most people think. The main Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek is open Wednesday through Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m. A second gallery, the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery at 6299 Gushee Street in Felton, is open Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday, same afternoon hours. Donations are appreciated, but nobody's standing at the door with a cash box.

And 2026 is the year to finally go, because the historical society behind the museum is turning 50. The whole year is built around that anniversary, with a retrospective exhibit, monthly heritage demonstrations, and a big dinner in August. Hours and programs do change, so confirm at slvmuseum.org before a special trip.

Two galleries, both former churches

The museum's odd superpower is its buildings. Both galleries are 19th- and early-20th-century churches, and both are listed on the National Register of Historic Places, which means the containers are exhibits too.

The Boulder Creek building is the original Grace Episcopal Church, a small Gothic Revival church built in 1906 and listed on the National Register in 2006 for its architecture: the steep gable roofs, pointed arches, and tracery you can still see from Highway 9. The historical society notes the church originally stood in what was the separate town of Lorenzo, a settlement south of Boulder Creek proper that faded away while its church survived. If you've read our short history of Boulder Creek, this is the building that closes that story.

The Felton gallery has a longer resume. It began as the Felton Presbyterian Church, dedicated in 1893, then spent decades as Felton's public library under the name Faye G. Belardi Memorial Library before the library moved to a new building in 2019. It was listed on the National Register back in 1978. The historical society opened it as its second gallery in 2020, and it now carries the Belardi name forward as the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery.

Fifty years of the historical society

The museum didn't start with either building. The society was founded in Boulder Creek in March 1976 by three locals, Ralph Wilcox, Walt Bachrach, and John Holm, as the Boulder Creek Historical Society. For about twenty years its museum lived in a second-floor office downtown, the kind of operation you'd only find if you knew to look.

December 1999 changed that, when the society opened the museum in the renovated Grace Episcopal Church building. In 2012 the organization renamed itself the San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society to match the territory it actually covers, from Boulder Creek down through Brookdale, Ben Lomond, and Felton. The Felton gallery followed in 2020, and the two-gallery setup lets the museum run a long-term exhibit in one building while rotating shows through the other.

What's on in 2026

The anniversary year has a full calendar. As of mid-July 2026, here's what the museum has posted:

Retrospect, at the Grace gallery in Boulder Creek through late 2026, looks back at the historical society's own first 50 years. It's the anniversary show, and a good first visit if you've never been.

The Road I Call Home, running through September 20, 2026, brings photographer Randy Bacon's portrait work documenting 32 people experiencing homelessness. It's a heavier show than the museum's usual fare, and worth your time.

Americana is an open call for submissions: the museum is asking local artists for 2D work on American cultural themes, with submissions accepted August 1 through 31, 2026. If you make art in the valley, this is your invitation.

Heritage Demonstrations run monthly from April through August on the third Saturday, with hands-on demonstrations of the skills that built the valley.

The centerpiece is the 50th Anniversary Dinner & Auction on Saturday, August 8, 2026, at Scopazzi's in Boulder Creek, one of the town's longest-running restaurants and a fitting room for it. (If dinner out is your angle, our guide to eating in Boulder Creek covers Scopazzi's and everyone else.) Tickets and details are on the museum site.

The archives are the deep end

The galleries are what most visitors see, but the archives are what make the museum matter to anyone researching a house, a family, or a piece of valley history. The collection at the Boulder Creek building includes local newspapers from the 1890s to the present, business and organizational records, maps, forestry archives, family records, and roughly 5,000 photographs in the Fredda C. Carr collection, shot around the valley between 1950 and 1970.

Access is by appointment: call (831) 338-8382 or email archives@slvmuseum.org. There's also an online research request form if you'd rather the staff dig for you. The policies are friendly by archive standards. Non-flash photography of materials is allowed, and the museum will provide scans for non-commercial use on request.

The society also keeps a reference library of about 900 local and regional history books at the Felton gallery, browsable during regular hours or by appointment through library@slvmuseum.org, with the full catalog searchable online through LibraryThing.

How to support it

Free admission is a gift, and it only works if enough people give back. Four honest ways to do that, in rough order of commitment:

Visit and drop something in the donation box. The lowest bar, and it counts.

Become a member. Membership and donations run through Zeffy links on the museum's site. Members get a 15 percent discount in the museum store and the printed newsletter. The site has current levels and pricing.

Volunteer as a docent. Docents welcome visitors at both galleries, handle the bookstore, and guide people through the exhibits. The museum asks for a one-year commitment at a minimum of three hours a month, provides training, and gives volunteers a one-year individual membership plus a volunteers-only recognition event. For a three-hour monthly shift, that's a fair trade.

Adopt a native plant bed. The Grace gallery grounds double as Flora, a living exhibition of native plants and their historic uses, and the museum runs an Adopt-a-Native-Plant-Bed program for people who'd rather contribute with a trowel than a checkbook.

If you can't make it in person

The museum's Museum From Home page is better than most small museums manage. There are virtual exhibits on women's suffrage in Santa Cruz County, valley ingenuity ("Gadgets & Gizmos"), the San Lorenzo Valley's Chinese community, and grizzly bear encounters, plus a feature on Google Arts & Culture, a photograph and postcard collection on Flickr, and an active blog. It's a good rainy-week rabbit hole, and a good preview before you visit.

One housekeeping note: the museum's site is slvmuseum.org. The .com version of the address redirects there, so either gets you to the right place, but the .org is the one to save.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does the San Lorenzo Valley Museum cost?

Nothing. Admission to both galleries is free, and donations are appreciated. Memberships and donations fund the operation, so if you visit regularly, joining is the fair-play move.

When is the museum open?

As of July 2026: the Grace Episcopal Gallery in Boulder Creek is open Wednesday through Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m., and the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery in Felton is open Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m. The office keeps Wednesday through Saturday hours. Check slvmuseum.org before you go, since hours change with seasons and staffing.

Can the museum help me research my house or family?

Yes, that's what the archives are for. Access is by appointment at (831) 338-8382 or archives@slvmuseum.org, and there's an online research request form. Holdings include newspapers back to the 1890s, maps, business records, family records, and thousands of photographs.

Which gallery should I visit first?

If you're in Boulder Creek, start with the Grace Episcopal Gallery, both for the 1906 building and for the Retrospect anniversary exhibit running through late 2026. The Felton gallery is the natural second stop, and it holds the reference library.

How do I volunteer?

Contact the museum at (831) 338-8382 or info@slvmuseum.org. Docents commit to at least a year at three hours a month, training included, and get a free membership out of it.

san-lorenzo-valley-railroad-history.title: Railroads of the San Lorenzo Valley: The Line That Built Boulder Creek
san-lorenzo-valley-railroad-history.html:
Trains ran to Boulder Creek for just under fifty years, from April 1885 to January 1934, and the town you drive through today is largely the town they built. A narrow-gauge branch climbed the valley from Felton, hauled out the lumber that made Boulder Creek one of the busiest shipping points in the redwoods, brought in the tourists who replaced the loggers, and then quietly went away. What it left behind is concrete piers in the river and a right-of-way you can still trace in places along Highway 9. The most complete record of all of it is rail historian Derek Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains project, which this article leans on throughout.

Before the trains: a flume that couldn't keep up

The railroad exists because a wooden trough failed. A flume company incorporated in August 1874 to move the upper valley's lumber, and by October 1875 its V-shaped flume was floating rough-cut boards roughly twelve miles from the headwaters of the San Lorenzo down to Felton. Whaley's history of the company reads like a punch list of trouble: the 40-inch trough couldn't handle the valley's output, it leaked, its water ran dangerously low every summer, and a winter storm in early 1876 tore it up badly.

In 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad took over the flume company. The South Pacific Coast was the ambitious narrow-gauge line that, per the Santa Cruz Trains FAQ, opened its route through the Santa Cruz Mountains in 1880, connecting Santa Cruz to San Francisco Bay in about four hours, ferry ride from Alameda Point included. Once that mainline was running through Felton, replacing the leaky flume with a branch up the valley was the obvious next move.

The Felton & Pescadero Railroad, 1883 to 1885

On paper, the branch started out as something grander. On June 13, 1883, the South Pacific Coast incorporated a subsidiary called the Felton & Pescadero Railroad: a first stage running up the San Lorenzo Valley, a second stage meant to push on toward Pescadero. Grading the narrow-gauge line took nearly two years. The route bypassed downtown Felton, followed the river north, and aimed for a clearing just south of where the San Lorenzo meets Boulder and Bear creeks, a three-way junction the railroad called the Turkey Foot.

During construction, the end of track sat briefly at Lorenzo, the small rival town a mile south of Boulder Creek, and Lorenzo's station served as a temporary terminus. But the permanent terminal went in at Boulder Creek, and when the first train rolled into town in April 1885, Whaley writes, the prosperity of Boulder Creek truly began. The flume was demolished along the new route. The Pescadero half of the name never happened. The tracks never went further north than the logging country above town.

The corporate name on the locomotives changed almost immediately. On May 23, 1887, the South Pacific Coast consolidated its railroads into the South Pacific Coast Railway, and on July 1 of that year the whole system was leased to Southern Pacific. Under SP, the line became first the Felton Branch and eventually the Boulder Creek Branch, and after the 1906 earthquake, which did little damage in Boulder Creek, the tracks were converted from narrow gauge to standard gauge.

End of the line: Boulder Creek's railroad years

For three decades the branch was a lumber conveyor. Boulder Creek ranked, in Whaley's words, as one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915, and the town's year-round population passed 800 on the strength of the freight yard at the center of it.

The branch carried people too, and the string of little stations between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek tells you who they were. Whaley's route survey lists stops like Phillipshurst, established for a Dr. Phillips; Siesta, set up near Brookdale at the urging of promoter Fred Swanton; Fish Hatchery, serving the state facility at Brookdale; Harris, which catered to Camp Joy; and Filbert, the renamed Lorenzo station near the end of Grove Street, which served the Redwood Rest Resort. Between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek the line hugged the west bank of the San Lorenzo and crossed the river at least seven times, running for one stretch in a cut between River Road and the water.

One place the trains never went: Big Basin. After the park was established in 1902, proposals to reach it by rail came and went for years, and Whaley's Big Basin history is direct about the outcome: rail access never came. Southern Pacific surveyed toward Pescadero Creek in 1905 with an eye on a park branch, but the 1906 earthquake shelved the plan. Visitors rode stages instead, and as early as 1886 the Moody and Cress livery stable in Boulder Creek was hauling picnickers and campers up toward the basin. That rail-to-stagecoach transfer made Boulder Creek the gateway town, a role it never gave up.

The Dougherty Extension: the loggers' own railroad

North of town, the rails kept going, but they didn't belong to Southern Pacific. After the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company's mill burned in 1886, brothers James and William P. Dougherty built their own narrow-gauge line north from the Boulder Creek freight yard rather than wait for an official extension. Construction began in the late winter of 1887, and the Dougherty Extension Railroad eventually ran roughly eight and a half miles up the San Lorenzo, reaching a bend of the river below Castle Rock Ridge by 1899. Spurs served the Morrell mill at Two Bar Creek, Cunningham & Company, and the S. H. Chase Lumber Company.

The Dougherty line was a freight operation with one charming exception. Starting May 19, 1909, a real estate promoter borrowed the line's little locomotive, known as the Dinkey, to shuttle prospective lot buyers up to the new Wildwood subdivision, and the summer runs continued for five seasons. Regular service on the line ended around 1915, and on April 4, 1917, the rails were sold for scrap, pulling the railroad's northern reach out of the valley for good.

Decline and the last train

The branch outlived the logging boom by about two decades, but the arithmetic only got worse. Regularly scheduled evening freight service ended in 1921. In March 1931, Southern Pacific replaced the branch's two daily passenger trains with Pacific Greyhound buses. And in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek, closing the town's railroad era after 49 years. Whaley's summary is that the town transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town, a shift our short history of Boulder Creek traces in full.

The valley's rail connection to the outside world didn't last much longer. A severe storm on February 26, 1940 wrecked the old mountain mainline between Santa Cruz and Los Gatos, and Southern Pacific won formal abandonment that November. With that, the age of trains in the San Lorenzo Valley effectively closed.

Tracing the line today

You can still find the railroad if you know where to look. In Boulder Creek, the station grounds sat just behind today's fire department, at the heart of the downtown blocks the freight yard once dominated. In Brookdale, Whaley points to the bottom of Pacific Street as the most accessible piece of the old alignment. Along the river between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek, concrete piers and sawed-off pilings from the line's many crossings still stand in and beside the water.

A word of caution before you go hunting: much of the old right-of-way crosses private property, so treat road crossings and public riverbanks as your vantage points rather than walking the grade. For photographs, timetables, and the deeper archive, the San Lorenzo Valley Museum at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek keeps the valley's rail history, and Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains site documents nearly every station, bridge, and spur if you want to go deeper still.

Frequently Asked Questions

When did trains stop running to Boulder Creek?

Passenger service ended in March 1931, when Southern Pacific replaced the two daily trains with Pacific Greyhound buses. The last freight train left Boulder Creek in January 1934, ending 49 years of rail service that began in April 1885.

Did the railroad ever reach Big Basin?

No. Proposals circulated for decades, and Southern Pacific surveyed a possible route in 1905, but the 1906 earthquake shelved the plan and no rail line was ever built into the park. Visitors transferred to stagecoaches in Boulder Creek and Ben Lomond, which is how Boulder Creek became the gateway to Big Basin.

Why was the line called the Felton & Pescadero Railroad?

The 1883 charter imagined a line that would continue past Boulder Creek to Pescadero. The first stage, Felton to Boulder Creek, opened in April 1885, but the extension was never built, and the removal of the logging tracks north of town in 1917 ended the idea permanently.

Can I walk the old railroad grade?

Mostly no. Much of the right-of-way between Felton and Boulder Creek now runs through private property. The practical way to see the line's remains is from public spots: the bottom of Pacific Street in Brookdale, road crossings along Highway 9 and River Road, and the riverbanks where concrete piers and old pilings are visible.

What was the Dougherty Extension?

A private narrow-gauge logging railroad built north from Boulder Creek starting in 1887 by the Dougherty brothers of the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company. It grew to roughly eight and a half miles, served several mills, briefly carried summer passengers to the Wildwood subdivision starting in 1909, and was scrapped in 1917.

schools-daycare-preschool-slv.title: Schools, Preschools, and Daycare in the San Lorenzo Valley
schools-daycare-preschool-slv.html:
Ask three valley parents where their kid goes and you'll get three different kinds of answers, all of them correct. There isn't one system here, there are four. Public school runs through a single district, and everything before kindergarten splits into licensed preschools, licensed family daycares, one licensed childcare center, and a handful of recreation programs that don't carry a childcare license at all. They're licensed differently and they run different hours, and knowing which is which saves a lot of confused phone calls. Openings change constantly, so treat each link and phone number below as the source of truth, and never assume a spot is open without calling.

One rule we hold to throughout: for the home-based family daycares, we list the community and phone number only, never a home address. That's a privacy line we don't cross, and the phone call is how you'd arrange a visit anyway.

Public school: SLVUSD

Boulder Creek and the rest of the valley are served by the San Lorenzo Valley Unified School District, which runs transitional kindergarten through 12th grade out of its offices at 325 Marion Avenue in Ben Lomond (831-336-5194). The in-town elementary campus is Boulder Creek Elementary at 400 Lomond Street, a short walk from downtown. Older students head to the district's middle and high school campuses in Felton. For the full, current roster of school sites and attendance areas, the district office is the place to confirm.

Enrollment starts with a phone call, not an online portal. Contact the registrar at your assigned school first; once pre-enrollment is set up, the registrar emails you instructions to finish registering online. For the 2026-27 year, kindergartners need to turn five on or before September 1, and TK students need their fourth birthday before that date. Have proof of age, immunization records, proof of address, and an oral health assessment for the youngest grades ready. Our family resources guide covers the wider picture: library programs, the seasonal pool, camps.

Tier one: licensed preschools

Licensed preschools are state-licensed programs built around early learning for roughly the 2-to-5 age range, usually on a school-day schedule. Two operate in Boulder Creek. Seeds of Knowledge Preschool at 16900 Highway 9 runs a licensed weekday program, Monday through Friday 7 a.m. to 5 p.m., with a hands-on, child-centered, play-based approach, and welcomes visits from prospective families. Little People's School at 13155 Oak Street (831-566-4236) has been a preschool and childcare option in town for years, with Montessori and after-school programs; it is open and taking inquiries, so contact it directly for current openings and details.

Tier two: licensed family daycares

A family child care home is a state-licensed program run out of the provider's own house, which usually means smaller groups, mixed ages, and hours that stretch further than a school-day preschool. In Boulder Creek, Adina's Daycare (831-338-3873) has operated as a licensed family child care home since 1991, serving infants through preschool age on weekdays. Busy Bees Playschool, founded by Michelle Hamblin Mathews, runs a play-based licensed family child care home in Boulder Creek and recently opened a second, larger location: Busy Bees Playschool Two, a licensed childcare center at 7985 B Highway 9 in Ben Lomond for ages 2 to 6.

Down the valley in Ben Lomond, two more licensed family child care homes serve San Lorenzo Valley families. Mountain Monsters Daycare (831-239-0323) offers full-day care with a large yard, meals and snacks, and pickup from local schools. Osito's Day Care (831-336-8486), run by Jesucita Licona, is a bilingual English-and-Spanish home with a Montessori-influenced approach for children from about 12 months through preschool age, open weekdays 8:30 a.m. to 4 p.m. For all four, call to arrange a visit; the addresses stay unpublished.

Tier three: the licensed center

The valley's licensed childcare center sits in the redwoods beside the San Lorenzo River. The Highlands Park Early Education Center at 8500 Highway 9 in Ben Lomond (831-400-1170) is a Community Bridges program, open weekdays 8 a.m. to 4 p.m., offering infant, toddler, and preschool care. It also runs the California State Preschool Program for income-eligible families. A center like this is licensed and staffed differently than a home daycare, and its state-preschool slots are a real resource for families who qualify.

Tier four: license-exempt recreation programs

The last category trips people up. Some preschool-age programs are run by parks and recreation agencies as enrichment rather than licensed childcare, which means no state childcare license is required, and the schedules tend to be partial-day and part-week. Good for socialization and routine. Not a full day of daycare. Two are verified nearby. The Circle program from the Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District serves ages 3 to 5, Tuesday and Thursday 9 a.m. to 1 p.m., at the Bear Creek Community Center for $50 a day; contact Shanda at 831-234-2727. Kreative Kids Preschool, run by Santa Cruz County Parks, meets Monday through Thursday 9:30 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. at Wilder Hall in Highlands Park for ages 3 to 6, with art, music, nature crafts, and monthly field trips. The rec district also runs other seasonal preschool-age recreation programs, so check the BCRPD kids-classes page for the current lineup.

When you're not sure who to call

Weighing options, or working out what you can afford? Start with the county's Child Development Resource Center at 400 Encinal Street in Santa Cruz (831-466-5820), open weekdays 8 to 5. Its specialists give free referrals to licensed centers and licensed family child care homes anywhere in the county, and they can walk you through subsidized programs and preschool options. It's the fastest way to find current openings without cold-calling every provider yourself.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the difference between a licensed daycare and a recreation preschool program?

A licensed daycare or preschool holds a California childcare license and can provide full-day care with the staffing and safety standards that come with it. A license-exempt recreation program, like BCRPD's Circle or the county's Kreative Kids, is run by a parks agency as partial-day enrichment and doesn't require a childcare license. Recreation programs work well for routine and play, but they aren't a substitute for full-day daycare.

Is Little People's School still open?

Yes. Little People's School at 13155 Oak Street in Boulder Creek is open and taking inquiries. Call 831-566-4236 for current openings and program details.

How do I find licensed childcare with an opening?

Call the county Child Development Resource Center at 831-466-5820 for free referrals to licensed centers and family child care homes with current availability. In Boulder Creek, Seeds of Knowledge and Little People's School are licensed preschools, and Adina's and Busy Bees are licensed family child care homes; contact each directly.

How do I enroll my child in public school here?

Contact the registrar at your assigned SLVUSD school first, which for in-town elementary kids is Boulder Creek Elementary. The registrar sets up pre-enrollment and sends online registration instructions. You'll need proof of age, immunization records, proof of address, and an oral health assessment for the youngest grades.

Inclusion here is a starting point, not an endorsement. See the full directory for contact details, and if a program, opening, or listing has changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit so the next family gets it right.

self-care-day-boulder-creek.title: A Self-Care Day in Boulder Creek
self-care-day-boulder-creek.html:
Three hair salons, a day spa, a nurse-run med spa, and a massage practice a block off Highway 9, all inside a town most people drive through in under a minute. You can build most of a self-care day here without starting the car again. Hair, facials, massage, and a med spa all operate right in town, and coffee and lunch are a short walk from any of them. The exceptions: nails mean a drive to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz, and a couple of the wind-down options sit in Felton or Scotts Valley. One reality check before you plan anything. Almost everything on this list runs by appointment. These are small, often single-practitioner operations, so the day works if you book a few days ahead and treat the itinerary below as a menu, not a checklist. Pick two or three stages, put them in order, and you've got a day.

Start with coffee

Both downtown coffee stops open before the salons do, which is the whole point of putting this stage first.

Jenna Sue's Cafe, 13090 Highway 9, (831) 338-7008. Open Monday to Friday 6:00am to 2:30pm, weekends 7:00am to 2:30pm. Espresso drinks, breakfast, and house-baked goods in the middle of downtown, and it opens early enough that you can eat before the first appointment of the day.

The Tree House Cafe, 13266 CA-9, (831) 610-8912. A cafe and espresso bar at the edge of town with giant redwoods growing through the interior, which is about as Boulder Creek as a coffee stop gets. Current hours aren't posted in our data, so check before you count on it.

Hair

All three of these are in Boulder Creek proper, so this is the easiest stage to keep local. For the full rundown, including the Ben Lomond and Felton options, see our haircut guide.

Hair Hut, 13266 Highway 9, Suite A, 831-239-0317. Open Tuesday to Thursday 9am to 7pm, Friday 9am to 5:30pm, Saturday 10am to 5pm. Full-service cuts, color, highlights, perms, and extensions, with the most regular posted hours of the three.

Central Avenue Salon, 13162 Central Avenue, 415-725-0850, by appointment. Stylist Dana Bernhardt's single-chair shop offering cuts, color, and extensions. Book ahead; there's no drop-in.

Oh Suzannah Hair Designers, 13090 Highway 9, 831-338-3883. Open Tuesday to Friday 10am to 5pm, Saturday 10am to 4pm. Color, cuts, perms, and relaxers, plus facial waxing if you want a small add-on without a separate stop.

Facial or med spa

This stage surprises people. A town this size having both a day spa and a nurse-run med spa isn't typical.

Supernatural Beauty & Aesthetics, 12615 Highway 9, 661-889-5728. A med spa in Boulder Creek founded by an aesthetic RN, offering neurotoxin injections, dermal fillers, chemical peels, microneedling, red light therapy, and medical-grade skincare. Book through the website; we don't have posted hours to share.

Redwood Mountain Spa, 12560 Highway 9, 831-703-4519, by appointment. A day spa at the south end of town offering massage, facials, skin care, waxing, and wellness treatments. Because it covers both facials and massage, it can anchor two stages of this day in one building.

Lilia Skin, Felton. A licensed esthetician offering holistic facials by appointment, with booking through Instagram. This one's a drive down Highway 9, worth it if a slower, holistic approach is what you're after.

Lunch break

Keep it quick so the afternoon appointments stay on schedule.

Taqueria Los Gallos, 13070 Central Ave, (831) 217-5000. A downtown counter-service taqueria serving tacos, burritos, and other Mexican dishes, easy to fit between appointments. Find it in our food guide.

Johnnie's Super Market & Liquor, 13225 Highway 9. The deli counter makes fresh sandwiches daily, and the market's open daily from 8:00am, so it works no matter how your morning ran long.

Massage and bodywork

Elevated Wellness Massage & Bodywork, 127 Forest Street, 831-345-8043, by appointment. An independent practice providing customized therapeutic massage and bodywork, a block off the main drag.

Redwood Mountain Spa, 12560 Highway 9, 831-703-4519, by appointment. The same day spa from the facial stage also does massage, so if you'd rather park once and stay put, this is the move.

Two verified massage options in town is where the count stands right now. If you run a bodywork practice in the valley that isn't listed, tell us.

Nails

There's no nail salon in Boulder Creek, Brookdale, Ben Lomond, or Felton that we've been able to verify. Nails are the one stage of this day that requires leaving the valley.

Queen Nails, 266 Mt Hermon Road, Suite M, Scotts Valley, 831-438-8736. Manicures, pedicures, acrylic, gel, and dip powder, about twenty-five minutes from downtown Boulder Creek. We don't have current hours confirmed, so call first.

Oli Nail Art, Santa Cruz. An independent nail artist working by appointment, with booking through Instagram. Appointment-based rather than walk-in, so this pairs well with a planned Santa Cruz errand run.

Wind down

Spa Santangelo, Felton, 831-335-8335. A women-owned wellness spa on a redwood property offering energy work, sound baths, guided meditations, and self-care workshops by appointment. Posted hours: weekends 11am to 5pm, plus Tuesday, Thursday, and Friday evenings 6pm to 9pm.

Kalani Day Spa, 245 Mt Hermon Road, Suite D, Scotts Valley, 831-600-7116. Open Monday to Friday 9am to 7pm, Saturday 9am to 5pm. A full-service day spa with customized facials, HydraFacials, massage, waxing, and lash and brow services, an option if you're already in Scotts Valley for nails.

Or skip the second spa and end with dinner in town. Scopazzi's, the Sawmill, Tae's, and the rest of the sit-down roster are covered in our dinner guide, and a table at the end of a day like this is its own kind of treatment.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I do a full spa day without leaving Boulder Creek?

Most of one. Coffee, hair, a facial or med spa treatment, massage, and lunch are all available in town. What you can't do locally is nails, and some of the wind-down options, like sound baths at Spa Santangelo, are in Felton or Scotts Valley.

Do I need appointments, or can I wing it?

Book ahead. Redwood Mountain Spa, Elevated Wellness, Central Avenue Salon, Lilia Skin, and Oli Nail Art all work by appointment, and Supernatural Beauty books through its website. The coffee and lunch stops are the only stages where you can reliably walk in.

Is there really no nail salon in the San Lorenzo Valley?

Not one we've been able to verify. The closest verified options are Queen Nails in Scotts Valley and appointment-based nail artists in Santa Cruz. If we've missed one, we'd like to know at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

How much does a day like this cost?

We don't track or publish prices, and they change too often for a list like this to keep up with. Every business above takes calls or online bookings, so check current pricing directly when you book.

These are verified facts to plan around, nothing more. Contact details for every stop live in the business directory, the food stops are in the food guide, and if anything here has changed or we're missing a place, tell us through the submit page.

septic-basics-boulder-creek.title: Septic Systems 101 for New Mountain Homeowners
septic-basics-boulder-creek.html:
If you just bought a home in Boulder Creek, odds are good you also bought a small wastewater treatment plant, because most homes here run on septic systems rather than sewer. The whole job of owning one comes down to a few habits: get the system inspected on a regular cycle and pumped when it needs it, which for a typical household tank is every three to five years by EPA's general guidance, keep grease, wipes, and chemicals out of the drains, and keep vehicles and tree roots off the drainfield. Do that, and a septic system is quiet, cheap company for decades. Ignore it, and you'll eventually meet one of the most expensive smells in homeownership. Here's the new-owner's orientation, with the county contacts you'll actually need.

First, Confirm What You Have

Most Boulder Creek properties treat wastewater on site, overseen by Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, which regulates what it calls onsite wastewater treatment systems, or OWTS. There are two exceptions worth ruling out. A portion of town near the core connects to a county-run sewer, County Service Area 7, Boulder Creek Sanitation, and connected homes pay an annual sewer charge on the property tax bill. A small pocket of about 56 homes in Bear Creek Estates sits on a wastewater system run by San Lorenzo Valley Water District. So check your property tax bill for a sewer service charge, dig out your escrow disclosures, or call the county. Our utilities guide walks through who serves what in more detail.

If nobody can tell you where your tank and drainfield actually are, ask Environmental Health what records exist for your parcel, and ask the pumper to mark components the first time the tank is opened. Knowing where everything sits is the foundation for every other habit in this article.

How the Thing Actually Works

A conventional system has two parts, and EPA's explainer covers them in about a minute. The septic tank is a buried, watertight container, usually concrete, fiberglass, or polyethylene. Everything from your drains flows into it, where solids settle to the bottom as sludge and grease floats to the top as scum. The liquid in the middle, called effluent, flows out to the drainfield, a set of shallow trenches where it trickles through soil. The soil is the real treatment plant; it filters out harmful bacteria, viruses, and excess nutrients before the water rejoins the groundwater.

Two takeaways follow directly from that design. The tank fills with solids over time, which is why pumping exists. And the drainfield only works when its soil can drain, which is why compaction and saturation are the enemies.

Some mountain lots have more complicated setups, with pumps, float switches, or alternative treatment components, especially where the house sits below the drainfield or the lot is tight. EPA's guidance is that alternative systems with mechanical parts should be inspected more often, generally once a year. If your system has a pump, it also has an opinion about power outages, something to fold into your outage planning.

The Maintenance Rhythm

EPA's general numbers: have the average household system inspected at least every three years by a professional, and expect to pump the tank every three to five years. Those are national ranges, not a promise for your parcel. The real interval for your tank depends on tank size, how many people live in the house, and your water habits, which is exactly why the inspection matters; a pro measures the sludge and scum layers and tells you when pumping is actually due. A garbage disposal shortens the cycle, since food waste builds solids faster than the tank's bacteria can digest them.

Keep a folder with pump-out receipts, inspection reports, and a sketch of where the tank and drainfield sit. Future you will use it, and so will your eventual buyer.

The economics favor diligence by a comical margin. EPA pegs routine maintenance at roughly $250 to $500 every three to five years, against $5,000 to $15,000 or more to replace a failed conventional system, and failed alternative systems cost even more. There's no cheaper insurance in your house.

What Not to Send Down the Drain

Your tank runs on bacteria, and the care rules are mostly about not poisoning or clogging them. Toilets take toilet paper and what your body produces, nothing else: no wipes of any kind, no feminine hygiene products, dental floss, cat litter, paper towels, or leftover pharmaceuticals. At the sink, keep cooking grease and oils out, go easy on harsh cleaners, and skip chemical drain openers; a pot of boiling water or a snake clears most clogs without carpet-bombing your tank's biology. As for the additives sold as tank boosters, EPA says plainly that they aren't necessary for a properly functioning system.

Water use is the sleeper issue. Every gallon you send down the drain has to pass through the tank and drainfield, so efficient fixtures and spread-out laundry loads genuinely protect the system. That matters most here in the wet season, when weeks of rain have already saturated the mountain and your drainfield has less capacity to spare.

Protect the Drainfield

The drainfield is the expensive part, and the rules for it are physical, not chemical. Never park or drive on it, and keep that in mind when a contractor wants to stage equipment on your lot; it's a question worth adding to the list in our contractor hiring guide. Don't plant trees on or near it, because roots hunt for exactly the moisture those pipes carry, and on a redwood lot the trees will win. And keep roof drains, gutters, and other runoff pointed away from it; the field can't treat wastewater while it's busy absorbing your roof's winter.

Know the Warning Signs

Septic systems usually fail politely before they fail disgustingly. EPA's list of malfunction signs is worth recognizing on sight: water or sewage backing up into drains, tubs and sinks draining slowly, gurgling in the plumbing, sewage odors around the tank or drainfield, standing water or damp spots over the system, and the classic, a stripe of bright green, spongy grass over the drainfield even in dry weather. On well properties, rising nitrates or coliform in a water test can be the tip-off.

Catch any of these and call a septic professional promptly, because the early version of the problem is often a pump-out or a repair, and the late version is a replacement. In a valley of small lots, steep slopes, and creeks that feed the San Lorenzo River, a failing system isn't just your problem, which is one reason the county takes these systems seriously.

Where the County Fits

Santa Cruz County Environmental Health permits new septic systems, repairs, and upgrades, and runs the maintenance oversight programs for the county's OWTS inventory. If you're repairing a drainfield, remodeling in a way that adds bedrooms, buying or selling, or just trying to understand a system you inherited, they're the authority, and local rules on any of it come from them, not from a general article like this one. Reach them at (831) 454-2022 or EnvironmentalHealth@santacruzcountyca.gov. Involve them early; a contractor who starts digging before the county is looped in can turn a repair into a much longer story.

When you hire, septic installation and repair is licensed work under the C-42 Sanitation System classification, so run any bidder through the CSLB license lookup. Our directory lists local septic and pumping services as a starting point; inclusion isn't an endorsement, and the license check plus a county-savvy attitude are your real filters.

Frequently Asked Questions

How often should I pump my septic tank?

EPA's general range is every three to five years for a typical household tank, with an inspection at least every three years. Your real interval depends on tank size, household size, and water use, and a disposal shortens it. Let inspections set the schedule rather than guessing, and treat these as general ranges, not a rule for your specific parcel.

Are septic additives worth buying?

Per EPA, additives aren't necessary for a system to work properly when it's treating normal household wastewater. Regular pumping does what the additives promise. Put the money toward the next inspection instead.

I'm buying a house in Boulder Creek. What should I ask about the septic system?

Ask where the tank and drainfield are, when the tank was last pumped and inspected, and for any records, then confirm the property is actually on septic rather than the CSA 7 sewer by checking the tax bill for a sewer charge. Questions about permits, past repairs, and what's on file for the parcel go to County Environmental Health at (831) 454-2022.

What are the signs my septic system is failing?

Slow drains, gurgling pipes, sewage smells outside, backups, soggy ground or standing water near the drainfield, and unusually lush green grass over the system even in dry weather. Any of these means call a septic professional now, while it's still a small problem.

Can I plant a garden over the drainfield?

Keep it shallow-rooted; lawn or unirrigated groundcover is the safe zone, and EPA's core drainfield rules are no vehicles, no trees nearby, and no added drainage on top of it. Skip vegetable beds you'd water regularly, and give redwoods and other thirsty trees a wide berth, because their roots will find the pipes.

summer-camps-san-lorenzo-valley.title: Summer Camps and Kids Classes in the San Lorenzo Valley: The 2026 Guide
summer-camps-san-lorenzo-valley.html:
Between Boulder Creek and the towns just down the hill, families had more than thirty summer camp and class options in 2026: recreation-district day camps in town, gymnastics and horse camps in Felton, art and nature programs, and a handful of overnight camps within a short drive. This page is the reference version of that list, grouped by type, with one-line facts and a link to each program's page. It isn't a ranking, and inclusion here isn't an endorsement. Most open registration between December and April and fill through the spring, so if you're planning ahead, bookmark the pages that interest you over the winter. Camps change year to year, so treat the links as your source of truth.

In town: BCRPD, the pool, and the library

The Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District is the closest thing the valley has to a home-base camp program, at 13333 Middleton Avenue (831-338-4144). This summer's BCRPD flyers listed four camps. Adventure Camps served ages 2 to 5 in half-day themed weeks (Kids in the Kitchen, Ocean Explorers, Mini Makers), 9 a.m. to 1 p.m., at $240 a week. Bear Creek Camp ran seven themed weeks for ages 6 to 11, full days 9 to 4 with daily pool time and snacks, at $310 a week. A Gymnastics and Dance Camp covered ages 3 to 6 over one late-June week, 10 to 2, at $250. And the Whitewater Rafting Campout took older kids (the flyer listed ages 11 to 15) down the South Fork of the American River on a three-day, two-night trip in mid-July for $650. Registration runs through Wufoo forms linked from the district site, and by mid-summer the rafting trip was already waitlisted.

The Bear Creek pool at 15685 Forest Hill Drive runs its own season, June 6 through September 27 in 2026, with recreational swim most afternoons and the pool closed Mondays. Current hours and season passes are posted here. Worth setting expectations on: as of 2026, BCRPD didn't publish swim lessons or a junior-lifeguard program anywhere on its site, so don't count on those.

The library is the free option worth building around. The Boulder Creek branch of Santa Cruz Public Libraries runs a summer reading program every year (2026's was "Plant a Seed, Read"), where kids log reading from June 1 through July 31 and earn prizes, with magicians, puppeteers, and craft events through the summer. Sign up at the branch or through Beanstack. The branch also holds a Toddler and Preschool Storytime on Wednesday mornings; check the branch calendar for the current week before you go.

Gymnastics and active camps

Community Mountain Gym runs day camps out of Felton (6171 Highway 9) and Scotts Valley. This summer's flyer listed an Active Kids Gymnastics Summer Camp across eight weeks, June 8 through July 31, at $325 a week, blending gymnastics with science, library trips, and games, plus a drop-in Kids Summer Camp on Fridays, 9 to 3 at the Felton Community Hall, for $50 a day. The gym's site confirms the format but doesn't publish dates or prices, so treat the flyer numbers as this-year figures and confirm on the weekly registration form.

La Madrona Athletic Club in the Scotts Valley area (1897 La Madrona Drive) ran three camps over ten weeks, June 1 through August 7, open to members and non-members. Sports Camp covered ages 6 to 10, 8:45 to 3, at $389 member and $466 non-member per week; a Summer Pool Guards Camp for roughly ages 6 to 12 ran mornings at $230 and $276; and a Tennis and Pickleball Camp for ages 6 to 12 ran 10 to 1 at $270 and $324. Registration packets are on the club's site.

Valley Sports Camp is a private operator running a multi-sports camp out of local school facilities. In 2026 it held three June weeks in Ben Lomond (June 8-12, 15-19, and 22-26) and three July weeks in Scotts Valley. It serves ages 4 through 13, with full days 9 to 3 at $390 a week and half days at $320, plus early-bird pricing for those who signed up by mid-March.

Horse camps

Tails of Redwood Trails runs horse programs out of the Covered Bridge Equestrian Center at 220 Covered Bridge Road in Felton. Its site lists school-break day programs, Pioneer Ponies for ages 5 to 7 and Horse Hollow for ages 8 to 13, covering riding, grooming, farm chores, and creek play. This summer's flyer described a fuller June-through-August horse camp with certified lifeguards, crafts, and Felton library visits, and noted full and partial scholarships. There's no online registration; text Kristina Nielsen at 831-706-7482 about the current season.

Trinity Rivers Equestrian at White Rock Ranch ran horsemanship camps on a published 2026 schedule of six weeks between June 8 and July 24 (no camp the week of June 29). Days run 9 to 3:30, with extended care to 5 for an extra $100 a week, at $650 a week including instruction, lunch, pool time, and a Friday show. Register through the RegFox link on the ranch's page.

Art and making

The Santa Cruz Mountains Art Center in Ben Lomond (9341 Mill Street) ran youth art classes over two weeks in June 2026, for ages 7 to 14, with some specialized classes for ages 9 and up. The format is a-la-carte: kids stack individual classes (painting, drawing, ceramics, printmaking, jewelry, tie-dye, and more) back to back, with a supervised lunch option to make a full day. The center doesn't publish a price online and offers sliding-scale help through the Gloria Seneres Memorial Fund, so email info@mountainartcenter.org or call 831-336-3513 for pricing and scholarships.

Nature and farm camps

WOLF School runs a Summer Nature Day Camp for kindergarten through sixth grade, split into ages 5 to 7 and 8 to 11. It's a drop-off day camp, 8:30 to 3:30, across eight weekly sessions from June 8 through July 31, at $525 a week early-bird (through the end of April) or $575 after. Note before you plan around it: the camp is held at Camp Monte Toyon in Aptos, not in the valley, so figure on a 20-to-30-minute drive from Ben Lomond.

Farm Discovery at Live Earth Farm runs a Farm Camp for ages 5 to 13 across ten weeks, June 1 through August 7, at $425 a week, with early- and late-care add-ons at $100 each and a Leaders-in-Training track for ages 13 and up. Scholarships are available. It's a day camp in Watsonville.

Quail Hollow Ranch County Park in Ben Lomond runs Santa Cruz County youth day camps for first through sixth graders, with hikes, science, games, and crafts. The county registers these through its ActiveNet portal rather than a static page, so the summer dates and prices live there. Check the county page or call 831-454-7941 for the session list.

Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park in Felton isn't a camp, but it's a free option worth knowing. From Memorial Day through Labor Day, interpreters lead drop-in programs in the campground, including Junior Rangers for ages 7 to 12 and Little Rangers for younger kids, where they explore and earn prizes. There's no pre-registration, just the $10 day-use parking fee. Times vary, so check the park's calendar or the visitor center (831-335-7077).

Water and ocean

Wave Warrior Camp, now run by OXA Training near Santa Cruz and Capitola, teaches ocean skills to kids ages 7 and up: surfing, bodysurfing, ocean safety, and basic first aid, 10 to 3 Monday through Friday, at $750 a week. There's also a lower-cost Wave Warrior Little's class at $165. The camp page can lag behind, so confirm the current summer's dates before you plan (831-291-0968). For an option closer to home, see the BCRPD rafting campout above.

Overnight and traditional camps

YMCA Camp Campbell on Highway 9 north of Boulder Creek runs both a day camp (8 to 5, campers grouped by age and folded into overnight-camp activities) and overnight camps in traditional, teen-adventure, and leadership tracks. Session dates and prices move around and sell through the YMCA registration portal; by mid-2026 the remaining bookable overnight weeks were six-day mini-camps for ages 7 to 16 around $1,024 for members, and those were waitlisted. Financial assistance is available, and a Labor Day family camp weekend closes the season.

Mount Hermon in Felton runs faith-based overnight camps. Redwood Camp serves grades 2 through 6 in weekly sessions at $799 a week (meals, lodging, and program included, with sibling and bring-a-friend discounts), and Ponderosa Lodge covers grades 7 through 12 at $990 a week. Registration opens in the off-season (a recent year opened in early December) through register.mounthermon.org.

Kennolyn Camps in Soquel is a regional staple with a day camp for kindergarten through 11th grade (from $745 a week), a residential overnight camp for grades 1 through 12 (from $3,325 a week), and a Huntington Lake overnight for older kids. It registers on a rolling basis, and by mid-summer 2026 many sessions were full with only a couple of open weeks left, so book early.

Camp Harmon in Boulder Creek, run by Easterseals Central California, is a fully accessible overnight camp built for children and adults with disabilities, with a 60-year history and a 1-to-3 staff-to-camper ratio (1-to-1 available). Its summer runs as nine five-day sessions, the youngest group starting at age 10, across an animal farm, garden, arts, and pool. Sessions in 2026 ran $1,450 each at the standard ratio, with Regional Center funding and scholarships available through the camp's scholarships page. Applications close once the season fills, so for next summer, watch campharmon.org and apply in the off-season.

Regional day camps down the hill

Santa Cruz Learning Center runs a day camp for ages 5 to 11, weekly from June 1 through July 31 plus a short August mini-session (no camp the week of June 29), 10 to 4 with extended care available. The center dropped its 2026 pricing to a sliding scale of $200 to $300 a week, one of the more affordable full-week camps around. It also runs short tween and teen camps like Forensics and Physiology and Coastal Crafts and Design.

Seven Directions in Santa Cruz ran a deep art-and-nature schedule in 2026: nineteen one-week day camps from June through mid-August for ages 5 to 18, most full days 10 to 3:30 at $425 to $440 a week, with some half-day camps from $220. Themes ranged from prehistoric animal art and anime to a nature adventure camp at Henry Cowell and end-of-summer eco art, and the studio offers multi-camp discounts. Each camp registers separately through the schedule page.

When registration opens

There's a rhythm worth knowing for next year. Many camps open registration in the off-season, roughly December through spring; Mount Hermon has opened as early as December, and Camp Harmon's applications open in the off-season too. Early-bird pricing tends to cluster from late January (Farm Discovery's ended January 31) through spring (Valley Sports' ran through mid-March, WOLF's through the end of April). By mid-July, popular sessions are often full or waitlisted: this summer the BCRPD rafting campout, the YMCA mini-camps, and much of Kennolyn's schedule were already spoken for. If a specific camp matters to your family, get on its page over the winter and register by early spring.

Frequently asked questions

When should I register for summer camp in the San Lorenzo Valley?

Plan to register in the off-season, roughly December through early spring. Several camps open registration as early as December, early-bird deadlines cluster from late January through March, and by mid-summer the popular sessions are frequently full or waitlisted.

What are the cheapest summer options for kids?

The library's summer reading program and Henry Cowell's drop-in Junior and Little Rangers programs are free (Henry Cowell just charges $10 for parking). Among paid camps, Community Mountain Gym's Friday drop-in ran $50 a day this summer, and Santa Cruz Learning Center's full-week day camp ran a sliding $200 to $300. Several camps also offer scholarships.

What's available for toddlers and preschoolers?

The youngest camps in town were BCRPD's Adventure Camps for ages 2 to 5 and its Gymnastics and Dance Camp for ages 3 to 6. Community Mountain Gym runs ongoing tiny-tot and preschool classes outside of camp, and the Boulder Creek library's Wednesday-morning storytime is a free weekly option.

Which camps offer scholarships or financial help?

Several. Camp Harmon offers scholarships and Regional Center funding, Farm Discovery lists scholarships for its farm camp, and this summer's Tails of Redwood Trails flyer noted full and partial scholarships. The Santa Cruz Mountains Art Center runs a sliding scale through the Gloria Seneres Memorial Fund, the YMCA offers financial assistance, and Santa Cruz Learning Center moved to sliding-scale pricing. Ask each program directly, since terms change year to year.

For the broader picture on schools, childcare, and year-round kids' programs in the valley, see our family resources guide.

Planning the rest of your season? Check what's coming up on the Boulder Creek Local events page, find local businesses in the directory, and if a camp here has changed its dates or you know one we missed, send it to us.

swimming-boulder-creek-san-lorenzo-river.title: Where to Swim Near Boulder Creek
swimming-boulder-creek-san-lorenzo-river.html:
The short version: the closest place to swim in Boulder Creek is the San Lorenzo River at Junction Park, one block off Highway 9 downtown, where the Rec and Park District keeps a small river beach that locals use as a summer swimming hole. There are no lifeguards there, or anywhere on the river, so it's swim at your own risk. If you'd rather have a chlorinated pool with set hours, the district runs a seasonal community pool a couple of minutes away. Whatever you choose, the water safety basics below aren't fine print. They're the difference between a good afternoon and a bad one.

The river at Junction Park

Junction Park is the easy answer. The Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District maintains it at Middleton and Railroad Avenues, one block off the main street, with a beach, San Lorenzo River access, a grassy area, and picnic tables. As the district puts it, in summer the river here is "a favorite swimming hole for many of our community members." It's walkable from downtown, shaded, and the natural place to spend a hot afternoon.

Just downstream, Barbara Day Park, which longtime residents still call the Dam Park, also touches the river. The district says it's currently undergoing some beautification and new features, so expect it to be a work in progress for now. Both parks are leashed-dogs-only.

A note on the other district park people ask about: Garrahan Park, up Highway 9 north of town, is a fields-and-track park with a walking loop, play structures, a gazebo, and barbecues, not a developed river beach. It's a fine place for a picnic or to let kids run, but Junction Park is the swimming spot in town.

The honest safety part

Nobody lifeguards the San Lorenzo River. That single fact shapes everything else, because drowning is not what most people picture. The CDC says drowning is fast and often silent, and can happen in as little as 20 to 60 seconds, without the splashing and shouting movies train us to expect. It's the leading cause of death for children ages 1 to 4 and the second-leading cause of unintentional injury death for kids 5 to 14, and more than 40 percent of drownings among children 5 to 14 happen in natural water like rivers.

Natural water is riskier than a pool for reasons you can't always see. The CDC lists hidden hazards in rivers and lakes: currents, cold water, rocks and vegetation, and limited visibility that hides how deep or shallow the next step is. A few habits carry almost all the safety here, and every one of them comes straight from the CDC and the American Red Cross:

  • Never swim alone, and swim where you can. Choose spots with other people around, and when it's an option, swim where there are lifeguards.
  • Watch kids within arm's reach. For young or weak swimmers, an adult should stay within an arm's length in or near the water. Pick one adult to be the water watcher, and have them put the phone down and skip the drinks. Drowning happens too fast and too quietly to catch while you're distracted.
  • Respect cold water and current. Even in summer, mountain river water is colder than it looks, and a cold plunge can steal your breath and strength in seconds. After rain, the river runs higher, faster, and colder, sometimes for days, so give it time to settle.
  • Don't count on jumping. People do jump from rocks along the river, but depth changes with the season and with what the last storm moved, and a pool that was deep last August may not be this year. If you can't see the bottom, don't jump.
  • If someone's in trouble, reach or throw, don't go. The Red Cross rule is to extend a branch or throw something that floats rather than jump in after them, because panicked swimmers pull rescuers under. If it's an emergency, call 911.

None of this makes the river safe. It stays swim at your own risk. It just stacks the odds in your favor.

What about the water quality

Santa Cruz County Environmental Health routinely tests rivers, creeks, and ocean swimming areas across the county for bacteria that signal pollution, and the San Lorenzo River watershed gets intensive monitoring under a long-running partnership that includes the county, the City of Santa Cruz, the Coastal Watershed Council, Surfrider, and the regional water board. When bacteria run high, the county posts advisory signs: a Warning sign, black lettering on white, when levels exceed the standard, and a Danger sign, red on white, for a confirmed sewage problem.

Two practical things follow from that. First, water quality changes, especially after rain, when runoff can carry bacteria into the river for up to three days. So the honest answer to "is it clean today" is that it depends on the day, and we won't publish a number here that would be stale by the time you read it. Check the county's recreational water quality information or call the county for the current posting, look for any signs at the park, and when in doubt after a storm, wait a few dry days. Second, don't swim with an open wound, and try not to swallow river water. That's good practice on any natural waterway.

If you'd rather have a pool

For a swim with more structure and no current, the Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District runs a seasonal community pool at its Bear Creek site on Forest Hill Drive. It opens for a summer season, roughly early June into late September, with expanded summer hours and scheduled Adult Swim and Rec Swim sessions, plus a summer membership option. Hours shift year to year, so check the current schedule with BCRPD or call the office before you load the car.

Beyond the valley, La Madrona Athletic Club in Santa Cruz is a private membership club with heated outdoor pools and swim lessons, if a club setup and structured lessons are what you're after. And if you want lifeguarded open water, the nearest is the Santa Cruz coast, where city and state beaches staff lifeguards during the summer season. Guarded hours and locations vary, so confirm with the City of Santa Cruz or State Parks for the day you go.

A few more San Lorenzo River spots

The river runs the length of the valley, and there are well-known swim spots downstream of Boulder Creek, from Highlands County Park in Ben Lomond to the Garden of Eden area inside Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park near Felton. They're scenic and popular, and every one of them is unguarded, with the same cold-water, current, and changing-depth realities as the river in town. If you explore them, bring the same habits, check each park's official page for access and rules, and remember that state parks have their own regulations.

Frequently asked questions

Can you swim in the San Lorenzo River at Boulder Creek?

Yes, at Junction Park the Rec and Park District keeps a small river beach that the community uses as a summer swimming hole. There are no lifeguards, so it's swim at your own risk. Watch the water safety basics above, especially with kids, and give the river time to settle after rain.

Are there lifeguards anywhere in Boulder Creek?

Not on the river. Junction Park and the other river beaches are unguarded. The district's seasonal pool has scheduled swim sessions, and the nearest lifeguarded open-water swimming is at the Santa Cruz coast in season. Confirm hours with each place before you go.

Is the water clean enough to swim in?

It depends on the day. The county monitors the San Lorenzo River for bacteria and posts advisory signs when levels are high, and runoff after rain can raise bacteria for a few days. Check the county's recreational water quality info, look for signs at the park, don't swallow the water, and skip it with an open wound.

When is the river best for swimming?

Deeper, colder, faster flow comes early in the season and after storms; by late summer the water is warmer but the pools get shallower. Early to mid-summer on a stretch of dry weather tends to be the sweet spot, but conditions vary year to year, so look before you wade in.

Is there a public pool near Boulder Creek?

Yes. The Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District runs a seasonal community pool at its Bear Creek site, open roughly June through late September with scheduled swim sessions. Check current hours with BCRPD.

In an emergency, call 911. For more on getting around the mountain safely, see the Boulder Creek Local directory, and check conditions after any storm on Mountain Status. Notice a detail here that needs fixing? Send it to us.

tree-services-boulder-creek.title: Tree Services in Boulder Creek
tree-services-boulder-creek.html:
A redwood that starts leaning the wrong way after a winter storm is not a problem you want to solve by grabbing the first name off a search page. If you need tree work done in Boulder Creek, four tree services are based right here in town. That's a genuine advantage in a place where the wrong crew, or the wrong call on a redwood, is an expensive mistake. What follows is who works locally and what they say they do, grouped so you can start calling for bids. Tree work is the most consequential home service up here, so before you sign anything, read our companion guide on the questions to ask before anyone touches a redwood. It covers permits, licensing, and insurance in detail, and all of it applies to every company on this page.

One thing drives most tree work in these mountains, and that's fire. Clearing trees is not the same as creating defensible space, and some removals up here need a permit. Our Residents page points to the Santa Cruz County tree protection and removal rules, which can require county sign-off depending on your parcel's overlays, creeks, and habitat. Start there before you assume a tree can come down at all.

Christianson Tree Experts (Boulder Creek)

Address: 13041 Pine Street, Boulder Creek. Phone: 831-338-6984. Website: christiansontreeexperts.com.

Christianson is a Boulder Creek tree-care company, and one of the few on this list with a listed street address in town rather than a service-only operation. Its own description covers pruning, removal, hazard assessment, cabling, and emergency services, a full range for storm-country work. The hazard assessment and cabling are worth a second look, because they point to keeping trees standing safely, not only felling them.

Stellar Tree Care (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-227-5414. Website: stellartreecare.com. Service area: Boulder Creek and the Santa Cruz Mountains. No public storefront address.

Stellar Tree Care is a Boulder Creek-based service that offers trimming, removal, firebreaks, and property-safety work. The firebreak focus speaks directly to defensible-space planning around a mountain home. Like several local tree outfits, it works from the field rather than a storefront, so contact runs through its site or by phone.

Sunderland Tree Service (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-338-2197. Website: sunderlandtree.com. Service area: Boulder Creek and the Santa Cruz Mountains. No public storefront address.

Sunderland Tree Service handles removal, pruning, firebreak, and defensible-space work for Boulder Creek and the surrounding mountains. Its description names defensible space by name, so it's a reasonable call when your goal is meeting fire-safety spacing rather than clearing a lot. Ask for a zone-by-zone plan rather than a blanket "take them all down" bid.

Travis Tree Professionals (Boulder Creek)

Phone: 831-338-6361. Website: travistreeprofessionals.com. Hours: daily 8am to 5pm, with 24-hour emergency response advertised. Service area: Boulder Creek and the San Lorenzo Valley.

Travis Tree Professionals offers pruning, removal, fire mitigation, land clearing, and emergency response, and it's the one company here that advertises a daily schedule plus round-the-clock emergency service. That kind of availability matters at 2am, when a winter storm has dropped a limb across your driveway. Storm season or not, slow down on the paperwork even when the situation feels urgent, and verify before you commit.

Before you hire any of them

Every company here is Boulder Creek based, which is a real plus for local knowledge of our slopes, soils, and creeks. It doesn't replace the checks, though. In California, tree work over $1,000 is licensed contracting under the C-49 Tree and Palm classification, and tree contractors are required to carry workers' compensation insurance whether or not they have employees. Verify any company's license and workers' comp status in the state licensing lookup, and ask their insurer to send liability and workers' comp certificates directly. Our tree-work questions guide walks through exactly how, and the Residents page links the county permit rules you may need to clear first. Being listed in our directory is a starting point, not an endorsement, so run the checks regardless of who you call.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a permit to remove a tree in Boulder Creek?

Sometimes, and it's parcel-specific. Santa Cruz County can require a permit depending on the Coastal Zone, riparian corridors along creeks, sensitive habitat, and tree size. Check your parcel and the county rules before cutting. Our Residents page links the county's tree protection and removal information, and our tree-work questions guide explains the thresholds. Don't rely on a contractor's verbal "no permit needed."

Will these companies handle defensible-space and fire clearing?

Several name firebreak or defensible-space work directly, including Stellar Tree Care and Sunderland Tree Service, with Travis Tree Professionals listing fire mitigation. Keep in mind that defensible space is about spacing, limbing, and fuel reduction across zones, not blanket removal, so ask any bidder for a zone-by-zone plan rather than a clear-cut quote.

How do I check that a tree company is licensed and insured?

Look up the C-49 license and workers' comp status in the state contractor license system, and ask for insurance certificates sent directly from the company's insurer. Tree contractors must carry workers' comp even as one-person operations, so verify it rather than taking a business card's word. The full step-by-step is in our tree-work questions guide.

Who do I call for an emergency, like a tree on a structure or across the road?

For a true emergency, safety comes first. If the tree is on power lines, stay clear and call PG&E; if it's blocking a public road, that's a county road matter. Among local companies, Travis Tree Professionals advertises 24-hour emergency response. Document the situation with photos before any work, and note that genuine emergencies are treated differently under the county code.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, only a verified place to start. See the full directory for local tree services and other trades, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so the next neighbor gets accurate information.

vets-pet-services-boulder-creek.title: Vets and Pet Services In and Around Boulder Creek
vets-pet-services-boulder-creek.html:
Most mountain towns send you down the hill for a vet. Boulder Creek doesn't. There's a full-service veterinary clinic right on Highway 9 in town, and another a few minutes south in Felton, so routine care, shots, dental work, and daytime sick visits are all handled close to home. The one gap is overnight. Neither valley clinic is a 24-hour hospital, so for a middle-of-the-night crisis you're driving to Santa Cruz. Below is who does what, grouped by need. Hours change, so call ahead.

The vet in town

Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic is the full-service clinic right in town at 12870 Highway 9, reachable at 831-338-7205. It's open Monday and Wednesday 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., Tuesday, Thursday, and Friday 8 a.m. to 6 p.m., and Saturday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., closed Sunday. Those two late nights a week are genuinely handy in a valley where most things close early.

They cover the whole range of companion-animal care: puppy and kitten wellness, spay and neuter, dental cleanings with digital x-rays, laser therapy, in-house lab work, ultrasound, and even exotic pets, plus therapeutic bathing and boarding. Their site says they're staffed and equipped to handle emergencies during open hours, so if something happens on a weekday, call them first. Just know that "emergencies during open hours" isn't the same as an overnight ER, which is covered further down.

The vet in Felton

A short drive south, Felton Veterinary Hospital at 5996 Highway 9 is the valley's other clinic, at 831-335-3466. It's open weekdays 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. and Saturday 8 a.m. to 2 p.m.

Along with preventive care, diagnostics, dentistry, surgery, and exotics, they also offer boarding and grooming, so your dog can get a checkup and a bath in the same trip. It's about fifteen minutes down Highway 9 from Boulder Creek, a reasonable backup if the in-town clinic is booked or closed on the day you need them.

After-hours and overnight emergencies

There's no 24-hour animal hospital in the San Lorenzo Valley. If your pet has an emergency at night, on a Sunday, or after the local clinics close, the nearest round-the-clock animal ER is Pacific and Santa Cruz Veterinary Specialists at 2585 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz. They're open 24 hours a day, seven days a week, all year, at 831-475-5400.

They take walk-ins, and while they recommend calling ahead so their team can prepare, they know emergencies don't wait. From Boulder Creek, plan on thirty to forty minutes down Highway 9 and Highway 17. Put that number and address in your phone now, before you need it at 2 a.m. with a scared animal in the back seat.

Grooming

For grooming, you've got a few options within a short drive. Dapper Dogs and Divas is an appointment-based dog grooming shop at 6144 Highway 9, Unit B, in Felton, run by groomer Jackson Hamilton, open Tuesday through Saturday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m., at 831-216-6462.

In Ben Lomond, Mountain Paw Spa is a home-based grooming business run by Kristin Miller, offering full grooms, bath and brush, nail trims, and ear cleaning by appointment at 831-266-7998. Since it works out of a home, there's no walk-in storefront; book ahead. And as noted above, Felton Veterinary Hospital offers grooming alongside its boarding, handy if you'd rather keep everything under one roof.

Boarding, daycare, and pet sitting

If you're heading out of town, Camp Happy Tails covers the San Lorenzo Valley with doggy daycare, boarding, grooming, and in-home pet sitting. It's a licensed, bonded, and insured operation reachable at 831-247-8245. Because it serves the valley rather than running a public storefront, call to arrange details.

Both valley vet clinics board too. Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic offers boarding in town, and Felton Veterinary Hospital offers supervised dog and cat boarding, which can be reassuring if your pet has a medical condition and you'd rather it stay somewhere with a vet on site.

Wildlife: injured, sick, or orphaned wild animals
