This is redwood country, so sooner or later you'll find a wild animal that seems to be in trouble, a fledgling bird, a fawn, an injured raccoon. Wildlife doesn't go to the vet or the pound. It goes to Native Animal Rescue, the licensed nonprofit that cares for Santa Cruz County's sick, injured, and orphaned wildlife, at their hotline, 831-462-0726.

Their guidance is worth following: not every wild animal actually needs rescue, and picking one up can do more harm than good. Take a photo, call the hotline first, and let their wildlife dispatcher tell you what to do before you touch anything. Their intake is at 1855 17th Avenue in Santa Cruz, but the call comes first.

Strays, lost pets, and animal control

For a stray dog, a lost cat, or a domestic animal in trouble, that's the Santa Cruz County Animal Shelter at 831-454-7224. The main shelter is at 1001 Rodriguez Street in Santa Cruz, and because Boulder Creek is unincorporated county, the shelter and its animal control officers serve our area. They're who you call to report a stray, look for a lost pet, or ask about a dangerous or aggressive animal.

The shelter also points wildlife calls to Native Animal Rescue and to Wildlife Emergency Services at 831-498-9453. As a rule of thumb: if it's someone's pet, or should be, call the county shelter; if it's wild, call Native Animal Rescue first.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a vet in Boulder Creek?

Yes. Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic is a full-service companion-animal clinic in town at 12870 Highway 9, open six days a week with two late nights, at 831-338-7205. Felton Veterinary Hospital is a second option about fifteen minutes south on Highway 9.

Where do I take my pet for an emergency at night?

Neither valley clinic is open 24 hours. The nearest round-the-clock animal ER is Pacific and Santa Cruz Veterinary Specialists at 2585 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz, open 24/7 at 831-475-5400, about thirty to forty minutes away. They take walk-ins but appreciate a call ahead.

Who do I call about an injured wild animal?

Native Animal Rescue, at 831-462-0726. They handle the county's sick, injured, and orphaned wildlife. Take a photo and call before you try to help, since not every animal needs rescue and handling can hurt. Wildlife doesn't go to a vet clinic or the animal shelter.

What about a stray or lost dog?

Call the Santa Cruz County Animal Shelter at 831-454-7224. Boulder Creek is unincorporated county, so the shelter and its animal control officers cover our area for strays, lost and found pets, and dangerous-animal reports.

Where can I get my dog groomed near Boulder Creek?

Dapper Dogs and Divas in Felton and Mountain Paw Spa in Ben Lomond both groom by appointment, and Felton Veterinary Hospital offers grooming with its boarding. Camp Happy Tails also grooms as part of its valley pet-care services. Call ahead at any of them, since these are small, appointment-driven shops.

wedding-venues-san-lorenzo-valley.title: Getting Married in the San Lorenzo Valley: Venues and Local Vendors
wedding-venues-san-lorenzo-valley.html:
If you want to get married under redwoods, the San Lorenzo Valley has nine verified venues within a short drive of Boulder Creek, from a historic Brookdale hotel to a steam-railroad grove in Felton to county park halls you can rent yourself. And the rest of a wedding, the florals, cake, photos, music, hair and makeup, officiant, rentals, is more covered up here than it used to be: we've since verified vendors in every one of those categories who serve the valley. Below are the venues operating in the area, followed by local vendors organized by what you're booking. This isn't a ranking, and nothing here is an endorsement. It's a starting point built from each business's own information, so you can start making calls. Every listing is in our directory, and where a vendor's status is uncertain we say so instead of guessing.

The Venues

Brookdale Lodge (Brookdale)

Address: 11570 Highway 9, Brookdale. Phone: 831-609-6010. Website: brookdalelodge.com.

The historic Brookdale Lodge hosts weddings and parties in its River Run lounge and event space, and its remodeled rooms mean guests can stay on site. The on-site Brookdale Diner can handle catering. It's the closest full-service wedding-and-lodging venue to Boulder Creek.

Roaring Camp Railroads (Felton)

Address: 5401 Graham Hill Road, Felton. Phone: 831-335-4484. Website: roaringcamp.com.

Roaring Camp runs ceremonies at Cathedral Grove (reached by steam train) or the Redwood Grove, with receptions in Bret Harte Hall for roughly 80 to 300 guests. Its wedding coordinator is Stacey Mabuhay (staceym@roaringcamp.com). This is the marquee redwood-railroad option, and it books well ahead.

The Sequoia Retreat Center (Ben Lomond)

Address: 11445 Alba Road, Ben Lomond. Phone: 831-218-5872. Website: sequoiaretreatcenter.com.

The Sequoia Retreat Center holds ceremonies at Peace Park among old-growth sequoias, with receptions in Las Alas Lodge or the string-lit Pixie's Garden patio. On-site cabins give the wedding party a place to get ready and guests a place to sleep, and the venue supplies tables and chairs.

Waterfall Lodge and Retreat (Ben Lomond)

Address: 325 Hidden Falls Trail, Ben Lomond. Phone: 831-212-3895. Website: waterfallsantacruz.com.

Waterfall Lodge is a private 80-acre redwood property with a sunny ceremony meadow and 32 cabins and cottages that sleep up to 86 overnight. It's a bring-your-own-vendor venue and hosts weddings of up to 150 daytime guests, plus retreats.

The Barn at River House (Ben Lomond)

Locality: Ben Lomond. Phone: 831-688-0355. Booking is by phone (ask for Casey). Web presence: Facebook page.

The Barn at River House centers on a 3,500-square-foot barn on a private stretch of the San Lorenzo River, with a wisteria arbor, a gazebo over the water, a redwood ceremony grove, and about 10,000 square feet of lawn seating up to 250 for dinner. It's available April through December and includes a bridal cottage and catering station. The exact street address isn't published; confirm details by phone.

Highlands House at Highlands County Park (Ben Lomond)

Address: 8500 Highway 9, Ben Lomond. Booking phone: 831-454-7901 (Santa Cruz County Parks). Website: Highlands County Park.

Highlands House is a historic 1940s house you rent for weddings and parties at a county park, with a grand room for dancing, a prep kitchen, stored tables and chairs, and two upstairs rooms for the wedding party. Capacity runs up to 200 guests April through October (100 November through March). One thing to know up front: you book it through Santa Cruz County Parks at 831-454-7901, not through a private event company, so the process runs on the county's reservation system.

Sydalyn Hill (Felton)

Locality: Felton (exact address not published; tours by appointment). Phone: 408-892-1994. Website: sydalynhill.com.

Sydalyn Hill is a family-run farm venue on a sunny hilltop off Highway 9, surrounded by Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park, with valley views, orchards, gardens, and an open meadow. It hosts a limited number of weddings each year between May and October, up to 150 guests for full packages, plus micro-weddings and elopements.

Felton Guild (Felton)

Address: 5449 Highway 9, Felton. Phone: 831-336-8093. Website: feltonguildweddings.com.

Felton Guild has hosted outdoor weddings since 1994, built around a redwood cathedral ceremony space seating about 130 and a reception lawn that holds more than 200. The on-site Cowboy Bar & Grill provides catering and full bar service, with couples building a menu directly with the restaurant.

Kennolyn Events (Soquel)

Address: 8400 Glen Haven Road, Soquel. Phone: 831-479-6700. Website: kennolyn.com.

Kennolyn is a 450-acre estate and camp venue with two settings: the hilltop Hacienda Estate overlooking Monterey Bay, and Stone Creek Village, a redwood setting suited to weekend-long destination weddings of roughly 75 to 150. It's over the ridge in Soquel rather than in the valley itself, included here as the nearby regional option for couples who want scale.

A few more venues worth a call if you'll travel a little: the Amphitheater of the Redwoods at Pema Osel Ling in Watsonville (a 102-acre redwood retreat with on-site lodging and catering), Quail Hollow Ranch County Park in Felton (a county-permit ranch house and lawn), and the Hilton Santa Cruz / Scotts Valley for a full-service hotel wedding.

Florists

Boulder Creek has in-town floral makers plus reliable valley delivery. Boulder Creek Flowers & Lokahi Designs (also known as Everly Designs) is a Boulder Creek florist doing wedding and event flowers by appointment. Woodland Floral & Creations (801-564-9944) makes hand-painted sola wood flower bouquets and arch pieces, a keepsake alternative to fresh flowers. Camp Joy Gardens (831-338-3651), the organic farm nonprofit since 1971, arranges flowers for weddings and events. Down the valley, Wild Iris Floral and Botanical in Felton (831-278-0151) and Pretty Petals Arrangements serve Ben Lomond and the valley. For studios that cover the county, there's Ferrari Florist & Gardens (since 1946), Redwood & Ranunculus, Quaintrelle Farms, Dahlias & Details, and Seascape Flowers in Aptos.

Cakes and Desserts

Right in Boulder Creek, Dessert First Bakery (831-338-7008) makes custom wedding and celebration cakes and shares a building with Jenna Sue's Cafe, and Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed cottage bakery taking custom orders. Across the county, verified wedding-cake makers include Edith Meyer Wedding Cakes (a Santa Cruz wedding-cake specialist by appointment), The Buttery, Companion Bakeshop, Sugar Bakery Co. in Soquel, Gayle's Bakery in Capitola, and Freedom Bakery & Confections in Watsonville.

Catering and Bar

Couples here mix dedicated caterers with restaurant catering lines. Closest is Wylder Space, farm-to-table wedding catering from Chef Molly Bravo in the Felton and Scotts Valley area. Around the county: Humble Bumble Catering, Feel Good Foods Catering, Santa Cruz Catering Company, Sunny's Catering, Aptos St. BBQ for barbecue, and D'La Colmena for Mexican catering. The Wild Bite is a women-owned small-bites caterer who also does day-of coordination, and Busy Bee's Cafe & Catering caters county-wide. On the restaurant side, the Brookdale Diner caters at 831-609-6126 and Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge in Boulder Creek (831-338-4444) has private-event rooms. For bar, Tip Top Taps is a mobile tap bar covering the county.

Photographers and Video

Several wedding photographers work the valley, and a few are Boulder Creek locals. Kassandra Thomsen Photography names San Lorenzo Valley redwood weddings in her own service list, and Kyer Wiltshire specializes in redwood-forest weddings and shoots both photo and video. De Joy Photography (Amber Amelingmeier) brings 18-plus years and 300-plus events, Sand and Stone Media covers photo and video, and Kelly Griffiths Photography travels throughout the county. One honest note: P.I.C. Photo (Partners In Camera), a Boulder Creek duo, has an active listing but a placeholder website, so confirm booking directly. Statuses as of mid-July 2026.

Music and DJs

ICON DJ explicitly serves Felton, Scotts Valley, and redwood estates with no local travel fee, and DJ Warwick has a dedicated Ben Lomond wedding page, so both are natural first calls up here. Santa Cruz DJ Company in Scotts Valley (founded 2008) runs 200-plus events a year, and SIM Events is a full-service DJ and entertainment company covering Santa Cruz and Monterey.

Hair and Makeup

Wedding hair and makeup here is mobile, coming to your venue or getting-ready suite. Kelly Jo Beauty works studio and mobile, Cristal Stephani is a mobile makeup artist, Meghan Elisabeth (the Nomadic Stylist) does mobile bridal hair, and Kala Ketchum and Malina Terra both do on-location wedding hair and makeup. Spa Bella Mobile Spa serves the Santa Cruz area; confirm it covers your valley venue when you book.

Planners and Coordinators

Weddings By Aimee names the San Lorenzo Valley on its site and works with a low-waste focus, which makes it the strongest local-fit planner. Mia Jasmine Weddings specializes in redwood and mountain weddings, and Wedding Connection and Event by Dawn both plan across the county. For day-of coordination specifically, A Paper Flower Wedding (Sarah G.) and The Wild Bite both offer it.

Officiants

You do have valley-serving officiants. Santa Cruz Officiant lists Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, and Felton directly in its service area. Marry Me In Santa Cruz (Jane Bendick) performs ceremonies and elopements across the county, and Elope Santa Cruz and Monterey Bay is a separate officiant-and-photographer team focused on elopements.

Balloons, Decor, and Rentals

For balloon styling and party decor, Luzovich Celebrations is a San Lorenzo Valley stylist who also makes party favors, and Bash Works and Aesthetic Events & Co. do balloon garlands and event styling (Bash Works also rents bounce houses and marquee letters). For tables, chairs, tents, and lighting, verified rental companies serving the county include M&M Party Rentals in Watsonville, Alexis Party Rental, A to Z Party Rental, Special Events Rentals, and Chic Event Rentals. Because delivery up a mountain road can carry a distance fee, confirm your exact venue address when you book.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which valley venues let you bring your own caterer and vendors?

Waterfall Lodge is explicitly a bring-your-own-vendor property, and Highlands House through the county is self-catered by design. Others, like Felton Guild with its Cowboy Bar & Grill and Brookdale Lodge with the Brookdale Diner, pair you with in-house food and bar. Always confirm the vendor policy with the venue before booking outside help.

How do I book Highlands House for a wedding?

Highlands House sits in Highlands County Park and is reserved through Santa Cruz County Parks, not a private operator. Call the county parks reservation line at 831-454-7901 to check availability and the rental process. Capacity is up to 200 guests April through October and 100 November through March.

Are there any wedding venues actually in Boulder Creek?

Brookdale Lodge in neighboring Brookdale is the closest full-service hotel-and-event venue, and Boulder Creek itself has florists, bakeries, and photographers who live in town. The dedicated ceremony-and-reception estates cluster just south in Ben Lomond, Felton, and Soquel, a short drive down Highway 9.

Is there a wedding officiant who serves the San Lorenzo Valley?

Yes. Santa Cruz Officiant lists Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, and Felton in its service area, and Marry Me In Santa Cruz and Elope Santa Cruz both perform ceremonies and elopements across the county. Contact each to confirm your date and location.

Who bakes wedding cakes near Boulder Creek?

Dessert First Bakery in Boulder Creek (831-338-7008) specializes in custom wedding and celebration cakes, and Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed Boulder Creek cottage bakery. For more options, Edith Meyer Wedding Cakes, The Buttery, Companion Bakeshop, Sugar Bakery Co., Gayle's, and Freedom Bakery all make wedding cakes across the county.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a verified place to start. Browse the full directory for local vendors, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so the next couple gets accurate information.

what-are-the-sirens-for-boulder-creek.title: What Are the Sirens For? How to Look Up Boulder Creek Emergency Calls
what-are-the-sirens-for-boulder-creek.html:
A siren climbs Highway 9, a couple of engines pull out of the station on Central Avenue, and half the town lands on the same question at once: what's going on? Here's the fastest answer we know of. Open PulsePoint's web feed for Boulder Creek Fire in any browser. No app, no account. It shows the fire district's active and recent calls, usually within a minute or two of dispatch: the incident type, the time, the street, and which units are responding. Most days you'll find a medical call, because that's most of what our fire district actually does.

That single link answers the question maybe eight times out of ten. The other two times, the sirens belong to an agency whose calls show up somewhere else, or nowhere at all. This guide walks through who responds in Boulder Creek, where each agency's calls can be looked up, and what stays off the public record on purpose.

Two Different Sirens, and Only One Is a Warning

Before anything else, know that "siren" in Boulder Creek means two different things.

The fixed siren at the fire station is a community warning. Boulder Creek Fire Protection District sounds it in a disaster or large-scale emergency to tell residents to go get information. It was used during the CZU fire. If you hear it outside the weekly test, treat it as real.

What to do when the station siren sounds:

  1. Stay calm and get information. The tone is the same no matter the emergency, so the siren itself does not tell you what happened or which way to go.
  2. Tune in: KBCZ 89.3 FM, KSCO 1080 AM, or KSCO 104.1 FM, plus local TV.
  3. Check your phone for a CruzAware alert and look up your evacuation zone at Genasys Protect.
  4. Do not call 911 to ask what the siren was. 911 is for a life-threatening emergency, and asking ties up a line someone else needs.

The weekly test is Tuesdays at 7 p.m. A siren at that time is a test. A siren at any other time is not, so go get information. The district also sounds it occasionally for ceremonial reasons, such as opening the Fourth of July pancake breakfast or honoring a member who has died.

Moving sirens are something else entirely. An engine, medic unit, or patrol car on its way to a call is not a warning to anyone. That is what the rest of this page is about, and what PulsePoint will usually explain in under a minute.

Who Actually Responds Here

Boulder Creek doesn't have one emergency service. It has several, and knowing which is which tells you where to look.

The Boulder Creek Fire Protection District is the first responder for most of it. The district's volunteers cover 21 square miles of mountain terrain, including Brookdale, and answered more than 1,300 calls last year. Per the district's own site, that workload runs from medical aids and vehicle accidents to hazmat, vehicle fires, wildland fires, and structure fires. The station is at 13230 Central Ave, business line (831) 338-7222.

American Medical Response runs the ambulances. Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1, the dispatch center for the county, describes AMR as "the local paramedic and ambulance transport provider," and dispatches it alongside every fire district in the county. On a medical call you'll typically see both: a BCFD engine plus an AMR medic unit.

CAL FIRE CZU, headquartered in Felton at (831) 335-5353, covers wildland fire and backs up local districts. On a recent Boulder Creek structure fire, the PulsePoint feed showed CZU units in the response alongside district engines. During fire season, a column of smoke can draw a response that looks enormous from the road. That's by design up here.

The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office handles law enforcement, since Boulder Creek is unincorporated. Non-emergency dispatch is (831) 471-1121.

The CHP covers the state highways, which in our case means Highway 9 and Highway 236. A crash on 9 is usually a CHP incident with fire and AMR responding too.

Nearly all of it funnels through one room: Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1, also called NetCom, answers the 911 calls and dispatches the sheriff, the county's fire districts, and AMR.

Fire and Medical Calls: PulsePoint

PulsePoint is a service that participating fire agencies use to publish their dispatch activity in close to real time, and Boulder Creek FPD is a participating agency. You can see the feed two ways.

In a browser, use web.pulsepoint.org and select Boulder Creek FPD, or jump straight to the saved Boulder Creek feed. On your phone, download PulsePoint Respond for iOS or Android, then follow Boulder Creek FPD as your agency. The app can notify you of significant incidents; the web view is better for a quick "what was that" check.

Each entry lists the call type (medical emergency, traffic collision, fire alarm, structure fire, vegetation fire, and so on), when it came in, where, and the responding units. Two details worth knowing. First, medical calls are shown at the street level only, no house numbers, which is a reasonable privacy line for what are usually someone's worst days. Fires show the full address. Second, closed calls stay visible in the recent list with their duration, so you can still check an hour after the sirens passed.

If you want a wider view, the same picker offers neighboring agencies: Felton FD, a Santa Cruz County agency covering unincorporated areas like Bonny Doon and Davenport, Central Fire, and Santa Cruz FD. Adding Felton and Santa Cruz County catches most of what BCFD gets called to assist on.

Wildfire: Watch Duty

For anything that smells like wildfire, Watch Duty is the tool this valley reaches for, and the Fire Safe Council of Santa Cruz County recommends it for exactly that. Watch Duty is a nonprofit. Its updates are posted by humans, largely retired dispatchers, firefighters, and reporters listening to radio traffic and verifying before they publish. It covers all 50 states, Santa Cruz County included, and it will alert you to a new fire start near you faster than most official channels will.

PulsePoint tells you an engine went somewhere. Watch Duty tells you whether a vegetation fire is growing, staffed, and mapped. They're complementary, and both belong on a Boulder Creek phone.

Highway 9 and 236: The CHP Incident Page

Crashes and hazards on the state highways live on the CHP Traffic Incident Information Page. It's a plain, old-school page, and it works. From the Communications Centers menu, choose Monterey, which is the CHP center that covers Santa Cruz County, and click OK. You'll get a live table of incidents; the Area column shows "Santa Cruz" for our county's calls, and the page refreshes every 60 seconds. When traffic stops dead on 9 south of town, this page usually says why before anything else does.

Sheriff Calls: Mostly Not Public, and That's Deliberate

Here's the gap people run into. There is no live public feed of sheriff calls in Santa Cruz County. If deputies fly past with lights on, no app will tell you where they're going. Law enforcement calls stay off public feeds for practical reasons: officer safety on in-progress calls, victim and reporting-party privacy, and open investigations.

What does exist is a lookback tool. The Sheriff's Office runs a Civilian Calls for Service search where you can enter a street address and see calls from the last three years. It's useful for questions like "what's the history on that property," not "what's happening right now." The office's own disclaimer says it doesn't include all calls and excludes certain incident types entirely, so treat an empty result as not shown, not as nothing happened.

For anything you need to report or ask about, the non-emergency dispatch line is (831) 471-1121. The full local list, including the Boulder Creek service center, is in our emergency contacts one-pager.

If you're the scanner type, a live Santa Cruz County Fire audio feed runs on Broadcastify, carrying the county fire channels. Radio traffic is raw and easy to misread, so treat it as background, not fact.

The Hard Rule

All of these tools are for curiosity and awareness. None of them is how you should decide to evacuate. Feeds lag, calls get typed under generic categories, and no service guarantees completeness or timeliness. When something big is happening, evacuation decisions come from CruzAware alerts and official orders from the Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE, period. Know your evacuation zone before you need it: look up your address at Genasys Protect, the tool the fire district points residents to. If you haven't registered for alerts yet, our CruzAware setup guide walks through it in about ten minutes. And if you're looking at an actual emergency, don't check an app first. Call 911.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do fire engines respond to medical calls?

Because in Boulder Creek, the fire district is the medical first responder. BCFD lists medical aids among its core call types, and its engines carry trained personnel who are usually closer than the ambulance, which may be coming from elsewhere in the valley. Watch the PulsePoint feed for a week and you'll see the pattern: a district engine and an AMR medic unit on the same call, with the engine often arriving first. The engine isn't there because of a fire. It's there because it's the fastest help.

Why can't I see sheriff or CHP calls in PulsePoint?

PulsePoint publishes fire and EMS dispatches from participating fire agencies only. CHP incidents have their own page at cad.chp.ca.gov. Sheriff calls have no live public feed at all, only the address-history search above.

Sirens went by and nothing shows in any app. What happened?

It happens. The call may be law enforcement (not published), a CHP incident filed under the Monterey center page, units passing through to another district, or a feed running behind. A blank screen means not shown, nothing more. If you're worried something nearby is serious, check CruzAware and Watch Duty rather than refreshing a quiet feed.

Do I need the app, or is there a website?

Both exist. The web feed at web.pulsepoint.org needs nothing installed and is the fastest one-time check. The app adds notifications, which are worth having if you want to know about structure fires and vegetation fires the moment they're dispatched.

Are sirens ever a warning to residents, like an evacuation signal?

The fixed siren at the fire station is. Boulder Creek Fire sounds it in a disaster or large-scale emergency, as it did during CZU, to tell residents to go get information. It is tested Tuesdays at 7 p.m.; outside that, treat it as real. It does not tell you what happened or which way to go, so tune to KBCZ 89.3 FM or KSCO 1080 AM / 104.1 FM, check for a CruzAware alert, and look up your zone. Do not call 911 to ask about the siren.

Moving sirens are not a warning. Those are response vehicles on their way to a call: engines, medics, patrol cars. Evacuation orders arrive through CruzAware, wireless emergency alerts, and official announcements from the Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE, never by counting sirens on Highway 9. One more reason the CruzAware signup matters.

where-to-eat-boulder-creek.title: Where to Eat in Boulder Creek Right Now
where-to-eat-boulder-creek.html:
For a town this size, Boulder Creek keeps a real kitchen running. If you're hungry today, you've got four sit-down restaurants, a taqueria and a Mexican counter spot, a hot dog stand with a fire pit, three cafes, three bakeries, two full markets and a deli, a bar, and a winery tasting room up the mountain. Everything below is grouped by type, with a link to each business's own page where one exists. Mountain-town hours move with the seasons, the weather, and the occasional staffing crunch, so check before you drive.

Sit-down restaurants

Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge at 13300 Big Basin Way is the historic special-occasion room, an Italian restaurant and lounge that's fed Boulder Creek for generations. Breakfast runs Wednesday through Sunday from 10:30 to 2, lunch Wednesday through Saturday, and dinner Wednesday through Sunday starting at 4. It's closed Monday and Tuesday. There are private rooms if you're planning something bigger than dinner.

The Red Pearl at 13151 Highway 9 handles Chinese food for the whole valley, dine-in or takeout, Wednesday through Sunday 11 to 9. It's closed Monday and Tuesday, and the site is clear that it doesn't deliver, so plan on picking up.

Tae's Edo Sushi at 13271 Highway 9 is the sushi bar in town. Dinner runs 4 to 8 Tuesday through Thursday and Sunday; Friday and Saturday add a lunch window from noon to 3 ahead of the usual dinner hours. Closed Monday.

The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse at 15520 Highway 9, a few minutes south of downtown, does burgers, pub fare, and craft beer, with a big outdoor area and a music stage under the redwoods. It takes online orders through its site. Posted hours vary across sources right now, so go straight to the website or call 831-610-8196 before you head over.

Counter service, tacos, and hot dogs

El Rey Leon at 12890 Central Avenue is a Mexican food truck with counter service and online ordering. Posted hours are Monday through Saturday, 11 to 3 and again 4 to 7. Sunday isn't on the schedule they publish, so call before a Sunday run.

Taqueria Los Gallos at 13070 Highway 9 downtown doesn't keep a website, but it's on the Boulder Creek Business Association's current food listings. Call 831-217-5000 for hours before you go.

River Dogs of Boulder Creek at 123 Forest Street, tucked behind Joe's Bar and Scarborough's, is an outdoor stand doing Chicago-style Vienna Beef hot dogs and sausages, with a fire pit and a small stage for acoustic music. Its posted winter schedule is Tuesday through Thursday 11:30 to 3 and Friday through Sunday 11:30 to 5. It closes for bad weather and special events, which is what you'd expect from a place that's genuinely outdoors.

Coffee, breakfast, and lunch

Three cafes cover the morning shift. All three are small enough that calling ahead beats trusting the internet.

Jenna Sue's Cafe at 13090 Highway 9 is the downtown coffeehouse, pouring Java Bob's coffee alongside breakfast, lunch, and baked goods. Its own page is older, but the business association lists it as current at 831-703-4280; confirm hours by phone.

Bella's Cafe at 13132 Central Avenue serves American breakfast and lunch. Its Visit Santa Cruz County listing shows daily 8 to 2, but that listing predates this year, so treat the hours as a starting point and call 831-217-5070 to be sure.

The Tree House Cafe at 13266 Highway 9 sits creekside at the north end of the business district, with coffee, breakfast, and sandwiches. It runs on Facebook rather than a website; the business association listing has it current at 831-610-8912.

Bakeries

Dessert First Bakery at 13090 Highway 9, Suite 2, bakes custom goods with all-natural, locally sourced ingredients. Its Visit Santa Cruz County listing, updated in February 2026, shows daily hours of 6:30 a.m. to 1:30 p.m., which makes it the earliest food in town on paper. Custom work is the heart of the business, so call 831-338-7008 before you count on a walk-in case.

Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed cottage bakery working out of a Boulder Creek home kitchen, which means online orders rather than a storefront, and no published address. Cookies, brownies, mini donuts, and cookie cakes, with seasonal flavors rotating through the shop page, local delivery, and California-only shipping under cottage food rules.

Wildstone Bakery is Boulder Creek's naturally fermented bread and pastry maker, also without a storefront. You'll find its loaves, bagels, and seasonal tarts at the Downtown Santa Cruz Farmers Market on Wednesdays from 12:30 to 5, and at the Ben Lomond Village Market on first Saturdays, June through October 2026, from 10 to 2.

Markets and delis

Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9 has been the town grocery since 1938: produce, meat and seafood, a deli making sandwiches from family recipes, a bakery case, cheese, and a deep beer, wine, and spirits selection. Hours are Monday through Thursday and Sunday 8 to 9, Friday and Saturday 8 to 10, which makes it the latest food in town most nights.

Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural grocery, open daily 9 to 9, with a deli kitchen turning out prepared foods alongside produce, meat, and seafood sourced heavily from local farms.

The Redwood Keg at 12980 Highway 9 is the local liquor store with a deli counter, listed on the SLV Chamber's current food and takeout page. There's no website, so call 831-338-2727 to check deli hours before you make the trip for a sandwich.

A bar and a winery

Joe's Bar at 13118 Highway 9 is the downtown bar and live music room, the sort of place locals will tell you defines the town. It appears on Visit Santa Cruz County's current dining pages, and its listing shows daily hours from 10 a.m., though that listing hasn't been refreshed recently, so confirm by phone at 831-338-9417 if timing matters.

Big Basin Vineyards' estate tasting room at 830 Memory Lane pours its mountain-grown wines at the vineyard itself on Saturdays and Sundays from noon to 5. Reservations go through the booking system on its site. This is the Boulder Creek estate, separate from the winery's Santa Cruz tasting room.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's open on Monday in Boulder Creek?

Monday is the quiet day. Scopazzi's, the Red Pearl, and Tae's Edo are all closed, and River Dogs' winter schedule starts Tuesday. Your reliable Monday options are the markets, with Johnnie's and Wild Roots both open daily, plus El Rey Leon's posted Monday hours.

Which restaurants deliver?

Plan on pickup. The Red Pearl states plainly that it doesn't deliver, and El Rey Leon and the Sawmill run online ordering for pickup rather than delivery. The exception is baked goods: Butter Late Than Never delivers its cottage bakery orders locally.

Where can I eat early in the morning?

Dessert First's listing shows a 6:30 a.m. open, the earliest posted time in town. Bella's listing shows 8 a.m., and Johnnie's opens at 8 daily if a deli sandwich counts as breakfast. Call the cafes to confirm their current opening times.

Why aren't hours printed for every place on this list?

Because we couldn't verify them to a current source, and a wrong hour is worse than no hour. Where a business's posted schedule conflicted across sources or hadn't been updated recently, we left it out and gave you the phone number instead.

A place I know is missing. Why?

We list businesses we can point to through their own site or a current official listing. A few local food makers without any public web presence didn't make the cut yet, and we'll add them as soon as they have a page we can point to. If you run one of them, get in touch.

where-to-eat-breakfast-boulder-creek.title: Where to Eat Breakfast in Boulder Creek
where-to-eat-breakfast-boulder-creek.html:
For a sit-down breakfast in Boulder Creek, you've got three downtown cafes: Bella's Cafe, Jenna Sue's Cafe, and The Tree House Cafe. If all you want is coffee and something to eat on the way through, Johnnie's runs a deli counter that makes sandwiches fresh daily. Here's what's open for breakfast in town, each with the facts we can verify and a link to its listing.

Jenna Sue's Cafe

Address: 13090 Highway 9, Boulder Creek, CA 95006. Phone: (831) 703-4280. Hours vary, so confirm by phone.

Jenna Sue's Cafe is the downtown coffee shop and breakfast cafe, pouring espresso drinks alongside breakfast, sandwiches, and house-baked goods. Good place to land if you're up before the mountain is. We don't have its current hours confirmed, so call ahead.

Bella's Cafe

Address: 13132 Central Ave, Boulder Creek, CA 95006. Phone: (831) 217-5070.

Bella's Cafe is a locally owned American breakfast and lunch cafe in the heart of downtown. It's the classic sit-down morning plate, eggs and the rest of it, in a small room a block off Highway 9. Our listing doesn't carry confirmed current hours for Bella's, so call ahead at (831) 217-5070 before you make the drive, especially early or late in the day.

The Tree House Cafe

Address: 13266 CA-9, Boulder Creek, CA 95006. Phone: (831) 610-8912.

The Tree House Cafe is the cafe and espresso bar at the north edge of the business district, and it's a genuine Boulder Creek landmark: giant redwoods grow up through the interior of the building. Coffee and a light breakfast here come with a setting no chain can copy. We don't have verified current hours for it, so check by phone at (831) 610-8912 before heading over.

Johnnie's deli counter, for grab-and-go

Address: 13225 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Hours: Monday to Thursday 8:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m.; Friday and Saturday 8:00 a.m. to 10:00 p.m.; Sunday 8:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m.

Not every morning needs a table. Johnnie's Super Market & Liquor is the town grocery, and its deli counter makes fresh sandwiches daily, the reliable option when you'd rather eat in the truck or at the trailhead than in a dining room. The store opens at 8 a.m. every day, so it covers the mornings you're moving early and don't want to sit down. It's a full grocery too, so you can grab coffee, fruit, and a pastry from the bakery case in the same stop.

A later start: Scopazzi's brunch

If you're closer to a late-morning brunch than a sunrise breakfast, Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge at 13300 Big Basin Way serves breakfast Wednesday through Sunday from 10:30 a.m. to 2:00 p.m. It's the historic Italian room in town, and it's closed Monday and Tuesday. On a weekend morning when you want to linger rather than rush, it's the sit-down brunch table. Its dinner service is covered in our dinner guide.

What Boulder Creek doesn't have for breakfast

Worth setting expectations: our directory doesn't list a national coffee chain or a drive-through anywhere in town. Breakfast in Boulder Creek means an independent cafe, a grocery deli counter, or a weekend brunch table, not a franchise window. It's part of the character of the place, and it's the reason calling ahead beats assuming. Mountain-town cafe hours move with the season, the weather, and staffing, so a quick phone check saves a wasted drive.

For every meal beyond the morning, our full where to eat in Boulder Creek guide covers the sit-down restaurants, taquerias, markets, and the rest.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the earliest breakfast in Boulder Creek?

We don't have Jenna Sue's Cafe's current hours confirmed, so call ahead if an early start matters. Johnnie's Super Market opens its doors at 8:00 a.m. daily if a deli sandwich or a bakery-case pastry counts as breakfast for you.

Is there anywhere open for breakfast on Monday?

Jenna Sue's Cafe is open Monday, though we don't have its current hours confirmed, so call ahead. Johnnie's is open daily from 8:00 a.m. Scopazzi's brunch runs Wednesday through Sunday, so it's closed Monday. We don't have confirmed current hours for Bella's or The Tree House Cafe, so call before counting on either on a Monday.

Where can I get coffee to go?

Jenna Sue's and The Tree House Cafe are both espresso bars, and Johnnie's carries coffee in the store. There's no national coffee chain or drive-through in Boulder Creek, so a to-go cup comes from one of the local cafes or the market.

Why aren't hours listed for every cafe here?

Because we only publish hours we can verify to a current source, and a wrong hour is worse than none. Where our listing doesn't carry confirmed current hours, as with Bella's Cafe and The Tree House Cafe, we've given you the phone number instead so you can check directly.

A breakfast spot I know is missing. Why?

We list places we can point to through their own page or a current official listing. If a local morning spot doesn't have a page we can point to yet, it isn't here, and we'll add it as soon as it does. If you run one, or spot a detail that's changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

where-to-eat-dinner-boulder-creek.title: Dinner in Boulder Creek: Every Sit-Down Option in Town
where-to-eat-dinner-boulder-creek.html:
Once the light goes off the redwoods, dinner in Boulder Creek comes down to six kitchens: El Rey Leon, Red Pearl Chinese Cuisine, Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge, Tae's Edo Sushi Bar, Taqueria Los Gallos, and The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse. That's the whole evening roster in town. It runs Italian, Chinese, Japanese, Mexican, and American pub fare, and it ranges from sit-down dining rooms to counter service to El Rey Leon's Mexican food truck. Here's what's actually open for dinner, grouped by kind, with the hours we can verify.

Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge

Address: 13300 Big Basin Way, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 338-4444. Dinner hours: Wednesday through Sunday, 4:00 p.m. to closing. Closed Monday and Tuesday.

Scopazzi's Restaurant & Lounge is the historic Italian restaurant and lounge in town, the special-occasion room that's fed Boulder Creek for generations. Dinner runs Wednesday through Sunday starting at 4 p.m. There's a lounge on site, plus private-event rooms if you're planning something larger than a table for two. It's closed Monday and Tuesday, so weekend plans are the safe bet.

Red Pearl Chinese Cuisine

Address: 13151 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 338-9800. Hours: Wednesday through Sunday, 11:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. Closed Monday and Tuesday.

Red Pearl Chinese Cuisine covers Chinese food for the valley, dine-in or takeout, from downtown Boulder Creek. The kitchen runs to 9 p.m. Wednesday through Sunday, which makes it one of the later dinners in town on the nights it's open. Like several places here, it takes Monday and Tuesday off.

Tae's Edo Sushi Bar

Address: 13271 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 338-2099. Hours: Tuesday through Thursday and Sunday 4:00 to 8:00 p.m.; Friday and Saturday 12:00 to 3:00 p.m. and 4:00 to 8:00 p.m. Closed Monday.

Tae's Edo Sushi Bar is the Japanese and sushi restaurant in town, serving sushi and other Japanese dishes for dinner, with an added lunch window on Friday and Saturday. Dinner service ends at 8 p.m., earlier than the pub and the Chinese kitchen, so think of it as an earlier-evening plan rather than a late one. It's closed Monday.

El Rey Leon

Address: 12890 Central Avenue, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 282-7792. Hours: Monday through Saturday, 11:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. and 4:00 to 7:00 p.m.

El Rey Leon is a Mexican food truck with counter service and online ordering. Its posted evening window runs to 7 p.m., Monday through Saturday, which makes it more of an early-dinner or takeout-home option than a linger-late one. It's also one of the few kitchens in town posting Monday hours, so keep it in mind at the start of the week.

Taqueria Los Gallos

Address: 13070 Central Ave, Boulder Creek, CA 95006. Phone: (831) 217-5000.

Taqueria Los Gallos is the downtown counter-service taqueria, serving tacos, burritos, and other Mexican dishes. It's a counter-style spot rather than a formal dining room, and a solid pick when you want dinner without a fuss. Our listing doesn't carry confirmed current hours for it, so call (831) 217-5000 before you go, particularly in the evening.

The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse

Address: 15520 Highway 9, Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 610-8196.

The Sawmill Restaurant & Alehouse sits a few minutes south of downtown and does burgers, pub fare, and craft beer, with outdoor seating and a music stage under the trees. On a warm evening on the patio, it's the closest thing to a dinner-and-a-night-out combination in town. We don't have verified current hours for it, so check by phone at (831) 610-8196 before heading over, and see our night out guide for the after-dinner side of the Sawmill.

What dinner in Boulder Creek doesn't include

A few things worth knowing before you head out. There's no late-night dining in town: the kitchens that post hours close by 8 or 9 p.m., and El Rey Leon wraps up at 7. Monday is the quietest night, with Red Pearl, Scopazzi's, and Tae's all closed, which leaves El Rey Leon's posted Monday hours and whatever the taqueria and the Sawmill are running that day. For grab-and-go, deli counters, markets, and the rest of the food picture, our full where to eat in Boulder Creek guide has it.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's open for dinner on Monday in Boulder Creek?

Monday is the slow night. Red Pearl, Scopazzi's, and Tae's Edo are all closed. El Rey Leon posts Monday hours until 7:00 p.m., and Taqueria Los Gallos and The Sawmill may be open, though we don't have their confirmed hours, so call first.

Which Boulder Creek restaurants are open latest?

Among the places that post hours, Red Pearl runs to 9:00 p.m. Wednesday through Sunday, the latest confirmed dinner service in town. Tae's Edo closes at 8:00 p.m. and El Rey Leon at 7:00 p.m. There's no late-night dining in Boulder Creek, so plan on an earlier evening.

Is there a sit-down restaurant open every night?

No. Several kitchens close Monday and Tuesday, so there's no single restaurant open seven nights a week. Check the day-by-day hours above, and remember that El Rey Leon is one of the few posting Monday service.

Why aren't hours listed for every restaurant?

We only publish hours we can verify to a current source. For Taqueria Los Gallos and The Sawmill, our listing doesn't carry confirmed current hours, so we've given you the phone number to check directly rather than print something that might be wrong.

A restaurant I know is missing. Why?

We list sit-down places we can point to through their own page or a current official listing. Grab-and-go spots, markets, and food stands are covered in our main eating guide. If a dinner spot is missing or a detail has changed, let us know at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

where-to-find-sourdough-boulder-creek.title: Where to Find Real Sourdough Around Boulder Creek
where-to-find-sourdough-boulder-creek.html:
Go looking for a bakery counter and a fresh sourdough loaf on a random Tuesday in Boulder Creek, and you'll come up empty. The town doesn't have one. What it has instead is a small network of cottage bakers and farm stands: Wildstone Bakery at regional farmers markets, Butter Late Than Never for small-batch and custom baked goods, and, a few minutes down the valley, Dough and Dahlias in Ben Lomond and the Doughlicious Farmstand on Lake Drive. Here's where the naturally leavened bread actually comes from around here, and how to get it.

Wildstone Bakery

Base: Boulder Creek. Phone: (831) 252-0706. Where to find it: Downtown Santa Cruz Farmers Market, Wednesdays 12:30 to 5:00 p.m.; Ben Lomond Village Market, first Saturdays June through October 2026, 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m.

Wildstone Bakery is the Boulder Creek maker most squarely built around naturally fermented bread. The loaves, bagels, pastries, and seasonal tarts go out to regional farmers markets rather than a storefront, so you catch it at a market stall, not a shop counter. Plan around the market schedule above, or call ahead at (831) 252-0706 about ordering. This is the closest thing the town has to a dedicated sourdough bakery.

Butter Late Than Never Baking Co.

Base: Boulder Creek. Phone: (510) 390-5548. Local delivery and California distribution.

Butter Late Than Never Baking Co. is a licensed Boulder Creek cottage bakery selling small-batch baked goods, custom orders, and wholesale. Our listing describes it as a small-batch and custom bakery rather than naming a specific sourdough line, so if a sourdough loaf is what you're after, ask about it as a custom order when you get in touch. As a cottage operation it works from a home kitchen, which means orders and delivery rather than a storefront, and no published address. Reach it at (510) 390-5548 or through its site to ask what's available.

Dough and Dahlias, Ben Lomond

Base: Ben Lomond, local pickup. Order and updates: Instagram, @doughandahlias.

Dough and Dahlias is a home-based Ben Lomond cottage operation offering homemade sourdough breads and baked goods for local pickup, along with cut-flower bouquets grown in the owner's garden. Being home-based, it has no storefront and no public address; ordering and pickup are arranged through its Instagram page. It's a short drive down Highway 9 from Boulder Creek, and the sourdough-and-flowers pairing is the kind of small mountain operation this valley runs on.

Doughlicious Farmstand

Address: 280 Lake Drive, Boulder Creek, CA 95006.

Doughlicious Farmstand is a neighborhood farm stand from baker Liz De Spain, known for sourdough and seasonal goods, at 280 Lake Drive on the north side of Boulder Creek. It's active on Facebook and Instagram, which is where stand updates and what's-available news tend to appear. Our listing doesn't carry set hours or a phone number for it, so those social pages are the place to check before you head over, since farm stands run on their own rhythm.

What Boulder Creek doesn't have

Worth saying plainly: there's no daily walk-in bakery counter for sourdough in Boulder Creek. The bread here comes from cottage bakers, farmers-market stalls, and farm stands, which means it rewards a little planning, following an Instagram page, catching a market day, or placing an order ahead. That's not a knock on the town. It's how small-batch, naturally leavened baking tends to work in a mountain community, and it's the difference between driving over the hill for a supermarket loaf and getting bread made by someone a few streets away. For everything else food-related in town, see our where to eat in Boulder Creek guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a sourdough bakery in Boulder Creek?

Not a storefront one. Wildstone Bakery is the Boulder Creek maker built around naturally fermented bread, but it sells at regional farmers markets rather than from a shop counter. For a walk-up address in town, the Doughlicious Farmstand at 280 Lake Drive is the closest option, on its own farm-stand schedule.

Where can I buy sourdough near Boulder Creek right now?

Catch Wildstone Bakery at the Downtown Santa Cruz Farmers Market on Wednesdays or the Ben Lomond Village Market on first Saturdays in season, check the Doughlicious Farmstand's social pages for stand days, or arrange pickup from Dough and Dahlias in Ben Lomond through its Instagram.

Does anyone deliver sourdough locally?

Butter Late Than Never offers local delivery and California distribution for its cottage-bakery orders, and it takes custom orders, so a sourdough loaf is worth asking about directly. Dough and Dahlias and Doughlicious work on local pickup rather than delivery.

Why don't these bakers have storefronts?

Most are cottage or home-based operations, which under California cottage food rules work from home kitchens with pickup, delivery, or market stalls rather than a public shop. That's also why we don't publish addresses for the home-based ones. It keeps small local baking possible in a town this size.

A baker I know is missing. Why?

We list makers we can point to through their own page or a current official listing. If a local sourdough baker has a page we can point to, or a detail here has changed, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

where-to-get-haircut-boulder-creek.title: Where to Get a Haircut in and Around Boulder Creek
where-to-get-haircut-boulder-creek.html:
You can walk to a haircut in Boulder Creek. Three salons work downtown, close enough that you could pass all three on one errand, and not a barber pole among them. Two more sit a few minutes south in Ben Lomond, and the valley's one dedicated barbershop is in Felton. What follows is who cuts hair and where, grouped by community, with the details we could confirm. Most of these are small, appointment-driven shops, so call first. Booking beats walking in almost everywhere on this list.

Worth saying up front: if you want a barbershop, with fades, tapers, beard work, and a straight-razor shave, there isn't a dedicated one in Boulder Creek or Ben Lomond. The in-valley shops are full-service hair salons that cut men's, women's, and kids' hair. The nearest true barbershop is in Felton, covered at the end.

Hair Hut (Boulder Creek)

Address: 13266 Highway 9, Suite A. Phone: 831-239-0317. Hours: Tuesday through Thursday 9am to 7pm, Friday 9am to 5:30pm, Saturday 10am to 5pm, closed Sunday and Monday.

Hair Hut is the long-running full-service salon at the north end of downtown. Cuts, color, highlights, perms, and extensions, for men, women, and children, which makes it the closest thing in town to a one-stop shop for a whole family. It also keeps the most regular posted hours of any salon in Boulder Creek, so it's a reasonable first call.

Central Avenue Salon (Boulder Creek)

Address: 13162 Central Avenue. Phone: 415-725-0850. Hours: by appointment.

Central Avenue Salon is stylist Dana Bernhardt's shop, a block off the main drag on Central. Cuts, color, and extensions, by appointment rather than drop-in, so plan to book ahead. It's a single-stylist operation, which some people prefer for the consistency of seeing the same person every time.

Oh Suzannah Hair Designers (Boulder Creek)

Address: 13090 Highway 9. Phone: 831-338-3883. Hours: Tuesday through Friday 10am to 5pm, Saturday 10am to 4pm, closed Sunday and Monday.

Oh Suzannah is the third downtown salon, offering color, cuts, perms, and relaxers, plus facial waxing if you want to add it to a visit. Its posted hours run a bit later in the week than some, with Saturday appointments available. As with the others, a quick call to confirm the day's schedule is the safe bet before you drive in.

VIP Hair Studio (Ben Lomond)

Address: 10090 Highway 9, Suite 2, Ben Lomond. Phone: 831-609-6563. Hours: by appointment only.

A few minutes south in Ben Lomond, VIP Hair Studio is run by longtime San Lorenzo Valley stylist Melissa Bumbaca. It's an appointment-only cut-and-color salon, worth knowing about when the downtown Boulder Creek shops are booked or closed on the day you need them. Being in Ben Lomond, it's a short drive down Highway 9 rather than an in-town option.

K-Salon (Ben Lomond)

Location: Ben Lomond (home-based, no public storefront address). Website: ksalon.us.

K-Salon is a home-based salon run by stylist Krishna, offering cut and color services in a private redwood setting. Because it works out of a home rather than a storefront, there's no walk-in address to list, and scheduling goes through the website. Good fit if you like a quieter, one-on-one setting over a busy shop floor.

Society Barbershop (Felton)

Address: 6287 Highway 9, Felton. Online booking available at societybarbershop.com. Phone: (408) 800-2219.

If a barbershop is what you're after, Society Barbershop in Felton is the closest one, about a fifteen-minute drive down Highway 9. It's a full-service shop offering fades and tapers, with online booking through its site. We don't have its current daily hours confirmed, so book online or call before heading down.

Where the valley comes up short

For everyday cuts, color, and kids' trims, Boulder Creek is covered without leaving town. The gap is barbering as its own craft. None of the Boulder Creek or Ben Lomond shops is a dedicated barbershop, so a classic barber experience means driving to Felton. That's no knock on the local salons, which cut men's hair too. It's the arithmetic of a small town, and worth knowing before you go looking for a barber pole downtown.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a barbershop in Boulder Creek?

Not a dedicated one. The three hair salons in town cut men's, women's, and children's hair, but the nearest shop that specializes in barbering, with fades and tapers, is Society Barbershop in Felton, roughly fifteen minutes south on Highway 9.

Can I walk in for a haircut, or do I need an appointment?

Most of these shops lean toward appointments. Central Avenue Salon, VIP Hair Studio, and K-Salon are appointment-based, and Society Barbershop takes online bookings. Hair Hut and Oh Suzannah keep posted hours, but calling ahead is still the safe move at any small salon, since a single stylist can be fully booked on a given day.

Which salons are actually in Boulder Creek versus nearby?

Hair Hut, Central Avenue Salon, and Oh Suzannah Hair Designers are all in Boulder Creek proper. VIP Hair Studio and K-Salon are in Ben Lomond, and Society Barbershop is in Felton, all a short drive down Highway 9.

A shop I know is missing. Why?

This list only includes hair businesses we could verify as current listings in our directory. If a stylist or shop near Boulder Creek isn't here, it may not have a public listing we can confirm yet. If you run one, or want a correction, tell us at bouldercreeklocal.com/submit.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, it's a starting point. See the full business directory for contact details, and if anything above has changed, let us know through the submit page so the next neighbor gets it right.

where-to-stay-boulder-creek.title: Where to Stay in and Around Boulder Creek
where-to-stay-boulder-creek.html:
Boulder Creek has a handful of small lodges and cabin resorts, the closest chain hotel is a few miles south in Ben Lomond, and most visitors round out the options with a vacation rental. Below are the fixed places to stay, plus how to think about camping, group retreat centers, and booking an Airbnb or VRBO in the mountains. This isn't a ranking, and listing a place isn't an endorsement. It's a factual starting point so you can match the stay to your trip.

Lodges and Cabins

Masood's Lodge at 13420 Big Basin Way in Boulder Creek (831-246-4517) is a small lodge offering cabin and studio rooms in a wooded creekside setting, with check-in from 3 to 9 p.m. and check-out at 11 a.m. It's the in-town pick if you want to walk to downtown Boulder Creek.

Brookdale Lodge at 11570 Highway 9 in Brookdale (831-609-6010) is the historic landmark hotel just south of town, with remodeled, pet-friendly rooms and the on-site 1950s-style Brookdale Diner. Here's the precise status worth knowing: the rooms, the diner, and the River Run lounge and event space are operating, but the storied Brook Room dining hall, the one with a creek running through it, is not currently running as a standalone restaurant. So book it for the rooms and the history, and check ahead on which spaces are open during your dates.

Fern River Resort at 5250 Highway 9 in Felton (831-335-4412) is a third-generation family resort with rustic cabins overlooking the San Lorenzo River, some with kitchens or fireplaces, plus on-site kayaking. The office is open daily 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.

Quality Inn & Suites Santa Cruz Mountains at 9733 Highway 9 in Ben Lomond (831-336-2292) is the closest chain hotel to Boulder Creek, with rooms and suites, free hot breakfast, an outdoor pool, and a 24-hour front desk. It's the safe pick if you want predictable hotel amenities.

Camping and RV

Redwood Resort RV Park and Campground at 150 Grove Street in Boulder Creek (831-338-3413) offers seasonal tent and RV sites plus year-round monthly RV spaces, right in town.

For state-park camping, the Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park Campground at 101 North Big Trees Park Road in Felton (831-335-4598) has 107 reservable sites, each with a fire ring, picnic table, and bear-proof food locker, plus flush restrooms and coin-operated hot showers. It's open year-round, runs around $35 a night, and reserves through ReserveCalifornia at 800-444-7275.

Group and Retreat Centers (and What They Are Not)

A few large properties up here look like lodging on a map but are really group and retreat centers, so it helps to know what they are before you call. The Sequoia Retreat Center in Ben Lomond (831-218-5872) is a redwood wedding and retreat venue with on-site cabins, geared to booked events and groups rather than nightly walk-up rooms. Mount Hermon Christian Camps & Conference Center (831-335-4466) offers cabin and lodge stays with meals, but primarily for retreats, conferences, and organized groups. Redwood Christian Park in Boulder Creek (831-338-2134) is a Christian retreat and family-camp center that rents to church, youth, and family groups, not as an open hotel. In short, these are excellent for a reunion, a retreat, or a group getaway, and not the place to book a single room for a weekend.

Vacation Rentals: Airbnb and VRBO

Beyond the fixed lodges, most Boulder Creek stays happen in vacation rentals, cabins and homes tucked into the redwoods. We don't name individual listings, because a rental that exists only on a booking platform, with no independent website or identity of its own, isn't something we can verify and stand behind the way we do a named business. What we can do is point you to the search and tell you how to book a mountain rental well.

Start with an area search on Airbnb or VRBO for Boulder Creek, Ben Lomond, Brookdale, or Felton, and read recent reviews closely. Then keep a few mountain realities in mind that flatland trips don't have:

Cell coverage is spotty. Download your directions and the host's check-in instructions before you leave a reliable signal, and get a phone number you can call from a landline or Wi-Fi if you get turned around.

Arriving after dark on Highway 9 is harder than it looks. The road is winding, narrow in spots, and unlit, and many rental driveways are steep and unmarked. Aim to arrive in daylight for a first-time stay, and ask the host for a landmark, not just an address.

Fire season is real from roughly late summer into fall. Ask the host about their evacuation plan and access road, note that red-flag conditions can prompt precautionary power shutoffs, and check current conditions before and during your stay. Our Mountain Status page links the official sources for weather, roads, power, and air quality.

Build in flexibility. Winter storms can close or delay Highway 9, and summer wildfire activity can change plans fast, so favor a rental with a reasonable cancellation policy and confirm it before you book.

For live road and condition updates near your dates, check the Mountain Status page, and see our roads and parking notes for the durable patterns on Highway 9.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the closest hotel to Boulder Creek?

For an in-town small lodge, Masood's Lodge is right in Boulder Creek. For a full-service chain hotel with predictable amenities, the Quality Inn & Suites in Ben Lomond is the closest, a few miles south on Highway 9, with free breakfast, a pool, and a 24-hour front desk. Brookdale Lodge in neighboring Brookdale is the historic hotel option.

Can I camp near Boulder Creek?

Yes. Redwood Resort RV Park and Campground is in town with tent, RV, and monthly sites. Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park Campground in Felton has 107 reservable sites open year-round, at about $35 a night, booked through ReserveCalifornia at 800-444-7275. Sites include bear-proof food lockers, which you should use.

Is Brookdale Lodge open?

Yes, with a caveat. The remodeled, pet-friendly rooms, the Brookdale Diner, and the River Run lounge and event space are operating as of July 2026. The historic Brook Room dining hall, the one with the creek running through it, is not currently running as a standalone restaurant. Confirm which spaces are open for your dates when you book.

Should I book an Airbnb or a lodge?

It depends on your trip. A named lodge or resort gives you a front desk, set check-in, and a known operator, which is simpler for a short or first-time visit. A vacation rental can offer more space, a kitchen, and privacy, which suits families and longer stays, as long as you plan for spotty cell service, a daylight arrival, and a flexible cancellation policy. We don't rank individual rentals; search the platforms and read recent reviews.

Inclusion here isn't an endorsement, just a verified place to start. Browse the full directory for local lodging and services, and send corrections or additions through the submit page so the next visitor gets accurate information.

wildfire-evacuation-boulder-creek.title: How Boulder Creek Prepares for a Wildfire Evacuation
wildfire-evacuation-boulder-creek.html:
Boulder Creek is home to a lot of us, and if a wildfire threatens it, the decision to evacuate is not one you'll read here. Official evacuation warnings and orders come from the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE, and they reach you through CruzAware, the county's alert system. This site can't tell you a road is clear, a zone is safe, or that it's fine to stay. What it can do is help you get ready now, so that when an alert comes, you already know your zone, you have a bag packed, and you're not making big decisions for the first time in the dark. For anything happening right now, a fire you can see or smell, call 911. Everything below is for the calm days before a fire, which are the only days to do this.

Know Your Zone Before There's Ever a Fire

Santa Cruz County divides the whole county into small evacuation zones, and every address sits in one. During an emergency, the Sheriff and fire agencies issue warnings and orders zone by zone, so the most useful thing you can do ahead of time is learn yours.

Look it up on Genasys Protect, the county's evacuation map (formerly called Zonehaven), or through the county's "Know Your Zone" page. Type in your address, and the map shows your Zone ID along with the fire and law enforcement agencies that cover you. Boulder Creek zone IDs look like BOU-E028, sometimes with a letter on the end, like BOU-E021A. The zones are small, so look up your own address rather than assuming a neighbor's zone is yours, and write it down. Learn the zones your kids' school, your work, and your parents' place fall in too, because an order for one zone tells you nothing about the next one over.

Knowing your zone helps, but learn your surroundings too. The county notes it has more than 200 zones and sub-zones, and says public messaging should lean on recognizable streets and landmarks rather than zone numbers. So an alert may name your zone ID or it may describe the area in plain geography. Know both: your Zone ID, and the roads that bound your neighborhood.

Watch, Warning, Order: Three Levels, Not Two

Santa Cruz County uses three evacuation levels, and they don't mean the same thing. Learn all three now. Ready, Set, GO is the shorthand the county attaches to them.

Evacuation Watch ("Ready"). Notification of a possible threat to life or property. Get ready. If you need extra time to leave, consider evacuating early, at this stage. That includes people with disabilities, people with access and functional needs, and anyone with pets or livestock.

Evacuation Warning ("Set"). Notification of a potential threat to life or property. If you need extra time to leave, begin evacuating immediately. Everyone else: be packed, be fueled, be ready to move the moment an order comes. A warning is your cue to load the car, not to keep an eye on it.

Evacuation Order ("GO"). Mandatory. Leave immediately. There is an immediate threat to life, and the area is legally closed to public access. Don't stop to pack more, don't wait to see how it develops, and don't drive around road barricades. In a fast-moving mountain fire there may be no time for anyone to knock on your door, so the order is the knock.

Two more statuses come at the other end, and the difference matters:

Evacuation Orders Lifted means you may return, but some hazards or restrictions may remain. Downed lines, damaged roads, unstable trees, and closed areas are all still possible. Lifted is not safe.

All Clear means all hazards have been fully mitigated. Only this status means the incident hazards are resolved. Never read an all clear into a lifted order, and never read either one off a neighbor's post. Wait for the county to say it.

Shelter in place means stay where you are because leaving is riskier than going. If officials tell you to shelter in place: go indoors, close and lock all doors and windows, stay put, keep your phone on and charged, and wait to be contacted. That instruction, like the others, comes from officials, not from this site.

If an evacuation is ordered, officials may designate a Temporary Evacuation Point, a location announced at the time of the incident where evacuees can gather and get information. Its location is decided during the emergency, so there is no standing site to plan around. Watch CruzAware and the Sheriff's Office for where it is. This site will never name one in advance.

If You Can't Get Out

Plan to leave early so this never applies. If it does apply, if your route is blocked or you are cut off, do this:

  1. Call 911.
  2. Follow CAL FIRE's shelter-in-place guidance: go indoors, close and lock doors and windows, stay put, keep your phone on, and wait to be contacted.

Do not drive into smoke to force a route. Low visibility, downed lines, and stopped traffic ahead of you are how people get trapped in a car, and a car is worse shelter than a house. The county's own evacuation planning notes that residents of this area may have to shelter in place until roads are cleared. That is a real possibility here, and it's another argument for leaving at the watch or the warning rather than the order.

Where the Real Orders and Information Come From

In an emergency, rumors move faster than fire. A neighbor's group text, a screenshot of a screenshot, a well-meaning post with no source behind it: none of that is an evacuation order. Get your information from the places that issue it.

  • CruzAware is the county's alert system and the main way evacuation warnings and orders reach your phone by text, call, and email. If you do nothing else after reading this, sign up at cruzaware.org. Register more than one contact method and more than one address. We have a full walkthrough in our CruzAware setup guide.
  • Wireless Emergency Alerts are the loud, buzzing alerts that arrive on your phone with no signup at all. Officials can push them to every capable phone in an area, which means they reach visitors, guests, and phones that were never registered for anything. Leave them turned on. They are a different channel from CruzAware, and they cover the people CruzAware can't. CruzAware is the one you have to register for.
  • The Genasys Protect map shows the current status of every zone during an incident, color-coded, so you can see whether your zone is under a warning or an order.
  • The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE are the agencies that make and announce evacuation decisions. CAL FIRE's Active Incidents page tracks fires across the state.
  • 911 is for reporting a fire or an emergency and for a life-threatening situation right now. It is not a general information line, so keep it clear for people who need it.
  • Non-emergency questions go elsewhere: the Sheriff's Office main line is (831) 454-7600, and the Boulder Creek Fire Protection District is (831) 338-7222. Neither line issues evacuation orders, and neither can tell you a road is clear.

No alert system is perfect. Phones lose signal, texts fail, and power goes out, which is common in a mountain fire. That's exactly why you sign up for more than one channel and why knowing your zone and your plan ahead of time matters so much. Treat every alert as one layer, not the whole safety net.

The Highway 9 Reality: Know More Than One Way Out

Boulder Creek is a canyon town with limited roads, and most traffic funnels onto Highway 9. It isn't the only way out. Highway 9 runs both north and south, and Highway 236 and Bear Creek Road lead out too. All of them are narrow, and any of them can close. In a large evacuation, that road carries a lot of cars at once, and it can also be the road firefighters need. This is not a reason to panic. It's a reason to leave early and to know your options before you need them.

Learn more than one route away from your home, and drive them on an ordinary day so they're familiar. Keep at least half a tank of gas in the car through fire season, because a full evacuation is no time to be hunting for an open station. Back your car into the driveway or park it facing out, so you're ready to pull away without a three-point turn.

One thing this site will never do is map a "safe route" out of town, because no one can promise a given road is passable or clear when a fire is moving. Conditions change by the minute, and only the officials running the incident have the full picture. When an order comes, follow the routes and directions they give you, obey road closures and barricades, and if you're told a road is closed, believe it. The whole point of leaving early is to be gone before the roads are the problem.

Leave Early. Don't Wait to Be Sure

The hardest part of evacuating is deciding to do it. It always feels too soon, right up until it's too late. Every fire agency says the same thing: if you feel at risk, don't wait for a mandatory order, and if an order comes, don't wait at all.

Leaving early is safer for you and for everyone else. It keeps the roads clearer for the people still coming out behind you and for the trucks coming in. It gives you time to move calmly instead of racing. And it means a stressful evening at a friend's house instead of being caught on the road. If you ever find yourself weighing whether it's "bad enough" to go, that hesitation is your answer. Go.

If you have animals, medical needs, young kids, or anyone in the house who moves slowly, build your plan around leaving at the watch or the warning, not the order. The extra hour is the whole game.

Get Your Home and Your Go-Bag Ready Now

Two kinds of preparation make the biggest difference, and both are done long before any smoke: hardening your home and space around it, and having a bag ready to grab.

Defensible space and home hardening. CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program lays out the buffer zones around a house, from the ember-resistant five feet closest to the walls out to 100 feet, plus the home-hardening details like vents, gutters, roofs, and decks that decide whether flying embers find a way in. We've pulled all of it into a printable home-prep and defensible-space checklist so you can walk your property with it in hand. Some of this work, especially limbing up and removing trees near the house, is a job for a professional. Our guide to hiring local tree services can help you find someone reputable up here.

Your go-bag. A go-bag is the kit you can grab and be out the door with in two minutes. It holds a few days of essentials, your medications, copies of key documents, phone chargers, cash, and supplies for your pets. Our printable go-bag checklist, built from CAL FIRE and American Red Cross guidance, lays it out item by item. Pack it once, keep it by the door, and check it a couple of times a year.

Do these two things and you've handled most of what's actually in your control. Keep sturdy shoes and a flashlight by your bed, too, in case an alert comes at 2 a.m.

Pets and Livestock

Animals are part of the plan, and the plan has to account for them before an emergency, not during one.

For pets, keep a carrier for each one, and store their food, water, medications, and vaccination records with your go-bag. Make sure collars and ID tags are current. When a warning comes, bring pets inside and keep them close so you're not chasing a scared cat under the porch when it's time to leave.

Livestock and horses take more lead time, which is the main reason to leave at the warning, not the order. Arrange transport ahead of season if you don't have your own trailer, and know where you'd take animals, whether that's a fairground, a friend's property, or a boarding facility out of the area. CAL FIRE's Go Evacuation Guide has detailed livestock and pet checklists worth reading before you need them. Firefighters focus on the fire and may not be able to move your animals, so their safety comes down to your own advance planning.

Why This Matters So Much Here

Boulder Creek doesn't need to be told that wildfire is real. In August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex burned through the Santa Cruz Mountains, and our community was among the hardest hit. According to CAL FIRE, the fire covered about 86,000 acres before it was contained that September, and it destroyed 1,490 structures and took one life. Many neighbors are still rebuilding, and many more carry the memory of that evacuation.

We raise this plainly and without drama because the lesson from CZU is a practical one, not a fearful one. The people who had already looked up their zone, packed a bag, and decided in advance that they'd leave early were the ones who moved fastest and with the least panic when it counted. Preparation didn't stop the fire, but it gave people room to make good decisions. That's the whole aim of this page: not to frighten anyone, and never to tell you it's safe, but to help you be ready and to know exactly where to look when the county speaks.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find my evacuation zone in Boulder Creek?

Go to Genasys Protect and type in your address. The map shows your Zone ID, which for Boulder Creek starts with BOU, along with the agencies that cover you. Look up your home, work, and family members' addresses now, while it's calm, and write the zones down.

Who actually issues evacuation orders here?

The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE make and announce evacuation decisions. They reach you mainly through CruzAware alerts and the Genasys Protect map. This site does not issue orders and can't tell you it's safe to stay or go. For a fire or an emergency right now, call 911.

What's the difference between an evacuation watch, warning, and order?

There are three levels. A watch ("Ready") means a possible threat: get ready, and consider leaving early if you need extra time, have animals, or have access and functional needs. A warning ("Set") means a potential threat: if you need extra time, begin evacuating immediately. An order ("GO") is mandatory: leave immediately, the threat to life is immediate and the area is legally closed to public access. Afterward, "Evacuation Orders Lifted" means you may return but hazards or restrictions may remain, while "All Clear" means all hazards have been fully mitigated. A lifted order is not an all clear.

Should I wait for an official order before I leave?

No. Every fire agency advises leaving before a mandatory order if you feel at risk, and leaving immediately once an order is issued. Leaving early keeps you safer and keeps the roads clear for others and for firefighters. If you're unsure whether it's time, that uncertainty is your cue to go.

Is Highway 9 the only way out of Boulder Creek?

No. Highway 9 runs both north and south from town, and Highway 236 and Bear Creek Road are also routes out. All of them are narrow mountain roads, and any of them can close, so treat none of them as guaranteed. Know more than one way out and drive your routes on ordinary days so they're familiar. Keep at least half a tank of gas through fire season and park facing out. In an actual evacuation, follow the routes officials give you and obey every road closure. This site won't map a "safe route," because no one can promise a road is clear when a fire is moving.

How do I get ready before fire season?

Sign up for CruzAware, look up your zone, pack a go-bag, and work through defensible space and home hardening around your house. Our printable go-bag checklist and home-prep checklist walk through both, and our CruzAware setup guide covers the alert signup step by step.

wildlife-around-boulder-creek.title: Wildlife Around Boulder Creek: Who You'll Meet and How to Be a Good Neighbor
wildlife-around-boulder-creek.html:
Living in Boulder Creek means sharing the neighborhood with wildlife, and the honest summary is that it works out fine almost all the time. Deer will eat your roses, turkeys will own your driveway for an afternoon, raccoons will test your trash cans, and once in a long while a mountain lion will pass through without you ever knowing. The whole system runs on a few simple habits: secure your trash and pet food, bring pets in at night, and never feed wild animals.
